title: A circular biorefinery approach for mango and lemon industrial waste: on-site Aspergillus welwitschiae cellulase production, enzymatic hydrolysis and bioethanol fermentation
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Biomass and Bioenergy
excerpt: Publication date: November 2026Source: Biomass and Bioenergy, Volume 214Author(s): Oscar Fernando Vázquez-Vuelvas, Juana María Jiménez-Vargas, María del Pilar Escalante-Minakata, Alejandra González-De la Cerda, Francisco J. Anguiano-Muñiz, Edgar Levi Miramontes-Salinas, Ernesto Gabriel Leal-Aguilar, Danna Paola Santana-Contreras, Carlos Eduardo Barajas-Saucedo, Roberto Muñiz-Valencia, María Luisa Esquivel-Esquivel, Laura Leticia Valdez-Velázquez
summary: This paper describes a circular biorefinery system that uses the fungus *Aspergillus welwitschiae* to convert mango and lemon processing waste into bioethanol. The fungus was grown on-site within the waste material itself, where it produced cellulase enzymes—proteins that break down plant cell walls. These enzymes then hydrolyzed the cellulose in the fruit waste into simpler sugars, which were subsequently fermented into bioethanol. The approach treats industrial fruit waste not as a disposal problem but as a feedstock for enzyme production and fuel generation within a single integrated process.  Within the material economy of fruit processing, this research documents how a fungal pathogen of plant tissue becomes simultaneously a production asset and a remediation tool. The same enzymatic capacity that allows *Aspergillus welwitschiae* to colonize and degrade fruit matter in storage—a sign of spoilage and loss—is here harnessed to metabolize industrial waste into marketable fuel. The system reveals a deeper entanglement: the waste streams of agricultural capitalism, the metabolic capabilities of fungi adapted to plant decomposition, and the infrastructure of energy production become bound together through fungal fermentation, such that economic value emerges from material that would otherwise accumulate as industrial excess.

title: Prenatal exposure to gas stoves, mold, and water damage: Associations with gestational duration and fetal growth in the ECHO cohort
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Environmental Pollution
excerpt: Epidemiological evidence on prenatal exposure to household environmental factors (gas stoves, mold, and dampness) and adverse birth outcomes remains limited and inconsistent. We examined associations of these exposures with gestational duration and fetal growth in 11,483 mother-infant dyads from 31 sites in the United States Environmental influences on Child Health Outcomes (ECHO) Cohort. Participants reported presence of a gas stove, mold/mildew (outside the shower/bathtub), and water damage in the home where they lived for the longest duration during their pregnancy (2000-2023). Gestational age at birth and birth-weight-for-gestational-age z-scores were primarily abstracted from medical records and analyzed continuously and categorically. We used covariate-adjusted linear or logistic regression models with a random effect for site to examine primary associations. We conducted exploratory analyses to assess effect modification by maternal race/ethnicity, education, hood/fan presence/use, and home construction year. Among participants, 53% reported having a gas stove; 13% reported mold/mildew; and 15% reported water damage. Overall, these exposures were not associated with birth outcomes. Several instances of effect modification were observed, particularly between mold/mildew and water damage and shorter gestation. Adverse associations with mold/mildew were more pronounced in newer homes (early-term odds ratio [OR]=1.35, 95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.98-1.84) and among mothers with higher educational attainment (preterm OR=1.44, 95% CI: 1.01-2.06). Conversely, the inverse association between water damage and preterm birth was stronger among non-Hispanic White participants (OR=0.70, 95% CI: 0.50-0.97). This large multi-site study observed no consistent associations between the measured household environmental exposures during pregnancy and gestational duration or fetal growth.
summary: This paper examined associations between three household environmental exposures during pregnancy—gas stoves, mold or mildew, and water damage—and birth outcomes in 11,483 pregnant people across 31 United States sites between 2000 and 2023. Participants reported the presence of these exposures in their homes during pregnancy, and researchers obtained gestational age and birth weight measurements from medical records. Gas stoves were reported by 53% of participants, mold or mildew by 13%, and water damage by 15%. Using statistical regression models adjusted for multiple factors and accounting for site variation, this study found no overall associations between these three exposures and either gestational duration or fetal growth measured as birth weight relative to gestational age.  The findings reveal a more complex pattern when examined by demographic subgroups. Mold or mildew showed adverse associations with shorter gestation specifically among mothers with higher educational attainment and those living in newer homes, while water damage showed a protective association with preterm birth only among non-Hispanic White participants. These divergent patterns across maternal race, education, and housing characteristics suggest that the relationship between household dampness and mould and fetal development is not uniform but rather contingent on intersecting conditions of housing quality, maternal social position, and structural inequality. The absence of consistent associations at the population level masks the uneven distribution of risk and protection across different groups—a pattern that reflects how exposure to the material conditions of dwelling is itself stratified by race, class, and the age of housing stock.

title: Across climates and limits: a Brazil-Mediterranean benchmark of aflatoxins in peanuts and peanut butters
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Research International
excerpt: Aflatoxins in peanuts and peanut products remain a key food-safety concern, with occurrence shaped by climate and post-harvest conditions. This study aimed to compare fungal contamination and aflatoxin occurrence in peanuts and peanut butters acquired in Brazil and in Mediterranean markets. Thirty-two samples (21 peanuts; 11 peanut butters) were analyzed for water activity (a w ), moisture, aflatoxins (HPLC), microbiological counts, Salmonella and Listeria monocytogenes detection, culture-dependent fungal identification, and fungal metataxonomics. Total aflatoxins in Brazilian peanuts ranged from 2.10 to 9.28 μg/kg, none exceeding Brazil's 20 μg/kg limit. In Mediterranean peanuts, one sample exceeded the European Union (EU) 4.0 μg/kg total-aflatoxin limit. In Mediterranean peanut butters, two samples exceeded the EU limit. No Brazilian peanut butter exceeded the national limit. Isolates from Mediterranean peanuts were identified as Cladosporium (50%), followed by Aspergillus (25%), Penicillium (12.5%), Talaromyces (6.25%), and Trechispora (6.25%), whereas Brazilian isolates were exclusively Aspergillus spp.. Metataxonomics of the most contaminated Brazilian peanuts revealed communities dominated by Aspergillus and Fusarium , with occasional co-occurrence of eight other fungal genera. Despite generally compliant aflatoxin levels, exceedances against EU limits were observed in some Mediterranean products. Fungal community structure, particularly dominance of Aspergillus section Flavi, appears more indicative of aflatoxin risk than total fungal load, underscoring the value of integrated monitoring (aw/moisture), targeted mycological surveillance, and molecular profiling across production chains. • Aflatoxins in peanuts and peanut products acquired in Brazil and Mediterranean. • Metataxonomics revealed communities of Aspergillus in Brazilian samples. • Cladosporium fungi dominated Mediterranean peanuts products. • Brazilian peanuts and peanut butters complied with natio
summary: This paper documents fungal contamination and aflatoxin occurrence across peanut supply chains in Brazil and Mediterranean regions, revealing distinct geographic patterns in both toxin levels and fungal community composition. Brazilian peanuts contained total aflatoxins ranging from 2.10 to 9.28 micrograms per kilogram, all within the national regulatory limit of 20 micrograms per kilogram, while one Mediterranean peanut sample and two Mediterranean peanut butter samples exceeded the European Union limit of 4.0 micrograms per kilogram. Fungal isolates differed markedly between regions: Mediterranean peanuts hosted diverse communities dominated by Cladosporium (50%), with smaller proportions of Aspergillus (25%), Penicillium (12.5%), Talaromyces (6.25%), and Trechispora (6.25%), whereas Brazilian isolates consisted exclusively of Aspergillus species. Genetic sequencing of the most contaminated Brazilian peanuts revealed fungal communities centred on Aspergillus and Fusarium, with eight additional fungal genera present at lower frequencies. The paper concludes that the specific composition of fungal communities—particularly dominance of Aspergillus section Flavi—serves as a more reliable indicator of aflatoxin risk than total fungal abundance alone, and recommends integrated monitoring of water activity and moisture alongside targeted fungal identification and molecular profiling across production and distribution chains.  Across these two geographically distinct supply systems, the same regulatory commodity reveals divergent material entanglements. The stricter European Union limit creates a different contamination profile than Brazil's higher threshold, yet both regions show that fungal risk is not evenly distributed—certain species assemblages, particularly Aspergillus section Flavi, concentrate aflatoxin production regardless of total fungal presence. This spatial variation in permissible toxin levels and dominant fungal taxa reflects not just climate and post-harvest handling, but also the regulatory architectures that shape what counts as safe food in different markets, and which fungal metabolisms become economically and epidemiologically consequential.

title: Broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity of a citric acid–phenolic formulation in animal feed matrices
source: Frontiers in Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: Microbial contamination in animal feed undermines livestock safety and storage stability, driving a critical demand for potent natural preservatives. This study investigated the antimicrobial efficacy of Alquermold Natural Plus L (AMNL), a formulation combining citric acid and bioactive monoterpenes, within compound feed and ground corn matrices. AMNL, applied at a dosage of 0.5 L/ton, was evaluated against a commercial benchmark at 2 L/ton. Both treatments were tested against key bacterial pathogens (Escherichia coli, Salmonella Typhimurium, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa) and fungal contaminants (Aspergillus niger, Fusarium oxysporum, and Rhizopus nigricans). Substrates were standardized to a contamination level of 106 CFU/g, with microbial reduction measured at 24 h and 7 days. The results revealed that AMNL consistently achieved greater reductions in bacterial and fungal populations than the competitor (a commercial feed preservative based primarily on organic acids), despite being applied at one-quarter of the inclusion rate. Antimicrobial performance improved over storage duration, particularly against bacterial taxa, while maintaining consistent efficacy across diverse microbial
summary: Microbial contamination in animal feed—particularly from bacteria such as *Escherichia coli*, *Salmonella Typhimurium*, and *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*, and fungi including *Aspergillus niger*, *Fusarium oxysporum*, and *Rhizopus nigricans*—compromises livestock safety and feed storage. This paper tested Alquermold Natural Plus L, a formulation combining citric acid and bioactive monoterpenes, in compound feed and ground corn matrices against a commercial organic acid-based preservative. Applied at 0.5 liters per ton, Alquermold Natural Plus L achieved greater reductions in both bacterial and fungal populations than the commercial benchmark at 2 liters per ton when growth media were contaminated at 106 colony-forming units per gram and measured at 24 hours and 7 days. Antimicrobial performance strengthened over the storage period, particularly against bacteria.  The formulation's efficacy at one-quarter the competitor's dosage reveals a material entanglement between chemical potency and economic viability in feed preservation. Yet this efficiency also indexes a deeper pattern: the selective pressure toward concentrated antimicrobial compounds in animal feed creates conditions favoring rapid microbial adaptation and resistance emergence, while simultaneously embedding chemical dependencies into livestock production systems that remain vulnerable to supply disruptions or regulatory shifts affecting natural preservative availability.

title: Secreted proteins from Bacillus subtilis K1 induce apple resistance against gray mold
source: Frontiers in Plant Science | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: The antagonistic Bacillus subtilis K1, previously isolated in our laboratory, exhibited significant inhibitory activity against Botrytis cinerea. To investigate the underlying molecular mechanisms, genomic bioinformatics analysis predicted 93 potential secreted proteins. Preliminary screening via gene cloning and an Agrobacterium-mediated transient expression system in tobacco identified a gene, GM001344, which elicited a strong hypersensitive response on tobacco. The GM001344-His recombinant protein was successfully purified using a prokaryotic expression system. Apple fruit inoculation assays showed that pretreatment with GM001344 protein 24 h prior to B. cinerea challenge markedly reduced both disease incidence (63.5% vs. 100% in control) and disease severity index (38.43 vs. 76.75 in control), demonstrating effective disease control. GM001344 enhanced apple defenses through boosted activities of key defense-related enzymes and reduced malondialdehyde (MDA) content. Furthermore, GM001344 treatment improved postharvest fruit quality. After 6 d of treatment, treated fruit maintained significantly higher firmness, total soluble solid and ascorbic acid compared to the control. In co
summary: This paper describes the isolation and characterization of a secreted protein from the bacterium Bacillus subtilis K1 that protects apple fruit against gray mold caused by Botrytis cinerea. Genomic analysis predicted 93 potential secreted proteins from this strain; screening via gene cloning and transient expression in tobacco leaves identified a gene designated GM001344 that triggered a strong localized cell death response in tobacco tissue. The purified GM001344 protein, produced in a bacterial expression system, was tested on apple fruit: pretreatment 24 hours before exposure to Botrytis cinerea reduced disease incidence from 100% in untreated controls to 63.5% and lowered disease severity from 76.75 to 38.43. The protein strengthened apple defenses by increasing the activity of defense-related enzymes and reducing malondialdehyde, a marker of cellular damage. Treated fruit also retained better postharvest quality after 6 days, with higher firmness, soluble sugars, and vitamin C compared to untreated controls.  Within the context of turbulent agricultural systems, this paper documents how a single microbial protein functions simultaneously as a plant defense activator and a quality-preservation agent. The same secreted molecule that triggers cell death in tobacco tissue—a sign of immune activation—becomes a tool for extending postharvest shelf life and maintaining fruit marketability, revealing how microbial metabolites operate across multiple registers of value: pathogen suppression, plant physiology, and commodity preservation.

title: Immune interaction between Aspergillus fumigatus and non-tuberculous mycobacteria
source: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: IntroductionThe global prevalence of non-tuberculous mycobacterial pulmonary disease (NTM-PD) is increasing. Individuals with NTM-PD frequently develop chronic pulmonary aspergillosis, which is associated with poor clinical outcomes. However, the biological mechanisms underlying the interaction between Aspergillus species and non-tuberculous mycobacteria (NTM) remain poorly understood. This study aimed to investigate the interaction between Aspergillus fumigatus and NTM and to identify mechanisms that may inform novel therapeutic strategies.MethodsA. fumigatus was incubated with NTM culture supernatants, and biofilm formation was quantified using the crystal violet assay. Phagocytic activity against A. fumigatus conidia were assessed in THP-1-derived macrophages infected with Mycobacterium avium. Conversely, phagocytosis of M. avium was evaluated in macrophages exposed to A. fumigatus culture supernatants. Finally, fungal clearance in vivo was assessed in mice pre-infected with M. avium.ResultsNTM supernatants significantly enhanced A. fumigatus growth. M. avium infection decreased the macrophages-phagocytosis rate of A. fumigatus by approximately 40% compared to uninfected control
summary: Aspergillus fumigatus growth was substantially increased when exposed to culture supernatants from non-tuberculous mycobacteria, according to this study's surface-colonising microbial communities assays using crystal violet staining. When macrophages derived from the THP-1 cell line were infected with Mycobacterium avium, their capacity to engulf A. fumigatus spores decreased by approximately 40 percent relative to uninfected control macrophages. Conversely, macrophages exposed to A. fumigatus culture supernatants showed altered capacity to engulf M. avium cells. This paper also assessed fungal clearance in living mice that had been pre-infected with M. avium, though the results section provided in this abstract does not detail those findings.  The documented suppression of immune cell activity against A. fumigatus in the presence of mycobacterial infection reveals a material entanglement in the lungs where two distinct pathogens create conditions favourable to one another—not through direct microbial cooperation, but through their capacity to manipulate the immune defences that would normally contain them. The clinical consequence, as noted in the introduction, is the frequent emergence of chronic aspergillosis in patients already managing mycobacterial disease, a pattern that reflects not merely coincidental co-infection but a structured vulnerability produced by the presence of one organism reshaping immune tolerance to the other.

title: trans‐2‐Hexenal exposure enhances disease resistance against Penicillium digitatum by stimulating phenylpropanoid biosynthesis and antioxidant capacity in postharvest citrus fruit
source: Wiley: Journal of the Science of Food and Agriculture: Table of Contents
excerpt: BACKGROUND: trans-2-Hexenal (TH) is a promising alternative to chemical fungicide in controlling postharvest disease in fruits. This research sought to disclose how TH controls citrus green mold and induces fruit resistance through RNA-Seq, physiochemical and natural decay. RESULTS: . Correspondingly, the protective effects of TH on citrus fruits decreased its susceptibility to Penicillium digitatum. CONCLUSION: induces disease resistance against P. digitatum by stimulating the biosynthesis of lignin and flavonoid, along with the AsA-GSH cycle in the peel of citrus fruit, thus improving antioxidant capacity and maintaining redox balance in the citrus fruit. These findings suggest that TH has potential for use as a protective measure against P. digitatum in citrus fruit. © 2026 Society of Chemical Industry.
summary: This paper reports that trans-2-hexenal, a volatile organic compound, reduces susceptibility of citrus fruit to Penicillium digitatum infection by activating two biochemical pathways in the fruit peel. Gene sequencing analysis revealed that trans-2-hexenal stimulates the production of lignin and flavonoid compounds—structural and protective molecules that strengthen cell walls and provide chemical defense. The treatment also activated the ascorbic acid-glutathione cycle, a cellular system that neutralizes reactive molecules produced during stress, thereby maintaining the fruit's internal chemical balance and antioxidant capacity. Measurements of natural fruit decay confirmed that trans-2-hexenal exposure reduced disease development in stored citrus.  Within postharvest storage systems, this research documents how a volatile chemical triggers dormant defense mechanisms in the fruit itself rather than directly killing the fungus. The same compound that signals resistance in the fruit peel—activating lignin and flavonoid biosynthesis—simultaneously reveals a dependency: citrus fruit requires external chemical stimulation to mount defenses against a pathogen that thrives precisely in the conditions of industrial storage. What emerges is not a solution to postharvest disease but rather a managed intervention that exposes how fruit bodies in global supply chains have become metabolically dependent on external treatments to survive the very conditions of their preservation.

title: Linking microbiome structure to functional analysis identifies resilient Pseudarthrobacter, Pseudomonas, and Streptomyces antagonists of Phytophthora infestans in tomato
source: Frontiers in Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: IntroductionLate blight, caused by Phytophthora infestans, remains one of the most destructive tomato diseases, driving the need for sustainable measures to reduce intensive fungicide use. Plant-associated microbial communities offer a promising but still underexplored source of biological control agents. Moreover, links between community-level microbiome patterns and functionally effective antagonists remain poorly resolved.MethodsIn this study, we combine culture-dependent isolation, functional profiling of lytic enzymes and siderophores, with 16S rRNA Illumina-based microbiome analysis to identify ecologically relevant bacterial antagonists of P. infestans in tomato. Healthy and P. infestans-challenged tomato plants cultivated in soils from two organic tomato farms in the Rhine-Main region of Germany were analyzed.ResultsOf the 594 bacterial isolates from tomato rhizosphere and phyllosphere, 84 inhibited P. infestans, and 63 of these suppressed Alternaria solani in vitro. Functional screening identified 28 isolates with broad-spectrum antagonistic potential, predominantly affiliated with the genera Pseudomonas, Bacillus, Streptomyces, Paenibacillus, and Pseudarthrobacter, charac
summary: This paper combines culture-based isolation, functional enzyme screening, and genetic sequencing to identify bacterial antagonists of *Phytophthora infestans* in tomato plants. Researchers collected 594 bacterial isolates from the root zone and leaf surfaces of healthy and *Phytophthora infestans*-infected tomato plants grown in soils from two organic farms in the Rhine-Main region of Germany. Of these isolates, 84 inhibited *Phytophthora infestans* growth in laboratory conditions, and 63 of those also suppressed *Alternaria solani*. Functional screening for lytic enzymes and iron-scavenging compounds identified 28 isolates with broad-spectrum counteracting potential, predominantly belonging to *Pseudomonas*, *Bacillus*, *Streptomyces*, *Paenibacillus*, and *Pseudarthrobacter* genera.  The isolates recovered from these organic farms represent a material archive of microbial capacity already present in soil where late blight pressure is managed without intensive fungicide application. Yet the paper reveals an underlying tension: the same bacterial taxa identified as disease suppressors in laboratory assays may or may not function as antagonists within the living plant-soil system, and their ecological persistence depends on conditions—soil type, plant health status, competitive microbial assemblages—that remain partially opaque. The shift from functional screening to field deployment thus exposes a gap between what these bacteria *can* do biochemically and what they *will* do under the variable, resource-limited conditions of actual cultivation.

title: Enhancing thermotolerance of Aspergillus niger for sodium gluconate production by combining metabolic engineering and fermentation process optimization
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Bioresource Technology
excerpt: Aspergillus niger (A. niger) is a leading industrial platform for sodium gluconate (SG) production. However, heat stress, arising from rapid catalytic reactions and neutralization, can impair the production rate and even compromise cell viability. To develop a thermotolerant cell factory, a pyrG* auxotrophic chassis using DNA-free CRISPR-Cas9 editing, thereby avoiding the 0.04 mol mol<sup>-1</sup> yield-loss from glucose to SG associated with gene editing at 37°C. Subsequently, yeast-derived thermotolerance-related genes, specifically sterol C-5 desaturase (erg3) and AMP deaminase (amd), were site-specifically overexpressed at the pyrG locus and amd-overexpressing strain (COE-AMD) exhibited superior thermotolerance compared to the erg3-overexpressing strain. Notably, COE-AMD achieved a 1.6-fold higher SG production rate than the wild type (WT), with an SG yield of 0.76 ± 0.03 mol mol<sup>-1</sup> at 45°C. To elucidate the underlying mechanism of thermotolerance in A. niger, integrated multi-omics analyses revealed that amd overexpression induced systematic metabolic reprogramming. In particular, enhanced intracellular NH<sub>4</sub><sup>+</sup> assimilation during the late logarithmic phase contributed to the accelerated growth of COE-AMD at elevated temperature. Guided by these omics insights, supplementing the initial medium with 0.4 g L<sup>-1</sup> ammonium increased biomass, enzyme activity, and product formation rate by 3.0-, 4.0-, and 1.6-fold, respectively. In summary, the thermotolerant strain COE-AMD produced 311.5 ± 2.0 g L<sup>-1</sup> SG within 66 h at 45°C, achieving a yield of 0.78 ± 0.03 mol mol<sup>-1</sup>. Overall, this work establishes a complete pipeline for SG process intensification, and the omics-guided bioprocess optimization provides fundamental insights into the global metabolic basis of A. niger thermotolerance.
summary: Aspergillus niger is widely used in industry to produce sodium gluconate, a chemical compound derived from glucose. This paper reports that heat stress—generated during the rapid chemical reactions and neutralization steps of production—reduces output and can kill the cells. The researchers created a heat-tolerant strain using DNA-free CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing to avoid a 0.04 mol mol⁻¹ loss in glucose-to-sodium gluconate conversion that occurs at 37°C. They inserted two yeast-derived genes—one encoding sterol C-5 desaturase and another encoding AMP deaminase—into the fungal genome. The strain overexpressing AMP deaminase (called COE-AMD) showed superior heat tolerance, producing sodium gluconate 1.6 times faster than the unmodified strain and achieving a yield of 0.76 ± 0.03 mol mol⁻¹ at 45°C. Multi-omics analysis—examining the organism's complete set of proteins, metabolites, and gene activity—revealed that AMP deaminase overexpression triggered widespread metabolic reorganization, particularly enhancing the cell's ability to take up and use ammonia during late growth phases at elevated temperature. When the researchers added 0.4 g L⁻¹ ammonium to the growth medium based on these findings, biomass increased threefold, enzyme activity quadrupled, and production rate increased 1.6-fold. The optimized COE-AMD strain produced 311.5 ± 2.0 g L⁻¹ sodium gluconate in 66 hours at 45°C with a yield of 0.78 ± 0.03 mol mol⁻¹.  Within industrial fermentation, heat emerges as both constraint and opportunity. The same thermal stress that threatens cell survival becomes, through genetic modification and nutrient adjustment, a condition under which the organism reorganizes its metabolism to accelerate production. This paper documents how metabolic engineering and process intensification are entangled: the fungal cell's capacity to metabolize ammonia more efficiently at high temperature is not simply a trait to be exploited, but a material reconfiguration that reveals deeper dependencies between organism, nutrient availability, and industrial output. The omics-guided optimization reveals that thermotolerance in this system is not a fixed property but an emergent outcome of specific material interventions—gene insertion, temperature elevation, ammonia supplementation—each resh

title: Functional role of calcineurin regulatory subunit B (CAR1) in lipid accumulation in oleaginous fungus Mucor circinelloides
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Bioresource Technology
excerpt: Calcium signalling plays a central role in cellular metabolism and is mediated by calcineurin, a calcium/calmodulin-dependent phosphatase composed of a catalytic subunit A (Cna) and a regulatory subunit B (CAR1). Nevertheless, its role in lipid biosynthesis in oleaginous fungi remains unclear. Elevated intracellular calcium activates the calmodulin-CAR1 complex, enabling calcineurin-dependent dephosphorylation of CRZ1, which may indirectly influence lipid metabolism. However, the regulatory role of the CAR1 in lipid accumulation in oleaginous fungi has not yet been reported. Therefore, we employed genetic manipulation combined with computationalanalyses toelucidate the potential regulatory role of CAR1 in growth and lipid accumulation in Mucor circinelloides WJ11, using overexpression and knockout strategies. The results showed that CAR1 overexpression increased biomass by 31% and lipid accumulation by 22%, whereas CAR1 knockout reduced both parameters compared with the control strain. Furthermore, CAR1 expression altered the fatty acid profile, revealing a strong inverse correlation between palmitic acid and γ-linolenic acid. Interestingly, CAR1 overexpression was associated with enhancing extracellular calcium uptake, which correlated with higher CRZ1 transcription and upregulation of key lipogenic genes. Our results demonstrated that CAR1 may play an important regulatory role in linking calcium-related signalling to lipid metabolism, highlighting CAR1 as a novel target for metabolic engineering in oleaginous microorganisms. To our knowledge, this is the first study to establish a functional link between CAR1 expression and lipid accumulation. These findings also uncover novel mechanisms by which calcium signalling governs lipid biosynthesis. Demonstrating that genetic manipulation of CAR1 significantly enhanced lipid accumulation and FA biosynthesis.
summary: Calcium signalling regulates lipid accumulation in the oleaginous fungus Mucor circinelloides through the regulatory subunit CAR1, which forms part of the calcineurin phosphatase complex. This paper employed genetic manipulation—both overexpression and knockout strategies—to test CAR1's role in the fungus Mucor circinelloides WJ11. Overexpression of CAR1 increased biomass by 31 percent and lipid accumulation by 22 percent relative to control strains, while knockout reduced both measures. The manipulation of CAR1 altered the fatty acid profile, with an inverse relationship between palmitic acid and γ-linolenic acid levels. CAR1 overexpression enhanced uptake of calcium from outside the cell, which correlated with increased transcription of CRZ1 and increased activity of genes involved in lipid synthesis. This paper presents the first documented functional link between CAR1 expression and lipid accumulation in oleaginous fungi.  Within the material assemblages of industrial fermentation, this fungus emerges as both a lipid producer and a site where calcium-dependent signalling becomes a lever for metabolic control. The capacity to engineer CAR1 expression reveals how microbial lipid synthesis can be redirected through manipulation of intracellular signalling pathways—a finding that positions oleaginous fungi as platforms for metabolic engineering aimed at producing oils and fats at scale. Yet this same capacity for genetic modification and enhanced lipid yield also embeds the organism more deeply within industrial dependencies, where the fungus becomes a controlled metabolic system optimized for resource extraction rather than autonomous growth.

title: Soil type, texture and minimum temperature are key factors shaping the interaction of Fusarium species and soil saprophages in agroecosystems
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Science of The Total Environment
excerpt: In recent years, phytopathogenic soil fungi such as Fusarium have become increasingly resistant to the fungicides available and have overcome resistances in crop varieties. Against this background, bioregulation by soil animals has gained importance. However, there is a lack of knowledge about which key environmental factors influence interactions of Fusarium species and antagonistic soil invertebrates in agroecosystems to what extent. To fill this gap, a meta-study was performed based on an extensive dataset, which combined the results of 14 experiments assessing the effects of various environmental factors on Fusarium and antagonistic soil saprophages under different soil and climatic conditions. Quantitative relationships between key environmental factors and the abundance of soil saprophages (here: Collembola and Lumbricidae) and Fusarium species were identified. Based on simulations using a set of optimal models, the predicted potential of soil saprophages to reduce Fusarium species (expressed as the ratio of their abundances) increased to different extents depending on soil type when shifting from conventional to conservation tillage: by 112% in Luvisol and by 427% in Phaeozem. Additionally, the ratio of Fusarium species to soil saprophages was negatively associated with minimum air temperature and positively associated with soil clay content. A deeper understanding of the soil factors which determine interactions between beneficial and harmful soil organisms opens up new possibilities for a sustainable control of pathogens. The findings gained can be applied in the development of new complex Fusarium control strategies in which biological soil services play an essential role.
summary: Soil type and minimum temperature emerge as primary determinants of how soil-dwelling predatory invertebrates—specifically springtails (Collembola) and earthworms (Lumbricidae)—interact with disease-causing Fusarium fungi in cultivated fields. This paper synthesized data from 14 separate experiments to quantify these relationships across different soil and climate conditions. When farming practices shifted from conventional to conservation tillage, the predicted capacity of soil saprophages to suppress Fusarium populations increased substantially, though the magnitude varied by soil type: a 112 percent increase in Luvisol soils and a 427 percent increase in Phaeozem soils. The ratio of Fusarium to soil saprophages declined as minimum air temperature dropped and increased with higher clay content in the soil. These findings indicate that soil organisms can serve as biological regulators of fungal pathogens, particularly as chemical fungicides lose effectiveness against resistant Fusarium strains.  Within agroecosystems shaped by tillage practices and soil composition, Fusarium and its soil predators exist in a relationship mediated by climatic and edaphic conditions. The paper reveals how conservation tillage—a practice often adopted for soil conservation—simultaneously creates conditions favoring biological control, yet this benefit is unevenly distributed across soil types, suggesting that pathogen suppression through biological means cannot be decoupled from the material properties of the soil itself. Minimum temperature functions here not merely as an environmental backdrop but as a material constraint that restructures the balance between pathogen and antagonist, implying that climate variability will reshape the viability of biological control strategies across different agricultural regions.

title: Toward sustainable food safety: Natural ligand–gold nanoparticle plasmonic sensors for on-site mycotoxin detection
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Chemistry
excerpt: Mycotoxins remain a critical challenge to food safety, with limitations in conventional methods driving demand for rapid, portable alternatives. Gold nanoparticle (AuNP)-based sensors, exploiting localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), offer rapid and highly sensitive detection, typically achieving detection limits in the low ppb (μg/kg) to ng/mL range, with performance in food matrices further improved through suitable ligand functionalization and optimized assay design. This review highlights recent advances in the use of natural ligands to design sustainable plasmonic nanosensors for on-site mycotoxin detection. Moreover, natural ligands, such as polyphenols, flavonoids, and organic acids, enable green synthesis while enhancing selectivity and biocompatibility. We discuss structure-function relationships at the ligand-AuNP interface, linking functional groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl) to stability and specificity. Case studies illustrate improvements in detection limits and applicability in food matrices. Recent trends point toward dual-ligand systems, portable paper-based devices, smartphone integration, and IoT/blockchain-enabled monitoring. Challenges such as reproducibility, standardization, and regulatory approval are considered, alongside opportunities for digital integration and global-scale food safety networks. This review positions metabolite-functionalized plasmonic sensors as a sustainable, next-generation trend in food safety surveillance.
summary: Mycotoxins—toxic compounds produced by fungi—remain a persistent food safety problem, with conventional detection methods limited by complexity, cost, and time requirements. This paper reviews gold nanoparticle-based sensors that exploit localized surface plasmon resonance and surface-enhanced Raman scattering to detect mycotoxins rapidly and with high sensitivity, typically achieving detection limits between low parts per billion and nanograms per milliliter ranges. The review emphasizes the use of natural ligands—including polyphenols, flavonoids, and organic acids—to synthesize these sensors through green chemistry methods while improving selectivity and biocompatibility. The paper examines how specific functional groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl) on ligand molecules determine sensor stability and specificity at the ligand-nanoparticle interface, and presents case studies demonstrating improved detection performance in actual food matrices. Emerging applications include dual-ligand systems, portable paper-based devices, smartphone integration, and internet-connected monitoring with blockchain verification.  Mycotoxin detection technology reveals a material entanglement between food safety surveillance and the chemical properties of fungal metabolites themselves. The same fungi that contaminate crops—and whose toxic products demand rapid detection—have their molecular signatures read through nanoparticles functionalized with plant-derived compounds, creating a detection apparatus that depends on metabolic cross-kingdom relationships. Yet this technological solution, framed as sustainable and portable, also embeds new dependencies: the scaling of gold nanoparticle production, the standardization required for regulatory approval, and the digital infrastructure needed for global monitoring networks all represent forms of capital accumulation and governance that extend far beyond the laboratory detection of a single contaminant.

title: Mining of FDRs-carrying microbes involved in aflatoxin B1 degradation
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Chemistry
excerpt: Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), a potent hepatocarcinogenic mycotoxin commonly found in food and feed, poses significant threats to food safety and public health. Microbes reduce AFB1 via biotransformation, so mining degrading strains is key. In this study, a novel AFB1 degrader, Mycobacterium sp. strain HM-7, was isolated from an AFB1-degrading bacterial consortium (designated A-2). Genomic analysis of the reconstructed metagenome-assembled genome (MAG) 12 and strain HM-7 revealed six putative F<sub>420</sub>H<sub>2</sub>-dependent reductases (FDRs), which are essential for the biotransformation of AFB1. When strain HM-7 was applied to animal feed, it achieved a significant reduction in AFB1 levels. Furthermore, bioinformatics mining based on the Genome Taxonomy Database (GTDB) identified a wide diversity of FDRs-carrying microbes involved in AFB1 degradation, mainly those belonging to the phylum Actinomycetota, highlighting their potential for bioremediation applications. This study provides valuable insights into the diversity of FDRs-carrying microbes involved in AFB1 degradation.
summary: Mycobacterium sp. strain HM-7, isolated from an aflatoxin B1-degrading bacterial consortium, carries six putative enzymes called F₄₂₀H₂-dependent reductases that enable the breakdown of aflatoxin B1, a carcinogenic fungal toxin common in food and animal feed. Genomic analysis of both the strain and a reconstructed metagenome-assembled genome identified these reductases as essential for biotransformation of the toxin. When applied to animal feed, strain HM-7 achieved measurable reduction in aflatoxin B1 levels. Bioinformatic screening of the Genome Taxonomy Database revealed a diverse array of microbes carrying these reductase enzymes across the bacterial phylum Actinomycetota, suggesting broad potential for using such organisms in contamination remediation.  Within the turbulent material landscape of food production, this paper documents a dual character in microbial action: the same bacterial strains and enzymatic pathways that pose risks through mycotoxin production or persistence can be mobilized as remediation tools when the right strain is isolated and deployed. The capacity of Actinomycetota to degrade aflatoxin B1 in feed matrices reveals how microbial metabolism becomes a site where contamination control and industrial application converge—a resource emerging from the same ecological assemblages that generate the contamination problem itself. Yet the effectiveness of such biological cleanup depends entirely on strain selection and process conditions, meaning that the solution remains bound to the same contingencies of control and specificity that characterize the problem.

title: Environmental Circulation of Aspergillus fumigatus With Reduced Susceptibility to Agricultural Triazole in Brazil: Clonal Dissemination of Potentially Resistant Genotypes
source: Wiley: Mycoses: Table of Contents
excerpt: BACKGROUND: Aspergillus fumigatus resistance to triazole antifungals poses an increasing global health concern. Moreover, the cross-resistance between azole antifungal agents used in clinical settings and those applied in agriculture has become an important emerging issue. OBJECTIVES: In this study, we investigated the five environmental A. fumigatus strains showing reduced susceptibility to tebuconazole. METHODS: Fungal strains were recovered from air samples collected around the homes of two patients with suspected aspergillosis caused by resistant isolates. Species identification was performed by sequencing the β-tubulin gene, and minimum inhibitory concentrations were determined by broth microdilution. The cyp51A gene was sequenced to detect mutations, and CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing was employed to investigate their influence on susceptibility patterns. Microsatellite genotyping was performed to assess genetic variability, followed by whole genome sequencing and single nucleotide polymorphism analysis. RESULTS: The environmental strains presented the same cyp51A genotype characterised by the M172V substitution and silent mutations. Microsatellite genotyping and whole genome sequencing confirmed that the strains were clonal. Functional validation demonstrated that the M172V and silent mutations partially contribute to reduced susceptibility to tebuconazole but are not the main mechanism of resistance involved. Analysis of polymorphisms in genes other than cyp51A revealed no resistance-conferring mutations. CONCLUSIONS: The findings described herein suggest the possibility of local clonal dissemination of environmental strains under selective pressure from agricultural azoles in a major agribusiness region of the Midwest of Brazil. This study highlights the silent spread of potentially resistant genotypes in urban areas and reinforces the need for environmental surveillance and expanded genomic monitoring in South America.
summary: This study recovered five environmental strains of Aspergillus fumigatus from air samples collected around homes in Brazil's Midwest agribusiness region, all showing reduced susceptibility to tebuconazole, an agricultural triazole antifungal. Genetic sequencing of the cyp51A gene revealed that all strains carried an identical mutation (M172V substitution) alongside silent mutations with no known function. Microsatellite genetic profiling and whole genome sequencing confirmed the strains were clonal—genetically identical copies derived from a single source. Functional testing using genetic editing demonstrated that the M172V and silent mutations only partially explained the reduced susceptibility; the paper identifies no other resistance-conferring mutations in genes outside cyp51A, suggesting an unknown mechanism of resistance remains uncharacterized. The authors conclude that these identical environmental strains indicate local clonal spread under selective pressure from agricultural azole use.  The circulation of genetically uniform, triazole-reduced-susceptibility strains in urban air around residential homes documents a material entanglement between agricultural chemical use and human exposure pathways. These strains represent neither simple clinical pathogens nor simple agricultural byproducts, but rather organisms shaped by—and now circulating through—the selective pressures of agribusiness, their genetic uniformity marking them as products of industrial fungicide application rather than random environmental variation. The incomplete understanding of their resistance mechanism reveals a deeper dependency: clinical antifungal efficacy now depends on monitoring agricultural chemical use in regions thousands of kilometres away, yet the surveillance systems to track such connections remain fragmented and geographically limited.

title: Prevalence of fungal infections in a patient cohort in The Gambia: Identification and characterization of three priority fungal species in patients with symptoms suggestive of TB
source: PLOS One
excerpt:
by Zackary Salem-Bango, Mary Basiru Njai, Zainab Tambajang, Tessa Rose Cornell, Jayne Sutherland, Karen Forrest, Omai Garner, Behzad Nadjm, Saffiatou Darboe, Basil Sambou, Sheikh Jarju

Invasive fungal infections are an increasing global health concern, particularly in low-resource settings where diagnostic capacity is limited. In The Gambia, where tuberculosis is highly prevalent, fungal infections may be misdiagnosed as mycobacterial disease due to overlapping clinical symptoms and limited access to fungal testing. This study aimed to determine the presence of three priority fungal pathogens by the World Health Organization (WHO) classification system—Aspergillus species (spp.), Histoplasma spp., and Pneumocystis jirovecii—in patients with symptoms suggestive of tuberculosis, and to develop a molecular tool to support future surveillance. A multiplex quantitative polymerase chain reaction assay was developed and validated for simultaneous detection of Aspergillus spp., Histoplasma spp., and Pneumocystis jirovecii DNA in human sputum. The assay was applied to 273 stored sputum samples collected from adult patients presenting with respiratory symptoms concerning for tuberculosis in
summary: Invasive fungal infections represent an increasing health concern in low-resource settings where diagnostic capacity is constrained, particularly in regions where tuberculosis is endemic. This study from The Gambia addresses the diagnostic challenge that fungal infections present when they produce respiratory symptoms overlapping with tuberculosis, making clinical differentiation difficult without access to fungal testing. The researchers developed a molecular detection tool—a multiplex quantitative polymerase chain reaction assay—capable of identifying three World Health Organization-priority fungal pathogens simultaneously: Aspergillus species, Histoplasma species, and Pneumocystis jirovecii. The assay was designed to detect fungal DNA in sputum samples and was applied to 273 stored samples collected from adult patients presenting with respiratory symptoms suggestive of tuberculosis.  The diagnostic infrastructure gap documented in this paper reveals how the same clinical presentation—respiratory illness in a tuberculosis-endemic setting—can mask multiple pathogenic agents competing for recognition within constrained laboratory systems. The development of a multiplex molecular assay represents both a technical response to diagnostic scarcity and an implicit acknowledgment that fungal pathogens circulate undetected within populations where tuberculosis surveillance dominates; the tool itself becomes a mechanism through which previously invisible fungal disease burdens become legible to public health systems, potentially reshaping how respiratory illness is classified and treated in settings where tuberculosis has historically monopolized diagnostic attention and clinical resources.

title: Diagnostic performance of serum galactomannan for detecting breakthrough invasive fungal infections in pediatric hematologic malignancies receiving mold-active prophylaxis
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Diagnostic Microbiology and Infectious Disease
excerpt:
Background

This study aimed to evaluate the diagnostic performance of serum galactomannan for invasive fungal infections in children with hematologic malignancies and to assess the impact of mold-active antifungal prophylaxis on test sensitivity and specificity.

Methods

We retrospectively evaluated 280 pediatric patients with acute leukemia or mixed-phenotypic leukemia treated between February 2019 and December 2023 at a tertiary referral center. Demographic characteristics, antifungal prophylaxis, serum and BAL GM levels, clinical and radiologic findings, and outcomes were recorded. IFIs were classified according to the 2020 EORTC/MSGERC criteria. Diagnostic performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, LR⁺, LR⁻) were calculated, and multivariate logistic regression was used to identify independent predictors of IFI.

Results

Among 280 pediatric patients with hematologic malignancies, 89 (31.8%) met IFI criteria, including 73 possible, 10 probable, and 6 proven cases. In the primary analysis restricted to proven/probable IFI, 16 patients (5.7%) were classified as having IFI and 264 (94.3%) as not having IFI. Baseline demographic and clinical characteristics were comparable between groups. Serum GM positivity was significantly associated with proven/probable IFI (62.5% vs. 2.3%, p < 0.001) and remained the only independent predictor in multivariable analysis (OR 73.040, 95% CI 11.979-445.344; p < 0.001). Serum GM showed moderate sensitivity (66.7%) and high specificity (97.3%), with PPV and NPV of 62.5% and 97.7%, respectively. GM positivity remained significant irrespective of antifungal prophylaxis, while mold-active prophylaxis did not significantly affect GM positivity or IFI rates.

Conclusions

Serum galactomannan was a highly specific but moderately sensitive marker for proven/probable IFI in pediatric patients with hematologic malignancies. While a positive result supported the diagnosis, a negative result was insufficien
summary: Serum galactomannan testing detected proven or probable invasive fungal infections in 62.5% of pediatric patients with acute leukemia or mixed-observable-trait leukemia, compared to 2.3% of uninfected patients, with high specificity of 97.3%. This paper evaluated 280 children treated between February 2019 and December 2023, classifying 16 patients as having proven or probable invasive fungal infection and 264 as uninfected; serum galactomannan remained the only independent predictor of infection in multivariate analysis, with an odds ratio of 73.040. The test showed moderate sensitivity (66.7%) but high specificity (97.3%), meaning a positive result strongly indicated infection while a negative result could not reliably exclude it. Mold-active antifungal preventive treatment did not significantly affect either the rate of galactomannan positivity or the overall rate of invasive fungal infections in this cohort.  Within the clinical assemblage of pediatric leukemia treatment, serum galactomannan emerges as a diagnostic tool that performs differently depending on infection status—highly reliable for confirming infection but unreliable for ruling it out. The failure of mold-active preventive treatment to suppress galactomannan detection or infection rates suggests that chemical intervention does not eliminate the material conditions enabling fungal breakthrough; rather, the fungus persists as a clinical presence despite pharmaceutical intervention, revealing the limits of preventive control in immune-weakened bodies and the ongoing entanglement between treatment protocols and the organisms they attempt to contain.

title: Engineering pH and thermostability of β-mannanase from Aspergillus niger for simplified and direct production of manno-oligosaccharides from guar split
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Bioresource Technology
excerpt: Guar split represents a promising renewable resource for manno-oligosaccharide production. To enable the direct and efficient hydrolysis of this substrate, a β-mannanase from Aspergillus niger was engineered through directed evolution to improve its performance under near-neutral conditions. A mutant, designated mAnMan26, was obtained after two rounds of screening. It exhibited optimal activity at pH 6.5 and 45 °C, with significantly enhanced catalytic efficiency and stability compared to the wild-type enzyme. Three amino acid substitutions (Gln38Lys, Asp224Ala, and Ala272Thr) were identified and contributed collectively to these improvements. The mutant was further expressed in Komagataella phaffii GS115, reaching an expression level of 26,000 U mL<sup>-1</sup>. Using this biocatalyst, direct hydrolysis of 10% (w/v) guar split yielded 73 g manno-oligosaccharide per 100 g substrate after 8 h, corresponding to a 92.2% hydrolysis rate of galactomannan. The resulting manno-oligosaccharide had a weight-average molecular weight of 1475 Da, with components of DP 2-10 accounting for 87%. In vitro fermentation assays demonstrated its superior prebiotic activity, stimulating the growth of various Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium strains more effectively than partially hydrolyzed guar gum or fructo-oligosaccharide. This study provides a novel and efficient strategy for the direct hydrolysis of guar split into high-value manno-oligosaccharides, offering a cost-effective alternative to conventional processes.
summary: Guar split, a byproduct of guar bean processing, was processed using an engineered fungal enzyme to produce manno-oligosaccharides—short chains of mannose sugars with potential health benefits. This paper describes the development of mAnMan26, a mutant β-mannanase enzyme derived from Aspergillus niger, which was optimized through directed evolution to function at near-neutral pH (6.5) and moderate temperature (45 °C). Three specific amino acid changes in the enzyme's structure (Gln38Lys, Asp224Ala, and Ala272Thr) collectively enhanced its catalytic efficiency and thermal stability relative to the wild-type version. When produced at industrial scale in the yeast Komagataella phaffii GS115 at 26,000 units per millilitre, this enzyme converted 10% guar split suspension into manno-oligosaccharides with 92.2% efficiency in 8 hours, yielding 73 grams of product per 100 grams of raw material. The resulting oligosaccharides had an average molecular weight of 1475 Daltons, with 87% consisting of chains containing 2 to 10 sugar units. Laboratory fermentation tests showed these oligosaccharides stimulated growth of beneficial bacteria (Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium species) more effectively than partially hydrolyzed guar gum or fructo-oligosaccharide controls.  This study documents a material transformation that renders an agricultural residue into a biochemical commodity through fungal labour. The engineered enzyme operates at conditions that reduce energy inputs and chemical processing steps—a simplification that simultaneously creates economic value from waste material and establishes dependency on a living biocatalyst whose performance is contingent on continued cultivation and expression. The shift from conventional hydrolysis processes to direct enzymatic conversion reveals how microbial metabolism can be instrumentalized to bypass industrial infrastructure, yet this efficiency gain also concentrates production around the specific genetic and physiological characteristics of a single engineered strain.

title: Mycotoxins in Plant-Based fermented products: challenges and perspectives
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Current Opinion in Food Science
excerpt: The adoption of plant-based diet has expanded worldwide, with more people using plant-based products for their perceived sustainability benefits and health-promoting objectives. Within this trend, fermentation of plant-based products is gaining attention for a variety of reasons as its role in extending shelf life, improving nutritional quality, digestibility, sensory attributes, and functional properties is very crucial. However, given the susceptibility of plant-based raw materials to mycotoxin contamination, alongside these benefits, plant-based fermented foods also face this emerging safety challenge. Mycotoxin contamination is originated from raw plant substrates such as cereals, fruits, legumes, nuts, and seeds, from unsuitable environmental conditions during storage, or from fermentation-associated fungi themselves. The current work summarizes the occurrence of regulated and emerging mycotoxins in plant-based matrices and discuss fermentation as a double-edged strategy for mycotoxin risk, with outcomes strongly dependent on the strain–matrix combination and process control. • Regulatory frameworks for mycotoxins is lacking for the fermented plant-based products • Use of well-characterized fermentative strains, selected for their inability to produce mycotoxins should be considered • Microbial detoxification of mycotoxins can be achieved during fermentation
summary: Plant-based fermented foods present a paradox of food safety: fermentation extends shelf life and enhances nutritional quality, yet the same fermentation process can either reduce or amplify mycotoxin contamination depending on which microorganism dominates the fermentation and how the process is controlled. This paper documents mycotoxin occurrence across plant-based matrices including cereals, fruits, legumes, nuts, and seeds, identifying contamination sources as raw plant growth media, unsuitable storage conditions, and fermentation-associated fungi themselves. The study frames fermentation as a double-edged strategy, where outcomes depend critically on the specific strain-matrix combination and process parameters. Current regulatory frameworks lack specific guidance for mycotoxins in fermented plant-based products, creating a gap between production practice and safety oversight.  The material entanglement here reflects broader patterns of how food systems navigate competing demands: the same fermented plant-based products marketed for sustainability and health benefits carry embedded exposure to mycotoxins—chemical hazards whose presence or absence hinges on microbial selection and operational control. The paper identifies microbial detoxification during fermentation as a potential pathway, yet this capacity is not inherent to fermentation itself but contingent on deliberate strain selection and process management, revealing how food safety in plant-based systems requires active intervention rather than emerging naturally from the fermentation process.

title: Inactivation of Aspergillus brasiliensis ATCC 16404 conidia by biocides and UV irradiation alone and in combination
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Research International
excerpt: Fungal contamination is a major concern in the food production chain, particularly in the dairy industry. Traditional cleaning and disinfection techniques, though effective, can produce undesirable by-products ( e.g. chloramines) and may not comply with increasing consumers' demands for natural, non-toxic, and environmentally friendly solutions. This study explores the efficacy of biocides (ethanol, peracetic acid, acetic acid) and UV irradiation, alone and in combination, for different duration and intensities. Aspergillus brasiliensis ATCC 16404 was used as it is a reference strain in disinfection evaluation standards. Unlike many previous studies solely based on hydrated conidia, dry conidia were also tested to mimic airborne conidia that are responsible for contamination. Dry conidia were more sensitive to UV than the hydrated ones, but their aggregation could significantly reduce UV efficiency. Overall, they exhibited higher sensitivity to alcohol-based biocides. Finally, the effect of combination treatments was assessed using biocides containing less than 1% active compounds and short times (1 to 5 min) UV irradiations to allow survivors after each treatment. A synergistic effect, ranging from 1.33 to 3.93 log additional reduction, was obtained when an acid-based disinfectant was used sequentially with UV for both hydrated and dry conidia, thus achieving more than 4 log reductions. Detailed schematic of the individual and combined inactivation procedures on both hydrated and dry A. brasiliensis ATCC 16404 conidia. • The physiological state of conidia impacted the efficacy of fungicidal treatments. • Hydrated conidia were more resistant than dry ones to biocides and UV treatments. • Alcohol- and oxidizing-based biocides reduced conidia viability by >4 log. • UV treatment reached >4 log reduction for dry conidia but not for hydrated ones. • UV/oxidizing biocide combinations showed efficient synergistic effects.
summary: Fungal contamination in dairy production and other food chains is typically managed through chemical cleaning and disinfection, but these methods generate problematic by-products such as chloramines and conflict with consumer preferences for non-toxic, environmentally sound alternatives. This study tested the effectiveness of three biocides—ethanol, peracetic acid, and acetic acid—alongside ultraviolet irradiation, applied individually and in combination at varying durations and intensities, using Aspergillus brasiliensis ATCC 16404 as a reference test organism. The research distinguished between hydrated conidia (spores in moisture) and dry conidia (mimicking airborne spores responsible for actual contamination), finding that dry conidia showed greater sensitivity to ultraviolet light but that clumping of dry spores substantially diminished ultraviolet effectiveness; conversely, dry conidia proved more vulnerable to alcohol-based biocides overall. When acid-based disinfectants were applied sequentially with brief ultraviolet exposures (1 to 5 minutes) at low biocide concentrations (less than 1% active compounds), the treatments produced combined-effect effects—additional reductions of 1.33 to 3.93 log units—achieving more than 4 log reductions in both hydrated and dry spore populations.  The physiological state of fungal spores emerges as a decisive factor in how contamination control operates at the point of food production. Dry airborne spores, the actual vectors of contamination in industrial settings, respond differently to chemical and physical interventions than laboratory-prepared hydrated spores, yet their tendency to aggregate—a material property arising from their dispersal through air—can neutralize the very physical treatment designed to address them. The combined-effect pairing of acid chemistry with ultraviolet light reveals a material dependency: neither treatment alone achieves sufficient reduction under practical constraints (low chemical concentration, brief exposure), but their sequential application produces an effect greater than the sum of its parts, suggesting that industrial food safety may increasingly depend on layered, combined interventions rather than single-agent solutions.

title: Functional analysis of the class III chitin synthase genes in the filamentous basidiomycete Pleurotus ostreatus
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Fungal Genetics and Biology
excerpt: Class III chitin synthases (CHSs) are specific to filamentous fungi and are associated with biosynthesis of chitin, a key structural component of fungal cell walls. While extensively studied in ascomycetes, their roles in basidiomycetes remain poorly understood. In this study, we present the first functional analysis of class III chss in the white-rot basidiomycete Pleurotus ostreatus. Through targeted single gene disruption of the class III chss, chs7 and chs9, as well as double disruption of chs7chs9, we demonstrate their distinct and sometimes overlapping roles in regulating hyphal growth, cell wall thickness, and stress resilience. Despite reduced cell wall thickness and decreased growth rates in disruption strains, compensatory upregulation of other chitin and glucan synthase genes maintains polysaccharide composition, revealing a robust regulatory network. Notably chs9 disruption strains had more severe impacts than those of chs7, suggesting that chs9 is the main class III chs and chs7 provides more of a supporting role. Double disruption strains also revealed straighter hyphae suggesting redundancy and highly specialized functions of clade III chss in hyphal straightness in P. ostreatus. Our findings highlight the evolutionary divergence and seemingly unique functions of class III CHSs in comparison to the previously analyzed basidiomycete-specific classes, providing foundational insights into filamentous basidiomycete cell wall dynamics.
summary: Oyster mushrooms (*Pleurotus ostreatus*) possess two class III chitin synthase genes—chs7 and chs9—that regulate the construction and mechanical properties of their cell walls. This paper reports the first functional analysis of these genes in a basidiomycete fungus, using targeted genetic disruption to observe their individual and combined effects. When either gene was deleted, the resulting strains showed thinner cell walls and slower growth rates, yet compensatory activation of other cell wall-building genes maintained the overall balance of structural polysaccharides. The chs9 gene emerged as the primary functional copy, with chs9 deletion producing more severe effects than chs7 deletion; chs7 appears to play a secondary supporting role. Double deletion of both genes produced a distinctive observable trait profile: straighter hyphae, suggesting these genes normally work together to regulate the curvature of fungal filaments.  The regulatory redundancy documented here—where loss of one gene triggers increased activity of others to preserve cell wall integrity—reveals a material dependency embedded in the fungal body itself. The oyster mushroom's capacity to compensate for genetic disruption demonstrates how robustness emerges not from a single critical pathway but from distributed, overlapping functions. Yet this same compensatory flexibility masks an underlying fragility: the cell wall remains thinner, growth remains slower, and the fungus's capacity to respond to external stresses may be compromised even as its basic architecture holds. In contexts where *Pleurotus ostreatus* is cultivated for food or deployed for biological cleanup, such genetic or chemical perturbations—whether accidental or deliberate—could shift the organism from a productive asset into a compromised one, revealing how fungal robustness and vulnerability are not opposites but entangled properties of the same biological system.

title: Upcycling of industrial hemp byproducts into a sustainable molded fiber packaging system
source: ScienceDirect Publication: International Journal of Biological Macromolecules
excerpt: This study presents the fabrication and characterization of fully bio-based molded fiber composites using industrial hemp (Cannabis sativa L.) hurds, starch, and cellulose nanofibrils (CNF) for sustainable single-use packaging applications. Hemp hurds, a low-value agricultural byproduct, were directly upcycled into value-added molded fiber composites by varying their loadings to evaluate their influence on rheological, mechanical, and morphological properties. Rheological analysis revealed that excessive solid content increased slurry heterogeneity and entrapped microbubbles, reducing both storage and loss moduli. However, during hot pressing, these trapped air voids were released, resulting in densified fiber networks. Consequently, the mechanical performance improved with increasing hurd content up to a threshold. Among all formulations, the composite containing 20 g of hemp hurds (H20) exhibited the most balanced properties, achieving high compressive (8.70 ± 0.34 MPa), flexural (7.56 ± 0.51 MPa), and impact strength (13.83 ± 1.19 J/m), comparable to or exceeding those of the higher-loading formulation containing 28 g of hemp hurds (H28). Compressive strength increased by more than 300% compared with conventional molded pulp reported in the literature, while flexural strength nearly doubled (∼90% increase) from H12 to H20. SEM analysis confirmed that H20 possessed a uniform interfacial adhesion between the starch-CNF matrix and hemp fibers, while both excessive (H28) and insufficient (H12, 12 g hemp hurds) fiber loadings resulted in structural irregularities. These findings demonstrate a value-added upcycling strategy that converts underutilized hemp byproducts into high-performance molded fiber packaging materials composed entirely of bio-derived constituents.
summary: Hemp hurds, a low-value agricultural byproduct from Cannabis sativa L. processing, were combined with starch and cellulose nanofibrils to produce molded fiber composites for single-use packaging. This paper varied the loading of hemp hurds and found that excessive solid content created trapped air bubbles in the slurry, reducing its elastic and viscous properties before pressing; however, these air voids were released during hot pressing, creating denser fiber networks. The formulation containing 20 grams of hemp hurds achieved the most balanced performance, with compressive strength of 8.70 megapascals, flexural strength of 7.56 megapascals, and impact strength of 13.83 joules per meter—exceeding conventional molded pulp by more than 300 percent in compression and nearly 90 percent in flexural strength. Scanning electron microscope imaging showed that H20 possessed uniform bonding between the starch-cellulose nanofibrils matrix and hemp fibers, while both higher and lower loadings produced structural irregularities.  Within the material transformations of the Anthropocene, this work reveals how agricultural waste—the fibrous residue left after hemp processing—can be repositioned as industrial feedstock through composite engineering. The same hemp byproduct that represents disposal burden and economic loss becomes, under specific material conditions and processing parameters, a strengthening agent that outperforms established packaging materials. Yet the optimization required—the precise threshold of 20 grams, the critical role of air entrapment and release, the dependence on starch and cellulose nanofibrils as binding matrices—exposes how this "upcycling" remains embedded in controlled laboratory conditions and industrial processing infrastructure, raising questions about whether such gains in material performance can be sustained at scale without reproducing the resource dependencies and energy inputs that characterize conventional packaging systems.

title: Revealing the effects of Aspergillus cristatus, golden flower fungus, fermenting on the roots of the medicinal and edible plant Panax ginseng
source: Frontiers in Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: Panax ginseng is one of the most important medicinal and edible plants with pharmacological compounds mainly concentrated in the roots. Although chemical transformations of ginseng active compounds have been studied, their biotransformation processes by beneficial microbes are less reported. This study aimed to reveal changes in the functional components in P. ginseng roots fermented by the golden flower fungus Aspergillus cristatus. P. ginseng roots were incubated with A. cristatus, and the final product was given the name “Golden Flower Chinese Ginseng (GFCG).” A high-performance liquid chromatography (HP-LC) and liquid chromatograph mass spectrometer (LC–MS) system revealed that fermentation by A. cristatus caused metabolite changes in GFCG and promoted the production of rare ginsenosides. Transcriptomic analysis demonstrated that more than 72% of significantly differentially expressed genes in A. cristatus showed a decrease during interaction with P. ginseng. In the meantime, transcription-related genes were suppressed, while translational and post-translational events were activated, suggesting a special role of the fungal microbe when it is colonizing medical and edible plant
summary: Panax ginseng roots were fermented with the fungus Aspergillus cristatus, a process that produced a product designated "Golden Flower Chinese Ginseng." Chemical analysis using high-performance liquid chromatography and liquid chromatography mass spectrometry detected metabolite changes in the fermented roots and showed that the fungal fermentation promoted production of rare ginsenosides—compounds with potential medicinal value. gene-expression analysis analysis of the fungus revealed that more than 72 percent of genes showing significant expression changes showed decreased activity during its interaction with the ginseng roots, while genes involved in translation and post-translational modification were activated, indicating a shift in fungal metabolic activity during colonization of the plant tissue.  Within the material assemblages of medicinal plant cultivation, this fermentation process reveals how a fungal colonizer simultaneously reduces its own genetic activity while intensifying its biochemical work on the host plant. The fungus becomes a metabolic tool—transforming ginseng's active compounds into forms of greater pharmaceutical value—yet this transformation depends on the fungus suppressing its own transcriptional machinery, suggesting a controlled parasitism or mutualism shaped by the specific chemical environment of the plant root. The production of rare ginsenosides through fungal fermentation thus emerges as a site where biological capacity and economic value converge: the same fungal colonization that signals microbial invasion also generates marketable compounds, making the boundary between contamination and enhancement a matter of timing, control, and commercial intent.

title: Single-cell RNA sequencing analysis revealed a potential association between ELK3 expression and the progression of multiple myeloma
source: Frontiers in Immunology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: BackgroundMultiple myeloma (MM) typically evolves from monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS) and smoldering multiple myeloma (SMM). Its progression is accompanied by significant tumor heterogeneity and immune microenvironment remodeling, and MM remains largely incurable despite therapeutic advances. Elucidating cellular heterogeneity and regulatory mechanisms involved in disease progression is critical for understanding MM pathogenesis.MethodsSingle-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) provides important support for systematically elucidating the molecular mechanisms and immune regulatory pathways associated with the progression of MM. The scRNA-seq datasets from healthy donors (HD), MGUS, SMM, and MM patients were obtained from the GEO database to analyze plasma cell transcriptional heterogeneity within the bone marrow microenvironment. Plasma cell differentiation trajectories were inferred using pseudotime analysis, and intercellular communication and transcription factor regulatory networks were predicted using CellChat and SCENIC analyses. In addition, functional validation of ELK3 was performed in vitro using MM cell lines RPMI 8226 and U266.ResultsIn this work,
