title: Kernel structure and varietal attributes determine aflatoxin production by Aspergillus flavus in maize (Zea mays L.)
source: Latest Results
excerpt: Maize (Zea mays L.) is a globally significant crop that is susceptible to contamination by aflatoxigenic fungi during storage. Aspergillus flavus produces aflatoxins, which are toxic and carcinogenic, posing substantial food safety risks, particularly in regions where maize is a dietary staple. Due to the chemical stability of aflatoxins, early prevention of contamination is essential. This study evaluated five maize varieties (blue, purple, yellow, white Bajío, and white Sinaloa) to determine whether their physical and chemical grain characteristics or antioxidant content influence aflatoxin production under storage-like conditions. Maize kernels were inoculated with A. flavus and maintained under controlled storage conditions. Total aflatoxin levels were quantified, and grain properties, including thousand-kernel weight, flotation index, and texture, as well as total phenolic content and antioxidant activity, were analysed. Pearson correlation and path analysis indicated that physical traits associated with density, starch content, and hardness significantly influenced aflatoxin accumulation, whereas antioxidant content did not. These findings demonstrate that the structural and physical properties of maize kernels are more critical than antioxidant content in limiting fungal growth and aflatoxin production during storage. Selecting maize varieties with robust physical grain traits may reduce the risk of aflatoxin contamination during storage.
summary: Maize kernels from five varieties—blue, purple, yellow, white Bajío, and white Sinaloa—were inoculated with *Aspergillus flavus* and stored under controlled conditions to test whether grain structure and chemical composition affect aflatoxin production. This paper measured total aflatoxin levels alongside physical properties of the kernels (thousand-kernel weight, flotation index, texture) and chemical markers (total phenolic content and antioxidant activity). Statistical analysis using Pearson correlation and path analysis revealed that physical traits linked to kernel density, starch content, and hardness significantly predicted aflatoxin accumulation, whereas the antioxidant content of the grain did not influence fungal toxin production.  The findings expose a material paradox in maize storage: the same physical properties that make kernels denser and harder—traits typically associated with crop quality and yield—also create conditions that either favour or inhibit fungal colonization and toxin synthesis. Rather than operating as a protective barrier, kernel structure becomes a site where the fungus's metabolic capacity and the grain's material composition become entangled, determining whether *Aspergillus flavus* will proliferate and accumulate carcinogenic compounds. This suggests that varietal selection for storage resilience cannot rely on breeding for antioxidant content alone, but must attend to the specific material architecture of the grain itself—a finding with direct implications for food security in regions where maize contamination poses documented health risks.

title: Diagnosis of Mucormycosis: Current Situation, Challenges and Future Prospects
source: Wiley: Mycoses: Table of Contents
excerpt: Mucormycosis, an aggressive fungal infection caused by members of the order Mucorales, progresses rapidly and is associated with high mortality, particularly in immunocompromised hosts such as patients with uncontrolled diabetes, transplant recipients or those with COVID-19-associated immunosuppression. Early diagnosis remains challenging with current clinical methods, yet it is essential to reduce mortality. This review examines the evolution of diagnostic strategies for mucormycosis, ranging from conventional techniques such as histopathology, culture and microscopy to advanced and emerging methods including molecular assays, serological testing, imaging and metabolomics. We also explore the ongoing transition towards integrated, rapid and non-invasive diagnostic platforms that leverage novel biomarkers, portable devices and artificial intelligence. These new technologies have the potential to facilitate early diagnosis, thereby enabling early treatment and reducing the mortality rate of mucormycosis.
summary: Mucormycosis, caused by organisms in the order Mucorales, is an aggressive fungal infection that spreads rapidly and carries high mortality rates, particularly among immune-weakened populations including those with uncontrolled diabetes, transplant recipients, and people with COVID-19-associated immune suppression. This paper reviews the evolution of diagnostic approaches, documenting the shift from conventional methods—histopathology, culture, and microscopy—to advanced techniques including molecular assays, serological testing, imaging, and metabolite-level analysis analysis. The review identifies a transition toward integrated diagnostic platforms that combine rapid, non-invasive methods with novel biomarkers, portable devices, and artificial intelligence systems, with the stated aim of enabling earlier detection and treatment to reduce mortality.  The diagnostic challenge documented here reveals a material entanglement between clinical urgency and technological capacity. Mucormycosis emerges not simply as a pathological problem but as a site where diagnostic innovation becomes bound to questions of access and timing—early detection depends on technologies that remain unevenly distributed across healthcare systems, while the fungal organisms themselves continue to circulate through immune-weakened bodies shaped by broader patterns of metabolic disease, transplant dependency, and pandemic-induced immune vulnerability. The review's focus on "integrated platforms" and "portable devices" signals an implicit recognition that mortality from this infection is not merely a biological fact but a product of where diagnostic tools are available and how quickly they can be deployed.

title: Morphological differentiation and its application in industrial filamentous fungi
source: tandf: Mycology: Table of Contents
excerpt: Filamentous fungi are essential ecosystem members and major platforms for industrial biomanufacturing, where their morphological differentiation strongly influences broth rheology, mass/oxygen transfer, secretion efficiency, stress tolerance, and ultimately productivity. However, the intricate network in which process-specific cues are sensed and integrated to reprogram the endogenous developmental pathways remains incompletely understood, limiting rational morphology engineering of the industrial filamentous fungi. This review summarizes recent advances in the regulation of morphological differentiation of filamentous fungi, integrating internal and external signaling along with molecular control of spore germination, polarity establishment, hyphal extension/branching, and cell wall-plasma membrane homeostasis. We discuss how distinct morphologies affect performance in submerged and solid-state fermentation, and we overview the practical control strategies including inoculation design, environmental and hydrodynamic optimization, microparticle-enhanced cultivation, and genetic reprogramming. Finally, we highlight emerging monitoring approaches and recent progress in machine learning and modeling within AI-enabled biomanufacturing, proposing an integrated gene–morphology–function–titer perspective for next-generation fungal cell factories.
summary: Filamentous fungi serve dual roles in both natural ecosystems and industrial bioprocessing, where their physical structure—the arrangement and density of branching filaments—directly determines how efficiently they mix in fermentation broths, how well oxygen reaches their cells, how effectively they secrete proteins or metabolites, and ultimately how much product they yield. This paper reviews how filamentous fungi sense environmental and chemical signals, integrate those signals, and reprogram their developmental pathways to adopt different morphologies, covering the molecular mechanisms that control spore germination, the establishment of directional growth, hyphal branching patterns, and the maintenance of cell wall and membrane integrity. The review also examines how different fungal morphologies perform under submerged fermentation (liquid culture) versus solid-state fermentation (growth on solid growth media), and describes practical strategies for controlling morphology through inoculum design, adjustments to temperature and oxygen levels, addition of microparticles to the culture medium, and genetic modification. The paper proposes that future industrial fungal cell factories should integrate knowledge of genes, morphology, function, and product yield into a unified framework, and notes that emerging machine learning and computational modeling approaches are beginning to enable this integration within artificial intelligence-enabled bioprocessing systems.  Within industrial bioprocessing, fungal morphology operates as both constraint and opportunity: the same filamentous structures that enable rapid growth and metabolite secretion under optimized conditions can become liabilities under stress, creating bottlenecks in oxygen transfer or accumulating toxic metabolic byproducts. This review documents how morphological engineering—the deliberate manipulation of fungal form through environmental, genetic, and cultivation design—reveals the material entanglement between biological development and industrial productivity, where control over a fungus's physical organization becomes a lever for extracting value from living systems under conditions of high-throughput manufacturing.

title: Establishing links between biosynthetic gene clusters in fungi and their secondary metabolites: A case study of Aspergillus
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Bioresource Technology
excerpt: In this study, an integrative genome-metabolite mining approach was employed to connect biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) with their secondary metabolites (SMs), taking one of the most important fungal genera, Aspergillus, as an example. A total of 1,373 Aspergillus genomes were analyzed by using antiSMASH to catalog all BGCs and their types, and a phylogenetic tree of these strains was built, with the periphery decorated with antiSMASH results. Subsequently, the BGCs were clustered into gene cluster families (GCFs) using BiG-SLiCE, and 4,724 Reaxys-indexed Aspergillus SMs were similarly clustered by chemical similarity into molecular networks (MNs). By associating known BGC-SM pairs with the MIBiG database, the GCF-BGC and MN-SM networks were integrated for anchor-based co-occurrence scoring to identify possible GCF-MN matches. These results allowed us to determine which GCFs had known SMs and to estimate the vast proportion of Aspergillus biosynthetic capacity yet to be explored. Furthermore, these predictions were experimentally validated by chemical investigation of a marine-derived strain A. niger L14, which was shown to make unusual polyketides and non-ribosomal peptides using one strain many compounds (OSMAC) strategy and global transcription machinery engineering (gTME). Our fungal BGC-SM connections will facilitate the efficient discovery of biosynthetic potential of microbes and their SMs.
summary: This paper describes an integrative approach to mapping the relationship between fungal genes and the chemical compounds they produce. The study analyzed 1,373 genomes of *Aspergillus* fungi using computational tools to identify biosynthetic gene clusters—stretches of genetic code that encode the instructions for making secondary metabolites—and cataloged them into 4,724 known chemical compounds indexed in the Reaxys database. The researchers organized these gene clusters into families using the BiG-SLiCE tool and grouped the chemical compounds into molecular networks based on structural similarity, then used the MIBiG database of known gene-compound pairs as anchors to predict which gene cluster families produce which compounds. To test these predictions, they isolated a marine-derived strain of *Aspergillus niger* and used two experimental strategies—culturing the fungus under varied conditions and genetically rewiring its transcriptional machinery—to induce production of unusual polyketide and non-ribosomal peptide compounds.  The dual character of this research reflects a broader pattern in fungal biotechnology: the same computational infrastructure that identifies biosynthetic pathways as threats to food security or human health simultaneously reveals them as repositories of industrial potential. The *Aspergillus* strains examined here carry genetic instructions for producing compounds of pharmaceutical interest, yet these same fungi are also notorious contaminants of agricultural products and human habitats. By systematically mapping which genes produce which metabolites across hundreds of genomes, this paper renders fungal chemistry legible to industrial extraction while implicitly documenting the vast biosynthetic capacity that remains uncharacterized—a gap that reflects both the limits of current knowledge and the economic incentives that shape which organisms receive scientific attention.

title: Isolation of cold-adapted cellulose-degrading fungus Penicillium oxalicum G6 and related molecular mechanisms
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Bioresource Technology
excerpt: Cellulose in agricultural waste is recalcitrant to degradation in cold regions, severely limiting the utilization of resources. To obtain efficient cellulose-degrading microorganisms adapted to low temperatures, fungal strain G6 was isolated from sheep manure and identified as Penicillium oxalicum. This is the first report of a cold-adapted cellulose-degrading P. oxalicum strain isolated from sheep manure. Enzyme activities of G6 were tested at 0°C, the enzyme activities of G6 (carboxymethylcellulase, 153.25 U·mL<sup>-1</sup>; filter paperase, 136.67 U·mL<sup>-1</sup>) were approximately twice those under intermediate mesophilic conditions. After static fermentation at 15°C for 7 days, the straw degradation rate reached 73.70%. Whole-genome CAZyme annotation revealed that G6 harbors abundant genes encoding key cellulose/hemicellulose-degrading enzyme families, such as GH5, GH3, and GH43, together with a significantly enriched glycolysis/gluconeogenesis pathway (ko00010), ensuring an adequate energy supply and metabolic balance at low temperatures (0-15°C). Metabolomic profiling further confirmed that G6 responds to cold stress by dynamically regulating the secretion of multiple carbon flux metabolites (e.g., glucose and arabitol), thereby maintaining highly efficient degradation. Thus, the cold-adapted cellulolytic enzymes and metabolic regulatory mechanisms of G6 were systematically elucidated in this study, providing a potential functional fungal resource for the biological treatment of biomass waste in cold regions.
summary: Penicillium oxalicum strain G6, isolated from sheep manure, demonstrates exceptional cellulose-degrading capacity at temperatures between 0 and 15 degrees Celsius. At 0 degrees Celsius, the strain produced carboxymethylcellulase at 153.25 units per millilitre and filter paperase at 136.67 units per millilitre—approximately double the activity observed under intermediate mesophilic conditions. After seven days of static fermentation at 15 degrees Celsius, the strain degraded straw at a rate of 73.70 percent. Genomic analysis identified abundant genes encoding key cellulose and hemicellulose-degrading enzyme families (GH5, GH3, and GH43 glycoside hydrolase families), alongside a significantly enriched glycolysis and gluconeogenesis metabolic pathway that sustains energy production at low temperatures. metabolite-level analysis profiling revealed that G6 responds to cold stress by dynamically regulating the secretion of multiple carbon-flux metabolites, including glucose and arabitol, which maintain efficient degradation capacity.  This paper presents a fungal strain that transforms agricultural waste—specifically straw and manure residues—into a metabolically accessible resource under conditions where such materials would otherwise remain inert. The cold-adapted cellulolytic capacity of G6 emerges not as an incidental trait but as a coordinated metabolic reconfiguration: the same enzymatic machinery and regulatory pathways that enable survival in marginal thermal conditions simultaneously unlock the material value locked within cellulose. In cold regions where agricultural waste accumulates as an underutilized byproduct, this fungus represents a potential site where biological constraint and economic opportunity converge—where the material recalcitrance of biomass meets the metabolic plasticity of a microorganism, creating conditions for resource extraction that would otherwise require energy-intensive industrial processing.

title: N'-(4-fluorophenyl)-2-pyridinecarbohydrazide targets succinate dehydrogenase to control citrus blue mold
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Microbiology
excerpt: In this study, N'-(4-fluorophenyl)-2-pyridinecarbohydrazide (2-FPH), N'-(4-fluorophenyl)-3-pyridinecarbohydrazide (3-FPH), and N'-(4-fluorophenyl)-4-pyridinecarbohydrazide (4-FPH), which showed considerable antifungal activities against the prochloraz-resistant Penicillium italicum strain (PiR2), were designed and synthesized. Among these, 2-FPH displayed the strongest antifungal activity against PiR2 growth, with minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) of 12.5 μg/mL and minimum fungicidal concentrations (MFC) of 25 μg/mL. In vivo antifungal and hemolytic toxicity tests showed that 2-FPH effectively prevented PiR2 infection of citrus fruits, but exhibited exceptionally low hemolytic toxicity. At equivalent concentrations, its biological safety and control efficacy were far superior to those of prochloraz. According to morphological findings, 2-FPH increased cell membrane permeability, caused cell swelling and morphological abnormalities, disrupted structural integrity, and decreased chitin content in cell walls. Physiological, biochemical, and molecular biology investigations indicated that 2-FPH binds to succinate dehydrogenase (SDH), inhibits its enzymatic activity, and suppresses associated gene expression. This decreases intracellular ATP levels, disrupts normal mitochondrial function, and triggers a substantial increase in reactive oxygen species (ROS). In summary, 2-FPH shows promising potential as a possible fungicide and succinate dehydrogenase inhibitor for managing citrus blue mold.
summary: This study describes the design and synthesis of three fluorinated pyridine compounds targeting a fungal enzyme, with the lead compound 2-FPH demonstrating potent activity against a prochloraz-resistant strain of *Penicillium italicum*, the causative agent of citrus blue mold. The compound achieved minimum inhibitory concentrations of 12.5 micrograms per milliliter and minimum fungicidal concentrations of 25 micrograms per milliliter against the resistant strain. In laboratory and whole-fruit trials, 2-FPH prevented infection while showing negligible damage to red blood cells, substantially outperforming the commercial fungicide prochloraz at equivalent doses. Mechanistically, the compound disrupted fungal cell membranes, reduced chitin content in cell walls, and bound directly to succinate dehydrogenase—an enzyme central to energy production in mitochondria—thereby collapsing intracellular energy reserves and triggering accumulation of reactive oxygen species that damaged cellular machinery.  The efficacy of 2-FPH reveals a material entanglement characteristic of contemporary crop protection: the same enzymatic vulnerability that makes *Penicillium italicum* susceptible to this compound also reflects the fungus's adaptive response to prior chemical pressure. The resistance to prochloraz that prompted this research emerged from repeated exposure to a different class of fungicide, yet the solution—a new inhibitor targeting a conserved metabolic pathway—carries no guarantee against future resistance evolution. The compound's low toxicity to mammalian cells, while advantageous for human safety, does not address the ecological consequences of introducing another selective pressure into citrus agroecosystems, where the fungus persists within complex communities of competing microorganisms and where resistance mechanisms may already be circulating among untreated populations.

title: Transformation patterns of masked fusarium mycotoxins during barley malting: Mechanistic evidence and multimodal detection in industrial products
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Chemistry
excerpt: Malt, a key food industry ingredient, faces safety risks from Fusarium mycotoxins and masked derivatives. While malt's agricultural role is clear, biotransformation pathways of masked mycotoxins during barley malting remain poorly understood. This study assesses mycotoxin contamination in commercial malt and links fungal communities to mycotoxin metabolism. Deoxynivalenol dominated, detected in 55% beer and 70% malt samples, with low adult exposure risk. Using ultra performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry and internal transcribed spacer sequencing, natural malting showed biphasic dynamics: toxigenic fungi and masked mycotoxins first declined, then resurged. Artificial trials revealed deoxynivalenol converted to deoxynivalenol-3-glucoside with 30% efficiency (based on deoxynivalenol loss), while zearalenone derivatives fluctuated. This study provides the first systematic evidence demonstrating the conversion of deoxynivalenol to deoxynivalenol-3-glucoside during the malting process of barley, offering actionable insights for the development of tailored mycotoxin mitigation strategies in industrial malt production and beer brewing.
summary: Barley malting transforms mycotoxin contamination in ways that both reduce and regenerate fungal toxins. This study detected deoxynivalenol in 55% of beer samples and 70% of malt samples, with zearalenone derivatives also present, using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry and fungal genetic sequencing to track contamination across commercial products. During natural malting, toxigenic fungi and masked mycotoxins initially declined, then resurged in a two-phase pattern. Controlled trials demonstrated that deoxynivalenol was converted to deoxynivalenol-3-glucoside with 30% efficiency based on the loss of the original toxin, while zearalenone derivatives showed variable transformation. This paper provides the first direct evidence that barley malting converts deoxynivalenol into a masked derivative form.  The malting process emerges as a site where fungal toxins are simultaneously rendered less bioavailable and chemically restructured into forms whose long-term safety remains uncertain. The biphasic dynamics—initial decline followed by resurgence of both fungi and masked toxins—reveal that malting does not eliminate contamination but rather redistributes it across chemical and biological forms. This transformation pattern reflects deeper dependencies within industrial grain processing: the same microbial activity that produces fermentable sugars also metabolizes toxins into derivatives that may escape detection in standard safety testing, creating a gap between measured risk and actual exposure that varies across beer and malt products.

title: Pathotype-specific roles of putative conidiation regulators in development, secondary metabolism, and pathogenicity in Fusarium fujikuroi
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Fungal Genetics and Biology
excerpt: Fusarium fujikuroi, the causal agent of rice bakanae disease, comprises two pathotypes with distinct symptoms: the fumonisin-producing blight type (B14) and the gibberellin-producing elongation type (B20). Asexual spore production and secondary metabolism are essential for disease transmission and development in F. fujikuroi, and conidiation regulators frequently influence secondary metabolism and other phenotypes in filamentous fungi. To investigate the genetic basis underlying differences between the two pathotypes, we generated deletion mutants for 14 genes corresponding to well-characterized conidiation regulators (upstream activators, central regulators, and velvet complex components) in both B14 and B20. Phenotypic analyses revealed that several upstream regulators, including FfflbD, are essential for macroconidiation in both strains, whereas others, such as FfflbA, FfflbB, and FfflbE, showed pathotype-dependent roles. The central regulator FfabaA was indispensable for conidiation in both backgrounds, while FfwetA displayed divergent roles in conidial maturation. Velvet regulators (Ffvel1, Ffvel2, and Fflae1) promoted conidiation in both backgrounds, with Fflae1 additionally affecting macroconidiation in B20. In B14, deletion of Ffvel1, Ffvel2, FfabaA, or FfwetA abolished or significantly reduced virulence and was associated with decreased FUM1 expression, linking conidiation with fumonisin biosynthesis and pathogenicity. In B20, several mutants partially shifted toward blight-like symptoms and altered gibberellin gene expression. Notably, ΔFflae1 in B20 caused >500-fold FUM1 upregulation, increased fumonisin accumulation to near-B14 levels, and induced blight-like disease symptoms. Transcriptional profiling across mutants revealed distinct regulatory network architectures between B14 and B20. Together, these results show extensive divergence in developmental regulatory networks between F. fujikuroi pathotypes and implicate chromatin-associated control in path
summary: Fusarium fujikuroi, which causes rice bakanae disease, exists as two pathotypes that produce different toxins and trigger distinct symptoms: strain B14 produces fumonisin and causes blight, while strain B20 produces gibberellin and causes abnormal elongation. This paper deleted 14 genes encoding conidiation regulators—proteins that control asexual spore formation—in both pathotypes to identify genetic differences underlying their distinct disease presentations. Several upstream regulators, particularly FfflbD, were required for spore production in both strains, while others like FfflbA, FfflbB, and FfflbE showed pathotype-specific roles. The central regulator FfabaA was essential for spore formation in both backgrounds, and velvet complex regulators (Ffvel1, Ffvel2, and Fflae1) promoted spore production, with Fflae1 additionally affecting spore maturation specifically in B20. In B14, loss of Ffvel1, Ffvel2, FfabaA, or FfwetA reduced disease-causing ability and decreased expression of FUM1, the gene controlling fumonisin production, directly linking spore development to toxin biosynthesis and disease severity. In B20, deletion of Fflae1 caused a dramatic shift: fumonisin production increased over 500-fold and blight-like symptoms emerged, indicating that a single developmental regulator can redirect the pathotype's metabolic output and disease observable trait profile.  The findings expose how a fungal pathogen's capacity to harm rice—its disease-causing ability—is materially entangled with its capacity to produce specific chemical compounds and generate spores. The same regulatory genes that control reproduction simultaneously govern toxin synthesis, meaning that disrupting spore formation automatically diminishes pathogenicity in B14. More striking is the plasticity revealed in B20: a single genetic deletion can reprogram the strain's entire metabolic strategy, converting it from a gibberellin-producer into a fumonisin-producer and altering its disease presentation. This demonstrates that pathotype identity is not fixed but contingent on active regulatory control—a finding that complicates any simple classification of Fusarium strains as discrete disease agents and suggests instead that pathogenicity emerges from dynamic, reversible alignments between development, chemistry, and host response.

title: Anticancer therapeutic efficacy of PEGylated β-galactosidase from Aspergillus terreus in DMBA-induced breast cancer: Toxicological, molecular and histopathological evaluation in Wistar rats
source: ScienceDirect Publication: International Journal of Biological Macromolecules
excerpt: Breast cancer remains a leading cause of cancer-related morbidity and mortality among women worldwide, underscoring the need for safer and more effective therapeutic strategies. Polyethylene glycol (PEG) conjugation is a well-established approach to enhance the stability, bioavailability, and in vivo performance of protein/enzymatic therapeutics. To our knowledge, this is the first study to demonstrate PEG conjugation of β-galactosidase and to provide in vivo evidence of its antitumor efficacy. Here, β-galactosidase from Aspergillus terreus was PEGylated using mPEG-NHS (5 kDa) and conjugation was validated by HPLC (retention time shift) and FTIR spectroscopy (PEG-associated ether bands with preserved protein amide signatures). Safety was evaluated following ARRIVE and OECD acute oral toxicity (TG 423) and 28-day repeated-dose oral toxicity (TG 407) protocols, showing no mortality, overt clinical toxicity, or treatment-related changes in hepatic or renal biochemical indices across the tested doses. Antitumor efficacy was assessed in a 7,12-dimethylbenz[a]anthracene (DMBA)-induced mammary carcinoma model in female Wistar rats. Oral administration of PEGylated β-galactosidase (50 mg/kg for 30 days) attenuated DMBA-associated metabolic and oxidative disturbances, reflected by reduced lipid peroxidation (malondialdehyde) and restoration of enzymatic (SOD, CAT, GPx, GR, GST) and non-enzymatic (GSH, vitamins C and E) antioxidant defenses. Treatment further normalized membrane-bound ATPase activities, reduced tumor-associated enzyme markers (5'-nucleotidase, γ-glutamyltransferase, cathepsin D), regulated apoptosis-related endpoints (BAX, BCL-XL, P53; caspase-3) and improved mammary tissue histoarchitecture. Collectively, these findings support PEGylated β-galactosidase as a well-tolerated macromolecular candidate with in vivo antitumor potential mediated primarily through redox restoration and apoptosis regulation.
summary: This paper documents the chemical modification of a fungal enzyme—β-galactosidase extracted from Aspergillus terreus—through polyethylene glycol conjugation, and tests its capacity to suppress breast tumors induced chemically in laboratory rats. The enzyme was attached to polyethylene glycol molecules (5 kilodalton weight) using standard chemical coupling, confirmed by high-performance liquid chromatography and infrared spectroscopy. Safety testing in rats following standardized protocols showed no deaths, visible illness, or damage to liver or kidney function across all tested doses. In tumor-bearing rats receiving oral doses of the modified enzyme for 30 days, the treatment reduced markers of cellular damage from oxidative stress (cell membrane damage caused by oxidation products), restored multiple protective enzyme systems in cells (superoxide dismutase, catalase, glutathione peroxidase, glutathione reductase, glutathione transferase), replenished cellular antioxidant molecules (reduced glutathione, vitamin C, vitamin E), normalized energy-producing protein pumps in cell membranes, reduced tumor-linked enzymes (5'-nucleotidase, gamma-glutamyltransferase, cathepsin D), and triggered programmed cell death pathways in tumor tissue while restoring normal tissue structure.  The dual character of this fungal enzyme—extracted from a genus often associated with clinical infection and contamination, yet engineered here as a therapeutic agent—reveals how the same biological material can be repositioned across registers of harm and healing depending on chemical modification and controlled administration. The enzyme's capacity to restore cellular antioxidant defenses and trigger tumor cell death operates within the body as a form of metabolic repair, yet this remediation depends entirely on the synthetic polymer wrapper that makes the fungal protein legible and tolerable to mammalian physiology. What emerges is not simply a new drug but a material assemblage in which fungal biology, synthetic chemistry, and laboratory rat metabolism become entangled in the production of therapeutic value.

title: Harnessing the power of morphological modulation: Peptidoglycan-based filamentous microparticles for prolonged immune response and oral vaccine efficacy
source: ScienceDirect Publication: International Journal of Biological Macromolecules
excerpt: Peptidoglycan (PGN) plays a pivotal role in determining the diverse morphology of bacteria and enabling a range of surface biological functions. Inspired by the interesting phenomenon of bacterial morphological transformation under environmental stress, chemically enhancing the artificial adjustability of bacterial morphology opens a new approach for its use as a highly effective and versatile carrier. This study presents a novel approach using morphologically elongated purified probiotic PGN, presenting filamentous bacterium-like particles (fBLPs). By optimizing the conditions, bacterium-like particle (BLP) morphology was elongated 10- to 20-fold, and the antigenic loading was increased. Compared to traditional BLP, fBLP exhibited enhanced stability in gastric acid and a 4- to 6-fold higher uptake via Peyer's patches (PPs) (P ≤ 0.05). Furthermore, in vitro macrophage phagocytosis assays demonstrated that fBLP prolonged the phagocytosis process, leading to extended vaccine retention in vivo. In mice, a single dose of the fBLP-based vaccine provided 100% protection and prolonged the duration of immunity. Additionally, the co-administration of fBLP and Alum as oral adjuvants elicits a robust, synergistic immune response. This study presents a simple and cost-effective biochemical method that can generate advanced functions and therapeutic benefits by easily modulating bacterial morphology without complex genetic editing.
summary: Peptidoglycan, the structural polymer that determines bacterial shape, was chemically modified in this study to elongate purified probiotic bacterial particles into filamentous forms (fBLPs) measuring 10- to 20-fold longer than conventional spherical bacterial particles (BLPs). The elongated particles demonstrated enhanced resistance to gastric acid degradation and were taken up 4- to 6-fold more efficiently by Peyer's patches—the intestinal lymphoid tissues responsible for oral immune sampling. In laboratory assays, macrophages engulfed the filamentous particles more slowly than spherical ones, extending the time vaccine cargo remained inside immune cells before degradation. When administered orally to mice as a single dose, the filamentous particle vaccine provided complete protection against infection and sustained immunity longer than conventional formulations; combined administration with Alum adjuvant produced combined-effect immune amplification.  The morphological elongation documented in this paper reveals how deliberate manipulation of bacterial architecture—achieved through chemical rather than genetic means—converts a structural component into a functional asset for vaccine delivery. The same peptidoglycan that shapes bacterial cells under environmental stress becomes, when artificially extended, a mechanism for prolonging immune cell contact and delaying antigen degradation. This transformation from structural necessity to engineered advantage illustrates how material properties of microbial bodies can be decoupled from their original biological context and repurposed within human therapeutic systems, creating new dependencies between bacterial architecture and immune function that did not exist in nature.

title: Multi-Mycotoxin Quantification in Soy Protein Powders, Soymilk, Soybeans (Glycine max) and Other Legumes Using a New Targeted Differential Ion Mobility LCMS Method
source: acs: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry: Table of Contents
excerpt: Foods contaminated with fungal mycotoxins pose a serious risk to human health. Soy and other legumes are a vital source of alternative protein, yet the rates of mycotoxin contamination are not understood. We therefore developed a sensitive stable-isotope-dilution-differential-ion-mobility-spectrometry-LC-MS method for the quantification of 43 mycotoxins in soylike matrixes. Extraction by QuEChERS-d-SPE-PSA reduced matrix effects, while Differential-Ion-Mobility-Spectrometry (DMS) decreased background-noise and enabled the selective quantification of acetyl-deoxynivalenol (ACDON) isomers. Careful parameter selection was required since 3-ACDON produced tautomeric ion-mobility peaks. Mycotoxins were rarely found in whole soybeans, with only 3 out of 13 samples containing tentoxin, tenuazonic acid, or altenuene. However, mycotoxins were found in 52% non-soy legumes, 58% soy-protein powders, and 100% soy-milk samples including infant formulas. Alternaria mycotoxins were the most prevalent mycotoxins with Fusarium and Aspergillus mycotoxins also observed. Thus, although soybeans appear relatively resistant to mycotoxin contamination, even compared to other legumes, contamination may be introduced during storage and processing.
summary: Whole soybeans showed minimal mycotoxin contamination  with fungal toxins detected in only 3 of 13 samples—tentoxin, tenuazonic acid, and altenuene. However, processed soy products revealed substantially higher contamination rates: 58 percent of soy protein powders and 100 percent of soymilk samples, including infant formulas, contained measurable mycotoxins. Non-soy legumes fell between these extremes, with 52 percent of samples contaminated. The study employed a new analytical method combining stable-isotope labelling with differential-ion-mobility spectrometry and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry to quantify 43 distinct mycotoxins across these matrices. Alternaria species produced the most prevalent toxins, though Fusarium and Aspergillus mycotoxins were also documented.  The pattern documented here reveals how processing transforms the contamination profile of plant-based protein sources. Whole soybeans appear resistant to fungal colonization in the field, yet the same crop becomes a vector for mycotoxin exposure once reduced to powder or liquid form—a shift that occurs not through inherent biological change but through the material conditions of storage, milling, and industrial transformation. The presence of mycotoxins in 100 percent of soymilk samples, particularly those marketed as infant nutrition, exposes a dependency: the expansion of plant-based protein markets into processed, shelf-stable commodities has created new sites where fungal metabolites accumulate, making contamination not incidental to industrial food systems but structurally embedded within them.

title: Seroprevalence Screening of Chronic Aspergillus Infection in a Post‐Tuberculosis Cohort in Senegal: A Cross‐Sectional Study Comparing ELISA and Rapid Diagnostic Tests
source: Wiley: Mycoses: Table of Contents
excerpt: This study provided the first serological evidence of substantial Aspergillus antibody prevalence among post-TB patients in Senegal, suggesting a significant burden of undiagnosed CPA. The higher sensitivity of ELISA makes it essential for surveillance and confirmation, while the RDT's practicality offers a viable option for initial screening in peripheral clinics. These findings underscored the urgent need to integrate CPA diagnosis into routine post-TB care and to strengthen fungal diagnostic capacity in West Africa.
summary: This study documented serological evidence of Aspergillus antibodies in post-tuberculosis patients in Senegal using two diagnostic approaches: enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay and rapid diagnostic tests. The paper reports that ELISA demonstrated higher sensitivity than rapid diagnostic tests for detecting chronic pulmonary aspergillosis, while rapid diagnostic tests offered practical advantages for use in peripheral clinics with limited laboratory infrastructure. The findings indicate a substantial but previously undiagnosed burden of chronic pulmonary aspergillosis among individuals recovering from tuberculosis in West Africa, establishing the need for routine fungal screening integrated into post-tuberculosis care protocols.  The dual diagnostic capacity revealed in this study—where sensitivity and accessibility operate as competing rather than complementary pressures—reflects a material constraint embedded in healthcare systems shaped by colonial legacies and ongoing resource scarcity. The choice between diagnostic precision and logistical feasibility (rapid diagnostic tests) is not a technical problem to be solved but a manifestation of how fungal disease emerges differentially across geography: in Senegal, as in much of the Global South, the same post-tuberculosis patients who face elevated risk of aspergillosis simultaneously face the least reliable means of detection. The fungus itself—thriving in the scarred lungs left behind by tuberculosis—becomes visible only through the diagnostic apparatus available, making the prevalence of undiagnosed chronic pulmonary aspergillosis inseparable from the uneven distribution of laboratory capacity.

title: Fusarium infection in malting barley drives trichothecene transfer and transformation during brewing process, impacting beer safety and composition
source: Latest Results
excerpt: Fusarium contamination in malting barley poses a constant threat of mycotoxin exposure in brewing. However, the chemical transformations of trichothecenes toxins during processing and the broader impact of fungal contamination on beer quality are insufficiently understood. In this study, we inoculated barley grains with Fusarium culmorum to track the behavior of deoxynivalenol (DON) and its conjugates, deoxynivalenol-3-glucoside (DON-3G) and 3-acetyldeoxynivalenol (3-AcDON), through a controlled malting and brewing sequence. Quantitative LC-MS/MS and qPCR analyses revealed extensive fungal activity and toxin formation in malt, reaching 1,054 µg kg⁻¹ DON and 4,352 µg kg⁻¹ DON-3G, respectively. Mashing and lautering facilitated the release of matrix-bound toxins, whereas fermentation represented the key stage for biochemical transformation. During this phase, yeast metabolism markedly increased free DON through the hydrolysis of DON-3G and deacetylation of 3-AcDON. During maturation, a partial re-glycosylation of DON was observed. Despite toxin conversions, the final Fusarium infected batch (FIB) still contained substantial residues of the original toxins load in malt: 99% of DON, 50% of DON-3G, and 84% of 3-AcDON. Fusarium likewise affected chemical and sensory quality of beer: beer from infected kernels showed darker color, elevated soluble nitrogen, and a higher Thiobarbituric Acid Index, pointing toward intensified Maillard reaction activity. Untargeted FT-ICR-MS and LC-ToF-MS analysis confirmed infection-driven shifts in the beer’s molecular composition, revealing elevated Maillard-derived compounds and a distinct biochemical imprint of infection marked by fungal metabolites such as hydroxytrichodiene and host-defense conjugates including cinnamoyl-serotonin derivatives. Our findings show that F. culmorum infection affects both consumer safety of beer and the fundamental (bio)chemistry underlying its composition. Supplementary Information The online version conta
summary: Fusarium culmorum infection of barley grains during malting and brewing produces substantial quantities of trichothecene toxins, with this study documenting deoxynivalenol concentrations reaching 1,054 micrograms per kilogram and deoxynivalenol-3-glucoside reaching 4,352 micrograms per kilogram in malt. Using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry and quantitative polymerase chain reaction, the researchers tracked three forms of the toxin—deoxynivalenol, deoxynivalenol-3-glucoside, and 3-acetyldeoxynivalenol—through controlled malting and brewing sequences, finding that fermentation was the critical stage where yeast metabolism converted bound toxin forms into free deoxynivalenol through hydrolysis and deacetylation. Despite these chemical transformations during processing, the final infected beer retained 99 percent of the original deoxynivalenol load, 50 percent of deoxynivalenol-3-glucoside, and 84 percent of 3-acetyldeoxynivalenol from the contaminated malt. The fungal infection also altered beer's sensory and chemical properties, producing darker color, elevated soluble nitrogen, and increased markers of the Maillard reaction, with molecular analysis revealing fungal metabolites and plant defense compounds absent in uninfected batches.  Fusarium contamination thus operates simultaneously as a safety hazard and as a driver of chemical transformation that reshapes beer's fundamental composition. The toxins do not disappear through processing but rather shift between bound and free forms, with yeast metabolism paradoxically liberating the most bioavailable form of the toxin even as it performs its intended fermentation work. This entanglement—where the organism that produces flavor and alcohol also mobilizes toxins into their most accessible state—reveals how fungal presence becomes embedded in the material and sensory properties of the final product, making contamination inseparable from the beer's chemistry itself.

title: Is adherence to plant-based diet associated with higher exposure to mycotoxins?
source: Journal of Exposure Science & Environmental Epidemiology
excerpt: Journal of Exposure Science & Environmental Epidemiology, Published online: 10 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41370-026-00879-2Is adherence to plant-based diet associated with higher exposure to mycotoxins?
summary: This publication is still hot off the press. That means the paper is not yet indexed by global databases like CrossRef, Semantic Scholar or OpenAlex. If you want to learn what the research might have to say about life on Planet Mould you'll just have to read it yourself!

title: Biological dynamics and application prospects of Citri Reticulatae Pericarpium (CRP): a medicine and food perspective review
source: Frontiers in Pharmacology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: Citri Reticulatae Pericarpium (CRP) is a plant widely used in medicine and food in China, offering extensive health-promoting benefits. Consequently, it plays a pivotal role in the healthcare sector and exhibits a variety of clinical effects, including anti-inflammatory, anticancer, cardiovascular protective, intestinal regulatory, and lipid-lowering activities. Its efficacy is attributed to a diverse range of chemical components. However, variations in geographical origin, storage duration, aging time, and harvesting period can lead to changes in these chemical constituents, thereby influencing the final flavor and commercial value of CRP. Currently, research in this field remains relatively fragmented. Therefore, this review summarizes the dynamic changes and mechanisms of action of the major bioactive metabolites in CRP based on these different factors. Concurrently, it analyzes the microbial activity and safety of the dominant strain, Aspergillus spp., and elaborates on the development and application prospects of CRP-derived products. The objective is to enhance the medicinal value of CRP in clinical therapy, explore its optimal flavor profile for food applications, and deepen
summary: This paper reviews Citri Reticulatae Pericarpium, a plant material used in Chinese medicine and food, documenting its reported anti-inflammatory, anticancer, cardiovascular protective, intestinal regulatory, and lipid-lowering effects. The study identifies chemical composition as the mechanism underlying these clinical activities and notes that geographical origin, storage duration, aging time, and harvesting period all alter the chemical profile and thus the flavor and commercial value of the material. The paper examines the microbial activity and safety profile of Aspergillus species, the dominant fungal strain associated with the material, and discusses development and application prospects for products derived from this plant.  Within the material transformations of food and medicine production, this paper documents a case where a fungal presence—Aspergillus species—becomes inseparable from the value chain of a widely consumed plant material. The same storage conditions and aging processes that generate desirable chemical changes and commercial value also enable fungal colonization and metabolic activity, making microbial safety assessment a necessary counterpart to efficacy claims. The paper thus registers a tension between the therapeutic and commercial promise of aged plant material and the microbial liabilities that accompany the temporal and environmental conditions required to produce that promise.

title: High-throughput detection, dietary exposure assessment, and producing Fungi of 16 masked mycotoxins in dried longan in Asia
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Research International
excerpt: Dried longan is highly vulnerable to fungal infection and masked mycotoxin (MM) contamination, posing a potential health risk for consumers. However, limited data are available on the co-occurrence, dietary exposure assessment, and fungal sources of MMs in this product across Asia. In this study, a high-throughput analytical method based on ultra-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS/MS) was developed and validated for the simultaneous detection of 16 MMs in 90 dried longan samples collected from major Asian producing regions. Results showed that the optimized UPLC-MS/MS method exhibited a good linear relationship ranging from 5 to 800 ng/mL with correlation coefficients R<sup>2</sup> above 0.99026. Twelve MMs were 100% positively detected in all samples. The maxima of neosolaniol (NEO), 3-acetyl-deoxynivalenol (3ADON), and 15-acetyl-deoxynivalenol (15ADON) in all samples were 3346.42, 1768.67, and 971.91 μg/kg, respectively. Dietary exposure assessment indicated that the hazard index (HI) of all MMs was 65.382% (100% threshold), indicating a relatively low risk for consumers in the five surveyed Asian countries (China, Thailand, Vietnam, Malaysia, and Myanmar). Fungal community and correlation analysis further revealed that Fusarium croci was significantly (P < 0.05, Pearson r = 0.99) positive with NEO, 3ADON, and 15ADON, suggesting it as a potential producer of these mycotoxins. This work provided a comprehensive understanding of the contamination of MMs in dried longan.
summary: Dried longan samples collected across five Asian countries—China, Thailand, Vietnam, Malaysia, and Myanmar—tested positive for twelve distinct masked mycotoxins when analysed using ultra-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry. The analytical method developed in this study achieved reliable detection across a concentration range of 5 to 800 nanograms per millilitre, with correlation coefficients above 0.99026. Three masked mycotoxins reached particularly high levels: neosolaniol at 3346.42 micrograms per kilogram, 3-acetyl-deoxynivalenol at 1768.67 micrograms per kilogram, and 15-acetyl-deoxynivalenol at 971.91 micrograms per kilogram. Dietary exposure assessment across the surveyed regions produced a hazard index of 65.382 percent, indicating relatively low consumer risk. Fungal community analysis identified Fusarium croci as a significant producer of the three most abundant masked mycotoxins, with a strong positive correlation (Pearson r = 0.99, P < 0.05).  The contamination pattern documented in this paper reveals how a single fungal species operates simultaneously as a pathogenic threat to dried longan and as a metabolic agent producing compounds that persist through food processing and consumption. The widespread presence of masked mycotoxins—chemical forms that escape standard detection and regulatory frameworks—across multiple Asian supply chains suggests that contamination risk is distributed unevenly across producing regions and consumer populations, shaped by local agricultural conditions, storage infrastructure, and the economic pressures that determine which crops receive fungal management investment.

title: Otogenic Aspergillus Brain Abscess in an SLE teen: Successful case management from Eastern India
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Diagnostic Microbiology and Infectious Disease
excerpt: Neuroaspergillosis is a rare, high-mortality complication in Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE). We report an otogenic Aspergillus fumigatus brain abscess in a 17-year-old female with SLE and seizure disorder, managed with immunosuppressants. She presented with altered sensorium and hemiparesis. Despite initial aspiration and combination antifungal therapy (voriconazole and liposomal amphotericin B), clinical worsening demanding radical excision. Culture and sequencing confirmed A. fumigatus, likely spread via chronic otitis media. The patient recovered and was discharged on oral posaconazole, remaining healthy at two-month follow-up. This case underscores the risk of otogenic fungal spread in SLE and demonstrates that timely neurosurgical intervention combined with prolonged antifungal therapy can ensure survival in otherwise lethal cerebral aspergillosis.
summary: This paper documents a rare case of brain abscess caused by Aspergillus fumigatus in a 17-year-old female with Systemic Lupus Erythematosus and seizure disorder. The patient presented with altered consciousness and partial paralysis; imaging and clinical findings indicated a brain abscess that had likely spread from chronic infection in the middle ear. Initial treatment with antifungal drugs (voriconazole and liposomal amphotericin B) and needle aspiration proved insufficient, requiring surgical removal of the abscess. Culture and genetic sequencing confirmed Aspergillus fumigatus as the causative organism, and the patient recovered following surgery and prolonged oral antifungal therapy with posaconazole, remaining stable at two-month follow-up.  The case reveals how immunosuppression—in this instance driven by autoimmune disease rather than cancer treatment or genetic deficiency—creates pathways for environmental moulds to establish themselves in normally protected tissue. The fungus did not arrive as an acute invader but as a slow colonizer, exploiting damaged ear tissue before crossing into the brain; this temporal and spatial progression suggests that the same organism circulating harmlessly in hospital air or soil becomes a lethal threat when the body's defences are compromised by chronic inflammatory disease. The successful outcome depended not on antifungal drugs alone but on their combination with invasive surgery—a reminder that in the Anthropocene, survival in fungal infections often requires material intervention at multiple scales, from the cellular to the surgical, and that the boundary between pathogen and patient is negotiated through repeated interventions rather than resolved by treatment alone.

title: Successful treatment of pulmonary mucormycosis caused by Rhizopus arrhizus in a diabetic patient with combined bronchoscopic local and systemic antifungal therapy: A case report and literature review
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Diagnostic Microbiology and Infectious Disease
excerpt:
Objective

To present the clinical course and therapeutic outcome of a diabetic patient with bilateral pulmonary mucormycosis (PM) caused by Rhizopus arrhizus, successfully managed by a combined systemic and bronchoscopic local antifungal therapy.

Methods

A 52-year-old male with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presented with persistent fever, cough, and chest pain. Chest CT revealed bilateral lower lobe consolidations with cavitation. Bronchoscopy showed endobronchial obstruction by necrotic material. Diagnosis was established via rapid on-site evaluation (ROSE), histopathology, and targeted next-generation sequencing (tNGS) of bronchoalveolar lavage fluid, which identified Rhizopus arrhizus.

Results

Following an unsatisfactory response to initial intravenous amphotericin B monotherapy, a triple-modality regimen was initiated: continuous intravenous infusion of liposomal amphotericin B, oral isavuconazole, and bi-weekly bronchoscopic instillation of amphotericin B cholesteryl sulfate complex directly into the affected segment. This combined approach led to rapid symptom resolution, radiographic improvement, and patent bronchi on follow-up bronchoscopy. The patient remained well during long-term follow-up.

Conclusion

This case demonstrates the potential efficacy of an integrated strategy combining early tNGS-guided diagnosis, stringent glycemic control, and a synergistic systemic-local antifungal regimen for bilateral PM in a non-surgical candidate. While the relative contribution of each intervention cannot be determined from a single report, the successful outcome suggests that this triple-modality approach may be a viable option for selected patients with diffuse, non-surgical PM. It underscores the potential of bronchoscopic local therapy to enhance drug delivery and warrants further investigation through larger case series or registries.
summary: This paper documents a case of bilateral lung infection caused by Rhizopus arrhizus in a 52-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes. The infection presented with fever, cough, and chest pain; chest imaging showed consolidations with cavitation in both lower lobes, and bronchoscopy revealed blockage of airways by dead-tissue material. Diagnosis was confirmed through rapid on-site evaluation, tissue examination, and targeted next-generation sequencing of fluid collected from the lower airways, which identified the fungus. Initial treatment with intravenous amphotericin B alone proved insufficient, so the clinical team shifted to a three-part strategy: continuous intravenous infusion of liposomal amphotericin B, oral isavuconazole, and twice-weekly direct instillation of amphotericin B cholesteryl sulfate complex into affected lung segments via bronchoscope. This combined approach produced rapid improvement in symptoms, radiographic findings, and airway patency, with sustained clinical stability during follow-up.  The case illustrates how a fungal pathogen emerges from the material conditions of metabolic dysregulation—poorly controlled diabetes creates the physiological terrain where Rhizopus establishes itself—yet simultaneously becomes legible and actionable only through technological mediation: genetic sequencing identifies the specific organism, while bronchoscopic intervention creates a direct conduit for antifungal delivery to the site of infection. The success here depends not on eliminating the fungus from the patient's body alone, but on concentrating pharmaceutical force at the exact location of fungal colonization, revealing how treatment efficacy is bound up with the capacity to access and manipulate the infected tissue itself. This localized therapeutic strategy, applied to a patient deemed unsuitable for surgical removal, suggests that managing fungal infections in the body increasingly means managing them through precise spatial targeting rather than systemic elimination.

title: Smoldering of crop residues emits a large amount of intermediate volatility organic compounds to the atmosphere
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Journal of Hazardous Materials
excerpt: Intermediate volatility organic compounds (IVOCs) released from biomass combustion serve as significant precursors for secondary organic aerosol (SOA). As a biomass fuel with distinctive usage characteristics in China, combustion of crop residues under smoldering conditions still lacks quantitative investigation. In this study, emissions from the smoldering combustion of three typical crop residues (corn, rice, and wheat straw) in China were investigated in controlled smoldering experiments, using a FUSION proton transfer reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometry (FUSION PTR-TOF-MS) for online high-resolution detection of non-methane organic gases (NMOGs). A total of 97 NMOGs were identified, with IVOCs exhibiting emission factors (EFs) significantly higher than those reported under flaming or flaming-smoldering mixed combustion conditions. The contribution of IVOCs to the secondary organic aerosol formation potential (SOAFP) ranged from 35.2% to 55.1%, underscoring their role as dominant precursors of SOA. Moreover, model-predicted toxicity probabilities of the emitted IVOCs indicated a non-negligible potential for adverse health effects, emphasizing the importance of addressing crop residue smoldering. These findings highlight the critical role of IVOCs emitted during the smoldering of crop residues and suggest that IVOC emissions should be explicitly considered in assessments of the atmospheric and health impacts of crop residue burning.
summary: Smoldering combustion of crop residues—corn, wheat, and rice straw—releases intermediate volatility organic compounds at substantially higher rates than flaming or mixed combustion conditions, according to controlled experiments documented in this paper. Using online mass spectrometry detection, researchers identified 97 non-methane organic gases across the three crop types, with intermediate volatility organic compounds accounting for 35.2 to 55.1 percent of the secondary organic aerosol formation potential. Emission factors for intermediate volatility organic compounds exceeded those previously reported under other combustion modes, establishing smoldering as a distinctive source pathway. Toxicity modelling of the detected intermediate volatility organic compounds indicated measurable potential for adverse health effects from exposure to these emissions.  The material specificity of this finding—that smoldering of agricultural residues generates intermediate volatility organic compounds at rates diverging from other combustion pathways—reveals how the same agricultural material operates simultaneously as waste disposal solution and atmospheric pollutant precursor. The combustion conditions themselves, shaped by the practical constraints of field burning in China, produce chemical signatures that do not map onto existing emissions inventories built from flaming or mixed combustion data, exposing a gap between how crop residues are actually burned and how their atmospheric impacts are currently modelled and regulated.

title: Polydopamine surface-imprinted and chitosan-polyethyleneimine-scaffolded COF aerogel for efficient aflatoxins removal from infant foods
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Chemistry
excerpt: Aflatoxin contamination poses a serious threat to infant health, yet selective enrichment and separation of trace aflatoxins from complex food matrices remains challenging. Herein, we report a polydopamine surface-imprinted chitosan-polyethyleneimine-scaffolded COF aerogel (Fe₃O₄@COF@MIP-AG) featuring large pores, specific binding sites, and a unique medium-blocking effect that enhances resistance to matrix macromolecules and competing molecules. Adsorption follows pseudo-second-order kinetics and Freundlich isotherms, indicating chemisorption-dominated, heterogeneous multilayer adsorption. Notably, the material combines robust mechanical stability with ultrafast kinetics, making it highly suitable for solid-phase extraction (SPE). Density functional theory (DFT) confirmed the thermodynamic stability of the aflatoxin-adsorbent complex and directly links the gel's adsorption configuration to its high selectivity for target analytes. This work establishes a generalizable design strategy for adjustable-performance aerogels and offers mechanistic insights that support the development of highly specific aflatoxin enrichment technologies.
summary: Infant foods contaminated with aflatoxins—toxic fungal metabolites—present a detection problem because the target toxins exist in trace amounts within complex food matrices where other molecules interfere with separation. This paper describes a composite material composed of iron oxide magnetic nanoparticles, a covalent organic framework, and a polydopamine-imprinted polymer layer scaffolded with chitosan and polyethyleneimine (Fe₃O₄@COF@MIP-AG). The material features large pores and specific binding sites that selectively capture aflatoxins while resisting interference from macromolecules and competing compounds. Adsorption follows pseudo-second-order kinetics and Freundlich isotherms, indicating that aflatoxin molecules bind through chemical interactions across multiple layers of the material surface rather than through simple surface coating. Computational modelling confirmed the thermodynamic stability of the aflatoxin-adsorbent complex and linked the material's three-dimensional structure to its selectivity. The aerogel combines mechanical durability with rapid binding kinetics, making it suitable for solid-phase extraction—a laboratory technique that concentrates target compounds from samples before analysis.  The material embodies a characteristic tension of remediation technologies in food safety: it is engineered specifically to remove a fungal toxin that threatens vulnerable populations, yet its design depends on the same fungal metabolite as the target that justifies its existence. The selectivity of the imprinted polymer—its capacity to recognize and bind aflatoxins among thousands of food components—emerges not from the material's inherent properties but from the deliberate imprinting of aflatoxin's molecular shape into the polymer surface. This means the technology's effectiveness is calibrated to a particular hazard within a particular food system, revealing how remediation strategies are always situated within specific circuits of contamination and consumption.

title: AIE-active UiO-66@PtNPs nanozyme-amplified fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal magnetic immunosensing platform for effective detection of aflatoxin B1 in food products
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Chemistry
excerpt: Highly toxic aflatoxin B<sub>1</sub> (AFB<sub>1</sub>) frequently contaminates food products, posing a serious threat to human health. Developing accurate, efficient and user-friendly detection strategies is essential. This study synthesized a novel H<sub>4</sub>TCPE-UiO-66@PtNPs nanozyme with stable fluorescence and catalytic properties through integrating H<sub>4</sub>TCPE ligands possessing aggregation-induced luminescence (AIE) properties with in-situ grown PtNPs, and further developed a fluorescence-colorimetric dual-signal immunosensing platform for the detection of AFB<sub>1</sub> contamination in food products. Utilizing an immunocompetitive reaction and magnetic separation techniques, the platform enabled quantitative analysis of AFB<sub>1</sub>, achieving favorable linear range of 0.05-100 ng/mL and high sensitivity (fluorescence LOD: 0.022 ng/mL, colorimetric LOD: 0.047 ng/mL). Real samples tests yielded satisfactory recoveries (84.6%-110.1%) and good reproducibility (RSD ≤ 5.3%, n = 3), demonstrating the platform's potential for specific identification and screening of AFB<sub>1</sub> in complex matrices. The proposed dual-signal detection strategy provides an effective solution for self-calibrated and rapid analysis of foodborne or environmental pollutants.
summary: This paper describes the synthesis of a composite nanomaterial combining a metal-organic framework (UiO-66) with platinum nanoparticles and fluorescent organic ligands, deployed as a detection platform for aflatoxin B1 contamination in food. The nanomaterial was engineered to exhibit both fluorescent and catalytic properties, enabling dual-signal readout through fluorescence and colorimetric measurements. Using magnetic particle separation and competitive immunoassay principles, the platform quantified aflatoxin B1 across a range of 0.05 to 100 nanograms per milliliter, with detection limits of 0.022 nanograms per milliliter (fluorescence) and 0.047 nanograms per milliliter (colorimetric). Testing on real food samples produced recovery rates between 84.6 and 110.1 percent with reproducibility variation below 5.3 percent across three replicates.  Aflatoxin B1 emerges in this research as both a persistent food safety hazard requiring increasingly sensitive detection infrastructure and a driver of technological accumulation in biosensor development. The dual-signal approach—leveraging both optical and catalytic properties of the engineered nanomaterial—reflects a material strategy for managing detection in complex food matrices where contamination coexists with numerous other compounds. The platform's reliance on magnetic separation to isolate the immunoassay from interfering substances reveals a deeper dependency: the toxin's threat is inseparable from the detection apparatus itself, each demanding refinement as food supply chains remain exposed to mould-derived contamination.

title: Decoding azole resistance mechanisms and pathogenicity in Aspergillus section Fumigati through genomic analysis
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Fungal Genetics and Biology
excerpt: Aspergillus fumigatus is the primary causative agent of aspergillosis. Cryptic species may exhibit variable pathogenic potential and antifungal resistance and, particularly those within the section Fumigati, have significant clinical and environmental relevance. However, the genomic and proteomic bases underlying these differences remain poorly understood. This study presents a comparative analysis of 42 genomes (including five newly sequenced genomes), together with their corresponding predicted proteomes, from A. fumigatus , and from four cryptic species: A. lentulus , A. udagawae , A. felis and A. hiratsukae . Notably, these represent the only genomes currently available for these cryptic species, allowing us to integrate antifungal resistance mechanisms, and virulence factors across the full existing genomic landscape. We showed that A. fumigatus retains a highly conserved core proteome, whereas A. felis and A. lentulus exhibit greater genomic plasticity. Our preliminary findings suggest that azole resistance is primarily driven by species-specific point mutations, which are not shared across the section Fumigati . Secondary metabolism pathways are the main difference observed among section Fumigati , with variations in biosynthetic gene clusters and mycotoxin production. Our findings emphasize the evolutionary balance between genomic conservation and evolutionary divergence in A. fumigatus sensu lato, determining azole resistance and pathogenicity-associated fitness. • Comparative genomics uncovers azole resistance diversity in Fumigati species. • Species-specific point mutations drive azole resistance across Fumigati. • Distinct secondary metabolite clusters define cryptic species diversity. • Core proteome functional composition remains similar among species.
summary: This study presents a comparative genomic analysis of 42 Aspergillus fumigatus genomes alongside four cryptic species within the section Fumigati (A. lentulus, A. udagawae, A. felis, and A. hiratsukae), including five newly sequenced genomes. The research examined both predicted protein sequences and genetic sequences to identify the mechanisms underlying antifungal resistance and disease-causing potential across these species. The analysis revealed that A. fumigatus maintains a highly conserved set of core proteins shared across strains, while A. felis and A. lentulus show greater genomic flexibility and variation. Azole resistance in this fungal group is driven primarily by point mutations that are specific to individual species and not shared across the broader section Fumigati. The most significant differences among these species lie in secondary metabolism pathways—the genetic clusters that direct the production of toxins and specialized compounds—suggesting that each cryptic species has evolved distinct chemical arsenals.  Within the material assemblage of clinical and environmental fungal populations, this study documents how resistance and pathogenic capacity are not universal traits but rather emerge through species-specific genetic solutions to shared selective pressures. The genomic plasticity observed in some cryptic species, combined with the conservation of core functions in A. fumigatus, reveals an ecological tension: the same fungal lineages that pose standardized clinical threats through conserved disease-causing ability mechanisms simultaneously develop localized, non-transferable resistance strategies that fragment the landscape of antifungal vulnerability. This pattern suggests that control efforts targeting resistance mechanisms in the dominant species may leave adjacent species—already present in clinical and built environments—positioned to exploit therapeutic gaps.

title: Methyltransferase CclA-dependent control of secondary metabolite gene clusters and stress response in aspergillus nidulans
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Fungal Genetics and Biology
excerpt: Aspergillus species generate diverse secondary metabolites (SMs), including commercially valuable compounds and mycotoxins that affect food safety. The biosynthesis of these SMs is regulated through gene clusters controlled by epigenetic and transcriptional mechanisms. However, the function of the histone H3 lysine 4 (H3K4) methyltransferase CclA across different metabolic stages remains poorly understood during the metabolic switch from primary to secondary metabolism. We performed integrated transcriptomic, proteomic, and RT-qPCR analyses of a ΔcclA mutant and wild type Aspergillus nidulans during late exponential growth phase (20h) where primary metabolism (PM) is predominant and stationary phase (48 h) where secondary metabolism (SM) is predominant. At the PM stage, 2971 genes (~25% of the genome) were upregulated in ΔcclA, including SM clusters for sterigmatocystin (ST), monodictyphenone, emodin, orsellinic acid, austinol, and emericellamides, as well as genes involved in xylan metabolism and oxidative stress response. Conversely, genes controlling cell wall biosynthesis, filamentation, and sexual development were downregulated. At the SM stage, 2798 genes (~24% of the genome) were upregulated, but ST and terrequinone clusters were markedly downregulated, revealing CclA's dual role as a global repressor and selective activator of SMs. Proteomic assessment confirmed coherent expression patterns (Pearson r = 0.71-0.77) with transcriptomic data, and RT-qPCR validated important regulatory and SM genes (r = 0.9). Phenotypic tests demonstrated that ΔcclA exhibited improved resistance to oxidative, osmotic, and cell wall stress while maintaining conidiation and growth. Overall, these results identify CclA as a principal regulator of SM, development, and stress adaptation. Understanding its dual functions gives a foundation for reducing mycotoxin contamination and enhancing the production of valuable metabolites.
summary: Aspergillus nidulans produces a range of secondary metabolites—compounds made beyond those needed for basic growth—including sterigmatocystin (a mycotoxin) and commercially useful substances like monodictyphenone and emodin. This paper examined how a protein called CclA, which adds chemical tags to histone proteins to control gene activity, regulates the switch from primary metabolism (basic growth functions) to secondary metabolism (specialized compound production). The researchers compared a mutant strain lacking CclA with normal fungus during two growth phases: early stationary phase at 20 hours when primary metabolism dominates, and late stationary phase at 48 hours when secondary metabolism dominates. During primary metabolism, the CclA-deficient mutant activated approximately 2,971 genes (about 25 percent of its genome), including clusters for sterigmatocystin, monodictyphenone, emodin, orsellinic acid, austinol, and emericellamides, alongside genes involved in breaking down plant material and responding to oxidative stress; simultaneously, genes controlling cell wall construction, fungal branching, and sexual reproduction were suppressed. During secondary metabolism, roughly 2,798 genes (24 percent of the genome) were activated in the mutant, but notably the sterigmatocystin and terrequinone clusters were reduced, revealing that CclA both suppresses and selectively activates different secondary metabolite pathways depending on growth stage. Protein measurements confirmed these gene expression patterns, and the CclA-deficient mutant showed enhanced survival under oxidative, salt, and cell wall stress while maintaining normal spore production and growth.  The duality embedded in CclA's function mirrors the material entanglement characteristic of moulds in turbulent times: the same regulatory protein that prevents mycotoxin accumulation during certain growth phases simultaneously unleashes the production of other secondary metabolites and stress-response pathways. This reveals how fungal metabolism is not a fixed hierarchy but a contingent arrangement shaped by epigenetic control—the same molecular machinery that could reduce food contamination through sterigmatocystin suppression also determines whether valuable industrial compounds are produced or withheld. The mutant's enhanced stress tolerance alongside altered metabolite profiles suggests that attempts to engineer safer or more productive Aspergillus strains through epigenetic manipulation will necessarily reshape the organism's entire metabolic portfolio and its capacity to survive in hostile material conditions.

title: Environmentally friendly physical treatment strategies against spoilage molds in the food industry
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Fungal Biology Reviews
excerpt: This review explores physical treatment methods for the inactivation of spoilage molds in the food industry. Focus is placed on sustainable, chemical-free technologies that offer safe, effective, and environmentally friendly solutions for improving food safety and extending shelf life. A total of eight physical treatment categories were evaluated for their antifungal activity and inactivation mechanisms, namely ultraviolet light, pulsed light, gamma irradiation, microwave, ultrasound, cold plasma, high pressure, and pulsed electric fields. Ultraviolet and pulsed light treatments demonstrate promising results in rapidly inactivating fungal spores on surfaces as well as in liquids. Gamma irradiation offers a reliable means of achieving deep penetration and extended shelf life. Microwave and ultrasound technologies provide innovative approaches to disrupt fungal cell structures. Cold plasma and high-pressure treatments emerge as alternative options, capable of inactivating a broad spectrum of spoilage fungi without compromising food integrity while pulsed electric fields enhance permeability in fungal cells, leading to effective inactivation. The use of different physical treatments having different modes of actions could be of interest in agreement with the “hurdle” technology concept. Finally, a critical evaluation of the advantages and limitations of these approaches serves as a base for exploring new research perspectives. The comparative analysis revealed that while ultraviolet and pulsed light are the most cost-effective and rapid for surface decontamination, High-Pressure Processing (HPP) and cold plasma offer the best efficacy for preserving organoleptic quality.
