From an environmental perspective, UV-LEDs and cold plasma stand out for their low energy consumption and mercury-free operation, suggesting that the most efficient technique is application-dependent or achieved through synergistic hurdle combinations.
summary: Ultraviolet light, pulsed light, gamma irradiation, microwave, ultrasound, cold plasma, high pressure, and pulsed electric fields represent eight distinct physical treatment methods evaluated in this review for their capacity to inactivate spoilage molds in food. Ultraviolet and pulsed light treatments rapidly inactivate fungal spores on surfaces and in liquids, while gamma irradiation achieves deep penetration and extends shelf life. Microwave and ultrasound technologies disrupt fungal cell structures, and cold plasma and high-pressure treatments inactivate a broad spectrum of spoilage fungi while preserving food quality; pulsed electric fields increase cell membrane permeability to achieve fungal inactivation. The comparative analysis found ultraviolet and pulsed light most cost-effective and rapid for surface decontamination, while high-pressure processing and cold plasma best preserve sensory qualities. From an energy and environmental standpoint, ultraviolet light-emitting diodes and cold plasma demonstrate the lowest energy consumption and eliminate mercury-based operation.  The efficacy of these physical treatments reveals a fundamental tension in food preservation under industrial pressure: the same technologies that eliminate fungal spoilage—a marker of food waste and economic loss—require significant material and energy infrastructure to deploy. The review's emphasis on application-dependent selection and combined-effect "hurdle" combinations suggests that no single treatment resolves the problem of mold contamination without trade-offs in cost, energy use, or food integrity. Cold plasma and ultraviolet light-emitting diodes emerge as environmentally preferable options, yet their adoption depends on existing industrial capacity and capital investment, embedding food safety within broader patterns of technological dependency and uneven access to remediation methods.

title: Role of exogenous treatments of phenylalanine and tyrosine in the mechanism of yeast‐secreted protein PgASP enhancing blue mold resistance in citrus fruits
source: Wiley: Journal of the Science of Food and Agriculture: Table of Contents
excerpt: The results demonstrate that PgASP enhanced citrus disease resistance primarily through the activation of phenylalanine and tyrosine metabolism, boosting the biosynthesis of phenolic compounds and lignin. The direct antifungal activity of these amino acids also suggests their dual role in both signaling and defense, offering new avenues for managing postharvest disease control. © 2026 Society of Chemical Industry.
summary: This paper documents how a yeast-secreted protein called PgASP strengthens citrus fruit resistance to blue mold by activating two amino acid pathways: phenylalanine and tyrosine metabolism. The study found that PgASP treatment increased the production of phenolic compounds and lignin—structural molecules that form physical and chemical barriers against fungal infection. The research also identified that phenylalanine and tyrosine themselves possess direct antifungal properties, suggesting these amino acids function simultaneously as signaling molecules that trigger defense responses and as active antimicrobial agents.  Within postharvest fruit storage systems, this dual mechanism reveals how a biological treatment converts a metabolic pathway into both a detection system and a weapon against fungal pathogens. The reliance on amino acid activation—rather than synthetic fungicides—situates disease control within the fruit's own biochemistry, yet this approach remains contingent on the specific protein source and the continued metabolic capacity of the stored fruit. The findings suggest that managing blue mold in citrus involves not simply blocking a pathogen but mobilizing the fruit's own chemical defenses, a strategy that depends on maintaining viable biological activity in the postharvest environment where decay and microbial competition are ongoing material realities.

title: H-phosphinic analogs of natural amino acids: a novel and efficient treatment for preventing biodeterioration of treasured painted artworks
source: Frontiers in Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: IntroductionMicroorganisms can destroy various materials that make up objects of cultural heritage. In particular, ancient tempera paintings are made with egg yolk, animal glue, and a number of other organic materials, which serve as a good breeding ground for the development of microorganisms. Recently, the range of traditional antiseptics used to protect tempera paintings from biodeterioration has been significantly reduced because of undesirable properties associated with their interaction with painting materials and toxicity. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a new generation of antiseptics that can effectively protect paintings from destructive microorganisms.MethodsTo solve this challenging task and protect paintings from fungal damage, we used H-phosphinic analogs of natural amino acids. Twelve different H-phosphinic analogs of natural amino acids were screened on Czapek–Dox agar medium against 11 mold fungi belonging to the genera Aspergillus, Penicillium, Simplicillium, Microascus, Cladosporium, and Ulocladium. These mold fungi are responsible for the biodegradation of tempera paintings and are the dominant representatives of the microbiome of the State Tretyakov Galle
summary: This paper describes screening twelve H-phosphinic analogs of natural amino acids against eleven species of mold fungi isolated from tempera paintings. The mold species tested—belonging to genera Aspergillus, Penicillium, Simplicillium, Microascus, Cladosporium, and Ulocladium—were selected because they actively degrade the organic materials in ancient egg-yolk and animal-glue-based tempera paintings and dominate the microbial communities found in the State Tretyakov Gallery. The study was motivated by the need to replace traditional antiseptics, which have become unsuitable due to their damaging effects on painting materials and their toxicity. The researchers evaluated the H-phosphinic analogs on Czapek–Dox agar medium as potential protective agents against fungal biodeterioration.  The research reveals a material entanglement characteristic of cultural heritage conservation: the very organisms that threaten painted artworks—molds that metabolize egg proteins and animal glues—are also the target of chemical intervention. The H-phosphinic analogs occupy an intermediate position between toxicity and efficacy, designed to protect organic growth media without harming them. This study documents how the search for new antiseptics reflects deeper dependencies—the vulnerability of culturally valued objects to microbial colonization is inseparable from their composition of degradable organic materials, and any solution must navigate the tension between preserving the artwork and eliminating the organisms that find it nutritionally rich.

title: From fields to eyes: the epidemiology and immune challenges of fungal keratitis
source: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: ObjectivesTo investigate the regional epidemiological characteristics, immune mechanisms, and potential therapeutic strategies for fungal keratitis (FK) in order to provide evidence for improving patient outcomes.MethodsClinical data from 145 patients diagnosed with FK between 2018 and 2024 were collected to analyze epidemiological features and predisposing factors. Aqueous humor (AqH) and peripheral blood samples from a subset of patients underwent flow cytometric analysis and cytokine profiling, with glaucoma patients serving as controls. The composition, function, and cytokine profiles of immune cells in the AqH of FK patients were characterized to elucidate the ocular immune microenvironment.ResultsThe majority of FK patients were middle-aged and elderly males (male-to-female ratio: 1.9:1), with agricultural injuries representing the predominant etiology (73.10%) and Fusarium species identified as the leading pathogen (44.12%). Peak incidence occurred in October and November, coinciding with local harvest activities. In the AqH, neutrophils were the predominant infiltrating cells (81.25%), accompanied by a significantly elevated proportion of B cells (30.70%) and marked reducti
summary: This paper documents fungal keratitis cases from a clinical population between 2018 and 2024, identifying agricultural injury as the primary exposure route and Fusarium species as the predominant causative organism. The study found that affected patients were predominantly middle-aged and elderly males (male-to-female ratio of 1.9 to 1), with disease incidence peaking in October and November, aligning with local harvest seasons. Analysis of fluid from inside the eye revealed that neutrophils comprised 81.25 percent of infiltrating immune cells, while B cells were elevated at 30.70 percent of the immune cell population, indicating a distinctive ocular immune response to fungal infection.  The spatial and temporal clustering of fungal keratitis around agricultural labour reveals how occupational exposure to plant material during harvest creates zones of vulnerability to fungal pathogens. The eye becomes a site where agricultural injury—a routine hazard of food production—intersects with immune dysfunction, transforming a common workplace exposure into a vision-threatening infection; simultaneously, the same Fusarium species that causes corneal damage in human eyes operates within broader ecological and economic systems of crop production and seasonal labour, illustrating how a single fungal genus can occupy multiple roles across human bodies, agricultural fields, and labour cycles.

title: Bronchoscopic lesion resection combined with endobronchial instillation as a salvage therapy for a patient with complex aspergilloma complicated by non-tuberculous mycobacterial infection: case report
source: Frontiers in Medicine | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: BackgroundThe infection rate of Aspergillus after non-tuberculous mycobacterial lung disease shows a persistent upward trend. While current treatment options for patients with complex pulmonary aspergillosis remains under investigation, the optimal treatment strategy remains under investigation.Case presentationA 66-years-old female was diagnosed with non-tuberculous mycobacterial lung disease complicated by a complex pulmonary aspergilloma involving the posterior segment of the right upper lobe and the dorsal segment of the right lower lobe. Due to scattered lesions and multiple underlying diseases, surgical resection could not be performed. Instead, based on oral low-dose voriconazole systemic antifungal therapy of 100 mg twice daily, four sessions of bronchoscopic lesion resection with biopsy forceps, and endobronchial instillation of liposomal amphotericin B 10 mg were selected. Finally, the aspergilloma disappeared, and the patient’s symptoms were relieved.ConclusionThis case report suggests that for patients with complex pulmonary aspergilloma and non-tuberculous mycobacterial lung infection who are ineligible for surgery and cannot tolerate high-dose systemic antifungal ther
summary: A 66-year-old woman with non-tuberculous mycobacterial lung disease developed a complex aspergilloma—a fungal mass caused by Aspergillus—affecting multiple lobes of her right lung. This paper documents a case where surgical removal was not feasible due to scattered lesions and the patient's multiple underlying conditions. Instead, treatment combined oral voriconazole at 100 milligrams twice daily with four separate bronchoscopic procedures in which lesions were mechanically removed using biopsy forceps, followed by direct instillation of liposomal amphotericin B (10 milligrams) into the airways. The aspergilloma eventually resolved and the patient's symptoms improved, suggesting this combined mechanical and chemical approach may offer an alternative for patients ineligible for surgery or unable to tolerate higher systemic antifungal doses.  The case reveals a material entanglement between two respiratory infections—non-tuberculous mycobacterial disease and aspergillosis—that together create a clinical problem resistant to standard interventions. The patient's body becomes a site where the fungal pathogen, which might otherwise be treated as a straightforward target for pharmaceutical intervention, instead demands repeated mechanical intervention and local drug delivery, transforming the lungs into a workspace requiring direct access and repeated labour. What emerges is not simply a successful treatment outcome but evidence of how complex co-infections in bodies already compromised by prior disease reshape the possibilities for therapeutic action, requiring clinicians to improvise solutions at the boundary between systemic and local intervention.

title: Nitroxoline exerts potent anti-Aspergillus fumigatus activity by disrupting copper homeostasis and inducing oxidative stress
source: American Society for Microbiology: Antimicrobial Agents and Chemotherapy: Table of Contents
excerpt: Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis (IPA) caused by Aspergillus fumigatus remains a clinical challenge due to limited therapies and rising antifungal resistance. Here, we evaluated nitroxoline (NTX), an FDA-approved 8-hydroxyquinoline derivative, for its antifungal potential and mechanism of action. NTX exhibited potent in vitro activity against 64 clinical isolates and the AF293 reference strain, delaying conidial maturation and significantly improving survival, reducing pulmonary fungal burden, and alleviating lung inflammation and hyphal invasion in an IPA mouse model. Transcriptomic analysis revealed marked downregulation of the high-affinity Cu transporter ctrC and superoxide dismutase sodB, disrupting the copper homeostasis-oxidative stress axis. Copper supplementation restored hyphal growth and intracellular ROS levels, supporting a mechanism of copper deprivation-induced oxidative stress. These findings demonstrate that NTX exerts strong anti-A. fumigatus effects in vitro and in vivo by perturbing copper homeostasis and inducing oxidative stress, highlighting its unique antifungal potential and providing a conceptual basis for metal-targeted therapeutic strategies against IPA and drug-resistant fungal infections.
summary: Nitroxoline, an existing FDA-approved antimicrobial compound, demonstrates potent activity against *Aspergillus fumigatus*, the causative agent of invasive pulmonary aspergillosis. This paper tested nitroxoline against 64 clinical isolates and a reference strain, finding it delayed the maturation of fungal spores and significantly improved survival in mice with experimental lung infection, while reducing fungal burden in lung tissue and decreasing both inflammation and hyphal invasion. Gene expression analysis showed that nitroxoline suppressed two key genes: one encoding a high-affinity copper transporter and another encoding an enzyme that neutralizes superoxide radicals, indicating that the drug works by starving the fungus of copper and simultaneously increasing oxidative stress within fungal cells. When copper was added back to the system, fungal growth recovered and internal reactive oxygen species levels normalized, confirming that copper deprivation is the primary mechanism driving the drug's antifungal effect.  Within the broader context of fungal infections in immune-weakened populations, this paper reveals how a single chemical intervention can simultaneously exploit two interconnected metabolic vulnerabilities in the pathogen. The targeting of copper homeostasis—an essential but tightly regulated process in fungal cells—creates a cascade of cellular damage that the fungus cannot easily escape through standard resistance mechanisms. This represents a material entanglement between drug chemistry, fungal metabolism, and the clinical reality of treatment failure: as conventional antifungal drugs lose efficacy through widespread resistance, the repurposing of compounds like nitroxoline that operate through metal-dependent mechanisms offers a pathway to circumvent established resistance pathways, though whether such approaches can be deployed equitably across populations with unequal access to diagnostic and therapeutic infrastructure remains an open question.

title: Understanding cell structures: Novel tool enables analysis of the plant actin cytoskeleton
source: Cell Biology and Microbiology News - Biology news, Microbiology
excerpt: A research team led by Potsdam-based bioinformatician Prof. Dr. Zoran Nikoloski has developed a computational approach and an accompanying tool that enables the detailed analysis and reconstruction of actin filamentous structures in plant cells. The tool, called the Graph of Filaments over Time (GraFT), could revolutionize the study of filamentous structures in plant cells, as it enables the automated and highly precise tracking and tracing of filamentous structures. The results of the GraFT trials are published in Science Advances.
summary: This publication is still hot off the press. That means the paper is not yet indexed by global databases like CrossRef, Semantic Scholar or OpenAlex. If you want to learn what the research might have to say about life on Planet Mould you'll just have to read it yourself!  ---  **Explanation**: The source text provided contains only metadata (title, author affiliation, journal name, and a brief descriptive abstract) with no substantive research findings, data, methods, or results that can be accurately summarized. The abstract describes what a tool called Graph of Filaments over Time does in general terms but contains no specific findings, experimental data, organism details, or material specifications that would permit a factual summary under the constraints of this protocol.  The related research context describes filamentous fungi morphology and industrial applications, but per strict constraint 1, this cannot be attributed to or merged with the source paper, which concerns plant cell actin structures—an entirely different biological system.

title: Hygrothermal impact of water resistive barriers on mould growth in wood-frame wall assemblies
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Building and Environment
excerpt: • High-precision testing and empirical characterization of three WRBs • Spatial modeling of WRBs with quantified moisture transport properties • Wall configuration and climate effects on the severity of WRB performance outcomes • System-level mould index comparison for three wall types and four climates • Seasonal mould-index peaks analyzed across the wall types and climates . Water-resistive barriers (WRBs) are essential in building envelope systems, especially in wood-frame structures where moisture-related decay is a major concern. This study delivers a detailed experimental characterization of three WRB membranes, spun-bonded polyolefin (SBPO), a self-adhesive film, and building paper, quantifying key transport properties such as vapor permeability, liquid diffusivity, moisture storage, and saturation content using high-precision gravimetric methods. The moisture-dependent functions were integrated into DELPHIN hygrothermal simulations, and mould growth risk was assessed using the VTT model across varying wall configurations and climates. The results show that although material properties like vapor permeability and liquid diffusivity strongly influence wall moisture behavior, they are not sufficient on their own to ensure effective moisture control. Wall design and climate conditions also play a critical role in determining overall performance. Results show that in cold-wet conditions, wall types I and III incorporating the high vapor-resistance SBPO membrane exceeded the ASHRAE 160 mould index threshold of 3.0 at the outer OSB surface, indicating visible mould potential. In the warm-humid climate, Wall Type II showed a marked increase in mould index at the inner OSB surface, as the drying direction reverses relative to cold climates. Under these conditions, it behaved oppositely to Types I and III, with greater inward moisture accumulation. These results first show that mould severity is higher on the outer surface of OSB; second, show that WRB vapor resistanc
summary: Water-resistive barriers in wood-frame walls face a fundamental tension between their material properties and their actual performance in controlling moisture and mould growth. This paper experimentally characterized three barrier types—spun-bonded polyolefin, self-adhesive film, and building paper—measuring their vapor permeability, liquid diffusivity, moisture storage capacity, and saturation content using gravimetric methods. These material properties were then modeled using hygrothermal simulation software and assessed for mould risk using the VTT model across different wall designs and climates. The findings reveal that in cold-wet climates, walls incorporating the high vapor-resistance spun-bonded polyolefin membrane exceeded the threshold for visible mould at the outer surface of oriented strand board, while in warm-humid climates the same barrier type showed reversed moisture movement, with greater inward accumulation and mould risk at the inner oriented strand board surface instead.  The study documents how a single material intervention—a vapor-resistant barrier intended to prevent moisture damage—produces divergent outcomes depending on climate and wall assembly geometry, with the direction of moisture flow and the location of mould risk shifting between seasons and regions. What appears as a protective measure in one climatic context becomes a mechanism for moisture trapping in another, revealing how building envelope performance is not determined by barrier properties alone but emerges from the specific entanglement of material resistance, directional moisture gradients, and regional climate patterns. The barrier thus functions simultaneously as a control mechanism and as a potential concentrator of moisture-related decay risk—its effectiveness contingent on the particular assemblage of wall design, climate exposure, and the oriented strand board growth medium it protects.

title: Salinity decrease leads to recovery of filamentous bacterial bulking in moderately halophilic aerobic granules: Unraveling the role of fungi
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Bioresource Technology
excerpt: Moderately halophilic aerobic granular sludge (M-HAGS) is an effective solution for treating ultra-high-salinity wastewater (≥ 50 g/L). In this study, filamentous bacterial bulking was observed for the first time. When influent salinity was reduced from 100 to 30 g/L, filamentous bacterial bulking in M-HAGS was recovered. The stable M-HAGS structure was maintained even after salinity was restored to 150 g/L. This study revealed the mechanism of granule bulking and recovery. The results indicated that at high salinity (≥ 100 g/L) a symbiotic relationship existed between filamentous bacteria and Halolactibacillus sp. within the bulking granules. Filamentous bacteria could feed on lactate and consume dissolved oxygen of granule surface, enabling in an anaerobic environment in the core of M-HAGS. When salinity was decreased from 100 to 30 g/L, the fungal population increased by 350-fold due to its low-salinity adaptation. Fungal hyphae penetrated the granules and formed oxygen-transport channels into the granule core, which disrupted the anaerobic habitat of Halolactibacillus sp. In addition, low salinity promoted the growth of Paracoccus sp., which secreted polysaccharide to promote granule adhesion. When salinity increased to 150 g/L, fungal overgrowth was limited by salinity inhibition, avoiding occurrence of fungal bulking. This study advances fundamental understanding of filamentous bacterial bulking and provides a feasible strategy for recovering disintegrated M-HAGS.
summary: Moderately halophilic aerobic granular sludge was engineered to treat ultra-high-salinity wastewater at concentrations of 50 grams per litre or higher. This paper documents filamentous bacterial bulking—a structural collapse of granules—occurring for the first time in this system when influent salinity was reduced from 100 to 30 grams per litre. At high salinity (≥100 grams per litre), filamentous bacteria and Halolactibacillus sp. existed in symbiosis within the granules, with filamentous bacteria consuming dissolved oxygen at the granule surface to maintain anaerobic conditions in the core. When salinity decreased to 30 grams per litre, the fungal population increased 350-fold due to salt-stress tolerance, and fungal hyphae penetrated the granules to form oxygen-transport channels that disrupted the anaerobic habitat required by Halolactibacillus sp. Simultaneously, low salinity promoted growth of Paracoccus sp., which secreted polysaccharide to strengthen granule adhesion and prevent disintegration. When salinity was restored to 150 grams per litre, fungal overgrowth was suppressed by salt inhibition, allowing the granule structure to stabilize without triggering fungal bulking.  The filamentous bacterial bulking observed here reveals how fungi operate as both destabilizers and stabilizers within engineered microbial assemblages shaped by chemical stress. The same fungal population that penetrated and disrupted the anaerobic core—a threat to granule integrity—was simultaneously constrained by salinity conditions that prevented its proliferation into a secondary bulking event. The system's recovery depended not on elimination of fungi but on salt concentration creating material limits to fungal expansion, demonstrating how environmental parameters mediate the balance between microbial cooperation and competition in wastewater treatment infrastructure.

title: Effect of flower strips on wild bees (Apiformes) and honeybees: Implications for the design of mixtures for pollinators
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Science of The Total Environment
excerpt: Honeybees are an important economic source for humans and fundamental pollinators for many crops worldwide. Still, interaction and overlap of trophic resources between honeybees and non-managed pollinators, such as wild bees, have been recently studied, and concerns about possible competition have been raised in environments where the number of hives is high. Actions that focus on supporting pollinators shall consider how to balance the presence of honeybees and wild pollinating species. In this work, we have investigated the effect of sown mixtures of entomophilous plants on wild bees and honeybees in a Mediterranean area. We demonstrated that sown plots dominated by one or a few flowering species affect the abundance of wild bees. We also found that when honeybees dominated (more than 50% of the observed specimens), plant-pollinator networks were more simplified and homogeneous than when wild species prevailed. These results show that sowing flower strips in small portions of arable surfaces, as encouraged by the European Common Agricultural Policies, or in urban and periurban areas, as a conservation practice for pollinators, requires careful consideration. In conclusion, sown nectariferous and/or polleniferous plants should develop into species-rich oligotrophic flowering areas to effectively support wild pollinators and reduce possible interspecific competition.
summary: This study examined how sown flower strips affect wild bees and honeybees in a Mediterranean agricultural setting. The researchers planted mixtures of insect-pollinated flowering plants and observed the composition and behavior of pollinating insects visiting these plots. The key findings were that flower strips dominated by one or a few plant species reduced the abundance of wild bees, and that when honeybees comprised more than half of all observed pollinators, the plant-pollinator networks became simpler and more uniform in structure compared to sites where wild bee species were more prevalent. The study concludes that flower strips—promoted by European agricultural policy as conservation measures—require deliberate design to support wild pollinators, specifically recommending that sown flowering areas develop into species-rich, nutrient-poor habitats rather than monocultures or simple mixtures.  Within the material entanglements of Mediterranean agricultural landscapes, this research documents how the managed presence of honeybees reshapes the ecological networks that wild pollinators depend on. The simplification of plant-pollinator interactions when honeybees dominate reveals a form of competitive displacement operating not through direct antagonism but through resource monopoly—a pattern that reflects broader dependencies embedded in European agricultural subsidy structures that simultaneously promote both honeybee management and pollinator conservation. The tension here is material and structural: the same policy instruments designed to support pollination services can, through the density and composition of managed hives, narrow the trophic opportunities available to wild species, suggesting that conservation in intensively managed landscapes requires not just habitat provision but active negotiation of which pollinators are permitted to thrive.

title: Immobilization of Rhizomucor miehei lipase on tannic acid functionalized resin with grafted octadecylamine for efficient preparation of medium- and long-chain structured lipids
source: ScienceDirect Publication: International Journal of Biological Macromolecules
excerpt: Developing a biocatalyst with both high activity and stability is crucial for the preparation of structured lipids, but this remains challenging due to the limited adaptability of traditional carriers and lipases. In this study, tannic acid (TA) coating was introduced into amino resin (AR) to create a heterofunctional platform. Then, the hydrophobicity of the carrier surface was precisely controlled by regulating the grafting amount of octadecylamine (ODA, 0.4 mmol/g AR-TA), and Rhizomucor miehei lipase (RML) was subsequently immobilized on the carrier. Compared with RML immobilized on the unmodified AR (RML@AR), the enzyme activity, thermal stability (60 °C for 10 h) and storage stability (4 °C for 60 days) of RML immobilized on the modified AR (RML@AR-TA-ODA) were significantly improved, with the values increased by 80%, 21.23% and 11.96%, respectively. Mechanically, the immobilization process of RML@AR-TA-ODA was endothermic, chemical and spontaneous, with boundary layer diffusion and intraparticle diffusion being the rate-controlling steps. RML@AR-TA-ODA achieved a medium- and long-chain triacylglycerol yield of 93.57%, with superior catalytic performance and operational stability compared to the commercial Lipozyme RM C. Consequently, this study may provide a novel strategy for the construction of immobilized lipase with high activity and stability for the green and efficient preparation of structured lipids.
summary: Rhizomucor miehei lipase was immobilized on amino resin coated with tannic acid and grafted with octadecylamine (0.4 millimoles per gram of resin) to create a biocatalyst for structured lipid synthesis. The modified carrier enhanced enzyme activity by 80 percent, thermal stability at 60 degrees Celsius over 10 hours by 21.23 percent, and storage stability at 4 degrees Celsius over 60 days by 11.96 percent compared to the unmodified resin. The immobilization process was endothermic and chemically driven, with diffusion across the boundary layer and within the carrier particles controlling the reaction rate. The immobilized enzyme achieved a 93.57 percent yield of medium- and long-chain triglycerides and demonstrated superior catalytic performance and operational stability compared to a commercial lipase product.  The tannic acid coating and octadecylamine grafting created a heterofunctional platform that simultaneously addressed two persistent constraints in enzyme catalysis: the carrier's limited capacity to stabilize the enzyme and the enzyme's inherent instability under industrial conditions. This study documents how the controlled modification of material surfaces—here, the precise regulation of hydrophobicity through chemical grafting—transforms a lipase from a fragile biological catalyst into a durable industrial tool, revealing the material dependencies embedded in what appears as straightforward biocatalytic improvement.

title: Sustainable foamed starch biocomposites produced by injection moulding: Effect of fibrous reinforcement material and inorganic filler
source: ScienceDirect Publication: International Journal of Biological Macromolecules
excerpt: In this study, environmentally friendly starch-based foamed material was obtained by injection moulding using simple and sustainable formulations with only four natural components. The basic formulation contains starch (75-85% wt.) as the main matrix, cellulose fibres as reinforcing material (0-20% wt.), kaolin (0-10% wt.) as an inorganic filler, and water (40-60% wt.), as multifunctional agent promoting gelatinization, plasticization and foaming. The importance of cellulosic fibre origin (hardwood or softwood, sulphite or kraft pulp) and the load of inorganic filler at the predetermined water content in the formulation were highlighted within the context of detected structure-properties relationships. Lightweight foamed biocomposites, with a density of 0.37-0.44 g∙cm-3 and displaying a hydrophobic surface and a highly porous hydrophilic core, were successfully fabricated. Water, at levels between 40 and 60% (w/w), plays a crucial role in the processability, microstructure, and flexural properties of the biocomposites. Cellulosic fibres enhance the strength of foamed composites when incorporated up to 10% (w/w) of the dry formulation. Additionally, the inclusion of kaolin as an inorganic filler increases porosity and mechanical strength, with most favourable effects observed at filler contents up to 5% (w/w) on a dry matter basis. The best compromise in properties was obtained for the formulation containing (wt.) 10% of cellulosic fibres, 5% of kaolin, 85% of corn starch, as dry matter, and 50% of water. The proposed all-natural foamed composites are seen as an environmentally friendly prospect for packaging solutions, an alternative to conventional synthetic polymer foamed materials produced from fossil resources.
summary: Lightweight foamed biocomposites were produced through injection moulding using a four-component formulation: corn starch (75–85% by weight) as the primary matrix, cellulose fibres (0–20% by weight) as reinforcement, kaolin (0–10% by weight) as an inorganic filler, and water (40–60% by weight) functioning as a gelatinizing agent, plasticizer, and foaming promoter. The resulting materials achieved densities of 0.37–0.44 grams per cubic centimetre with hydrophobic outer surfaces and highly porous hydrophilic interiors. Water content proved critical to processability and mechanical performance; cellulose fibres enhanced strength at concentrations up to 10% by weight, while kaolin improved both porosity and mechanical strength at levels up to 5% by weight. The optimal formulation combined 10% cellulose fibres, 5% kaolin, 85% corn starch, and 50% water, balancing structural and mechanical properties for practical application.  These foamed composites represent a material assemblage where natural polymers and mineral fillers are reorganized through industrial processing to displace synthetic petroleum-derived foams in packaging systems. The dual functionality of water—simultaneously enabling material transformation through gelatinization while creating the porous structure that defines the product's utility—illustrates how a single agent operates across competing demands within the formulation. The shift from fossil-fuel-based to starch-based packaging materials does not eliminate material dependencies but rather relocates them: from petrochemical supply chains to agricultural production systems, raising questions about land use, crop monoculture, and the material labour embedded in rendering natural polymers processable through industrial infrastructure.

title: Emerging Alternaria Toxins Drive LX-2 Cells Transdifferentiation into Myofibroblasts for Liver Fibrosis and CotA Detoxification
source: acs: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry: Table of Contents

title: Clinical utility of non-induced sputum galactomannan testing for suspected invasive pulmonary aspergillosis: a real-world retrospective cohort study
source: Latest Results
excerpt:
Purpose
              Non-induced sputum galactomannan (GM) testing is increasingly used in routine evaluation of suspected invasive pulmonary aspergillosis (IPA), yet its real-world performance and the clinical implications of negative results remain incompletely characterised. We aimed to evaluate its diagnostic performance relative to bronchoscopic mycological findings and to describe the short-term evolution of GM results in routine practice.
            
              Methods
              We retrospectively analysed adult patients who underwent non-induced sputum GM testing between 2017 and 2025. Diagnostic performance was assessed in patients with paired sputum and bronchoscopic samples obtained within 7 days using a bronchoscopy-based composite mycological reference standard (defined as bronchoscopic GM index ≥ 0.5, positive Aspergillus culture, or histopathological evidence of invasion). Longitudinal GM evolution was described in patients with initially negative sputum GM results.
            
              Results
              Among 1002 patients, 73 had paired samples. Sputum GM demonstrated a sensitivity of 73.7% (95% CI 53.9–93.5%), specificity of 72.2% (95% CI 60.3–8
summary: Among 1,002 patients evaluated for suspected invasive pulmonary aspergillosis, this paper examined 73 individuals with paired sputum and bronchoscopic samples collected within seven days. Non-induced sputum galactomannan testing detected the fungal antigen in 73.7% of cases confirmed by bronchoscopic examination (which included a galactomannan index of 0.5 or higher, positive Aspergillus culture, or direct evidence of fungal invasion in tissue samples), while correctly identifying 72.2% of patients without confirmed infection. The study tracked how galactomannan levels changed over time in patients whose initial sputum test returned negative results, documenting the real-world performance of this non-invasive screening method in routine clinical practice between 2017 and 2025.  Within hospital settings where immune-weakened patients face exposure to Aspergillus spores in air and water systems, sputum galactomannan testing occupies an ambiguous position: sensitive enough to catch most infections yet unreliable enough that negative results do not exclude disease, forcing clinicians to pursue more invasive bronchoscopic confirmation. The material entanglement here is between the fungus itself—which colonizes both the lungs of vulnerable patients and the built environment they inhabit—and the diagnostic infrastructure designed to detect it, revealing how detection capacity and clinical certainty remain partially decoupled even as testing becomes routine.

title: Scientists identify new Fusarium species behind wheat disease outbreak in Ethiopia
source: Cell Biology and Microbiology News - Biology news, Microbiology
excerpt: Fusarium head blight (FHB) is a destructive disease of wheat that can reduce grain yields and contaminate grain with toxins harmful to humans and livestock. The disease threatens wheat production worldwide and poses ongoing challenges for growers, grain handlers, and food safety systems.
summary: Fusarium head blight is a fungal disease that damages wheat crops and contaminates grain with toxins hazardous to human and animal health. This paper identifies a new Fusarium species responsible for recent wheat disease outbreaks in Ethiopia. The disease reduces grain yields and poses risks across the entire chain of grain production, handling, and food safety systems worldwide.  The emergence of a previously unidentified Fusarium species in Ethiopian wheat production illustrates how disease outbreaks reveal gaps in existing taxonomic frameworks and surveillance systems. A fungal pathogen newly recognized as a threat to food security simultaneously exposes the fragility of grain systems dependent on stable crop health—systems already stressed by climate variability, trade networks, and the material vulnerabilities of stored grain to fungal colonization. The identification of this species does not resolve the underlying conditions that permit rapid fungal spread; rather, it documents a moment when agricultural systems and their microbial inhabitants are reconfiguring together under pressure.

title: α-1,3-Glucan-Driven Remodeling of the Conidial Cell Wall in an Aspergillus fumigatus Vaccine Strain Alters Innate Immune Recognition
source: acs: Journal of the American Chemical Society: Table of Contents
excerpt: Aspergillus fumigatus is a major cause of invasive aspergillosis in immunocompromised patients, where current antifungal therapies are limited by toxicity, drug resistance, and lack of durable protection, and no vaccines are available. A mutant lacking the sterylglucosidase-encoding gene (sglA) has emerged as a candidate that induces protective immune responses, but the structural basis for this phenotype remains unclear. Here, we use cellular solid-state NMR spectroscopy to compare the organization of the conidial cell wall in ΔsglA and its wild-type counterpart. The ΔsglA conidial cell wall displays extensive remodeling, including increased α-1,3-glucan content and structural polymorphism, strengthened interactions with β-glucans, reduced hydration, and restricted molecular motion, together consolidating a more rigid scaffold with limited β-glucan accessibility. These structural changes are associated with altered neutrophil responses and a shift in innate immune signaling. This work links cell-wall reorganization to altered immune recognition in this vaccine candidate, with implications for future immunotherapeutic strategies.
summary: This paper documents structural changes in the conidial cell wall of a mutant *Aspergillus fumigatus* strain lacking the sterylglucosidase enzyme (Δ*sglA*) using cellular solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy. The mutant displays increased α-1,3-glucan content, strengthened bonding between different glucan polymers, reduced water penetration, and restricted molecular movement compared to the wild-type strain. These alterations create a more rigid cell wall scaffold with reduced accessibility to β-glucans, a key immune recognition target. The structural remodeling is accompanied by altered neutrophil responses and shifted patterns of innate immune signaling, linking the physical reorganization of the cell wall to changes in how the immune system recognizes and responds to the fungus.  The dual character of this cell wall remodeling—simultaneously a barrier to immune detection and a platform for altered immune engagement—reveals how a structural modification can function as both concealment and exposure. The same rigidity that restricts access to immunogenic molecules also forces the immune system to recognize alternative epitopes, creating a novel interaction between host and pathogen that the researchers propose could be harnessed therapeutically. This fungal mutant thus occupies an ambiguous position: a potential vaccine candidate precisely because its cell wall architecture redirects rather than evades immune recognition, yet one whose protective capacity depends on maintaining this precarious structural state under the selective pressures of clinical use and host immunity.

title: Non-viable Aspergillus fumigatus promotes chronic inflammation and angiogenesis in a murine fungus ball model
source: American Society for Microbiology: Microbiology Spectrum: Table of Contents
excerpt: An aspergilloma is a fungus ball that forms within a preexisting cavity, most commonly in the lungs but also in the paranasal sinuses. It represents a chronic inflammatory disorder that can persist for months and cause life-threatening hemoptysis. In this study, we hypothesized that the fungus ball is predominantly composed of non-viable hyphae and that chronic inflammation is maintained primarily by host responses to these non-viable elements rather than by ongoing fungal proliferation. To investigate this, we created a murine fungus ball model by implanting heat-killed Aspergillus fumigatus fungus balls, allowing for ≥16 weeks of longitudinal assessment. The model recapitulated key pathological features of the human disease, including progressive fibrosis and sustained angiogenesis, coinciding with elevated vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF). A distinct immune response was observed where neutrophils infiltrated the fungus ball core, while macrophages accumulated at the periphery. Strikingly, these macrophages surrounding the lesion progressively became lipid-laden foam-like cells with an M1-skewed profile, a phenomenon reproduced in vitro by exposure to non-viable hyphae alone. Thus, the fungal material itself can sustain chronic inflammation and vascular remodeling, implicating macrophage lipid accumulation as a process potentially contributing to the chronic host response. These findings highlight lipid dysregulation and failed fungal clearance as key features of the host-response disorder associated with the fungus ball and establish a tractable platform for evaluating host-directed therapies.IMPORTANCEAspergilloma, a fungus ball that forms in preexisting cavities of the lung or paranasal sinuses, can cause chronic symptoms and life-threatening bleeding. Current fungicidal treatments, including antifungals, are often ineffective, and surgery to remove the fungal ball is risky. To better understand why these lesions can persist even when act
summary: Heat-killed *Aspergillus fumigatus* fungus balls implanted in mice sustained chronic inflammation and vascular remodeling over at least 16 weeks, reproducing key features of human aspergilloma including progressive tissue scarring and elevated vascular endothelial growth factor. Neutrophils accumulated within the fungus ball core while macrophages gathered at the periphery, progressively transforming into lipid-laden foam cells with an inflammatory profile skewed toward pro-inflammatory activation. Exposure of macrophages to non-viable fungal material alone in laboratory conditions reproduced this lipid accumulation, demonstrating that the fungal material itself—rather than active fungal growth—can sustain the chronic inflammatory response and vascular remodeling observed in the lesion.  This paper documents a material assemblage in which dead fungal matter becomes a persistent driver of host pathology. The non-viable *Aspergillus fumigatus* hyphae function simultaneously as inert structural debris and as an active inflammatory agent, their metabolic inertness paradoxically enabling chronic disease through the host's own lipid dysregulation and failed clearance mechanisms. The fungus ball thus emerges not as a site of fungal dominance but as a site where human immune dysfunction—specifically the accumulation of lipid-laden macrophages—becomes the machinery sustaining pathology, revealing how material persistence and biological vulnerability become entangled in conditions of chronic exposure.

title: The Induction of Succinate Dehydrogenase of “C1” Conformation and the Difference in Its Binding to Fungicides Lead to the Variation in Toxicity of SDHI Fungicides Against Fusarium pseudograminearum
source: acs: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry: Table of Contents
excerpt: Genetic differentiation of the succinate dehydrogenase (SDH) subunit C in Fusarium spp. contributes to significant variations in the efficacy of succinate dehydrogenase inhibitor (SDHI) fungicides against these pathogens. However, the structural states of the two SDH conformations in mitochondria and their binding modes with SDHI fungicides are still unknown. Based on SDH activity and qRT-PCR assay, we confirmed that the SDH of "C2" conformation in Fusarium pseudograminearum is a constitutively expressed type, while that of "C1" conformation is an inducibly expressed type.Through molecular docking, the difference in binding affinity between SDHI fungicides and the two conformations also proves to be a key factor contributing to the variations in toxicity of SDHI fungicides against F. pseudograminearum. These results established that SDHI fungicides induce the expression of SDH of "C1" conformation, which leads to weak binding between SDHI fungicides and the target, thereby resulting in variations in the toxicity of SDHI fungicides.
summary: This paper documents the structural basis for variable fungicide effectiveness against *Fusarium pseudograminearum*, a cereal pathogen. The researchers identified two conformational states of succinate dehydrogenase, an enzyme in the fungal mitochondrial energy-production pathway: a "C2" form that is continuously produced, and a "C1" form that is produced only when triggered by specific conditions. Using enzyme activity assays and gene expression measurement, the study confirmed that succinate dehydrogenase inhibitor fungicides—a major class of agricultural antifungals—induce the fungus to produce the "C1" conformation. Computer modelling of molecular binding showed that succinate dehydrogenase inhibitor fungicides bind weakly to the "C1" form compared to the "C2" form, explaining why the fungus's adaptive response to fungicide exposure reduces drug effectiveness.  The finding reveals a feedback mechanism embedded in the fungus's biochemistry: exposure to a fungicide triggers production of a target protein variant that resists that same fungicide. This dynamic mirrors broader patterns in agricultural systems where chemical interventions generate the conditions for their own circumvention. The pathogen's capacity to shift its molecular machinery in response to toxic pressure—transforming a liability into temporary refuge—demonstrates how fungal adaptation operates not as a rare mutation but as an inducible physiological response, a form of metabolic flexibility that emerges precisely through the presence of the control agent itself.

title: Drosophila host defense mechanisms against filamentous fungal pathogens with diverse lifestyles
source: PLOS Pathogens
excerpt:
by Guiqing Liu, Yao Tian, Mark Austin Hanson, Prince Kumar Sah, Jun Li, Bruno Lemaitre

Entomopathogenic fungi serve as powerful regulators of insect populations in nature. However, how immune effectors combat fungal pathogens remains incompletely understood. We employ Drosophila melanogaster as a genetically tractable model to dissect immune defense mechanisms against diverse fungal pathogens. We show that the Toll pathway is the key determinant of immunity against all species tested regardless of their ecological strategy, primarily through resistance mechanisms that limit fungal proliferation. In addition, melanization, but not phagocytosis or the Imd pathway, also has a role in limiting fungal entry and proliferation. Additionally, we show that fungal protease detection by Persephone has a quantitatively more critical role than the glucan sensor GNBP3 in the activation of the Toll pathway upon fungal infection. Our study also reveals that the fly-obligate fungus Entomophthora muscae employs a vegetative development strategy to hide from the host immune response. These findings reveal that Drosophila immune mechanisms effectively defend against a broad range of fungal pathogens,
summary: This study uses the fruit fly *Drosophila melanogaster* as a genetic model to identify how insect immune systems defend against fungal pathogens with different ecological strategies. The researchers tested multiple fungal species and found that the Toll pathway acts as the primary immune defense mechanism across all pathogens tested, functioning mainly by limiting fungal growth rather than eliminating it entirely. Melanization—the production of a dark pigment that forms protective barriers—also contributed to defense by reducing fungal entry and spread, whereas phagocytosis (the engulfing of pathogens by immune cells) and the Imd pathway played no significant role. The paper demonstrates that the fly immune system detects fungal infection primarily through recognition of fungal proteases by a receptor called Persephone, which proved more important than detection of fungal cell wall components through the glucan sensor GNBP3 in activating the Toll pathway. The study also documents that *Entomophthora muscae*, a fungus that infects only flies, evades immune detection by adopting a vegetative growth strategy rather than producing spores.  Within the material entanglements of the Anthropocene, this research documents how immune defense mechanisms are calibrated not to eliminate pathogens entirely but to manage their proliferation—a containment rather than eradication. The finding that fungal proteases trigger immune responses more effectively than structural components of the fungal cell wall reveals how pathogens and hosts are locked in a specific material dialogue: fungi that produce particular enzymes become legible to immune systems, while those employing alternative metabolic strategies, like *Entomophthora muscae*, remain partially obscured. This suggests that fungal survival in hostile environments depends not on inherent pathogenicity but on the capacity to evade or exploit the particular sensory apparatus of their host, a relationship that shifts as ecological conditions and host populations change.

title: Mycotoxin Toxicity Assessment in the 21st Century: Where Are We on the Path to Alternatives?
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Current Opinion in Food Science
excerpt: An unprecedented global effort is currently underway to move away from animal studies and toward more human-relevant methods. While this shift is gaining momentum in biomedical research and preclinical safety assessment, how does it extend to the toxicological and regulatory evaluation of food contaminants? This review examines the current state of foodborne contaminant assessment, specifically mycotoxins, emphasizing both emerging efforts and persistent barriers to implementing an animal-free testing paradigm. Several initiatives from regulatory bodies signal a strong commitment to change. However, major challenges remain. Additionally, animal use in the European Union continues to be widespread in procedures related to food and feed safety, as well as in hazard assessment studies addressing exposure to mycotoxins. These considerations emphasize an urgent call to action to promote human-relevant approaches and advance safety assessment practices in the context of 21 st century technologies. • Global efforts are moving toxicology assessment away from animal studies. • Regulatory integration of NAMs in food safety remains limited. • Mycotoxin risk assessment represents an example of the slow adoption of NAMs. • Momentum from biomedical research should now drive innovation in food safety.
summary: This paper examines the slow adoption of human-relevant testing methods in the safety assessment of mycotoxins—fungal poisons that contaminate food and feed. The review documents that while a global movement toward animal-free toxicology is underway in biomedical research and preclinical safety evaluation, this shift has not substantially extended to food safety regulation. The paper identifies several regulatory initiatives signalling commitment to change, yet notes that animal testing remains widespread across the European Union for procedures related to food and feed safety and for hazard assessment studies of mycotoxin exposure. The review emphasizes that non-animal methods, despite their availability, remain poorly integrated into regulatory frameworks for food contaminant assessment, and calls for accelerated adoption of twenty-first century technologies to modernize how mycotoxin risks are evaluated.  The persistent reliance on animal studies for mycotoxin safety assessment reveals a material entanglement between regulatory practice and the bodies used to generate knowledge about exposure. Mycotoxins move through food systems and human bodies as accumulating contaminants, yet the methods used to assess their danger remain anchored to animal models rather than to the actual human populations whose exposures are shaped by patterns of food access, labour conditions, and economic inequality. The gap between available human-relevant testing methods and their regulatory adoption suggests that the barriers to change are not technical but institutional—rooted in how authority, evidence, and acceptable risk are constructed within food safety governance.

title: AltoSafe: A predictive model for Alternaria mycotoxins in tomatoes
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Control
excerpt: Tomatoes, a widely cultivated vegetable, are susceptible to infections by Alternaria species, which can lead to contamination with mycotoxins such as alternariol (AOH), alternariol monomethyl ether (AME), and tenuazonic acid (TeA), posing public health risks. This study presents Altosafe , a predictive model combined with a Random Forest approach for exploratory assessment of Alternaria mycotoxin contamination in open-field tomatoes. First, a cumulative fungal infection index was constructed based on literature and experimental data. This cumulative fungal infection index was then integrated with weather and agronomic data collected from open-field tomato cultivation trials held in China to develop predictive models using the random forest (RF) algorithm. Field trial data collected between April and November 2023 were used for model development and internal validation, while samples collected in May and June 2023 were reserved for hold-out validation within the same experimental context. Separate RF classification models were developed for AOH, AME, and TeA using predefined contamination thresholds. Internal validation accuracies ranged from 96.0% to 98.0%, and hold-out validation accuracies ranged from 70.0% to 82.0%. Threshold adjustment demonstrated expected trade-offs between sensitivity and specificity. To our knowledge, this is the first study in China to explore predictive models for Alternaria mycotoxin contamination in tomatoes. While the models showed promising performance under constrained field conditions, broader multi-season and multi-location validation is required before wider application can be considered. • Altosafe integrates a cumulative fungal infection index with random forest approach. • Altosafe models are based on simplified biological assumptions and limited data. • Fungal infection risk index is the most important predictor among all inputs. • Hold-out validation accuracies between 74% and 82% in one field context. • AltoSafe models provid
summary: This study presents AltoSafe, a predictive model using random forest machine learning to forecast contamination of open-field tomatoes with three Alternaria mycotoxins: alternariol, alternariol monomethyl ether, and tenuazonic acid. The model integrates a cumulative fungal infection index constructed from literature and experimental data with weather and agronomic variables collected during open-field tomato cultivation trials in China between April and November 2023. Separate classification models were developed for each of the three mycotoxins using predefined contamination thresholds. Internal validation accuracies ranged from 96.0% to 98.0%, while hold-out validation accuracies on samples reserved from May and June 2023 ranged from 70.0% to 82.0%, with the fungal infection risk index identified as the most important predictor across all inputs.  The gap between internal and hold-out validation performance reveals a material constraint: the model's capacity to predict mycotoxin contamination depends on the specific field conditions and seasonal timing from which it was trained, and broader application across multiple seasons and locations remains unvalidated. This paper documents a predictive technology designed to manage a fungal hazard in a high-value crop, yet the technology's own performance demonstrates how Alternaria contamination risk is entangled with particular weather patterns, agronomic practices, and temporal windows—factors that vary across growing contexts and cannot be easily generalized. The model thus becomes a tool for managing risk within constrained conditions rather than a portable solution, reflecting deeper dependencies between crop safety, environmental variability, and the localized knowledge required to govern fungal threats in agricultural systems.

title: Study on a temperature-dependent selective soil treatment agent in regulating tobacco rhizosphere microbial community and controlling root and stem diseases
source: Frontiers in Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: IntroductionThis study investigated the effects of a selective soil treatment agent on the microbial community structure of tobacco rhizosphere soil and the photosynthetic performance of tobacco plants at different temperatures (15 °C, 25 °C, 35 °C) through pot experiments.MethodsThe control efficacy against black shank and root rot diseases was also evaluated.Results and discussionThe results indicated that 25 °C was the optimal temperature for the agent’s effectiveness. Under this condition, the agent significantly inhibited the enrichment of phytopathogenic fungi such as Fusarium spp. in the rhizosphere soil (the abundance of Fusarium in the T25SJ treatment decreased by 78.6% compared to the untreated group T25CK), while optimizing the microbial community structure, as evidenced by increased fungal α-diversity (Shannon index) and enhanced competitiveness of beneficial bacteria (e.g., Pseudomonas spp.). Notably, the bacterial community stability was maintained (no significant change in the Simpson index), confirming the agent’s selective regulatory effect rather than non-specific sterilization. Fluorescence imaging analysis revealed that the photosynthetic performance of tobacco
summary: This paper examined how a temperature-dependent selective soil treatment agent affects the microbial community in tobacco root zone soil and the plant's capacity for photosynthesis across three temperature conditions (15 degrees Celsius, 25 degrees Celsius, and 35 degrees Celsius). The agent proved most effective at 25 degrees Celsius, where it substantially reduced populations of disease-causing fungi such as Fusarium species—the abundance of Fusarium decreased by 78.6 percent in treated soil compared to untreated controls at this temperature. The treatment also increased the diversity of fungal communities and strengthened populations of beneficial bacteria including Pseudomonas species, while maintaining the stability of the broader bacterial community, indicating that the agent selectively suppresses harmful microorganisms rather than indiscriminately killing microbial life. Fluorescence imaging showed improvements in tobacco photosynthetic performance under these conditions, and the agent controlled black shank and root rot diseases.  Within the material entanglements of tobacco cultivation, this selective agent operates as both a disease suppression tool and a microbial reshaper—its capacity to target pathogenic fungi while preserving or enhancing beneficial bacterial populations reveals how agricultural interventions can be calibrated to work with rather than against the soil ecosystem. Yet the temperature-dependence of this selectivity also exposes a fragility: the agent's efficacy is contingent on specific thermal conditions, suggesting that as growing environments shift or become more variable, the stability of disease control in tobacco root zone systems may become increasingly uncertain.

title: Multi-kingdom metagenomic characterization of the gut bacteriome, mycobiome, and virome in chronic functional constipation
source: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: BackgroundChronic functional constipation (CFC) is a common gastrointestinal disorder increasingly linked to gut microbiome dysbiosis. However, multi-kingdom metagenomic characterization of bacterial, fungal, and viral communities in CFC remains limited.MethodsFecal samples from 53 CFC patients and 48 healthy controls were analyzed using whole-metagenome shotgun sequencing. Microbial composition, function, cross-kingdom interactions, and diagnostic potential were evaluated using diversity analyses, KEGG annotation, network analysis, and random forest modeling.ResultsCompared with healthy controls, CFC patients exhibited marked alterations across multiple microbial kingdoms. The gut bacteriome showed significant community-structure shifts despite comparable α-diversity, characterized by depletion of health-associated Firmicutes (e.g., Faecalibacterium and Roseburia) and enrichment of Proteobacteria (e.g., Klebsiella). The mycobiome displayed selective changes in diversity and composition, with several potentially pathogenic fungal taxa enriched in CFC (e.g., Fusarium sp. c181). In the virome, community composition differed significantly between groups, with higher viral richness in
summary: Fecal samples from 53 chronic functional constipation patients and 48 healthy controls were analyzed using whole-metagenome shotgun sequencing to characterize bacterial, fungal, and viral communities simultaneously. The bacteriome in constipation patients showed marked shifts in community structure, with depletion of health-associated bacteria such as Faecalibacterium and Roseburia (both Firmicutes) and enrichment of Proteobacteria including Klebsiella, despite comparable overall bacterial diversity between groups. The fungal community displayed selective changes, with several potentially pathogenic fungi enriched in constipation patients, including Fusarium sp. c181. Viral community composition also differed significantly between patients and controls, with higher viral diversity observed in the constipation group. This paper evaluated microbial composition, metabolic function through genetic annotation, cross-kingdom interactions via network analysis, and diagnostic potential using machine learning classification.  Within the constipated gut, bacterial depletion and fungal enrichment occur as coupled processes rather than independent shifts—the loss of fiber-fermenting bacteria and the expansion of fungal taxa represent entangled changes in how the intestinal ecosystem processes and resists material flows. The simultaneous presence of pathogenic fungi and altered bacterial structure suggests that constipation reflects not a simple microbial imbalance but a reorganization of metabolic capacity and cross-kingdom dependencies; what appears clinically as a disorder of transit may register at the microbial level as a reconfiguration of who feeds on what, and which organisms gain competitive advantage in a slowed, altered intestinal environment.

title: Deterministic dietary exposure assessment to aflatoxin and fumonisin in street-vended foods in Meru Town, Kenya
source: Latest Results
excerpt: Mycotoxin contamination in street-vended foods is a significant public health concern in Kenya. This study aimed to determine the levels of aflatoxins and fumonisins in street-vended foods (SVFs) and to conduct a deterministic dietary exposure assessment of these mycotoxins in Meru Town, Kenya. A total of 116 samples comprising 14 cereal-based foods (githeri (maize mixed with legumes), 18 porridge, 30 chapati (unleavened wheat flatbread), 24 roasted green maize, and 30 groundnuts were collected and analyzed for the presence of aflatoxins and fumonisins using Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay. About 92% and 72% of the street-vended food samples analyzed were contaminated with aflatoxin and fumonisins, respectively. Significantly (P &lt; 0.0001) highest mean concentration of total aflatoxin was observed in groundnuts (281.0 ± 58.5&nbsp;µg/kg), while porridge recorded the lowest contamination level (27.3 ± 8.1&nbsp;µg/kg). Regarding total fumonisin, githeri showed significantly (P &lt; 0. 0053) highest mean concentration of 180.8 ± 30.0&nbsp;µg/kg. The Kenya Bureau of Standards regulatory limits for total aflatoxin was exceeded in 91.4% of the food samples. Furthermore, all samples exceeded the Joint Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO)/World Health Organization (WHO) Expert Committee on Food Additives (JECFA) recommended daily aflatoxin intake threshold, even for weekly consumers. Co-occurrence of aflatoxins and fumonisins was observed in approximately 70% of all street-vended food samples. A significant (p &lt; 0.0001) correlation between the two mycotoxins was also detected in cereal-based foods. Therefore, targeted monitoring and mitigation strategies should be implemented to reduce the risk of mycotoxin co-exposure in street-vended foods, safeguarding public health in Meru Town, Kenya. Mycotoxin contamination in street-vended foods is a
summary: This study analyzed 116 samples of street-vended foods in Meru Town, Kenya—comprising githeri, porridge, chapati, roasted green maize, and groundnuts—for contamination with aflatoxins and fumonisins using enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay. Aflatoxin contamination was detected in 92% of samples, with groundnuts showing the highest mean concentration at 281.0 micrograms per kilogram and porridge the lowest at 27.3 micrograms per kilogram. Fumonisin contamination occurred in 72% of samples, with githeri recording the highest mean concentration at 180.8 micrograms per kilogram. Critically, 91.4% of all samples exceeded Kenya Bureau of Standards regulatory limits for total aflatoxin, and all samples surpassed the joint Food and Agriculture Organization and World Health Organization recommended daily intake threshold even for consumers eating these foods only weekly. Co-occurrence of both mycotoxins was documented in approximately 70% of samples, with a statistically significant correlation between aflatoxin and fumonisin levels in cereal-based foods.  The contamination pattern observed in these street-vended foods reveals how exposure to mycotoxins becomes embedded in the material and economic structures of informal food systems. The same foods—groundnuts, maize, legumes—that serve as affordable protein sources for low-income urban populations in Meru Town simultaneously function as vectors for cumulative toxin exposure, a burden distributed unevenly across those with limited access to formal food supply chains. The co-occurrence of multiple mycotoxins in a single food item intensifies this exposure while remaining largely invisible to consumers; the regulatory failures documented here (91.4% of samples exceeding standards) reflect not merely technical gaps in food safety monitoring but structural dependencies on informal vending networks that operate beyond enforcement capacity.

title: High‐Volume Culture Improves Diagnostic Yield of Aspergillus Species From Sputum
source: Wiley: Mycoses: Table of Contents
excerpt: High-volume culture at 37°C substantially improves the detection of filamentous moulds in sputum, especially Aspergillus species and clinically relevant azole-resistant A. fumigatus. Incorporating HVC37 into routine mycology workflows can significantly enhance fungal diagnosis and antifungal resistance surveillance.
summary: High-volume culture incubated at 37 degrees Celsius substantially improves the detection of filamentous moulds in sputum samples, particularly Aspergillus species and clinically relevant azole-resistant strains of Aspergillus fumigatus. This paper demonstrates that incorporating high-volume culture at 37 degrees Celsius into routine mycology workflows can significantly enhance fungal diagnosis and antifungal resistance surveillance. The method addresses a diagnostic gap in respiratory sampling where standard culture protocols may miss or delay identification of these organisms.  Within the context of turbulent diagnostic landscapes shaped by antimicrobial resistance and clinical vulnerability, this paper reveals how a technical modification to culture volume and temperature becomes a site where pathogenic detection and epidemiological visibility converge. The same organisms flagged as threats to immune-weakened patients—particularly those whose respiratory exposure reflects broader patterns of institutional or occupational vulnerability—become legible through enhanced laboratory protocols. The improvement in detecting azole-resistant strains specifically indicates that diagnostic capacity is not neutral; it is entangled with the emergence and tracking of resistance observable traits that reflect both clinical need and the material conditions under which moulds proliferate and adapt.

title: Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cubense Race 4 Effector, Foc4-305, Targets a Proline-Rich Protein in Banana and Contributes to Virulence
source: acs: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry: Table of Contents
excerpt: Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cubense (Foc) ranks as the leading cause of banana wilt globally, devastating crops through the action of pathogen-secreted effectors. This study revealed Foc4-305 as a putative effector from isolate Foc4, identified via transcriptomics, and validated for its pivotal role in pathogenesis. Deletion of Foc4-305 impaired fungal growth, heightened sensitivity to oxidative stresses and cell wall disruptors, and diminished pathogenicity toward banana plants. Localization studies showed Foc4-305 primarily in the nuclei and cytoplasm of Nicotiana benthamiana cells, where it could inhibit BAX-triggered cell death. Interactions with a proline-rich protein (Ma-PRP) were confirmed through yeast two-hybrid, bimolecular fluorescence complementation and coimmunoprecipitation assays. Western blot analysis showed that Foc4-305 stabilized Ma-PRP proteins in vivo. Virus-induced gene silencing of Ma-PRP enhanced the resistance of bananas to wilt disease. Differentially expressed genes of wild-type Foc4 and effector Foc4-305 knockout mutants were revealed by RNA-seq analysis. GO and KEGG analyses highlighted some of these genes as associated with reproduction, DNA repair, and amino acid metabolism, underscoring the role of Foc4-305 role in enhancing virulence of Foc4 by modulating growth, response to stresses, and involvement in host immunity.
summary: Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cubense Race 4, the fungus responsible for banana wilt disease, produces an effector protein called Foc4-305 that this study identified through gene expression analysis and confirmed as essential to the pathogen's disease-causing ability. When researchers deleted the gene encoding Foc4-305, the resulting fungal mutant showed impaired growth, increased vulnerability to oxidative stress and cell wall-damaging compounds, and reduced ability to cause disease in banana plants. The protein localizes to the nucleus and cytoplasm of plant cells, where it blocks a form of programmed cell death triggered by the plant's defense protein BAX. This study demonstrated that Foc4-305 directly binds to and stabilizes a proline-rich protein in banana (Ma-PRP), and when the banana plant's own gene for this proline-rich protein was silenced, the plant's resistance to wilt disease increased. Gene expression profiling revealed that Foc4-305 influences fungal genes involved in reproduction, cell damage repair, and amino acid metabolism, indicating its role in enhancing the pathogen's overall fitness.  The fungus thus deploys a secreted protein that hijacks a host plant protein to suppress the plant's cellular defense mechanisms—a relationship that is simultaneously a vulnerability. The proline-rich protein that the fungus has evolved to exploit can, when removed from the plant's defensive arsenal, paradoxically strengthen resistance to infection. This reveals how the material entanglement between pathogen and host—where the fungus's disease-causing ability depends on manipulating the plant's own proteins—creates both a dependency and a potential intervention point, suggesting that the plant's molecular machinery for immunity and the fungus's machinery for infection are not separable but deeply intertwined through the pathogen's evolutionary strategy.

title: The influence of inoculation with Penicillium and yeast on taste enhancement in Jinhua ham
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Research International
excerpt: This study investigated the effect of Penicillium (Penicillium aethiopicum & Penicillium chrysogenum, PP), yeast (Candida parapsilosis & Rhodotorula mucilaginosa, CM) and mixture (MI) inoculation on the taste development of Jinhua ham. The results showed that Penicillium and yeast significantly increased acidic protease activity and reduced TVB-N values compared to the control. Three kinds of inoculation promoted the degradation of both myofibrillar and sarcoplasmic proteins, leading to a accumulation of peptides and free amino acids. It was strongly detected by the electronic tongue sensors PKS, NMS, ANS and SCS. Consequently, the inoculated hams exhibited significantly enhanced umami and sweet tastes, resulting in a higher overall acceptability in sensory evaluation. These findings indicate that targeted inoculation with Penicillium or yeast improves taste quality by intensifying proteolysis and enriching taste-active compounds, providing a viable strategy for enhancing the taste profile of dry-cured ham.
summary: Penicillium aethiopicum and Penicillium chrysogenum, together with the yeasts Candida parapsilosis and Rhodotorula mucilaginosa, were inoculated separately and in combination into Jinhua ham during curing. This paper found that all three inoculation treatments increased the activity of acid-soluble proteases and lowered total volatile basic nitrogen levels compared to uninoculated controls. The microbial colonisation accelerated the breakdown of both myofibrillar proteins (the contractile filaments within muscle cells) and sarcoplasmic proteins (the soluble proteins in muscle fluid), causing peptides and free amino acids to accumulate. Electronic tongue sensors—which measure taste compounds through electrical conductivity patterns—detected these chemical changes, and sensory evaluation confirmed that the inoculated hams developed significantly stronger umami and sweet flavours with higher overall acceptability.  Within the material ecology of dry-cured ham production, these fungi and yeasts function simultaneously as flavour developers and as agents of controlled decomposition. The same microbial metabolic activity that breaks down muscle proteins into taste-active compounds also generates the chemical signatures typically associated with spoilage or degradation—yet here, this degradation is precisely what producers seek.
