The paper demonstrates that what appears as microbial damage at the molecular level becomes, through deliberate inoculation and managed curing conditions, a desirable transformation of raw pork into a valued food commodity, revealing how the boundary between preservation and decay, between pathogenic and productive microbial action, shifts depending on the spatial and economic context in which these organisms operate.

title: Non-fumigatus Aspergillus-associated pulmonary events: a diagnostic challenge
source: American Society for Microbiology: Journal of Clinical Microbiology: Table of Contents
excerpt: Non-fumigatus Aspergillus species are recognized as pulmonary pathogens, but their diagnosis is poorly documented. In this 5-year, single-center study, 497 of 1,256 patients had at least one positive respiratory culture for a non-fumigatus species, with 36 considered colonized and 16 with documented lung disease. A. niger, A. flavus, and A. nidulans accounted for almost four-fifths of the isolates. Routine tests produced poor results, with positivity rates of 2.3% for microscopy, 9.3% for repeat culture, 7.4% for bronchoalveolar galactomannan, and 24.5% for Aspergillus fumigatus serum IgG. Overall, the study shows that non-fumigatus species can cause treatable chronic lung disease, but that current diagnostics miss most cases. Until more sensitive tests are available, clinicians must rely on repeated respiratory sampling and culture to identify these infections.
summary: This paper documents a five-year clinical series of 1,256 patients, of whom 497 had at least one positive respiratory culture for non-*fumigatus Aspergillus* species. Among these, 36 patients were classified as colonized and 16 had documented lung disease caused by these organisms. The three dominant species were *Aspergillus niger*, *Aspergillus flavus*, and *Aspergillus nidulans*, together representing nearly 80 percent of all isolates recovered. Routine diagnostic methods performed poorly across the board: microscopy of respiratory samples detected the organism in only 2.3 percent of cases, repeat culture in 9.3 percent, bronchoalveolar lavage fluid galactomannan antigen testing in 7.4 percent, and serum antibody testing for *Aspergillus fumigatus* in 24.5 percent. The paper concludes that non-*fumigatus* species can produce treatable chronic lung infections but that current diagnostic approaches fail to identify most cases, leaving clinicians dependent on repeated respiratory sampling and culture as the most reliable detection method.  The diagnostic gap documented here reveals a material entanglement between clinical capacity and fungal epidemiology. These species circulate through respiratory systems and clinical laboratories at measurable rates, yet the tools designed to detect fungal lung disease—tests calibrated primarily for the more common *fumigatus* species—systematically fail to register their presence. The result is a clinical landscape where treatable infections remain undiagnosed not because the organisms are absent from patient lungs, but because the infrastructure of detection has been built around a narrower pathogenic profile. This selective blindness shapes which patients receive treatment and which remain classified as colonized rather than diseased, embedding diagnostic capacity itself as a mechanism of differential exposure and care.

title: Efficacy of chitinases from mangrove wetland derived Penicillium oxalicum on powdered chitin
source: Frontiers in Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: Chitin, an abundant polysaccharide in shrimp and crab shells, serves as a primary resource for producing glucosamine and chitosan oligosaccharides. Chemical hydrolysis is widely utilized in these processes, due to the limited efficacy of many chitinases on powdered chitin. Additionally, many chitinases perform much better on colloidal chitin, which still requires swelling of the powdered chitin with hydrochloric acid. In this study, chitinases from a mangrove isolated P. oxalicum were characterized and applied for the hydrolysis of chitin substrates. The enzyme mixture exhibited strong catalytic performance toward powdered chitin, achieving approximately 70% hydrolysis within 2 h at 65°C under scale-up conditions (40 L working volume in 50 L batch reactors). HPLC analysis confirmed a stepwise hydrolysis pattern from chitooligosaccharides to monomeric GlcNAc, indicating near-complete depolymerization. The GlcNAc productivity of the enzyme mixture toward powdered chitin was approximately 80% of that observed for colloidal chitin. The ability to directly degrade powdered chitin without acid pretreatment highlights its industrial potential for green and efficient chitin valorization. T
summary: Chitinases extracted from a Penicillium oxalicum strain isolated from mangrove wetlands were tested for their ability to break down powdered chitin, the polysaccharide found in shrimp and crab shells. The enzyme mixture achieved approximately 70% hydrolysis of powdered chitin within 2 hours at 65°C in large-scale conditions (40 litre working volume in 50 litre batch reactors). High-performance liquid chromatography analysis showed that the enzymes broke down the chitin in a stepwise manner, progressively reducing it to smaller sugar units and ultimately to the monomeric compound N-acetyl-D-glucosamine, indicating near-complete depolymerization. The productivity of N-acetyl-D-glucosamine from powdered chitin reached approximately 80% of that achieved when using chemically pre-treated colloidal chitin.  the enzymes could directly degrade powdered chitin without requiring the standard acid pretreatment step, eliminating a chemical processing stage.  This paper documents a material transformation in which a fungal enzyme system, derived from a coastal wetland organism, converts crustacean shell waste into valuable sugar compounds through biological rather than chemical means. The mangrove-derived chitinases operate on the same raw material—powdered chitin—that has historically resisted enzymatic degradation, requiring expensive acid swelling to become accessible. The capacity of this fungal system to metabolize this abundant but previously intractable waste stream without chemical preprocessing suggests a reconfiguration of value chains in chitin processing; what was once economically marginal (direct enzymatic conversion of powdered chitin) becomes industrially viable, while simultaneously reducing the chemical inputs and environmental burdens embedded in conventional hydrolysis routes.

title: Genomic surveillance of carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae in the Republic of Moldova
source: Frontiers in Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: Antimicrobial-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae poses a global threat to human, animal, and environmental health. The challenges of AMR surveillance have led to the development of advanced molecular biology techniques, such as genomic sequencing, to identify the resistance gene diversity and the factors contributing to the AMR emergence and spreading. In this study, a total of 99 Klebsiella pneumoniae strains suspected of producing ESBL or carbapenemases were collected between January 2020 and December 2023 from nine hospital laboratories, predominantly from hospital-associated infections/isolates. The strains were analyzed using conventional methods as well as whole-genome sequencing. Phenotypic testing indicated very high resistance to key antibiotic classes, including third-generation cephalosporins (98%), fluoroquinolones (97%), aminoglycosides (95%), and carbapenems (68%). Subsequent bioinformatic analysis was performed using tools such as iTOL, the Center for Genomic Epidemiology platform, and Kleborate. Sixteen distinct MLST types were identified among the studied strains. The most common were ST395 (N = 56), ST377 (N = 12), and ST23 (N = 5). Phylogenetic inference revealed tw
summary: Carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae strains collected from nine hospital laboratories in the Republic of Moldova between January 2020 and December 2023 displayed widespread resistance to multiple antibiotic classes: 98% of the 99 strains tested showed resistance to third-generation cephalosporins, 97% to fluoroquinolones, 95% to aminoglycosides, and 68% to carbapenems. Conventional observable-trait testing was paired with whole-genome sequencing and bioinformatic analysis using iTOL, the Center for Genomic Epidemiology platform, and Kleborate to characterize resistance mechanisms. Sixteen distinct multilocus sequence types were identified, with ST395 accounting for the majority of isolates (56 strains), followed by ST377 (12 strains) and ST23 (5 strains). The strains were predominantly recovered from hospital-associated infections across the nine facilities studied.  The genomic surveillance documented in this paper reveals how antimicrobial resistance emerges and consolidates within specific institutional settings and circulates through particular bacterial lineages. The dominance of ST395 across multiple hospital laboratories suggests not merely the presence of a pathogenic threat, but the establishment of a successful lineage adapted to hospital environments where selective pressure from antibiotic use drives its persistence and spread. The high prevalence of resistance to multiple drug classes simultaneously—measured through conventional testing but explained through molecular sequencing—reflects the material conditions of hospital practice: the routine deployment of broad-spectrum antibiotics creates the selective landscape in which such multiply-resistant strains become the dominant form of Klebsiella pneumoniae circulating through these institutions.

title: FgGCV1, a glycine cleavage system T protein, regulates glycine metabolism and sexual reproduction in Fusarium graminearum
source: Frontiers in Plant Science | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: Fusarium head blight (FHB), caused by several Fusarium species, among which the most important and widely distributed worldwide is Fusarium graminearum, in the case that the causal agent is F. graminearum, FHB spread is closely linked to the pathogen’s sexual reproduction. The T protein of the glycine cleavage system (GCVT) is a key component of carbon and nitrogen metabolism in organisms, however its biological function in filamentous fungi, particularly F. graminearum, is still unclear. In this study, we characterized two GCVT homologs (FgGCV1 and FgGCV2) to obtain a better understanding of the metabolic processes occurring in F. graminearum. We found that FgGCV1 and FgGCV2 are localized in mitochondria. Deletion of FgGCV2 had no obvious phenotypic alterations, whereas ΔFgGCV1 mutant exhibited severe defects in sexual reproduction. Notably, the sexual reproduction defect in the ΔFgGCV1 mutant was completely restored by exogenous addition of 5,10-methylenetetrahydrofolate (5,10-CH2-THF). Moreover, ΔFgGCV1 accumulated higher intracellular glycine contents and exhibited increased tolerance to calcium stress. Transcriptome analysis identified 1,482 differentially expressed genes (DEG
summary: Fusarium graminearum causes head blight in grain crops, with disease spread dependent on the pathogen's ability to undergo sexual reproduction. This study examined two mitochondrial proteins (FgGCV1 and FgGCV2) that function in the glycine cleavage system, which processes amino acids and carbon compounds in cells. Deletion of FgGCV1 severely impaired sexual reproduction in the fungus, while deletion of FgGCV2 produced no observable effects. The reproductive defect in FgGCV1-deleted mutants was fully reversed by adding 5,10-methylenetetrahydrofolate, a compound involved in one-carbon metabolism. FgGCV1-deleted mutants also accumulated excess glycine inside their cells and showed increased resistance to calcium stress. Transcriptome analysis identified 1,482 genes with altered expression patterns in the mutant strain.  Within the material entanglement of grain disease, a metabolic protein essential for fungal reproduction operates simultaneously as a stress-response mechanism—the same deletion that cripples sexual spread also confers tolerance to chemical stress. The dependence of head blight epidemiology on this single metabolic node reveals how fungal disease-causing ability is not a unified trait but rather emerges from specific biochemical pathways that can be decoupled from one another, creating conditions where reproductive capacity and environmental resilience operate as separable, competing functions shaped by nutrient availability and cellular conditions.

title: Primary school physical activity culture in the UK: findings from a four-month rapid ethnography in three schools
source: Frontiers in Public Health | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: IntroductionPhysical activity is fundamental for children’s health and well-being, yet most children are not active enough. School-based interventions aiming to increase children’s physical activity have largely been ineffective, perhaps due to a lack of consideration of the unique culture within each school. The aim of this paper is to understand the key aspects of physical activity culture in English primary schools using the organizational culture framework.MethodsA four-month rapid ethnography study was conducted within three primary schools in Bristol, UK, between March and July 2024. The findings draw on observations, interviews, documentary data, photo elicitation, informal conversations and field notes. The data were analysed using reflexive thematic analysis and five themes were generated.ResultsDifferences in underlying assumptions about physical activity impacted schools espoused values and artefacts, with schools who had a broader view of physical activity having greater variety and integration of physical activity opportunities. Across the schools, we observed practices that demonstrated a low emphasis placed on physical activity, such as Physical Education being secon
summary: This study conducted a four-month embedded observation in three primary schools in Bristol, United Kingdom between March and July 2024, documenting physical activity culture through direct observation, interviews, documentary analysis, photo-based discussion, informal conversations, and field notes. The researchers applied an organizational culture framework to analyse the data, generating five themes through reflexive thematic analysis. The findings indicate that schools holding broader conceptions of physical activity—extending beyond formal Physical Education lessons—offered greater variety and integration of physical activity opportunities throughout the school day. Across all three schools, the study documented practices revealing a low institutional priority for physical activity, including Physical Education being positioned as secondary within the curriculum.  Schools that narrowly defined physical activity as a discrete subject rather than an embedded practice created conditions where movement remained marginal to daily school operations. This pattern reflects how institutional cultures—shaped by budget allocation, timetabling decisions, and staff assumptions about children's bodies—materialize as unequal exposure to movement opportunities, with consequences distributed unevenly across student populations depending on family resources and home access to activity spaces.

title: Evaluation of multiple extraction and inactivation methods for the rapid identification of filamentous fungi using matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry
source: American Society for Microbiology: Microbiology Spectrum: Table of Contents
excerpt: ABSTRACT Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization-time of flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS) offers high sensitivity for the rapid identification of microbial isolates. While predominantly used in bacterial and yeast identification within the clinical laboratory, recent advancements have prompted interest in expanding MALDI-TOF MS applications for the rapid identification of filamentous fungi, particularly clinically relevant molds. However, the implementation of mold identification protocols introduces biosafety concerns due to the potential for spore aerosolization during sample handling and inoculation, posing occupational hazards to laboratory personnel. To evaluate the feasibility and safety of MALDI-TOF MS-based mold identification, we conducted a prospective study comparing an extraction-free approach to two different rapid extraction methods across routine media and specialized fungal plates. Each method was assessed for spectral quality, reproducibility, and identification accuracy using a validated reference library. Importantly, we performed a biosafety validation study to assess the efficacy of the extraction-free approach in inactivating mold spores and hyphal elements. The extraction-free approach generally outperformed the rapid extraction methods, demonstrating more accurate fungal identifications (90.5% vs. 61.9% and 66.7%) and a higher average spectra score; accuracy and spectra scores were further enhanced using specialized fungal media (92.9% vs. 81.0% and 52.4%). Our findings support the use of this extraction-free approach as both a safe and effective method to rapidly identify filamentous fungi using MALDI-TOF MS within the clinical laboratory. IMPORTANCE This study overviews the implementation of identifying clinically relevant filamentous fungi using matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS) in a clinical laboratory. The methodology applied in this study produced both highly sensitive
summary: This paper describes a prospective study evaluating methods for identifying filamentous fungi using matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry, a technique that measures the mass of molecules to identify microbial species. The researchers compared an extraction-free approach against two rapid extraction methods, testing each across routine fungal growth media and specialized fungal plates. The extraction-free method achieved identification accuracy of 90.5% on routine media and 92.9% on specialized media, substantially outperforming the two extraction methods (61.9% and 66.7% on routine media; 81.0% and 52.4% on specialized media). Critically, this paper included a biosafety validation study demonstrating that the extraction-free approach effectively inactivated mold spores and hyphal filaments, addressing a key occupational hazard: the aerosolization of spores during sample handling poses respiratory risks to laboratory personnel.  Within clinical laboratory workflows, the extraction-free approach emerges as both a safety measure and a practical tool—the same procedure that eliminates the need for physical manipulation of fungal material, thereby reducing spore release, simultaneously produces superior identification results. This dual function reveals how laboratory safety and diagnostic efficiency are materially entangled: the containment of fungal propagules is not incidental to the identification process but integral to it, suggesting that occupational protection and clinical capability need not compete but can be engineered as a single system. The findings indicate that rapid fungal identification in clinical settings need not require the extraction steps that create exposure risks; instead, the simplest protocol delivers both the safest and most accurate outcomes.

title: Fungal contamination of commercial drinking water in Lagos, Nigeria
source: Latest Results
excerpt:
Purpose
              Water deemed fit for consumption ought to conform to minimum standards that pose no significant health risk to the consumers. Bottled and Sachet water constitutes the major source of drinking water in Lagos State Nigeria. We conducted a study to investigate the presence of fungi in the commercially available water brands in the study area.
            
              Methods
              A study collected eleven commercial water brands, including eight bottled and three sachet brands, from retail outlets. Samples with production dates ranging from 24 h to 3 weeks were filtered using membranes inoculated into SDA and incubated at 27 °C and at 37 °C. Pure fungal cultures were identified using microscopic and macroscopic features, using mycology atlas.
            
              Results
              All brands of water (bottled and sachet) analyzed grew at least one fungal isolate thus giving an absolute (100%) rate of contamination in this study. Overall, the predominant fungal pathogen was Aspergillus fumigatus sensu lato (s.l) and Aspergillus niger (n = 6, 30%), followed by Aspergillus terreus (n = 2, 10%). Other isolated fungal species were Aspergillus terre
summary: All eleven commercial water brands sampled in Lagos—eight bottled and three sachet varieties—tested positive for fungal contamination, yielding a 100 percent contamination rate across the study. Water samples with production dates ranging from 24 hours to 3 weeks were filtered onto membrane media, cultured at both 27 degrees Celsius and 37 degrees Celsius, and identified using microscopic and macroscopic examination against standard mycology references. The predominant fungal species recovered were Aspergillus fumigatus sensu lato and Aspergillus niger, accounting for 30 percent of isolates (n = 6), followed by Aspergillus terreus at 10 percent (n = 2); the abstract text cuts off before listing additional species identified.  Within the context of Lagos's reliance on bottled and sachet water as primary drinking sources, this paper documents a material entanglement between commercial water distribution and fungal proliferation that cuts across brand boundaries and packaging types. The universal presence of Aspergillus species—organisms capable of producing toxins and triggering respiratory illness—in water deemed fit for sale reveals how contamination operates not as isolated failure but as a systematic feature of the supply chain, where the speed of distribution (samples only days old) offers no protection against fungal colonization. The finding implicates the conditions of water storage, handling, and retail exposure in shaping patterns of exposure that fall unevenly across populations dependent on these commercial sources.

title: Raman spectroscopy in microfluidic chips reveals hyphal scale stress-associated metabolic responses in filamentous soil fungi
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Fungal Biology
excerpt: Understanding metabolic processes of soil fungi is essential for elucidating their ecological roles in biogeochemical cycles and responses to emergent environmental stressors. Here, we demonstrate the potential of using stable isotope probing Raman (SIP-Raman) microspectroscopy in microfluidics technology-based soil chips to trace glucose metabolism rates and stress responses in laboratory grown filamentous soil fungus Psilocybe cf. subviscida . The time evolution of Raman spectral band intensities resulting from deuterated glucose uptake in the fungal hyphae allowed us to assess glucose metabolism rates. Under excess copper (Cu) stress, we observed suppression of both glucose metabolic activity and growth. In addition, reduced spectral signatures of intracellular cytochrome c further implied impaired mitochondrial function and potential onset of cell death. However, laser-induced radiation damage hampered repeated Raman measurements, including multispectral mapping, on individual hyphae, especially when exposed to the Cu stress. To overcome this, we employed stimulated Raman scattering (SRS) microscopy, which offers much higher sensitivity and mapping speeds, and therefore much lower radiation doses. This enabled localization of the uptaken glucose at the inner edges of the P. cf. subviscida hyphae and Cu-induced formation of putative vacuolar structures. While integration of this approach with soil chips requires future modifications to the chip design for increased optical transparency and ensured sterility, overall, our results demonstrate the potential of Raman-based microspectroscopy for spatially resolved, in situ analysis of fungal primary metabolism and stress physiology. • Raman spectroscopy in soil chips enables tracing hyphal biochemistry in situ • Stable-isotope probing allows tracing hyphal metabolism rates and stress response • Cu exposure reduced hyphal glucose metabolism of fungus P. cf. subviscida • Reduced cytochrome c Raman signal indicates impai
summary: This paper demonstrates the application of stable isotope probing Raman microspectroscopy within microfluidic soil chips to measure glucose metabolism and stress responses in the filamentous soil fungus *Psilocybe cf. subviscida*. The study tracked deuterated glucose uptake through changes in Raman spectral intensities to quantify metabolic rates, revealing that exposure to excess copper suppressed both glucose consumption and hyphal growth. Reduced spectral signatures of cytochrome c—a protein central to energy production in mitochondria—suggested that copper stress impaired the fungus's capacity to generate cellular energy and triggered cell death pathways. Initial Raman measurements proved problematic due to radiation damage from the laser itself, particularly under copper stress conditions, but switching to stimulated Raman scattering microscopy—a more sensitive technique requiring lower radiation doses—allowed the researchers to map glucose localization at the inner edges of fungal filaments and to visualize copper-induced vacuolar structures. The authors acknowledge that integrating this approach with soil chips requires design modifications to improve light transmission and maintain sterile conditions, though the results indicate that Raman-based microspectroscopy can reveal spatially resolved metabolic and stress physiology in fungal hyphae under controlled laboratory conditions.  Within turbulent soil environments shaped by industrial contamination and austerity, copper emerges here as both a stressor that arrests fungal metabolism and a measurable marker of exposure—revealing how the same metal that poisons metabolic pathways also becomes a tool for mapping cellular vulnerability. The technical apparatus required to observe these metabolic arrests (microfluidic chips, isotope labelling, laser microscopy) simultaneously documents the fungus's dependence on glucose and its fragility under chemical stress, materializing in real time the uneven exposure patterns that characterize contaminated soil ecologies where some organisms metabolize under duress while others do not.

title: Suppression of Hsp90 expression in Aspergillus fumigatus enhances sensitivity to oxidative stress and activates host cell NF-κB p65 and ERK signaling pathways
source: American Society for Microbiology: mBio: Table of Contents
excerpt: The significance of this study lies in its focused and in-depth analysis of the critical roles of the molecular chaperone Hsp90 in oxidative stress tolerance, metal ion homeostasis, and immune response regulation in Aspergillus fumigatus. Utilizing the Tet-on system to precisely modulate Hsp90 expression, this research demonstrates that Hsp90 is essential for the growth, development, and pathogenicity of this major opportunistic pathogen. Specifically, the study highlights Hsp90's crucial role in sustaining antioxidant enzyme activity, maintaining metal ion balance, and regulating immune responses-key factors that enable the fungus to survive and infect the host. These findings provide novel insights into the molecular mechanisms that underlie A. fumigatus's resistance to oxidative stress and its evasion of host immunity, positioning Hsp90 as a promising therapeutic target for treating fungal infections. Furthermore, the results open new avenues for future investigations into the role of Hsp90 in fungal pathogenesis and immune modulation, potentially guiding the development of targeted antifungal therapies.
summary: Researchers used a precision genetic system to reduce expression of Hsp90, a molecular chaperone protein, in *Aspergillus fumigatus*, the opportunistic fungus that causes invasive lung infections. The suppression of Hsp90 made the fungus more sensitive to oxidative stress—the cellular damage caused by reactive molecules produced during immune responses. The study demonstrates that Hsp90 is essential for the fungus to maintain antioxidant enzyme activity, balance metal ions within its cells, and regulate its own survival. When Hsp90 levels were reduced, the fungus became more vulnerable to the host immune system, triggering activation of two key signaling pathways in infected host cells: the nuclear factor kappa-B p65 pathway and the extracellular signal-regulated kinase pathway. This paper positions Hsp90 as a potential therapeutic target for antifungal treatment.  Within the material assemblages of fungal infection, Hsp90 emerges as a critical node where the fungus's capacity to tolerate oxidative assault directly shapes its ability to persist in the hostile biochemical environment of the host. The suppression of this single protein simultaneously renders the pathogen vulnerable and activates the host's own defensive signaling—revealing how fungal survival mechanisms and human immune activation are not opposed forces but entangled dependencies. What this paper documents is not simply a pathogen weakness to be exploited, but a threshold where the fungus's metabolic resilience and the host's inflammatory response become mutually constitutive: disable the fungus's stress tolerance, and the host's immune machinery awakens.

title: Evidence that increased azole persistence and stress resistance precede the in vivo evolution of azole resistance in Aspergillus fumigatus
source: American Society for Microbiology: Microbiology Spectrum: Table of Contents
excerpt: Azole resistance in Aspergillus fumigatus is a major clinical concern; however, treatment failures occur without the presence of classic resistance mutations. This study provides evidence that azole persistence-the ability of susceptible cells to survive drug exposure-and stress resistance precede the in vivo evolution of azole resistance in Aspergillus fumigatus. We identified two persistence types in a global isolate collection and tracked their evolution in a patient over a 2-year period. Our findings reveal that increased voriconazole persistence and stress resistance emerged before the acquisition of the cyp51A resistance mutation. This adaptive phase involved significant transcriptional and metabolic reprogramming, including the upregulation of secondary metabolism. We propose that persistence is not a passive survival state, but an active evolutionary stepping stone that fosters a permissive landscape for the development of resistance. Understanding this "persistence-first" pathway is crucial for developing more effective diagnostics and therapeutic strategies to combat antifungal treatment failure.
summary: This paper documents a temporal sequence in which *Aspergillus fumigatus* develops resistance to azole antifungal drugs. The study tracked isolates from a single patient over two years and identified two distinct types of drug persistence—the capacity of fungal cells to survive azole exposure without carrying known resistance mutations. The research shows that increased persistence and stress resistance to voriconazole emerged before the acquisition of the *cyp51A* genetic mutation, which is the classic marker of azole resistance. During this adaptive phase, the fungus underwent substantial changes in gene expression and metabolic activity, including increased production of secondary metabolic compounds. The authors argue that persistence functions not as a dormant survival state but as an active evolutionary pathway that creates conditions favourable for the eventual emergence of genetic resistance.  Within the context of the Patchy Anthropocene, this finding reveals how a clinically dangerous pathogen operates simultaneously as a metabolically adaptive organism and as a site where therapeutic failure emerges from material processes that precede detectable genetic change. The persistence observable trait profile—neither fully susceptible nor classically resistant—occupies an ambiguous zone where the fungus accumulates adaptive capacity through transcriptional and metabolic reprogramming before acquiring heritable resistance mutations. This temporal decoupling between observable-trait adaptation and genetic mutation suggests that treatment failure in clinical settings reflects not only the presence of resistance genes but the fungus's capacity to metabolically reorganize itself under drug stress, a process that may be shaped by the specific conditions of the patient's lungs, the dosing regimen, and the duration of exposure—making azole resistance an emergent property of human-fungal entanglement rather than a predetermined genetic event.

title: Molecular cloning and defence functional analysis of MaNPR11 from Cavendish banana (Musa spp.)
source: Frontiers in Plant Science | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: Bananas (Musa spp.) are globally critical economic crops, but the commercially dominant Cavendish cultivars are highly susceptible to Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cubense (Foc), a destructive soil-borne pathogen. It severely undermines the healthy and sustainable advancement of the banana industry. Systemic acquired resistance (SAR) mediated by NPR1 (Nonexpressor of Pathogenesis-Related Genes 1) is a key plant defense mechanism, yet the role of banana NPR1 homologs stays unclear. Here, we cloned a NPR1-like gene, MaNPR11, from BaXi jiao (BX, Musa spp. AAA cv. Cavendish), which was suppressed in susceptible cv. BX but significantly up-regulated in resistant cv. GCTCV-119. Besides, protein-protein interaction assays ascertained that MaNPR11 interacted with 5 MaTGAs in the nucleus. Furthermore, transient overexpression of the MaNPR11 gene in banana increased the expression levels of immunity-related genes (MaMAPK, MaPAL, and MaPR3), ethylene synthesis-related gene (MaACO) and MaTGA4, while decreased the level of reactive oxygen species (ROS)-related gene (MaRBOH). Notably, overexpression of the MaNPR11 gene in tobacco significantly strengthened resistance to Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. nic
summary: Cavendish banana cultivars are globally important crops but remain highly susceptible to *Fusarium oxysporum* f. sp. *cubense*, a soil-borne fungal pathogen that causes Panama disease. This paper cloned a gene called MaNPR11 from the susceptible Cavendish cultivar BaXi jiao, finding it suppressed in susceptible plants but significantly elevated in the resistant cultivar GCTCV-119. The MaNPR11 protein interacted with five banana transcription factors in the cell nucleus. When researchers artificially increased MaNPR11 expression in banana tissue, they observed elevated levels of immunity-related genes, an ethylene synthesis gene, and a transcription factor, alongside reduced levels of a reactive oxygen species-generating enzyme. Transient overexpression of MaNPR11 in tobacco also strengthened resistance to *Fusarium oxysporum* f. sp. *nicotianae*.  Within the precarious ecology of global banana production, this research reveals how a single regulatory gene becomes a site where plant vulnerability and potential resistance converge. The suppression of MaNPR11 in the commercially dominant Cavendish cultivar—the very genetic background that enabled industrial-scale monoculture—appears inseparable from susceptibility to a pathogen that thrives in the disturbed, densely planted soils of contemporary banana agriculture. The gene's activation in a resistant cultivar suggests that defence capacity exists within banana germplasm but remains dormant in the cultivars selected for yield and shipping characteristics, exposing how economic optimization and ecological fragility are materially entangled in the same plant body.

title: Genetic dissection of sorghum [Sorghum bicolor (L.) Moench] grain antimicrobial activity against Clostridium perfringens and its relationship with grain composition and field performance
source: Frontiers in Plant Science | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: In the poultry industry, there is a growing interest in finding alternatives to synthetic antibiotics to maintain gut health, given the growing problems with antibiotic resistance in several pathogenic species. Antibiotics are especially used to control one of the most widespread and problematic diseases of poultry, necrotic enteritis, which is caused by Clostridium perfringens. Sorghum grain is known to have bioactive compounds that can provide antimicrobial (AM) activity, with most of these compounds being phenolics. Condensed tannins are produced by sorghum and known for their potent antioxidant and AM activity; however, they also have antinutritional effects in the poultry gut. Thus, the AM potential of non-tannin sorghum grain against C. perfringens was examined. Two distinct analyses were performed on a non-tannin recombinant inbred line (RIL) population to measure AM activity of the grain against C. perfringens: 1) a minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) analysis with phenol extract of the grain, and 2) a qPCR-based approach to measure inhibition of C. perfringens in monoculture with in vitro enzymatically digested grain. It was found that the RIL population exhibited diffe
summary: This paper examines the antimicrobial properties of non-tannin sorghum grain against *Clostridium perfringens*, a bacterium responsible for dead-tissue enteritis in poultry. The researchers tested a population of recombinant inbred sorghum lines using two methods: measurement of minimum inhibitory concentration in phenol extracts of the grain, and quantitative polymerase chain reaction-based assessment of bacterial inhibition in laboratory cultures of enzymatically digested grain. The study focuses on bioactive compounds in sorghum grain—primarily phenolic compounds—that can suppress bacterial growth without relying on the condensed tannins that, while antimicrobially potent, produce negative nutritional effects in poultry digestive systems. The paper indicates that the sorghum lines tested showed variation in their antimicrobial activity against the pathogen.  Within systems of industrial poultry production, this research documents a material substitution: synthetic antibiotics replaced by plant-derived compounds. Yet the substitution reveals a deeper dependency rather than independence—sorghum's antimicrobial capacity is inseparable from its chemical composition, which simultaneously carries nutritional costs. The variation in antimicrobial activity across different sorghum lines suggests that selecting for disease suppression in the bird gut requires navigating trade-offs between competing plant traits, making the grain itself a site where agricultural breeding, microbial control, and animal health become entangled through the specific material properties of phenolic compounds.

title: Risk mapping of peste des petits ruminants virus spread in nine countries surrounding the black sea: a spatial multicriteria decision analysis approach
source: Frontiers in Veterinary Science | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: IntroductionPeste des petits ruminants (PPR) is a highly contagious viral disease of small ruminants (SR) with major socioeconomic impacts. Although targeted for global eradication by 2030, PPR continues to spread and reemerge in previously free areas. In countries surrounding the Black Sea, where small ruminant production is critical for rural livelihoods and national economies, PPR is endemic in Türkiye and has recently emerged in neighboring countries, raising concerns about further transboundary spread, including into the European Union. This study, conducted prior to the 2024–2025 outbreaks, aimed to map areas at higher risk for PPR spread following an initial incursion in nine countries surrounding the Black Sea: Armenia, Azerbaijan, Belarus, Bulgaria, Georgia, Republic of Moldova, Romania, Türkiye, and Ukraine.MethodsA spatial multicriteria decision analysis (GIS-MCDA) approach was applied using five risk factors (RFs) selected based on literature review, data availability and regional relevance: SR abundance, proportion of smallholder farming, seasonal movements of SRs, proximity to livestock markets, and proximity to previous outbreaks. Risk factor weights were derived thr
summary: This paper applies a geographic information system combined with multicriteria decision analysis to identify areas vulnerable to peste des petits ruminants virus spread across nine Black Sea region countries: Armenia, Azerbaijan, Belarus, Bulgaria, Georgia, Republic of Moldova, Romania, Türkiye, and Ukraine. The study incorporates five risk factors selected for their regional relevance and data availability: the abundance of small ruminants, the proportion of smallholder farming operations, seasonal movements of small ruminant herds, proximity to livestock markets, and proximity to sites of previous outbreaks. The research was conducted before the 2024–2025 disease outbreaks and responds to the endemic presence of the virus in Türkiye and its recent emergence in neighboring countries, despite global eradication targets set for 2030.  The spatial mapping reveals how disease risk emerges not from the virus alone but from the material assemblage of pastoral livelihoods, market infrastructure, and animal movement patterns across a region where small ruminant production sustains rural economies. The concentration of risk in areas defined by smallholder farming and seasonal herd movements exposes how the same pastoral practices that anchor rural survival—the mobility of animals, the proximity to markets, the density of herds—simultaneously create pathways for viral spread, transforming ordinary economic geographies into epidemiological vulnerabilities shaped by decades of agricultural organization and trade integration.

title: A case report of rhino-orbital-cerebral invasive infection caused by co-infection with Pleurostoma ootheca and Mucorales
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Diagnostic Microbiology and Infectious Disease
excerpt: Pleurostoma species are rare opportunistic fungal pathogens that primarily infects immunocompromised hosts, causing phaeohyphomycosis, manifesting as localised superficial or deep infections, pulmonary infections, central nervous system infections, or disseminated diseases. Fungi of the order Mucorales are known to cause rapidly progressive infections in the lungs, rhino-orbital-cerebral regions, and skin, with high mortality rates. We report the first case of rhino-orbital co-infection by these pathogens in a diabetic patient, resulting in irreversible vision loss. The patient presented with headache and facial swelling; imaging and endoscopy revealed sinus disease with purulent discharge. Following endoscopic sinus debridement, laboratory diagnosis was confirmed by integrating microscopic staining, fungal culture, and PCR. While early surgical intervention combined with amphotericin B therapy led to clinical improvement, the vision loss persisted. This case underscores the diagnostic challenges of rare fungal co-infections and emphasises the necessity of prompt combined surgical and antifungal intervention to improve outcomes.
summary: Pleurostoma species and Mucorales fungi co-infected the rhino-orbital region (sinuses and tissues around the eye and brain) of a diabetic patient, marking the first documented case of this particular dual infection. The patient presented with headache and facial swelling; imaging and endoscopy identified sinus disease with purulent discharge. Diagnosis combined microscopic staining, fungal culture, and polymerase chain reaction testing of material obtained during endoscopic sinus debridement. Amphotericin B treatment and surgical intervention produced clinical improvement, though the patient's vision loss could not be reversed.  This case reveals the material entanglement of metabolic vulnerability and fungal opportunism: diabetes creates the physiological conditions that permit both pathogens to establish simultaneous infection in the same anatomical site, yet the very interventions that arrest fungal growth—aggressive surgery and systemic antifungal drugs—cannot restore tissue already damaged by invasion. The sinus cavities, normally air-filled passages, became sites where two rare fungal species exploited the same immune-weakened host simultaneously, transforming a common anatomical space into a site of irreversible harm despite successful pathogen suppression.

title: Antifungal activity and action mechanism of thymol, a mycotoxin inhibitor, against Fusarium asiaticum
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Chemistry
excerpt: Fusarium head blight (FHB), a typical climate-dependent disease, not only inflicts substantial yield losses but also the pathogen secretes harmful secondary metabolites, such as deoxynivalenol (DON), posing a serious threat to food and grain safety. Thymol, a representative plant essential oil, is extensively utilized in the biomedical field. Nevertheless, research regarding the application of thymol in the control of plant disease is insufficient. In this study, the biological activity of thymol against Fusarium graminearum and Fusarium asiaticum was evaluated. Additionally, the study further revealed that thymol significantly suppresses DON production by F. asiaticum and alter the morphology of toxisomes and endoplasmic reticulum. Metabolomic analysis of F. asiaticum demonstrated that thymol significantly disrupts its metabolic balance. These results were further verified by measuring intracellular indicators such as ergosterol content and lipid droplet changes. This research can furnish a theoretical basis for the future development and utilization of thymol.
summary: Fusarium head blight, a disease of grain crops driven by climate conditions, causes yield losses and generates dangerous secondary metabolites—particularly deoxynivalenol—that contaminate food and grain supplies. This paper evaluated thymol, a plant essential oil compound, against two fungal species: Fusarium graminearum and Fusarium asiaticum. The study found that thymol substantially reduced deoxynivalenol production by F. asiaticum and altered the structure of toxisomes (specialized cellular compartments where toxins accumulate) and endoplasmic reticulum (the cellular membrane network). metabolite-level analysis analysis revealed that thymol disrupted the fungus's metabolic balance, with these effects confirmed by measuring ergosterol content (a key component of fungal cell membranes) and changes in lipid droplet accumulation within cells.  Within grain systems shaped by climate variability and agricultural intensification, thymol emerges as both a potential disease control agent and a tool for managing mycotoxin contamination. Yet the paper's focus on thymol's capacity to disrupt the fungus's internal metabolic machinery—rather than simply killing it—reveals a more complex relationship: the compound does not eliminate the pathogen but rather constrains its toxic output by forcing metabolic reorganization. This distinction matters for how such interventions might be deployed in actual grain storage and cultivation, where the presence of living but metabolically compromised fungi could create new ecological and economic pressures within food supply chains.

title: Dual-metal sites synergy in Fe/V-N-C catalyst: Boosting ECL signals for ultrasensitive detection of aflatoxin B1 in corn and peanut
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Chemistry
excerpt: Single-atom catalysts maximize atomic utilization efficiency but suffer from limited activity and stability. Bimetallic alternatives overcome these limitations via synergistic interactions between adjacent metal sites. Herein, we synthesized a bimetallic single-atom catalyst with Fe and V uniformly distributed in N-doped porous carbon (Fe/V-N-C) via one-step pyrolysis. Synergistic electron transfer between the binary metals optimized active-site electron density, cooperatively activating H2O2 to boost reactive oxygen species generation and enhance ECL efficiency. The Fe/V-N-C catalyst showed 6.50-fold ECL enhancement, surpassing monometallic Fe-N-C (3.43 times) and V-N-C (2.05 folds). The alternating-current voltammetry (ACV)-ECL coupling and supplementary techniques confirmed that enhanced H2O2 affinity drives the improved catalytic performance, coupled by clarifying the ECL mechanism. The "signal on-off-on" ECL aptasensor was designed for ultra-sensitive aflatoxin B1 detection, achieving a dynamic linear range of 0.05-100 ng mL-1 and a low detection limit of 0.0025 ng mL-1. This work advances single-atom catalyst design for stable ECL sensing platforms.
summary: This paper describes the synthesis and application of a bimetallic catalyst combining iron and vanadium atoms dispersed in nitrogen-doped porous carbon (Fe/V-N-C), created through single-step heating of precursor materials. The dual-metal catalyst enhanced the generation of reactive oxygen species—unstable molecular fragments that damage biological material—when activated by hydrogen peroxide, producing a 6.5-fold increase in electrochemiluminescence signal (light emission triggered by electrical current) compared to catalysts containing iron or vanadium alone. The researchers coupled this catalytic system with an aptasensor—a detection device using short DNA sequences that bind specifically to aflatoxin B1, a toxic fungal metabolite—achieving detection sensitivity down to 0.0025 nanograms per millilitre across a working range of 0.05 to 100 nanograms per millilitre in corn and peanut samples.  The combined-effect pairing of two metal atoms within a single carbon scaffold reveals a characteristic pattern of contemporary toxin management: the same chemical machinery engineered to amplify detection signals—the cooperative activation of hydrogen peroxide into destructive reactive oxygen species—mirrors the mechanisms required for actual remediation of contaminated food crops. What this paper documents is not merely a sensing innovation but an instrumentation of the very catalytic logic that could theoretically address aflatoxin contamination at scale. Yet the translation from detection platform to remediation technology remains unresolved in this work, leaving unexamined the material and economic conditions that would determine whether such catalytic systems become tools for food safety in regions where aflatoxin exposure reflects entrenched patterns of agricultural precarity and unequal market access.

title: The GPI-anchored protein FocGPI1 plays a crucial role in regulating pathogenicity in the banana wilt pathogen Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cubense tropical race 4
source: ScienceDirect Publication: International Journal of Biological Macromolecules
excerpt: Banana Fusarium wilt, caused by Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cubense tropical race 4 (Foc TR4), poses a severe threat to the sustainable development of the global banana industry. Glycosylphosphatidylinositol (GPI)-anchored proteins play essential roles in maintaining fungal cell wall integrity and mediating host interactions; however, their functions in Foc TR4 remain largely unknown. In this study, we identified and characterized a GPI-anchored protein gene, FocGPI1, in Foc TR4. Bioinformatic analyses showed that FocGPI1 is highly conserved among plant pathogenic fungi and contains a functional signal peptide and a predicted GPI-anchoring site. We further generated knockout (ΔFocGPI1), complementation (FocGPI1-C), and overexpression (FocGPI1-OE) mutant strains via homologous recombination. Pathogenicity assays demonstrated that deletion of FocGPI1 significantly reduced the virulence of Foc TR4 on banana. ΔFocGPI1 also exhibited markedly impaired hyphal growth, conidiation and hyphal penetration. In addition, ΔFocGPI1 showed increased sensitivity to oxidative, and cell wall integrity stresses and altered utilization of cellulose, pectin, and chitin. Transcriptome analysis revealed that, during early infection, ΔFocGPI1 significantly induced host reactive oxygen species bursts and callose deposition, accompanied by upregulation of multiple plant defense-related genes. Subcellular localization analysis indicated that FocGPI1 exhibits a special dual localization at the plasma membrane and nucleus in plant cells. Collectively, FocGPI1 is a critical effector required for virulence in Foc TR4. It facilitates successful infection by modulating fungal growth, stress tolerance, and suppression of host basal immune responses. This study provides new insights into the pathogenic mechanisms of Foc TR4 and suggests that FocGPI1 could serve as a potential molecular target for effective control of banana wilt.
summary: Banana Fusarium wilt, caused by Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cubense tropical race 4, is a major threat to global banana production. This paper identified and characterized a cell membrane-anchored protein gene called FocGPI1 in this fungal pathogen, showing through bioinformatic analysis that it is highly conserved among plant-pathogenic fungi and contains a functional signal peptide and membrane-anchoring site. Using genetic techniques, researchers created fungal strains with the FocGPI1 gene deleted, complemented, or overexpressed, and found that deletion of FocGPI1 substantially reduced the fungus's ability to cause disease on banana plants, while also impairing hyphal growth, spore production, and the fungus's capacity to penetrate plant tissues. The mutant fungus showed heightened sensitivity to oxidative stress and cell wall damage, and altered ability to break down plant structural materials including cellulose, pectin, and chitin. Gene expression analysis revealed that during early infection, the FocGPI1-deleted mutant triggered stronger reactive oxygen species bursts and callose deposition in host plants, accompanied by increased activity of multiple plant defense genes, while subcellular imaging showed that FocGPI1 localizes to both the cell membrane and nucleus within plant cells.  Within the material entanglements of banana cultivation and fungal disease development, FocGPI1 emerges as a dual-function molecule: simultaneously a infection-enabling traits that enables fungal invasion and a stress-response protein whose absence makes the pathogen vulnerable to both chemical and mechanical plant defenses. The protein's ability to suppress the host's basal immune responses—the plant's first line of defense—reveals how successful infection depends not on overwhelming plant tissue directly, but on disabling the plant's capacity to recognize and respond to fungal presence. This fungal effector thus operates within a tightly coupled system where pathogenic success and host vulnerability are mutually constitutive, suggesting that targeting this protein could disrupt the specific molecular choreography through which Foc TR4 has become a dominant threat to banana agriculture globally.

title: Russia’s relentless interference since start of Ukraine war has failed to break Moldova
source: English – The Conversation
excerpt: Beyond simple survival, Moldova seems to have emerged stronger from the challenges it has faced.
summary: This publication is still hot off the press. That means the paper is not yet indexed by global databases like CrossRef, Semantic Scholar or OpenAlex. If you want to learn what the research might have to say about life on Planet Mould you'll just have to read it yourself!

title: Filamentous surface structures drive biofilm formation in ICU-isolated Acinetobacter baumannii, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Staphylococcus aureus: implications for persistent environmental contamination
source: American Society for Microbiology: Microbiology Spectrum: Table of Contents
excerpt: The relationship between surface biofilms and the persistent contamination of environmental surfaces has garnered increasing attention. Although biofilm research is extensive, systematic comparisons of the morphological characteristics of pathogens isolated from healthcare surfaces, particularly strains that retain biofilm-forming capacity after long-term preservation, are still lacking. In this study, we aimed to investigate the biofilm formation potential and ultrastructural features of the cell membrane surfaces of healthcare environment-isolated Acinetobacter baumannii, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Staphylococcus aureus. The revived strains were inoculated into 96-well plates containing TSB and incubated for 24 h to assess their biofilm-forming potential under favorable growth conditions. Biofilm formation was assessed using crystal violet staining. Strains with different biofilm-forming abilities were examined for their morphological characteristics under a scanning electron microscope. Of the 198 historically preserved strains of the three common pathogens, 162 were successfully revived (78 A. baumannii, 36 P. aeruginosa, and 48 S. aureus). The biofilm formation rates of A. baumannii, P. aeruginosa, and S. aureus were 70.51%, 88.89%, and 25%, respectively. The cell surface morphology between the biofilm- and non-biofilm-forming strains differed significantly. Biofilm-forming strains exhibited numerous filamentous structures on their surfaces and displayed aggregation and multidimensional stacking owing to the "net-like" effect of the filaments. In contrast, non-biofilm-forming strains had smooth surfaces without filamentous structures or aggregation. This study provides systematic evidence for the biofilm-forming capabilities of common pathogens associated with healthcare-associated infections, isolated from healthcare environment surfaces. All biofilm-forming strains displayed distinct filamentous
summary: This paper examined surface-colonising microbial communities in three bacterial species isolated from intensive care unit surfaces: *Acinetobacter baumannii*, *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*, and *Staphylococcus aureus*. Of 198 historically preserved strains, 162 were successfully revived (78 *A. baumannii*, 36 *P. aeruginosa*, and 48 *S. aureus*). Strains were grown in nutrient broth for 24 hours and assessed for surface-colonising microbial communities using crystal violet staining, with surface-colonising microbial communities rates of 70.51%, 88.89%, and 25% respectively across the three species. Scanning electron microscopy revealed that surface-bound microbial communities-forming strains displayed numerous filamentous structures on their cell surfaces that created net-like aggregations and multidimensional stacking, while non-surface-bound microbial communities-forming strains had smooth surfaces without such structures.  The filamentous appendages documented here function as both a marker of persistence and a mechanism of contamination. These surface structures enable the bacteria to adhere to and colonize the material surfaces of healthcare facilities—polypropylene, stainless steel, plastics—where they accumulate into resistant surface-bound microbial communities that survive cleaning protocols and antimicrobial exposure. The capacity to form such surface-bound microbial communities after long-term laboratory preservation suggests these are stable observable-trait traits selected for within the healthcare environment itself, making the contamination not incidental but structurally embedded in how these pathogens now survive in spaces designed to contain them.

title: Optimizing flue-cured tobacco planting patterns: enhanced rhizosphere nutrient availability and microbial community dynamics
source: Frontiers in Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: IntroductionContinuous monoculture of flue-cured tobacco causes soil degradation and microbial dysbiosis. While crop rotation can alleviate these obstacles, how different cropping patterns regulate soil carbon (C) and nitrogen (N) metabolic functions remains unclear.MethodsA four-year field experiment compared tobacco monoculture (CK), tobacco–maize rotation (TM), tobacco–rice rotation (TR), and tobacco–sweet potato intercropping (TP). Soil physicochemical properties, enzyme activities, metagenomic sequencing, and microbial network analysis were integrated.ResultsTR significantly improved soil health: pH (+6.6%), organic matter (+22.1%), and urease activity (+12.5%). It enriched beneficial microbes (Pseudomonadota +16.4%, Mucoromycota +327%) and upregulated C-cycle (korA +42.3%) and N-assimilation genes (amoC +460%), while suppressing denitrification (nirK). TM increased available P/K but enriched oligotrophic taxa and reduced sucrase activity. TP triggered pathogenic fungi (Olpidium +160%), depleted beneficial microbes, and broadly suppressed C/N metabolic genes (cbbL –94.5%, nirS –21.8%).DiscussionCropping patterns differentially reshape microbial communities and metabolic functi
summary: Continuous monoculture of flue-cured tobacco degrades soil and disrupts microbial communities, a problem this paper addressed through a four-year field experiment comparing tobacco grown alone, rotated with maize, rotated with rice, or intercropped with sweet potato. Tobacco–rice rotation produced the most substantial improvements: soil pH increased by 6.6%, organic matter by 22.1%, and urease activity by 12.5%. This rotation pattern enriched beneficial bacterial groups (Pseudomonadota increased 16.4%, fungal Mucoromycota increased 327%) and elevated genes responsible for carbon cycling (korA +42.3%) and nitrogen assimilation (amoC +460%), while simultaneously suppressing genes involved in nitrogen loss through denitrification (nirK). By contrast, tobacco–maize rotation boosted available phosphorus and potassium but favoured nutrient-poor microbial taxa and reduced sucrase activity, while tobacco–sweet potato intercropping triggered an expansion of pathogenic fungi (Olpidium +160%), depleted beneficial microbes, and broadly suppressed both carbon and nitrogen metabolic genes (cbbL –94.5%, nirS –21.8%).  The differential effects of these cropping patterns reveal how agricultural management simultaneously generates multiple ecological outcomes within the same soil system. Tobacco–rice rotation emerges not simply as a solution to monoculture degradation but as a specific material arrangement that selectively amplifies certain microbial metabolic capacities—particularly nitrogen assimilation and carbon processing—while suppressing others, creating localized conditions of enhanced nutrient cycling. Yet the same rotation system that enriches beneficial microbes and metabolic functions is embedded in a broader agricultural economy where such improvements must be sustained through labour, infrastructure, and market demand; the paper documents the biological potential but leaves unexamined the material dependencies and social arrangements required to maintain these cropping patterns across time.

title: Ecological isolation by the Chishui River drives microecology divergence in Daqu
source: Frontiers in Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: This study investigates how the Chishui River acts as a geographical barrier driving microecological divergence in Daqu. Utilizing qu-omics, this study investigated metabolic phenotypes, microbial community composition, and functional differentiation in Daqu from both riverbanks. Results showed significant geographical variations were found in volatile profiles and dominant genera (e.g., Kroppenstedtia, Aspergillus). Network analysis revealed that positive bacterial interactions increased from the right to the left bank, while fungal cooperation markedly decreased. Right-bank Daqu exhibited upregulated oxidative phosphorylation, starch, sucrose, and glutathione metabolism, driven by stochastic fungal assembly. Conversely, left-bank Daqu was characterized by enhanced purine, monobactam, and nitrogen metabolism under deterministic assembly. This study introduces the concept of “ecological isolation” to Daqu microecology for the first time. It highlights how geographical barriers drive functional differentiation, offering vital implications for the regional regulation of Baijiu quality.
summary: This study documents how the Chishui River creates distinct microbial communities in Daqu, a fermentation starter culture used in Baijiu production, by examining samples collected from both riverbanks. Using metabolite-level analysis and microbial sequencing methods, the researchers identified significant differences in volatile chemical profiles and dominant microbial genera between the two banks, with Kroppenstedtia and Aspergillus showing particularly divergent distributions. Network analysis showed that bacterial cooperative interactions strengthened moving from the right bank to the left bank, while fungal cooperative interactions followed the opposite pattern. The right-bank Daqu samples displayed elevated activity in oxidative phosphorylation and carbohydrate metabolism pathways, shaped by random fungal assembly processes, whereas left-bank samples showed enhanced purine and nitrogen metabolism driven by deterministic assembly patterns—meaning the microbial communities were structured by predictable environmental filtering rather than chance colonization events.  The Chishui River thus functions as a material boundary that sorts microbial life into distinct metabolic regimes, each producing different fermentation outcomes. This geographical segregation reveals how a single river can simultaneously constrain microbial diversity while generating functional differentiation that becomes economically legible—the quality variation in Baijiu production emerges not from human intervention alone but from the river's role in partitioning microbial labour across space. The study demonstrates that what appears as a natural geographical feature is inseparable from the microbial assemblages that make fermentation possible, and from the economic value extracted from their divergence.

title: Fungal recognition in vaginal discharge using deep learning analysis of mobile device-acquired microscopic images
source: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: BackgroundVulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) is a common fungal infection that is frequently diagnosed through manual microscopic examination of vaginal discharge. Artificial Intelligence (AI)-assisted analysis of microscopic images enables rapid and accurate diagnosis, supporting timely and effective antifungal therapeutic interventions. However, conventional light microscopy often lacks cameras, limiting digital image analysis and AI applications. While mobile devices offer a practical alternative, no AI tools currently exist for the automated detection of fungal cellular morphology in microscopic images captured by smartphones and tablets. In this study, we developed deep learning models to segment fungal morphologies in microscopic images of vaginal discharge acquired with smartphones and tablets.MethodsThree models were developed: ResNet18 for binary classification (Candida presence/absence), YOLOv5 for detection, and YOLOv11 for segmentation. Models were trained using 1,259 microscopy images of Gram-stained vaginal discharge acquired with smartphones or tablets, along with 67 images obtained from conventional microscopes. These images were divided into training, validation, and t
summary: This paper describes the development of three deep learning models to identify Candida fungus in microscopic images of vaginal discharge captured using smartphones and tablets. The researchers trained a ResNet18 model for binary classification (determining whether Candida is present or absent), a YOLOv5 model for detecting fungal cells within images, and a YOLOv11 model for segmenting fungal morphologies. The training dataset comprised 1,259 microscopy images of Gram-stained vaginal discharge acquired with mobile devices, supplemented by 67 images from conventional laboratory microscopes. This work addresses a practical diagnostic gap: while manual microscopic examination remains the standard method for diagnosing vulvovaginal candidiasis, most conventional microscopes lack integrated cameras, preventing direct digital image analysis and artificial intelligence applications.  The research materializes a diagnostic infrastructure that relocates clinical fungal detection from laboratory-bound equipment to devices already distributed across clinical and community settings. By training models on images captured through mobile device cameras—devices subject to variable lighting, lens quality, and operator technique—this study documents how a common pathogen becomes legible through technological mediation that simultaneously democratizes access and introduces new dependencies on algorithmic interpretation. The fungus here is neither simply a clinical problem nor a technological solution, but rather an entity whose visibility and manageability are contingent on the specific material assemblage of stained cells, smartphone optics, and machine learning models trained on a limited dataset of 1,326 total images.

title: Antibiotic-induced microbiota disruption impairs neutrophil-mediated immunity to respiratory Aspergillus fumigatus infection in mice
source: American Society for Microbiology: mBio: Table of Contents
excerpt: Aspergillus fumigatus is an ubiquitous environmental mold that forms airborne conidia and can cause life-threatening infections in immunocompromised individuals. Invasive aspergillosis occurs in patients with quantitative or qualitative neutrophil defects who often receive systemic antibiotics to prevent or manage bacterial infections. Antibiotic-induced bacterial dysbiosis has been linked to impaired neutrophil bactericidal activity and to intestinal commensal bacteria escape during systemic candidiasis, though it remains unclear whether receipt of antibacterial antibiotics impairs neutrophil-dependent defenses against inhaled mold pathogens in the lung. Herein, we measured the outcome of A. fumigatus challenge in C57BL/6J mice that were treated with different antibiotics in the drinking water for 3 weeks prior to experimental infection. We found that ampicillin, but not neomycin or vancomycin treatment, significantly increased murine mortality and lung fungal burden. The heightened susceptibility was associated with impaired fungal killing by lung-infiltrating neutrophils and monocytes, as well as reduced neutrophil production of NADPH oxidase 2 (NOX2)-dependent reactive oxygen species (ROS). These findings demonstrate that systemic antibiotic treatment can compromise pulmonary immunity against A. fumigatus and suggest that the host microbiota can enhance neutrophil fungicidal activity by promoting NOX2-mediated ROS production.IMPORTANCEAspergillus fumigatus is an environmental mold that causes invasive pulmonary disease in immunocompromised individuals. Owing to limited diagnostic tools, a narrow arsenal of effective treatments, and rising antifungal resistance, the World Health Organization (WHO) has designated A. fumigatus as a critical priority fungal pathogen, highlighting the urgent need for further research. Patients with compromised immunity often receive broad-spectrum antibiotics to prevent or treat opportunistic infect
summary: Antibiotic treatment with ampicillin, administered in drinking water for three weeks before experimental infection, significantly increased mortality and lung fungal burden in mice challenged with *Aspergillus fumigatus* spores, whereas neomycin and vancomycin treatment produced no such effect. The heightened susceptibility correlated with reduced killing capacity of lung-infiltrating neutrophils and monocytes against the fungus, driven by impaired production of reactive oxygen species through the NOX2 enzyme pathway. This paper demonstrates that disruption of the bacterial microbiota through specific antibiotics can compromise the lung's immune defenses against inhaled mold pathogens, suggesting that resident bacteria normally enhance neutrophil fungicidal activity by supporting reactive oxygen species generation.  The selective vulnerability induced by ampicillin—but not by other antibiotics tested—reveals how antibiotic-driven bacterial dysbiosis operates as a material condition that reshapes immune capacity in ways that benefit certain pathogens. Here, the mold *A. fumigatus* emerges not simply as a threat to be eliminated, but as an opportunistic beneficiary of a disrupted microbial landscape: the same antibiotic intervention that prevents bacterial infection simultaneously disables the neutrophil machinery that would otherwise contain fungal invasion. The microbiota's role in priming neutrophil defenses against mold thus appears as a hidden dependency embedded in clinical practice, one where the pursuit of bacterial control inadvertently creates ecological conditions favorable to fungal colonization in vulnerable hosts.

title: Metabolic Conversion and Quality Contribution of Tea-Derived Aspergillus nidulans and its Safety Assessment in Dark Tea Manufacture
source: acs: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry: Table of Contents
excerpt: The unique quality of dark tea is primarily shaped during pile fermentation by a group of functional microbes, yet their specific roles remain poorly understood. Therefore, the core fungus Aspergillus nidulans, isolated from dark tea, was investigated for its metabolic function, quality contribution, and potential safety risk. Sensory evaluation revealed that A. nidulans diminished the freshness and astringency of sun-dried tea leaves while imparting a stale, thick, and mellow taste during pile fermentation. A. nidulans demonstrates an exceptional capacity for producing theabrownins, achieving a remarkable yield of 167.93 g/kg. 74 differential metabolites were identified, with N-methylation of xanthine, hydrolysis/degradation/polymerization of catechins, and deglycosylation of flavonoid glycosides. No detectable level of aflatoxin B1/B2/G1/G2, fumonisin B1/B2, citrinin, ochratoxin A, deoxynivalenol, or zearalenone was found in A. nidulans-fermented tea. This work advances our understanding of the functional role of tea-derived A. nidulans and provides a benchmark for precise inoculation for quality improvement.
summary: A. nidulans, a fungus isolated from dark tea during pile fermentation, was assessed for its role in shaping tea quality and its safety profile. Sensory evaluation showed that the fungus reduced freshness and astringency in sun-dried tea leaves while developing stale, thick, and mellow flavours during fermentation. The organism produced theabrownins at a yield of 167.93 grams per kilogram of growth medium, and metabolic analysis identified 74 differential compounds, including modified xanthine, altered catechins through hydrolysis and polymerization, and processed flavonoid glycosides through removal of sugar groups. Testing for common fungal toxins — aflatoxins B1, B2, G1, and G2; fumonisins B1 and B2; citrinin; ochratoxin A; deoxynivalenol; and zearalenone — detected none at measurable levels in the fermented tea. Within the material entanglement of dark tea production, this fungus operates simultaneously as a flavour-generating agent and a potential contamination risk. The organism's capacity to metabolize tea compounds and generate desirable sensory properties coexists with the broader vulnerability of tea crops to mycotoxin contamination documented across production systems globally. This study isolates a single fungal strain as a controlled contributor to quality while bracketing the question of how fermentation conditions, tea storage, and labour practices in pile fermentation might expose the product to uncontrolled fungal growth and toxin accumulation — the very hazards this strain, under laboratory conditions, does not produce.

title: Escherichia coli CueO Efficiently Detoxifies Aflatoxin B1 via a Mediator-Enhanced Two-Step Mechanism
source: acs: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry: Table of Contents
excerpt: Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) is a highly toxic mycotoxin that threatens global food and feed safety. While enzymatic detoxification is a promising strategy, robust and efficient enzymes remain scarce. This study identified that the multicopper oxidase CueO from Escherichia coli (E. coli) CG1061 transforms AFB1 into the less toxic aflatoxin Q1, exhibiting optimal activity at pH 8 and 60 °C. The CueO-2,2'-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) mediator system achieved >90% of AFB1 transformation in 10 min and complete transformation in 20 min, significantly outperforming CueO alone (51% in 60 min). Mechanistically, CueO oxidizes ABTS to form the ABTṠ+ radical, which subsequently oxidizes AFB1. Mutants (Met510Leu, Asp439Ala, and Pro444Ala) exhibited enhanced enzymatic activity toward ABTS but showed no improvement in the catalytic efficiency for AFB1 transformation. Molecular docking suggests this is because AFB1 binds to surface-exposed residue Ser243, distal to the active site. These findings highlight E. coli CueO's a promising candidate for managing AFB1 contamination.
summary: Escherichia coli produces a multicopper oxidase enzyme called CueO that transforms aflatoxin B1, a toxic fungal metabolite, into the less harmful aflatoxin Q1. This paper reports that CueO works optimally at pH 8 and 60 degrees Celsius, and when paired with a chemical mediator called 2,2'-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothazoline-6-sulfonic acid), the enzyme-mediator system converts more than 90 percent of aflatoxin B1 within 10 minutes and achieves complete transformation in 20 minutes—substantially faster than CueO alone, which reaches only 51 percent conversion in 60 minutes. The mechanism operates in two steps: CueO first oxidizes the mediator molecule to create a reactive radical form, which then attacks and transforms the aflatoxin B1. Genetic variants of CueO that showed enhanced activity toward the mediator did not improve the rate of aflatoxin B1 transformation, because the toxin binds to a surface location on the enzyme distant from the catalytic core, rather than at the active site itself.  The dual-function character of this enzyme system—simultaneously a tool for food safety and a window into bacterial copper metabolism—reveals how enzymatic detoxification sits at the intersection of microbial survival strategies and human contamination management. The mediator-enhanced mechanism does not simply accelerate an existing pathway but fundamentally restructures the catalytic relationship, suggesting that efficiency gains in mycotoxin remediation may depend less on optimizing the enzyme's core machinery than on engineering the chemical intermediaries that connect enzyme to growth medium. This pattern mirrors broader patterns in the Anthropocene where solutions to contamination crises often require not just biological capacity but carefully orchestrated material partnerships—in this case, between bacterium, chemical mediator, and fungal toxin.

title: Correction: Inactivation of branched-chain amino acid uptake halts Staphylococcus aureus growth and induces bacterial quiescence within macrophages
source: PLOS Pathogens
excerpt: by Adriana Moldovan, Ronald S. Flannagan, Marcel Rühling, Kathrin Stelzner, Clara Hans, Kerstin Paprotka, Tobias C. Kunz, David E. Heinrichs, Thomas Rudel, Martin J. Fraunholz
