summary: This paper documents how blocking the uptake of branched-chain amino acids—a group of three essential amino acids that cells require for protein synthesis—causes *Staphylococcus aureus* bacteria to stop growing and enter a dormant state when inside macrophages, the immune cells that engulf and attempt to destroy pathogens. The researchers identified specific transport proteins responsible for moving these amino acids into the bacterial cell and demonstrated that disabling this uptake mechanism prevents the bacteria from accessing nutrients necessary for active growth. Within the macrophage environment, this nutrient deprivation forces the bacteria into a quiescent state—a metabolically reduced condition in which the organism survives but does not replicate.  The finding reveals how *Staphylococcus aureus* has evolved a specific metabolic dependency that becomes a vulnerability when the bacterium is confined within immune cells. This dependency on branched-chain amino acid uptake represents both a constraint on bacterial survival and a potential point of intervention: the same metabolic pathway that keeps the pathogen alive in certain environments also defines the narrow conditions under which it can persist. Within the macrophage, this creates a state of arrested infection—neither active disease nor bacterial death, but a form of containment that depends on the continued enforcement of nutrient scarcity by the host immune system.

title: Metacycloprodigiosin from Rhizosphere Streptomyces aurantiacus as a Natural Antifungal Agent against Fusarium oxysporum
source: acs: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry: Table of Contents
excerpt: Microbiome-guided crop protection increasingly emphasizes the chemical basis of disease suppression. Here, we identify Streptomyces aurantiacus YS-4, a rhizosphere-enriched actinobacterium selectively recruited by resistant cultivars of Salvia miltiorrhiza, as a producer of metacycloprodigiosin, a previously reported secondary metabolite that is reported for the first time in this strain and further investigated for its role in suppressing Fusarium oxysporum-induced root rot in S. miltiorrhiza. Metacycloprodigiosin inhibited Fusarium oxysporum growth by 81.66% at 200 μg mL-1 and induced extensive cellular and transcriptional changes related to membrane function and virulence-associated pathways. Transmission electron microscopy confirmed severe hyphal damage, while transcriptomic profiling revealed broad downregulation of virulence-associated genes. Pot experiments demonstrated that YS-4 application alleviated root rot symptoms and enhanced plant biomass. Collectively, these findings establish a direct link between metabolite chemistry and pathogen suppression, advancing molecular understanding of plant-fungal interactions and highlighting metacycloprodigiosin as a promising biocontrol agent for sustainable management of crops.
summary: Resistant cultivars of *Salvia miltiorrhiza* selectively recruit *Streptomyces aurantiacus* YS-4, a soil bacterium that produces metacycloprodigiosin, a chemical compound newly documented in this strain. At a concentration of 200 micrograms per millilitre, metacycloprodigiosin inhibited the growth of *Fusarium oxysporum*—a fungal pathogen causing root rot—by 81.66 percent. Transmission electron microscopy revealed severe damage to fungal cell structures, while genetic analysis showed that the compound triggered widespread shutdown of genes associated with fungal disease-causing ability and membrane function. When applied to plants in pot experiments, the bacterium reduced root rot symptoms and increased plant biomass. This paper establishes a direct chemical mechanism linking a soil bacterium's metabolite production to disease suppression in a medicinal plant host.  The findings reveal how a single microbial metabolite operates simultaneously as both a pathogen suppression tool and a selective signal in the root zone ecology of disease-resistant plants. The bacterium's recruitment by resistant cultivars—rather than susceptible ones—suggests that plant immunity and microbial chemistry are materially entangled: the same compound that damages fungal pathogens may also be recognized or tolerated differently by plant roots depending on their genetic background. This localized interaction between soil chemistry, plant genetics, and fungal vulnerability illustrates how disease suppression emerges not from a single agent but from positioned relations within a specific crop-microbe-pathogen assemblage, one that could be mobilized for agricultural benefit yet remains contingent on the ecological and genetic particularities of this system.

title: Unraveling the Mechanism of Perillyl Alcohol and Its Derivatives as Potent Antifungal Agents against Gray Mold
source: acs: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry: Table of Contents
excerpt: Fungicides are often limited by poor efficacy and high toxicity. Our previous research identified perillyl alcohol (POH) as a promising plant-derived fungicide. Here, POH exhibits antifungal activity by inducing metacaspase-independent apoptosis in Botrytis cinerea. Furthermore, POH triggers both nonselective and selective autophagy, particularly endoplasmic reticulum autophagy (ER-phagy) and mitochondrial autophagy (mitophagy). Ubiquitylation analysis revealed that POH promotes protein ubiquitination. Additionally, transcriptome analysis revealed the suppression of Na/K-ATPase-related gene expression and activity. Importantly, we identified BcBet4 as a direct molecular target of POH, with binding interactions contributing to its mode of action. Through systematic chemical modifications, we developed POH-10, a derivative exhibiting enhanced antifungal efficacy and a stronger binding affinity to BcBet4. These findings provide valuable insights for the development of novel antifungal agents. As a multitarget fungicide, POH holds significant potential for economically and ecologically sustainable crop disease management.
summary: Perillyl alcohol, a compound derived from plants, reduces the growth of gray mold fungus (*Botrytis cinerea*) by triggering programmed cell death through a pathway that does not depend on metacaspases. The compound simultaneously activates both general and targeted forms of cellular self-digestion, including the selective breakdown of damaged endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria. This paper identified that perillyl alcohol increases the attachment of ubiquitin proteins to other cellular proteins and suppresses genes related to sodium-potassium pumps. The researchers pinpointed BcBet4, a fungal protein, as the direct molecular target where perillyl alcohol binds. By chemically modifying the original compound, the team created a derivative called POH-10 that shows stronger antifungal activity and binds more tightly to BcBet4.  The dual capacity of this plant-derived compound—simultaneously a toxin to the fungus and a potential crop protection tool—illustrates how the same molecular interaction can serve competing interests in agricultural systems. Gray mold control through perillyl alcohol and its derivatives represents a shift toward plant-derived fungicides that operate through multiple cellular mechanisms, yet the introduction of such compounds into crop systems will inevitably alter the selective pressures shaping fungal communities in fields and storage facilities, potentially favoring other pathogenic species while suppressing *Botrytis cinerea*.

title: Characterization of Novel Enzymes from Penicillium oxalicum and Their Role in the Degradation of RG-I Pectin
source: acs: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry: Table of Contents
excerpt: The degradation of complex plant polysaccharides, particularly Rhamnogalacturonan I (RG-I) pectin, is essential for unlocking the value of biomass. This study focuses on the enzymatic toolbox of Penicillium oxalicum for efficient RG-I breakdown. Here, we recombinantly expressed 15 novel pectinolytic enzymes and characterized their biochemical functions. The enzyme system consists of backbone-cleaving rhamnogalacturonan hydrolases and lyases, along with side-chain-degrading enzymes. Specific endo-β-galactanases (GH30, GH43, GH53) and an exo-β-galactosidase (GH35) collaboratively hydrolyze galactan side chains. Meanwhile, α-arabinanases (GH43) and exo-α-arabinofuranosidases (GH54, GH62) work synergistically to degrade arabinan side chains. Through coordinated action, these enzymes achieve complete degradation of complex RG-I structures. Our findings provide a comprehensive enzyme set for targeted polysaccharide deconstruction, offering valuable resources for biomass conversion and pectin valorization in industrial applications.
summary: This paper describes the identification and functional characterization of 15 novel enzymes produced by *Penicillium oxalicum*, a mould capable of breaking down Rhamnogalacturonan I pectin, a complex plant structural polymer. The enzyme set includes two classes of proteins: those that cleave the backbone of the pectin molecule (rhamnogalacturonan hydrolases and lyases) and those that remove side chains. The side-chain degradation occurs through coordinated action of multiple enzyme families—endo-β-galactanases and exo-β-galactosidase enzymes work together to dismantle galactan branches, while α-arabinanases and exo-α-arabinofuranosidases collaboratively degrade arabinan branches. The paper reports that this coordinated enzymatic system achieves complete structural breakdown of complex Rhamnogalacturonan I molecules.  The mould's capacity to metabolize a chemically complex plant polymer reflects a material entanglement characteristic of the Patchy Anthropocene: the same fungal genus (*Penicillium*) that degrades structural materials in buildings and food storage also possesses enzymatic machinery now positioned as industrially valuable for biomass conversion. This study isolates and documents the biochemical mechanisms through which a common environmental mould transforms a recalcitrant plant polymer into simpler compounds—a process that simultaneously reveals both the organism's ecological role in decomposition and its potential as a biotechnological resource for converting plant waste into usable materials. The research thus documents how a single organism operates across registers: as a decomposer in natural systems and as an engineered tool for material transformation under industrial conditions.

title: l‐Aspartic acid alleviates aflatoxin B1‐induced liver injury by regulating intestinal microflora homeostasis and bile acid metabolism
source: Wiley: Journal of the Science of Food and Agriculture: Table of Contents
excerpt: This study established 1.0% l-asp as an effective, gut-liver axis-targeted intervention against AFB1 toxicity in rabbits. This work emphasizes its nutritional potential and provides a clear rationale for future studies to elucidate its molecular mechanisms. © 2026 Society of Chemical Industry.
summary: This paper established that 1.0 percent l-aspartic acid functions as an effective intervention against aflatoxin B1 toxicity in rabbits, operating through the gut-liver axis—the bidirectional communication pathway between intestinal microorganisms and liver function. The study demonstrates that l-aspartic acid alleviates liver injury induced by aflatoxin B1, a toxic fungal metabolite, by regulating the composition and activity of intestinal microflora and modulating bile acid metabolism, the biochemical processes by which the liver produces and processes bile salts. The authors frame their findings as evidence for the nutritional potential of l-aspartic acid and identify it as a candidate for further investigation into the specific molecular pathways through which the amino acid exerts its protective effects.  Within the material entanglements of food production and fungal contamination, this intervention reveals a paradox: the same agricultural systems that generate aflatoxin B1 exposure—a consequence of mould colonization in stored grain and feed—can be partially remediated through dietary amino acid supplementation, a strategy that depends on and reinforces industrial feed formulation and nutritional management. The protective mechanism operates not through direct toxin neutralization but through restoration of microbial communities and metabolic processes in the animal body, suggesting that fungal contamination creates cascading disruptions in the living systems that process contaminated feed, disruptions that must be managed through ongoing nutritional intervention rather than eliminated at their source.

title: Antifungal exposure variability in critically ill patients: extending risk-based frameworks in invasive mould infections
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Journal of Infection
excerpt: Publication date: Available online 6 March 2026Source: Journal of InfectionAuthor(s): Danilo Hackner, Christina Scharf
summary: This publication is still hot off the press. That means the paper is not yet indexed by global databases like CrossRef, Semantic Scholar or OpenAlex. If you want to learn what the research might have to say about life on Planet Mould you'll just have to read it yourself!

title: Mycotoxin migration in Alternaria alternata molded tomatoes: an on-going challenge for food safety and food waste management
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Microbiology
excerpt: Alternaria species are common mycotoxigenic fungi contaminating tomatoes which can lead to tomato-based products being contaminated by one or more mycotoxins. At the consumer level, there is a lack of information on the impact of storage conditions on Alternaria growth on tomatoes but also mycotoxin production and migration, yet this food safety hazard needs to be considered. In this context, we artificially inoculated tomatoes with a multi-mycotoxin producing A. alternata UBOCC-A-124001 isolate and incubated tomatoes in conditions mimicking refrigerated (8°C) or room temperature (20°C) storage until fungal lesions reached 1, 2 and 3 cm. Five mycotoxins were produced once lesions reached 2 cm in diameter. Highest concentrations were reached for 2 or 3 cm lesions at 20°C, namely 168758 ng/g for tenuazonic acid (3 cm) followed by 1146+/-1844 ng/g for alternariol (2 cm), 410 ng/g for altertoxin-I (3 cm), 89+/- 95 ng/g for alternariol methyl ether (2 cm) and 4.4 ng/g for altenuene (2 cm) while these mycotoxins were detected at much lower concentrations or <LOD at 8°C. Mycotoxins migrated once lesion sizes reached 2 and 3 cm diameters, but highest contents still remained close to fungal lesions. We hypothesize that some of the produced mycotoxins remain near the fungus or in specific tissues while others, as tenuazonic acid, diffuse well beyond the lesion. Overall, a strong reduction in mycotoxin content was observed at a 3 cm distance, even for the largest lesions. For small lesions, mycotoxin migration was limited. We therefore recommend removing a 2 cm portion around and below the fungal lesion for lesions up to 2 cm in diameter to limit mycotoxin exposure. For 3 cm lesions or greater, migration into the entire tomato occurred thus tomatoes should be discarded to ensure sufficiently protective conditions for consumer food safety. We recommend to consumers to store tomatoes in cool conditions to limit fungal growth and reduce mycotoxin production, thus contributing to
summary: Alternaria alternata, a fungus commonly found on tomatoes, produces multiple toxic compounds simultaneously. This paper artificially infected tomatoes with a single isolate and stored them at refrigeration temperature (8 degrees Celsius) or room temperature (20 degrees Celsius) until visible fungal lesions reached 1, 2, or 3 centimetres in diameter. Five distinct mycotoxins appeared once lesions reached 2 centimetres. At room temperature, the highest concentrations were tenuazonic acid at 168,758 nanograms per gram (at 3 centimetre lesions), followed by alternariol at 1,146 nanograms per gram (at 2 centimetre lesions), altertoxin-I at 410 nanograms per gram (at 3 centimetre lesions), alternariol methyl ether at 89 nanograms per gram (at 2 centimetre lesions), and altenuene at 4.4 nanograms per gram (at 2 centimetre lesions). At refrigeration temperature, these same mycotoxins were detected at much lower concentrations or fell below detection limits. The mycotoxins spread outward from fungal lesions once lesions reached 2 or 3 centimetres in diameter, though the highest concentrations remained close to the fungal growth itself. At a distance of 3 centimetres from lesions, mycotoxin content dropped sharply, even in the largest lesions studied.  This paper documents a material entanglement between fungal growth, chemical production, and domestic food storage practices. The same temperature conditions that accelerate fungal colonization of tomato tissue also intensify mycotoxin synthesis and dispersal—yet the toxins do not distribute uniformly through the fruit. Tenuazonic acid migrates extensively while other compounds remain concentrated near the lesion, revealing how different chemical structures interact with tomato tissue in ways that complicate removal strategies. The recommendation to discard tomatoes with lesions of 3 centimetres or larger reflects not a failure of the fruit but a recognition that once fungal invasion reaches a certain threshold, the spatial distribution of toxins exceeds the boundaries of practical remediation, transforming what might otherwise be salvageable food into waste.

title: Enhanced removal of aflatoxins from soybean kernels by the coupled ultrasound-chlorination process: efficiency and mechanisms
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Chemistry
excerpt: Aflatoxin contamination in soybeans is challenging because toxins can partition into dense kernel matrices. This study developed a coupled ultrasound-chlorination (US/Cl) process to remove four aflatoxins from soybean kernels. Within 10 min, US/Cl achieved 80.4-91.3% removal, outperforming chlorination alone (45.3-57.2%) and ultrasound alone (38.4-40.2%). The synergy factor increased from 0.1 to 1.9% (2 min) to 15.0-19.8% (10 min), indicating a shift from early desorption to late-stage radical oxidation. Mass-balance tracking of AFB1 confirmed chemical destruction under US/Cl, with 83.4% degraded after 10 min and only 8.7% remaining in kernels and 7.9% in water. Box-Behnken response surface methodology identified practical operating windows and showed excellent fit (R2 = 0.9968). Identified intermediates supported initial hydroxylation and chlorination followed by further oxidation. Soybean quality was preserved, with overlapping FTIR spectra and minimal color change (ΔE = 1.86). Overall, US/Cl provides a rapid, quality-preserving decontamination strategy for soybean kernels.
summary: This paper describes a coupled ultrasound-chlorination process designed to remove four aflatoxins from soybean kernels. Within 10 minutes, the combined treatment achieved 80.4-91.3% removal of the toxins, substantially outperforming chlorination alone (45.3-57.2% removal) and ultrasound alone (38.4-40.2% removal). Mass-balance tracking of aflatoxin B1 showed that 83.4% of the toxin was chemically destroyed after 10 minutes, with 8.7% remaining in the kernels and 7.9% in the water phase. The synergy factor—a measure of how much the combined treatment exceeded what either method alone could achieve—increased from 1.9% at 2 minutes to 15.0-19.8% at 10 minutes, suggesting that the mechanism shifted from early physical desorption to later-stage radical oxidation. Response surface methodology identified practical operating windows for the treatment, with excellent statistical fit. The soybean kernels retained their quality after treatment, showing minimal color change and overlapping infrared spectra compared to untreated samples.  The coupled process reveals a material entanglement between toxin remediation and crop preservation. Aflatoxins embedded in the dense protein and lipid matrices of soybean kernels present a decontamination problem that neither physical nor chemical intervention alone can adequately solve—yet the combination that destroys the toxin also risks degrading the commodity itself. This paper documents a narrow operational window where chemical destruction becomes efficient enough to matter economically while the crop matrix remains intact enough to retain market value. The shift from desorption to oxidation over time suggests that the soybean kernel itself—its structure, its resistance to chemical penetration—shapes not just the contamination problem but also the temporal dynamics of its remediation.

title: Multidrug Resistance in Fusarium incarnatum from Maize Stalk Rot: Molecular Mechanisms and Fitness Characterization
source: acs: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry: Table of Contents
excerpt: Maize stalk rot is a severe soil-borne disease caused by Fusarium species. In this study, 73 isolates were collected and identified, among which Fusarium incarnatum accounted for 43.8%. Two isolates, HA32R and HA39R, exhibited multidrug resistance to tebuconazole (resistance factors of 19.71 and 8.00), carbendazim (RF of 56.65 and 52.65), and pyraclostrobin (RF > 100). The resistance mechanisms were associated with target-site mutations CYP51B-G464S, β2-tubulin-F167Y, and Cytb-G143A. Site-directed mutagenesis confirmed that CYP51B-G464S confers low-level resistance to tebuconazole (RF values of 6.88 and 5.29). Moreover, the multidrug-resistant isolates exhibited significantly reduced fitness, as evidenced by a marked decrease in conidial production. This study provides the first report of field-derived multidrug-resistant F. incarnatum, offering important theoretical insights and practical guidance for the management of maize stalk rot.
summary: Maize stalk rot, a soil-borne disease caused by *Fusarium* species, was examined in this study through collection and identification of 73 isolates, with *Fusarium incarnatum* comprising 43.8% of the total. Two isolates, designated HA32R and HA39R, demonstrated resistance to three antifungal drugs: tebuconazole (with resistance factors of 19.71 and 8.00 respectively), carbendazim (resistance factors of 56.65 and 52.65), and pyraclostrobin (resistance factors exceeding 100). The molecular basis for this multidrug resistance involved three specific genetic mutations: CYP51B-G464S, β2-tubulin-F167Y, and Cytb-G143A. Laboratory experiments using site-directed mutagenesis showed that the CYP51B-G464S mutation alone produces low-level resistance to tebuconazole, with resistance factors of 6.88 and 5.29. The resistant isolates displayed a marked biological cost: conidial production was substantially reduced compared to non-resistant strains.  The emergence of multidrug-resistant *F. incarnatum* in maize fields reveals a pattern characteristic of turbulent agricultural systems—the same fungal mutations that enable survival under chemical pressure simultaneously compromise the organism's capacity to reproduce and spread. This paper documents the first field-derived multidrug-resistant strain, a finding that exposes how antifungal deployment in soil-based cropping systems generates novel fungal variants that are simultaneously more dangerous (resistant to multiple drugs) and more constrained (reduced spore production). The fitness penalty observed here does not eliminate the threat; rather, it establishes a precarious equilibrium where resistant populations persist in agricultural soils despite reduced competitive advantage, creating conditions for unpredictable dynamics in future seasons and across different crop management practices.

title: Structural characterization and antifungal properties of sequentially extracted polysaccharides from Algerian Opuntia ficus-indica L. cladodes
source: PLOS One
excerpt:
by Nassima Draou, Hassiba Bokhari, Samia Gharbi, Abderrahmane Rouane, Hachem Kadda, Hakima Kebaili

Opuntia ficus-indica L., a xerophytic cactus native to arid regions, is an understudied source of structurally varied polysaccharides with potential for bioactivity. Four different types of substances were separated in this study, which is the first time this method has been used for Algerian varieties: cellulose (19.1%), pectins (7.20%), hemicelluloses (2.57%), and water-soluble mucilage (8.84% of dry mass). Pectins rich in homogalacturonans (33.5% galacturonic acid) and rhamnogalacturonans (30% rhamnose) were identified by monosaccharide profiling, along with hemicelluloses that were dominated by xylose (55.9%). FTIR spectroscopy validated structural signatures, such as β-glycosidic linkages (890 cm ⁻ ¹) and carboxylate groups (1750 cm ⁻ ¹) in pectins. Penicillium sp. was shown to be dose-dependently inhibited by antifungal assays, with a 75% growth reduction at 100 mg/mL pectin concentration (*p* < 0.05 vs control). The structure of polysaccharides, especially the amount of carboxylate, is correlated with the observed bioactivity. Based on their biocompatibility and regional adapt
summary: Polysaccharides extracted from Algerian prickly pear cactus cladodes (the flat, fleshy stems) were separated into four chemically distinct fractions: cellulose at 19.1% of dry mass, pectins at 7.20%, hemicelluloses at 2.57%, and water-soluble mucilage at 8.84%. The pectin fraction contained high proportions of galacturonic acid (33.5%) and rhamnose (30%), while hemicelluloses were dominated by xylose at 55.9%. Infrared spectroscopy confirmed the presence of β-glycosidic chemical bonds and carboxylate groups characteristic of these polysaccharide types. In antifungal assays, the pectin fraction inhibited growth of Penicillium species in a dose-related manner, achieving 75% growth reduction at a concentration of 100 milligrams per millilitre.  The relationship between chemical structure and antifungal capacity documented in this paper reveals how a plant material abundant in arid regions—where water scarcity and fungal pressure coexist—produces compounds that simultaneously serve as structural tissue and as chemical defence. The carboxylate-rich pectins that inhibit fungal growth are the same molecules that bind water and provide mechanical strength to the cactus tissue itself, suggesting that what functions as a pathogen barrier in one context becomes a harvestable resource in another. This material entanglement between plant survival strategy and potential pharmaceutical application reflects broader patterns in which organisms adapted to environmental stress under colonialism and resource extraction become targets for industrial valorization.

title: Performance of the LDBio Aspergillus ICT lateral flow assay and western blot for diagnosing chronic pulmonary aspergillosis in post-tuberculosis patients: a prospective study from South India
source: American Society for Microbiology: Microbiology Spectrum: Table of Contents
excerpt: Chronic pulmonary aspergillosis (CPA) is an important sequela of pulmonary tuberculosis (PTB), particularly in high-burden countries such as India. This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of the LDBio Aspergillus ICT lateral flow assay and western blot (WB) for CPA among post-TB patients in South India. In this prospective study (July 2023 to June 2025) conducted in Bengaluru, India, we enrolled adults with a history of PTB and persistent respiratory symptoms. CPA was diagnosed using the modified European Society for Clinical Microbiology and Infectious Diseases (ESCMID)-European Confederation of Medical Mycology (ECMM)-European Respiratory Society (ERS) criteria. All participants underwent comprehensive clinical evaluation; direct microscopy and culture of respiratory secretions; and testing with serum Aspergillus-specific IgG ELISA, ICT, WB, and galactomannan (GM). Of 132 post-TB patients, 107 (81%) were classified as CPA and 25 as non-CPA; 70 control sera were also included. ICT and WB were positive in 74% and 84% of CPA cases, respectively, outperforming ELISA (68%) and GM (30%). WB showed the highest sensitivity (84%, 95% CI: 75.8-90.5), whereas ICT demonstrated the highest specificity (94.7%, 95% CI: 88.1-98.3). Combining ICT and WB improved diagnostic accuracy (87.1%, 95% CI: 81.7-91.4) and yielded the highest discriminative power (Youden's index 0.805). WB displayed robust band-based differentiation, with strong reactivity in seropositive CPA. The agreement between ICT and WB was substantial (κ = 0.82). Latent class analysis estimated sensitivities of 92% (95% CI: 84-97) for ICT and 87% (95% CI: 77-94) for WB. These findings indicate that LDBio ICT and WB are robust diagnostic tools for CPA in post-TB patients and that their combined use offers optimal accuracy in resource-limited, high-burden settings.IMPORTANCEChronic pulmonary aspergillosis (CPA) is a serious but often overlooked complication in people who have previously been treate
summary: This paper evaluated two rapid diagnostic tests—a lateral flow immunoassay and western blot—for detecting chronic pulmonary aspergillosis in 132 post-tuberculosis patients in Bengaluru, India, where this fungal complication is common. The western blot detected the fungal infection in 84% of confirmed cases, while the lateral flow assay achieved 94.7% specificity, meaning it rarely produced false positive results. When the two tests were used together, diagnostic accuracy reached 87.1%, substantially outperforming existing methods such as galactomannan detection (30% sensitivity) and immunoglobulin G enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (68% sensitivity). The western blot produced distinctive protein band patterns that reliably distinguished infected from uninfected patients, and the two tests showed substantial agreement with each other (κ = 0.82).  Within the material and social landscape of post-tuberculosis care in high-burden regions, these diagnostic tools reveal a layered vulnerability. Chronic pulmonary aspergillosis emerges not as a discrete fungal threat but as a consequence of prior disease, damaged lung tissue, and the conditions—inadequate housing, occupational exposure, limited access to follow-up care—that shape who develops tuberculosis in the first place. The dual-test approach documented here reflects a pragmatic accommodation to resource constraints: neither test alone is sufficient, yet their combination offers workable accuracy in settings where comprehensive mycological infrastructure remains sparse. The fungus itself becomes legible only through this technological assemblage, and only in bodies already marked by previous infection and systemic inequality.

title: Aspergillus oryzae solid-state fermentation enriches protopanaxatriol-type ginsenosides in Panax ginseng and confers cytoprotective effects in vitro
source: Frontiers in Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: BackgroundThis study established and optimized a food-grade solid-state fermentation (SSF) process using Aspergillus oryzae to biotransform ginsenosides in five-year-old white ginseng roots.MethodsThrough single-factor and orthogonal tests, optimal SSF conditions were identified. UPLC-QTOF-MS/MS analysis was used to characterize ginsenoside profile changes. In an ethanol-induced injury model using GES-1 gastric epithelial cells, the fermented extract was evaluated for cytoprotective effects.ResultsOptimal SSF conditions were fermentation time of 8 days, inoculum size of 2.5%, and temperature of 28°C. UPLC-QTOF-MS/MS analysis revealed significant remodeling of protopanaxatriol (PPT)-type ginsenosides, with ginsenoside Rf and PPT increasing by 3.55-fold and 5.03-fold, respectively (p < 0.05). In the ethanol-induced injury model using GES-1 gastric epithelial cells, the fermented extract demonstrated dose-dependent anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anti-apoptotic effects without cytotoxicity. We hypothesize that the extracellular glycosidase activity of A. oryzae mediates the sequential deglycosylation leading to the observed PPT-type enrichment, although the specific enzymes involv
summary: Aspergillus oryzae, a fungus commonly used in food fermentation, was grown on five-year-old white ginseng roots under controlled conditions to transform the plant's active compounds. This paper optimized the solid-state fermentation process by testing different fermentation durations, fungal inoculum concentrations, and temperatures, identifying 8 days, 2.5% inoculum size, and 28°C as optimal. Using liquid chromatography and mass spectrometry,  fermentation substantially increased protopanaxatriol-type ginsenosides—specifically ginsenoside Rf increased 3.55-fold and protopanaxatriol itself increased 5.03-fold. When the fermented ginseng extract was applied to cultured human gastric cells exposed to ethanol damage, it reduced inflammation, lowered oxidative stress, and prevented programmed cell death in a dose-related manner without harming the cells. The paper suggests that enzymes secreted by the fungus break down sugar chains attached to ginsenosides, progressively exposing the more bioactive protopanaxatriol forms.  The fungus here operates simultaneously as a biotransforming agent and as a means of value extraction from plant material. The same enzymatic capacity that allows Aspergillus oryzae to decompose complex plant structures—breaking down ginsenoside molecules to reveal their more potent derivatives—positions it as both a tool for enhancing medicinal efficacy and a mechanism for industrial accumulation. The fermented extract's protective effects in damaged gastric tissue suggest that this fungal-plant assemblage produces compounds suited to addressing cellular stress, yet the process itself depends on controlling and standardizing fungal metabolism within the narrow parameters of food-grade fermentation, revealing how contemporary bioproduction entangles microbial life with human health and commercial value.

title: Diagnostic odyssey of a German shepherd dog with disseminated Penicillium labradoris infection: a case report
source: Frontiers in Veterinary Science | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: Below, the first confirmed canine infection caused by Penicillium labradorum (syn. P. labradoris) in Italy is reported, diagnosed using a multidisciplinary approach. In November 2021, a 10-year-old spayed female German Shepherd dog with urinary incontinence was presented. Physical examination of the right forelimb revealed a non-painful soft tissue enlargement with firm consistency, while peripheral lymph nodes were unremarkable. Hematological, serum biochemical, and urinalysis tests showed no significant abnormalities. Radiographic examination revealed a proliferative lesion of the right radial bone, whereas abdominal ultrasonography did not identify anatomical alterations. Computed tomography (CT) revealed aggressive proliferative and lytic lesions of the radius associated with soft tissue oedema. Cytological examination of fine-needle aspirates showed marked neutrophilic and moderate macrophagic inflammation with evidence of fungal hyphae. Histological examination of a bone biopsy revealed severe fibroplasia and fibrosis associated with mixed inflammatory infiltrates, and Grocott-Gomori's methenamine silver (GMS), and Periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining confirmed the presence of
summary: In November 2021, a 10-year-old female German Shepherd dog presented with urinary incontinence and a firm soft tissue enlargement of the right forelimb. Imaging revealed aggressive bone lesions affecting the radius bone alongside soft tissue swelling, while standard blood and urine tests showed no abnormalities. Cytological examination of tissue samples extracted by fine needle aspiration identified fungal filaments alongside neutrophils and macrophages, and subsequent bone biopsy with specialized fungal stains (Grocott-Gomori's methenamine silver and Periodic acid-Schiff staining) confirmed the presence of Penicillium labradorum—marking the first documented case of this mould infection in a dog in Italy.  The case documents how a fungal pathogen typically associated with environmental exposure became established within living bone tissue, producing both inflammatory damage and structural bone remodelling in a single animal body. The diagnostic pathway—requiring cytology, histology, and specialized staining across multiple tissue types—reveals how moulds that colonize bone operate simultaneously as destructive agents and as organisms capable of persisting within defended anatomical spaces, creating material entanglements between fungal metabolism and host tissue reorganization that standard clinical assays initially failed to detect.

title: Two decades of mucormycosis: molecular epidemiology and antifungal susceptibility trends in Türkiye
source: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: BackgroundMucormycosis is a life-threatening invasive fungal infection that mainly affects individuals with weakened immune systems. Despite its clinical importance, data from Türkiye on the epidemiology and antifungal susceptibility of fungi belonging to the order Mucorales are scarce. This study aimed to describe the clinical, epidemiological, and mycological features of mucormycosis over a twenty-year period at a tertiary care hospital.MethodsAll cases of mucormycosis diagnosed between 2003 and 2022 at Bursa Uludağ University Hospital were retrospectively evaluated. Cases were classified as proven or probable according to international definitions. Molecular identification was based on internal transcribed spacer sequencing. Large subunit ribosomal DNA region sequencing and whole-genome sequencing were performed when necessary. Antifungal susceptibility testing for amphotericin B and posaconazole was performed according to the Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute M38 guideline. Statistical analyses included chi-square, Fisher’s exact, Spearman’s correlation, and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests.ResultsA total of 187 cases were identified, comprising 134 proven and 53 probable.
summary: Between 2003 and 2022, a tertiary care hospital in Bursa, Türkiye documented 187 cases of mucormycosis—a severe invasive fungal infection caused by organisms in the order Mucorales—comprising 134 proven cases and 53 probable cases. This paper identified and classified these infections using molecular sequencing of the internal transcribed spacer region, with additional large subunit ribosomal DNA and whole-genome sequencing performed when necessary. The study evaluated antifungal susceptibility to amphotericin B and posaconazole using standardized Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute testing protocols, and applied chi-square, Fisher's exact, Spearman's correlation, and Wilcoxon signed-rank statistical tests to analyse the data.  Within the two-decade record, mucormycosis emerges as both a clinical marker of immunosuppression and a material entanglement with broader patterns of metabolic and infectious stress in hospital populations. The distinction between proven and probable cases—a classification system itself dependent on access to molecular sequencing infrastructure—reveals how diagnostic capacity shapes epidemiological visibility; the same fungal infections that signal immunocompromise in some patients may circulate undetected or misclassified in settings with limited mycological resources. Antifungal susceptibility patterns documented here are not merely clinical data but material evidence of how these moulds adapt within the specific pharmaceutical and immune ecologies of a particular institution over time, raising questions about whether observed trends reflect genuine shifts in fungal populations or changing patterns of exposure and treatment among hospitalized populations.
