title: Enzyme and biosurfactant production as strategy for bis(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate degradation by Fusarium culmorum: Metabolic pathway proposal using quantum chemical investigation
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Fungal Biology
excerpt: Bis(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate is a globally used plastic additive that can exhibit toxic effects on environmental and human health. In this study, Fusarium culmorum was grown on BEHP (3000 mg/L), used as a sole carbon source, in liquid fermentation. A glucose medium was used as a control. Growth kinetics, protein content, and esterase activity were determined. BEHP biodegradation compounds were detected through GC-MS, and a metabolic pathway was proposed using quantum chemical modeling to predict the stability of the generated products. F. culmorum had a μ value 1.6-fold more in the control medium (0.032 h-1) than in the BEHP-supplemented medium (0.02 h-1). The maximum volumetric esterase activity was 63-fold more in the BEHP-supplemented cultures (6007.5 U/L) than that in the control cultures (95.7 U/L). Five (28.4, 34.5, 47.9, 84, and 178.2 kDa approx.) and two (33 and 49.5 kDa approx.) esterase isoenzymes were detected in BEHP-supplemented and in the control cultures, respectively, indicating that the BEHP supplementation significantly induced esterase production. The GC-MS revealed that F. culmorum degraded BEHP and synthesized compounds, including oleic acid, lauric acid, palmitic acid, and palmitoleic acid, that can be converted into natural surfactants, which make the substrate bioavailable (through micelles formation) for uptake and metabolism. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study suggesting a microorganism-mediated degradation of a plasticizer (i.e. phthalate ester) associated with a concomitant natural surfactant production.
summary: Fusarium culmorum, a filamentous fungus, was cultured on bis(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate at a concentration of 3000 milligrams per litre as the sole carbon source in liquid fermentation, with glucose medium as a control. The fungus grew more slowly on the phthalate-supplemented medium (growth rate of 0.02 per hour) compared to glucose (0.032 per hour), but responded by producing dramatically elevated levels of esterase enzymes—proteins that break chemical bonds in esters. Esterase activity in the phthalate cultures reached 6007.5 units per litre, approximately 63 times higher than in control cultures. The fungus synthesized five distinct esterase variants in response to phthalate exposure, compared to only two variants in glucose cultures. Gas chromatography and mass spectrometry analysis identified degradation products including oleic acid, lauric acid, palmitic acid, and palmitoleic acid—fatty acids that can function as natural surfactants, which are molecules that reduce the surface tension of water and allow hydrophobic materials to dissolve into solution, making them accessible for further fungal metabolism.  The capacity of this fungus to simultaneously degrade a synthetic plastic additive while producing natural surfactants reveals an entanglement between toxicity and utility. The same metabolic stress that slows the fungus's growth rate triggers the production of enzymatic and chemical tools that render an industrial pollutant into bioavailable growth medium and, potentially, into commercially valuable compounds. This study documents not a simple remediation but rather a material transformation in which a planetary contaminant becomes a vehicle for fungal accumulation and, by extension, for potential industrial recovery.

title: Phosphoproteomic Insights into the Metabolic Reprogramming of Cell Growth and Lipid Accumulation in Mucor circinelloides
source: acs: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry: Table of Contents
excerpt: Mucor circinelloides is a model oleaginous fungus for studying microbial lipid accumulation, yet the post-translational mechanisms coordinating growth and lipid biosynthesis remain poorly understood. Here, we present the first systematic quantitative phosphoproteomic analysis of M. circinelloides across its life cycle, encompassing spores and mycelia at 6 h, 24 h (nitrogen depletion), and 96 h. A total of 1252 phosphoproteins and 1682 phosphopeptides were identified, revealing a phosphorylation-driven transition from growth-associated processes to lipid metabolism under nitrogen limitation. Dynamic phosphorylation of key enzymes, notably ATP-citrate lyase (ACL) and Acyl-CoA synthetase (ACS), directly mediated carbon flux redirection toward fatty acid synthesis. A conserved serine-rich motif in ACL suggests an evolutionarily conserved regulatory strategy for lipid accumulation. Integration with fluxomics and proteomics reveals a hierarchical network linking signaling, enzyme phosphorylation, and metabolic reprogramming. These findings provide new insights into phosphorylation-based regulation of fungal growth and lipid biosynthesis in oleaginous fungi.
summary: This paper presents the first systematic analysis of phosphorylated proteins across the life cycle of *Mucor circinelloides*, an oleaginous fungus used to model microbial lipid accumulation. The study identified 1252 phosphoproteins and 1682 phosphopeptides across spores and fungal filaments sampled at 6 hours, 24 hours (when nitrogen became depleted), and 96 hours. Two enzymes critical to fatty acid synthesis—ATP-citrate lyase and Acyl-CoA synthetase—showed dynamic phosphorylation patterns that directly controlled the redirection of carbon toward lipid production under nitrogen limitation. The fungal filaments exhibited a phosphorylation-driven metabolic shift away from growth-associated processes and toward lipid biosynthesis, with ATP-citrate lyase displaying a conserved serine-rich regulatory motif that suggests this phosphorylation strategy is evolutionarily preserved across fungal species. The paper integrated these phosphorylation findings with metabolic flux data and overall protein abundance measurements to map a hierarchical regulatory network linking cellular signaling, enzyme modification, and metabolic reprogramming.  Within the material assemblages of industrial fermentation, this research documents how *Mucor circinelloides* coordinates growth and lipid accumulation through reversible protein modifications—a regulatory capacity that becomes economically significant precisely when nitrogen becomes scarce. The fungus's ability to sense nutrient depletion and reprogram its metabolism toward fatty acid synthesis reveals the organism not simply as a biological system but as an industrial platform whose productivity depends on orchestrating specific chemical modifications in response to material scarcity. The phosphorylation of ATP-citrate lyase and Acyl-CoA synthetase thus operates simultaneously as a mechanism of cellular adaptation and as a target for metabolic engineering—the same regulatory pathway that enables survival under stress can be manipulated to increase lipid yields in fermentation systems designed to extract value from fungal metabolism.

title: Evidence of antibacterial properties by endophytic fungi of Hazelnut (Corylus avellana)
source: Frontiers in Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: New control strategies are necessary for the treatment of the bacterial diseases of hazelnut (Corylus avellana) incited by Pseudomonas avellanae and Xanthomonas arboricola pv. corylina, following the programmed phasing out of copper-based anti-cryptogamics. Based on recent evidence gathered on many crops, endophytic fungi are credited for playing a role as defensive mutualists of plants. Thus, an investigation was carried out in the hazelnut growing areas in Southern Italy in the aim to identify endophytic fungi possessing antimicrobial properties against these two pathogens. A panel of 50 endophytic isolates was selected, including species which are already known as being part of the hazelnut mycobiome, along with a few new records. These isolates were tested for antibiosis in an in vitro assay consisting in the inoculation of the two bacterial pathogens in their culture filtrates. Four isolates, belonging to Cladosporium perangustum, Talaromyces purpureogenus, Nemania diffusa and the Hypoxylon fuscum species complex, displayed consistent inhibitory effects, inducing about 90% growth suppression of both bacteria. Their capacity to effectively act as biocontrol agents will be furth
summary: Researchers working in hazelnut growing areas of Southern Italy isolated 50 plant-internal microbe fungi—organisms living inside plant tissues—from hazelnut trees to test their capacity to inhibit two bacterial pathogens: Pseudomonas avellanae and Xanthomonas arboricola pv. corylina, which cause significant bacterial diseases in the crop. The isolates included species already documented as part of the hazelnut fungal community alongside several newly recorded species. In laboratory culture filtrate assays, four isolates demonstrated consistent antimicrobial activity: Cladosporium perangustum, Talaromyces purpureogenus, Nemania diffusa, and members of the Hypoxylon fuscum species complex suppressed growth of both bacterial pathogens by approximately 90%.  This paper documents a shift in disease management strategy driven by regulatory restriction of copper-based fungicides—a material dependency that has shaped hazelnut cultivation for decades. The four fungal isolates identified here occupy an ambiguous position: they are simultaneously inhabitants of the hazelnut plant itself and potential tools for controlling bacterial pathogens that threaten crop productivity. The capacity of these plant-internal microbe fungi to produce antimicrobial compounds in culture suggests a form of plant defence already embedded in the hazelnut mycobiome, yet their deployment as biocontrol agents would constitute a deliberate restructuring of the fungal assemblages that constitute the living plant, transforming defensive mutualism into managed intervention.

title: Streptomyces rochei D74 improves tobacco growth and quality by regulating the rhizosphere microecological community
source: Frontiers in Plant Science | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: IntroductionStreptomyces rochei D74 promotes growth and enhances quality in crops such as wheat and tomato. However, its potential role and optimal application method in tobacco production remain unclear. This study for the first time investigated the effects of S. rochei D74 with different application methods on tobacco growth and quality, soil physicochemical properties, and rhizosphere microbial community structure.MethodsS. rochei D74 was applied via basal application (BA), foliar spray (FS), and their combination (BA-FS) under field conditions. Tobacco growth parameters, leaf yield and quality indicators, soil physicochemical properties, and rhizosphere microbial community structure were analyzed and compared across treatments.ResultsDifferent microbial treatments promoted tobacco growth compared to the control, as exemplified by notable increases in plant height (by 5.3~10.5%) and stem girth (by 7.0~15.6%), while also reducing the proportion of low-grade leaves (by 10.2~28.4%, p < 0.05). Particularly, the BA-FS treatment achieved the highest leaf yield and output value, alongside elevating the contents of total nitrogen (by 29.0~36.2%) and total alkaloids (by 34.3~66.8%) in C
summary: This paper documents the application of Streptomyces rochei D74, a soil bacterium, to tobacco cultivation under three regimes: soil incorporation at planting, foliar spraying during growth, and a combination of both methods. Across all treatments, tobacco plants showed increased height (5.3 to 10.5 percent) and stem diameter (7.0 to 15.6 percent) relative to untreated controls, while the proportion of commercially inferior leaves declined by 10.2 to 28.4 percent. The combined soil and foliar treatment produced the highest leaf yield and economic value, and elevated nitrogen content (29.0 to 36.2 percent above control) and alkaloid concentration (34.3 to 66.8 percent above control) in the harvested leaves. The study measured shifts in soil chemistry and the composition of microbial communities in the root zone across treatments, though the abstract does not detail specific findings from these measurements.  The bacterium's capacity to enhance a commercially valuable crop while simultaneously reshaping the microbial assemblage in the soil around plant roots reveals a material entanglement characteristic of agricultural intensification. Streptomyces rochei D74 functions simultaneously as a productivity tool—increasing yield and desirable chemical compounds in tobacco leaves—and as an agent of ecological reorganization in the root zone, the narrow zone where plant roots meet soil life. The study does not address whether this microbial restructuring persists, spreads to adjacent fields, or generates dependencies that require repeated application; these absences suggest that the bacterium's role as an economic input may obscure longer-term transformations in the soil community that sustains tobacco monoculture.

title: Pre-harvest dynamics of Aspergillus section Flavi and aflatoxin risk in hazelnut orchards of Azerbaijan
source: Frontiers in Plant Science | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: Hazelnuts (Corylus avellana L.) are susceptible to aflatoxin contamination, with recurrent notifications reported in Azerbaijan. A three-year study (2023–2025) was conducted in 30 orchards across three main hazelnut-producing districts to investigate the pre-harvest dynamics of Aspergillus section Flavi. Fungal populations were monitored at four phenological stages (BBCH 70–89), and aflatoxin levels were assessed at harvest and late-harvest. Fungal abundance and incidence were significantly affected by geographical area, year, and growth stage (P < 0.01). A. section Flavi populations were higher in warmer districts such as Qabala and Zaqatala (up to 1.7 Log10 CFU/g) compared with Khachmaz (1.0 Log10 CFU/g), with relative incidence reaching 13.8% in Zaqatala. Fungal abundance peaked during nut filling (BBCH 79), reaching 2.4 Log10 CFU/g, with a corresponding incidence of 18.5%. Marked interannual variability was observed, with A. section Flavi incidence increasing from 1.2% in 2023 to 40.8% in 2024. Despite the frequent isolation of A. section Flavi, aflatoxin levels in standard orchard samples collected at BBCH 89 remained generally below EU limits (≤5 μg/kg). In contrast, late-har
summary: This paper documents pre-harvest contamination patterns in hazelnut orchards across Azerbaijan between 2023 and 2025. Researchers monitored populations of Aspergillus section Flavi—a fungal group that produces aflatoxins, carcinogenic compounds—across 30 orchards in three districts at four growth stages from nut development through maturity. Fungal abundance and presence varied significantly by location, year, and growth stage. Warmer districts such as Qabala and Zaqatala harboured higher fungal populations (up to 1.7 Log10 colony-forming units per gram) and incidence rates (13.8% in Zaqatala) compared with the cooler Khachmaz district (1.0 Log10 colony-forming units per gram). Fungal populations peaked during nut filling, reaching 2.4 Log10 colony-forming units per gram with an incidence of 18.5%. The study revealed sharp year-to-year variation, with fungal incidence jumping from 1.2% in 2023 to 40.8% in 2024. Standard harvest samples remained below European Union limits (5 micrograms per kilogram), though the abstract indicates that late-harvest samples showed a different pattern—the text cuts off before reporting those results.  The dynamics documented here reveal how aflatoxin risk emerges not as a fixed threat but as a product of shifting material conditions. Warmer microclimates and particular growth stages create windows of vulnerability where fungal colonization accelerates, yet the same orchards that harbour high fungal populations can still yield nuts meeting regulatory standards at the moment of harvest. This temporal and spatial patchiness—where contamination risk concentrates in certain districts and seasons while remaining controlled in others—reflects how hazelnuts move through zones of differential exposure shaped by climate, agricultural timing, and regulatory thresholds rather than by the presence or absence of the fungus itself.

title: Proximity labelling suggests association of the nonhost receptor PSS1 protein with enzymes of multiple defense pathways in Arabidopsis
source: Frontiers in Plant Science | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: IntroductionPSS1 encoding a glycine-rich integral plasma membrane protein plays a pivotal role in nonhost resistance of Arabidopsis against the soybean pathogens, Phytophthora sojae and Fusarium virguliforme. Notably, P. sojae fails to penetrate the wildtype Col-0 ecotype but not the pss1 mutant. To elucidate the molecular basis of PSS1-mediated nonhost immunity, we employed a miniTurbo-based proximity labeling approach.MethodsThe miniTurbo fused to either wild-type PSS1 or its mutant variant, PSS1G119D, and expressed in the pss1 knockout mutant background.  Seedlings were challenged with or without P. sojae zoospores and biotinylated proteins were isolated for mass spectrometry analysis. Col-0 and PSS1G119D lines served as controls to validate labeling specificity and biological relevance.ResultsPrior to infection, spatial proximity between PSS1 and the plasma membrane H(+)-ATPase 6 transporter was detected; however, this association was not observed following P. sojae challenge. Several candidate PSS1-associated proteins were predominantly localized to the plastid and cytosol, suggesting a possible redistribution of PSS1 during infection, potentially to facilitate nonhost immunit
summary: This paper employs proximity labeling to map the molecular associations of PSS1, a plasma membrane protein that confers nonhost resistance in Arabidopsis against two soybean pathogens: Phytophthora sojae and Fusarium virguliforme. The study used a miniTurbo enzyme fused to wild-type or the G119D variant expressed in pss1 knockout plants, challenging seedlings with P. sojae zoospores and identifying biotinylated proteins through mass spectrometry. Before infection, PSS1 was found in spatial proximity to the plasma membrane H(+)-ATPase 6 transporter, but this association was lost following pathogen challenge. Multiple candidate proteins associated with PSS1 were predominantly localized to plastids and the cytosol, suggesting that PSS1 may redistribute during infection to coordinate immune responses.  The findings reveal a protein that operates at the intersection of constitutive cellular machinery and pathogen-triggered redistribution. The loss of PSS1-transporter association upon infection—coupled with the detection of cytosolic and plastid-localized proteins in proximity to PSS1—suggests that nonhost resistance depends not on a single fixed defense mechanism but on the dynamic reorganization of cellular components in response to pathogen presence. This pattern reflects how plant immunity emerges through the material rearrangement of existing cellular infrastructure, where a single protein acts as a nexus through which multiple metabolic and signaling pathways are coordinated under stress.

title: Synergistic effects of S100 calcium-binding protein A12 combined with Pentraxin 3 in invasive pulmonary aspergillosis and their clinical application prospects
source: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis (IPA) is a severe deep-seated fungal infection caused by fungi of the genus Aspergillus. In recent years, its global incidence has shown a marked upward trend, posing a serious threat especially to immunocompromised patients, such as hematopoietic stem cell transplant recipients, cancer patients undergoing chemotherapy, and individuals on long-term glucocorticoid therapy. The core clinical dilemmas lie in the difficulty of early diagnosis and the narrow therapeutic window. Currently used clinical diagnostic indicators, including the galactomannan (GM) assay, (1,3)-β-D-glucan(G) assay, and imaging examinations, suffer from insufficient sensitivity or specificity, while traditional microbiological detection methods have a relatively long turnaround time. S100 calcium-binding protein A12 (S100A12) and Pentraxin 3 (PTX3) are both key molecules in the innate immune response of the human body, playing central roles in the immune regulation of infectious diseases. Recent studies have demonstrated that both molecules are abnormally expressed in IPA patients and may participate in the processes of Aspergillus infection recognition, immune clearance, and infl
summary: This paper examines two immune signalling molecules—S100 calcium-binding protein A12 and Pentraxin 3—as potential diagnostic markers for invasive pulmonary aspergillosis, a severe fungal infection caused by Aspergillus species. The study is motivated by a documented rise in invasive pulmonary aspergillosis incidence globally, with particular impact on immune-weakened populations including recipients of stem cell transplants, patients undergoing cancer chemotherapy, and those on long-term corticosteroid treatment. This paper identifies a critical clinical problem: existing diagnostic methods—including galactomannan assay, (1,3)-β-D-glucan assay, and imaging—lack adequate sensitivity or specificity, while conventional microbiological detection requires extended processing time. The paper notes that both S100 calcium-binding protein A12 and Pentraxin 3 are central molecules in the innate immune response and are abnormally expressed in invasive pulmonary aspergillosis patients, suggesting they may participate in Aspergillus recognition and immune clearance.  Within the material entanglement of fungal infection and immune response, these two proteins operate as dual-function markers: they simultaneously indicate pathological immune activation in the infected host and reveal the body's recognition mechanisms for fungal threat. The abnormal expression of these molecules in invasive pulmonary aspergillosis patients reflects not merely individual immune dysregulation but the specific vulnerabilities created by immunosuppression—whether from transplant protocols, chemotherapy regimens, or steroid dependency—that render certain populations permeable to Aspergillus colonization. By positioning these immune molecules as diagnostic tools, this paper implicitly documents how fungal invasion reshapes the biochemical landscape of the compromised body, making visible the material dependencies and fragilities that structure survival in immune-weakened states.

title: Directed evolution of the pathogenic mold Aspergillus fumigatus reveals novel genes contributing to triazole resistance
source: American Society for Microbiology: Antimicrobial Agents and Chemotherapy: Table of Contents
excerpt: ABSTRACT Aspergillus fumigatus is the leading cause of invasive mold infections in immunocompromised patients. Current antifungal treatment primarily depends on the triazole antifungals, which act by inhibiting Erg11/Cyp51, a key enzyme in the ergosterol biosynthetic pathway. However, resistance is emerging at an increasing rate, reducing treatment efficacy and patient survival. Confirmed resistance mechanisms in clinical isolates include mutations in cyp51A , cyp51B , hmg1 , hapE , cox10 , and the overexpression of drug efflux pumps. To identify additional determinants of triazole resistance, we grew A. fumigatus wild-type and Δcyp51A mutant strains under increasing concentrations of voriconazole. Sequencing of the resultant resistant strains identified known mutations in cyp51A and cyp51B , and novel mutations in hmg1 , abcC (cdr1B) , ptaB, erg25B, and srbA . Mutations of hmg1 and ptaB occurred early during evolution, while mutations of erg25B and srbA occurred later. Reintroduction of the novel mutations in hmg1 , abcC , ptaB, and erg25B into wild-type A. fumigatus and correction of the srbA mutation in the evolved strain validated their contribution toward triazole resistance. Sterol profiling analysis indicated that mutation or deletion of erg25B is associated with a decrease in the accumulation of methylated sterols. Mutation or deletion of ptaB resulted in increased cyp51A , cyp51B , and erg25A expression. Sequence analysis of clinical isolates revealed enrichment of missense mutations in ptaB , hmg1 , abcC , and srbA among triazole-resistant strains.
summary: This paper documents the identification of novel genetic mutations conferring triazole resistance in Aspergillus fumigatus through directed laboratory evolution. The researchers exposed wild-type and cyp51A-deficient strains to increasing concentrations of voriconazole and sequenced the resulting resistant populations, identifying both previously known resistance mutations (in cyp51A and cyp51B) and six novel mutations in genes including hmg1, abcC, ptaB, erg25B, and srbA. Functional validation confirmed that mutations in hmg1, abcC, ptaB, and erg25B each contributed measurably to resistance when reintroduced into wild-type strains. Sterol profiling revealed that erg25B mutations reduced accumulation of methylated sterols, while ptaB mutations increased expression of multiple genes involved in ergosterol synthesis. Sequence analysis of clinical isolates showed enrichment of missense mutations in ptaB, hmg1, abcC, and srbA among triazole-resistant strains, suggesting these mutations occur under clinical drug pressure.  The mutations identified in this study reveal how a pathogenic mold adapts its fundamental metabolic machinery—the pathways that build its cell membrane—to survive antifungal attack. The same genes that normally construct the mold's protective sterol barriers become sites of alteration that simultaneously render the organism resistant to treatment and potentially alter its fitness in the human body. The temporal pattern of mutations, with some appearing early and others late during laboratory evolution, suggests a staged rewiring of metabolic regulation rather than a single catastrophic change. This staged adaptation, now documented in clinical isolates, indicates that triazole resistance in Aspergillus fumigatus emerges not as a rare accident but as a reproducible evolutionary pathway shaped by repeated exposure to antifungal drugs in immune-weakened patients.

title: A common CRISPR platform enables comparative studies of multicellularity in social amoebae
source: Cell Biology and Microbiology News - Biology news, Microbiology
excerpt: A research group led by Associate Professor Tetsuya Muramoto from the Faculty of Science, Toho University, has established a CRISPR genome editing technique that enables comparative analysis of the evolution of multicellularity across different species of social amoebae (cellular slime molds). Until now, genetic studies had largely been restricted to a single model species, limiting cross-species comparisons. The work is published in Scientific Reports.
summary: This paper establishes a CRISPR genome editing platform that allows researchers to conduct comparative genetic studies across multiple species of social amoebae, organisms commonly known as cellular slime molds. Previously, genetic investigation of these organisms had been confined largely to single model species, preventing systematic cross-species comparison of how multicellularity evolved. By developing a shared CRISPR technique applicable across different social amoeba species, this work removes a significant technical barrier to understanding the genetic basis of multicellular organization in this group.  The platform's capacity to enable parallel genetic studies across species reveals how a standardized molecular tool can shift what becomes visible and comparable in evolutionary biology. Social amoebae—organisms that cycle between single-celled and multicellular phases depending on nutrient availability—occupy an unusual ecological and developmental space, and the ability to manipulate their genomes systematically across species boundaries now makes their genetic diversity itself a resource for understanding how multicellularity assembles and persists. This comparative approach transforms the social amoebae from isolated laboratory curiosities into a tractable system for examining the material and genetic foundations of collective life.

title: Early-Warning Biosensing of Aflatoxin Contamination: From Fungal Colonization to Toxin Synthesis
source: acs: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry: Table of Contents
excerpt: Aflatoxin contamination in agricultural products poses a critical global food safety threat, demanding an urgent paradigm shift from conventional postcontamination quantification to proactive early warning strategies. This comprehensive review critically examines the revolutionary transition toward biomarker-driven biosensing platforms that enable the interception of aflatoxigenesis before toxin accumulation. By systematically analyzing stage-specific biomarkers─including fungal spores/volatile organic compounds, regulatory genes, metabolic proteins, and critical biosynthetic precursors─we elucidate how temporal biosensing creates crucial intervention windows. The review further evaluates advances in nanomaterial-enhanced transducers, multiplexed detection platforms, and AI-integrated sensor arrays while addressing persistent challenges in bioreceptor specificity and field deployment. Future progress hinges on the development of quantitative biomarker-toxin correlation models, advancing point-of-care devices, and implementing integrated monitoring networks. Distinct from existing reviews, this work presents a novel chronobiology-aligned framework for aflatoxin contamination by integrating biomarker discovery with biosensing innovation to enable proactive risk management and strengthen global food security.
summary: This paper presents a systematic examination of early-warning biosensing strategies for aflatoxin contamination, shifting focus from detecting toxins after they accumulate to identifying fungal activity before toxin synthesis occurs. The review identifies stage-specific biomarkers—including fungal spores, volatile organic compounds released by fungi, regulatory genes controlling toxin production, metabolic proteins, and precursor compounds in the biosynthetic pathway—that signal progression toward toxin accumulation. The paper evaluates technical advances in nanomaterial-based detection devices, multiplexed platforms capable of measuring multiple biomarkers simultaneously, and sensor arrays enhanced with machine learning, while acknowledging ongoing limitations in the specificity of biological recognition elements and the practical challenges of deploying sensors in agricultural field conditions. The authors propose that future development requires quantitative models linking specific biomarkers to actual toxin levels, portable devices suitable for on-site testing, and coordinated monitoring networks across supply chains.  This research documents a material entanglement between detection technology and agricultural vulnerability. The shift from postcontamination quantification to early-warning biosensing reveals how aflatoxin contamination—a fungal metabolic process shaped by storage conditions, crop stress, and economic pressures on postharvest handling—has become legible only through engineered biosensors. The biomarkers themselves (volatile compounds, regulatory genes, metabolic proteins) are not neutral signals but products of specific ecological and economic contexts: the same fungal colonization that signals contamination risk also reflects broader patterns of crop exposure, storage infrastructure, and the temporal pressures of global commodity chains where intervention windows are narrow and detection costs must remain competitive.

title: The chromatin remodeling factor Arp9 modulates drug-resistance and plays a key role in aflatoxins biosynthesis under mammalian-physiological-temperature in Aspergillus flavus
source: PLOS Pathogens
excerpt:
by Dongmei Ma, Yanfang Yao, Chi Yang, Hong Lin, Minghui Sun, Yuanyuan Gao, Wenwen Xin, Dandan Wu, Kangfu Ye, Wenxin Luo, Zhenhong Zhuang, Shihua Wang

Aspergillus flavus is the second most prevalent species of Aspergillus causing invasive aspergillosis, but its treatment efforts had been hindered by the continuous emergence of drug-resistant fungal strains, while the underlying mechanisms remain largely unexplored. In this study, we investigated the role of the chromatin remodeling factor Arp9 in A. flavus drug-resistant. We show that Arp9 up-regulates the chromatin accessibility of the Erg3 and Erg6 promoters, thereby increasing their transcription levels and enhancing ergosterol synthesis. Therefore, the absence of Arp9 enhances A. flavus sensitivity to amphotericin B (AMB). Additionally, by down-regulating chromatin accessibility of Erg11A gene promoter, Arp9 leads to the decrease of its transcription level and subsequently reduces A. flavus resistance to voriconazole (VOR). Co-immunoprecipitation analysis revealed that Arp9 exists in both SWI/SNF and RSC complex. Drug susceptibility test results indicated that the drug sensitivity response induced by Arp9 may be unique to Arp9,
summary: Aspergillus flavus, the second most common Aspergillus species causing invasive fungal infection in humans, develops resistance to antifungal drugs through mechanisms that remain poorly understood. This paper identifies the chromatin remodeling factor Arp9 as a key regulator of drug resistance in this organism. The study demonstrates that Arp9 increases accessibility of genetic material at the Erg3 and Erg6 gene promoters, thereby boosting production of ergosterol—a critical component of fungal cell membranes—which enhances resistance to amphotericin B. Conversely, Arp9 reduces accessibility at the Erg11A gene promoter, lowering resistance to voriconazole. Co-immunoprecipitation analysis revealed that Arp9 functions within two protein complexes involved in chromatin remodeling. The drug sensitivity patterns induced by Arp9 appear distinct to this particular factor.  Within the material ecology of clinical antifungal treatment, this paper documents how a single chromatin remodeling protein orchestrates competing metabolic priorities—simultaneously enhancing resistance to one drug class while reducing it to another. The fungus does not passively resist; rather, Arp9 actively modulates which chemical defenses are assembled and when, revealing drug resistance not as a fixed trait but as a plastic, negotiated outcome shaped by the organism's capacity to reorganize its own genetic material in response to pharmaceutical pressure. This adaptive flexibility suggests that antifungal resistance in Aspergillus flavus emerges not from random mutation alone but from the fungus's ability to reprogram its metabolic machinery—a capacity that complicates treatment strategies premised on single-drug efficacy.

title: Enhancement in the nutritional profile and hepatoprotective activity of pine pollen via Aspergillus niger fermentation
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Chemistry
excerpt: Fermentation with Aspergillus niger significantly enhances the hepatoprotective effects of pine pollen. This study evaluated the effects of Aspergillus niger-fermented pine pollen (FPP) against alcoholic fatty liver disease (AFLD) using ethanol-induced HepG2 cells and an AFLD mouse model. In AFLD mice, FPP markedly reduced hepatic triglyceride levels from 1.47 ± 0.13 to 0.90 ± 0.11 mg/g and serum alanine aminotransferase (ALT) from 2.27 ± 0.63 to 1.02 ± 0.15 U/mL, indicating significant alleviation of lipid accumulation and liver injury. Mechanistically, FPP suppressed lipogenesis by downregulating PPARγ and SREBP-1c, while promoting fatty acid oxidation through upregulation of PPARα and CPT1. Untargeted metabolomic analysis further revealed substantial fermentation-induced metabolic remodeling and identified six key metabolites closely correlated with improvements in liver injury, lipid metabolism, and oxidative stress. Overall, these findings demonstrate that Aspergillus niger-fermented pine pollen may serve as a promising functional food for AFLD prevention.
summary: Fermentation with Aspergillus niger substantially altered the biochemical profile of pine pollen and enhanced its protective effects against alcoholic fatty liver disease. This study tested fermented pine pollen in both cultured liver cells exposed to ethanol and in mice with alcohol-induced fatty liver disease. In diseased mice, fermented pine pollen reduced hepatic triglyceride accumulation from 1.47 ± 0.13 to 0.90 ± 0.11 milligrams per gram of tissue and lowered serum alanine aminotransferase (a marker of liver damage) from 2.27 ± 0.63 to 1.02 ± 0.15 units per millilitre, indicating reduced lipid buildup and liver injury. The mechanism involved suppression of fat synthesis genes (PPARγ and SREBP-1c) and activation of fat-burning pathways (PPARα and CPT1). Untargeted metabolite-level analysis analysis identified six key metabolites produced during fermentation that correlated with improvements in liver function, fat metabolism, and cellular stress resistance.  Aspergillus niger here functions simultaneously as a metabolic transformer and a therapeutic agent—the same fungus flagged in other contexts as a source of toxins becomes, through fermentation, a vehicle for producing compounds that protect liver tissue from alcohol-induced damage. The fermentation process does not simply concentrate existing pine pollen properties but generates novel metabolites whose appearance correlates with hepatoprotective effects, revealing how microbial metabolism can rewrite the therapeutic potential of plant material under conditions of controlled industrial cultivation.

title: Antifungal activity of the antimicrobial peptide RP557 against priority fungal pathogens
source: Microbiology: Most Recent Articles
excerpt:
Background. Natural host defence molecules, part of innate immunity and the first line of defence, are evolutionarily conserved. Some pharmaceutical properties undesirable for clinical use led to the rational design of synthetic molecules with constructed peptide arrangements, giving a novel therapeutic avenue. A prior publication showed synthetic peptide RP557 inhibition and killing of fluconazole-sensitive and resistant Candida species isolates, biofilm inhibition, no resistance induction, direct membrane action, negligible mammalian cell toxicity and topical efficacy in a rodent vaginal candidiasis model. These findings highlight the relevance of investigating RP557 activity against other fungal pathogens.

Objective. We evaluated the antifungal spectrum of the RP557 against World Health Organization-listed priority fungal pathogens, including endemic and skin fungal pathogens, both alone and in combination with commercial antifungal drugs.

Methods. The antifungal spectrum was evaluated by broth dilution vs. clinical isolates, and we present 76 MICs (mcg ml−1) performed according to M27 or M38 CLSI documents, 35 checkerboard interactions with antifungals and 10 minimum fu
summary: This paper presents findings on the antifungal properties of a synthetic peptide called RP557 tested against fungal pathogens designated as priorities by the World Health Organization. Prior work had demonstrated that RP557 could kill both drug-sensitive and drug-resistant Candida species, prevent surface-colonising microbial communities, act directly on fungal cell membranes, and show effectiveness in a rodent model of vaginal candidiasis without inducing resistance or harming mammalian cells. This study extended that investigation by evaluating RP557 activity against a broader range of fungal pathogens, including species that cause endemic infections and skin diseases, using standardized laboratory methods. The researchers measured the minimum inhibitory concentration (the lowest concentration needed to stop fungal growth) for 76 clinical isolates, tested 35 drug combinations pairing RP557 with existing antifungal medications, and conducted 10 additional experiments—though the abstract is incomplete and does not specify the outcomes of these tests.  The design of RP557 as a synthetic alternative to natural antimicrobial peptides reflects a particular response to pharmaceutical constraints: natural host defence molecules, while evolutionarily proven, required chemical redesign to become clinically viable. The testing strategy—pairing RP557 with existing antifungal drugs—reveals an underlying material dependency: rather than replacing established treatments, the peptide is being positioned as a complement to drugs already embedded in clinical practice and supply chains. The choice to test against World Health Organization-listed priority pathogens situates this work within global health hierarchies that define which fungal threats warrant investment, a categorization that itself reflects patterns of disease burden shaped by labour conditions, housing quality, and access to preventive care.

title: Microplastic-pathogen interactions differentially modulate tomato Fusarium wilt severity: The dependence of polymer biodegradability
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Journal of Hazardous Materials
excerpt: Microplastics (MPs) are pervasively present in agricultural soils, adversely affecting rhizosphere ecology and plant physiology; however, their influence on soil-borne pathogens and crop health remains poorly understood. Here, we systematically investigate the effects of exogenous MPs on Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. lycopersici (FOL) colonization, virulence and antagonistic network in tomato rhizosphere using hydroponic, soil-based, and in vitro co-culture assays. We found that conventional polyethylene (PE) exacerbated wilt severity by 19 %, whereas biodegradable polylactic acid (PLA) reduced it by 14 %. This divergence arose from MP-mediated pathogen colonization patterns: PE adhered to root surfaces and facilitated colonization, whereas PLA leachates generated during degradation suppressed growth. Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry analysis and in vitro antifungal assays identified two key antifungal pathways: the intermediate L-lactide induced rhizosphere acidification, and the leached plasticizer tris(2-butoxyethyl) phosphate, which exhibited direct antifungal activity (minimum inhibitory concentration = 50 mg·L-1; inhibition rate = 15.5 %). Transcriptomic profiling revealed that PE upregulated N transport and metabolism genes in FOL, whereas PLA downregulated the expression of the plant cell wall-degrading enzyme gene FOXG_11947 (log2 fold change = -3.18), reducing β-1,3-glucanase activity by 75.5 %, and consequently impairing pathogen virulence toward the host roots. Beyond singular interaction with FOL, PLA shifted rhizosphere microbiome toward an antagonistic network, which consequently reduced Fusarium abundance by 1.31 %. Our findings establish a polymer-dependent mechanism by which MPs regulate soil-borne disease and underscore an underappreciated co-exposure threat, providing a scientific basis for sustainable plastic use in agriculture.
summary: Microplastics in agricultural soil operate as dual agents in the pathogenic ecology of tomato wilt disease, their effects contingent on chemical composition and degradation pathways. This paper examined how conventional polyethylene and biodegradable polylactic acid microplastics influence the colonization and disease-causing ability of *Fusarium oxysporum* f. sp. *lycopersici* in tomato roots using hydroponic systems, soil-based experiments, and controlled culture assays. Conventional polyethylene increased wilt severity by 19 percent through direct adhesion to root surfaces that enhanced pathogen colonization, whereas polylactic acid reduced severity by 14 percent via degradation byproducts: the compound L-lactide triggered soil acidification, and the leached plasticizer tris(2-butoxyethyl) phosphate demonstrated direct antifungal activity at concentrations of 50 milligrams per litre. At the molecular level, polylactic acid suppressed expression of the fungal cell wall-degrading enzyme gene FOXG_11947 by 3.18-fold, reducing the enzyme β-1,3-glucanase activity by 75.5 percent and impairing the pathogen's capacity to breach root tissue. The polylactic acid treatment also restructured the root zone microbial community toward counteracting interactions, reducing *Fusarium* abundance by 1.31 percent.  The divergent outcomes reveal how the same material category—microplastics—becomes either a vector for disease intensification or a source of pathogen suppression depending on its chemical stability and degradation profile. What emerges from this assemblage is not simply a choice between harmful and beneficial plastics, but rather an exposure landscape where agricultural reliance on synthetic polymers creates simultaneous risks and remediation pathways, both rooted in the material transformations of degradation itself—a condition that binds crop health, soil chemistry, and microbial networks into entangled dependencies on plastic's chemical fate.

title: A dual-monomer magnetic imprinted sorbent for fast removal and trace-level monitoring of hazardous aflatoxin B1
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Chemistry
excerpt: Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) is a highly toxic contaminant commonly found in foods, and its trace detection faces severe interference from complex matrices. To achieve efficient enrichment and rapid detection of AFB1, this study designed and synthesized a magnetic dual-functional monomer molecularly imprinted polymer (MMIP) with magnetic nanoparticles as the core and dummy templated ECC as the imprinting molecule. Through surface imprinting technology combined with methacrylic acid and styrene as dual functional monomers, an imprinted layer with specific recognition sites was constructed, while systematically optimizing synthesis conditions and adsorption-desorption parameters. The results demonstrated that the prepared MMIP exhibited high adsorption capacity (15.385 mg/g), rapid adsorption kinetics (reaching equilibrium within 2 min), good selectivity (imprinting factor of 2.40), large enrichment factor (23.8), and excellent reusability (only 6.1% decrease in adsorption efficiency after 10 cycles). Using this material as a MMIP-based magnetic dispersive solid-phase extraction combined with UPLC-MS/MS, an analytical method for trace AFB1 in complex food matrices was established, showing a linear range of 0.005-50 μg/L, a detection limit of 0.002 μg/L, and recovery rates ranging from 86.13% to 113.44%. This study provides a reliable strategy for the highly selective enrichment and rapid detection of trace AFB1 in complex food matrices.
summary: Aflatoxin B1, a toxic fungal metabolite commonly found in foods, presents detection challenges in complex food matrices due to interference from surrounding compounds. This paper describes a magnetic polymer material engineered to selectively capture and concentrate aflatoxin B1 from food samples. The material combines magnetic nanoparticles as a core with an imprinted polymer layer constructed from methacrylic acid and styrene as dual functional monomers, designed to recognize aflatoxin B1 through surface imprinting technology. Testing demonstrated the material achieved high binding capacity (15.385 milligrams per gram), reached equilibrium binding within 2 minutes, showed selectivity with an imprinting factor of 2.40, concentrated the target compound 23.8-fold, and retained 93.9% of its binding efficiency after 10 reuse cycles. When deployed in a detection pipeline combining magnetic extraction with liquid chromatography and tandem mass spectrometry, the method detected aflatoxin B1 across a range of 0.005 to 50 micrograms per litre, with a detection limit of 0.002 micrograms per litre and recovery rates between 86.13% and 113.44%.  The fungal toxin that triggers this technological intervention is simultaneously a marker of material vulnerability and a driver of material innovation. The same contamination that signals food spoilage, crop loss, and human exposure—patterns shaped by storage conditions, labour practices, and economic pressures on agricultural systems—also catalyzes the development of increasingly sophisticated capture and detection polymers. The rapid kinetics and reusability of this magnetic imprinted material reveal how hazard mitigation becomes embedded in manufactured surfaces, shifting the burden of managing fungal metabolites from prevention and remediation to engineered detection and sequestration within industrial food supply chains.

title: Occurrence of mycotoxins contamination in commercial feline and canine foods from China
source: Latest Results
excerpt: Mycotoxins are widely present in commercial pet food and pose significant risks to pet health. This study evaluated the contamination status of 18 mycotoxins in 126 commercially available feline and canine foods collected from online and offline markets in China between 2021 and 2023, using ultra-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS/MS). Eight mycotoxins were detected, with fumonisin B1 (FB1) showing the highest occurrence (97.26% in canine food and 94.34% in feline food), at mean concentrations ranging from 886.1 to 903.88&nbsp;µg/kg, followed by zearalenone (ZEN) (95.89% in canine food and 90.57% in feline food; mean concentrations 410.2 and 459.02&nbsp;µg/kg). Co-occurrence analysis showed that 95.89% of canine and 92.46% of feline foods contained two or more mycotoxins, and detection of six mycotoxins within a single product was most common (42.42% in canine food and 35.85% in feline food). Regression analysis indicated that lower retail prices and the inclusion of corn were associated with higher ZEN exceeding regulatory limit rates, whereas higher crude fiber content was inversely correlated with ZEN contamination. These findings demonstrate the widespread presence of mycotoxins in Chinese pet foods and underscore the need for strengthened monitoring and raw material quality control to safeguard companion animal health.
summary: This study examined 126 commercially available pet foods for cats and dogs purchased from Chinese markets between 2021 and 2023, testing for 18 different mycotoxins using ultra-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry. Eight mycotoxins were detected across the samples. Fumonisin B1 appeared in 97.26 percent of dog foods and 94.34 percent of cat foods, with mean concentrations of 886.1 to 903.88 micrograms per kilogram, while zearalenone was found in 95.89 percent of dog foods and 90.57 percent of cat foods at mean concentrations of 410.2 to 459.02 micrograms per kilogram. The majority of products contained multiple mycotoxins simultaneously—95.89 percent of dog foods and 92.46 percent of cat foods held two or more toxins, with six different mycotoxins detected in a single product most frequently (42.42 percent of dog foods and 35.85 percent of cat foods). Analysis revealed that lower retail prices and the presence of corn in formulations correlated with higher rates of zearalenone exceeding regulatory limits, while higher crude fiber content showed an inverse relationship with zearalenone contamination.  Within the material chains of pet food production and distribution in China, mycotoxin contamination emerges not simply as a failure of quality control but as a structured outcome of cost-cutting practices and ingredient sourcing. The correlation between price point and toxin load—where cheaper products accumulate higher contamination—reveals how economic pressures shape the biological composition of food destined for companion animals, with corn-based formulations becoming vectors for fungal metabolites. The widespread co-occurrence of multiple mycotoxins in single products suggests that remediation or substitution of one contaminated ingredient does not resolve the underlying vulnerability; rather, it points toward systemic dependencies on agricultural materials already compromised by storage conditions, transport, or pre-existing fungal colonization in supply chains that prioritize cost over safety monitoring.

title: Aflatoxin levels in dried figs export from Turkey
source: Latest Results
excerpt: In this study, a total of 1253 dried fig samples were analyzed for aflatoxin contamination. 838 samples were collected during 2024, and 415 samples were collected during 2025 from different export companies in Aydın province in Turkey. Analyzes were carried out in an accredited laboratory operating in Aydın province and the HPLC-FLD method was used. While 172 (50.2%) of 838 dried fig samples examined in 2024 contained aflatoxin in the range of 0.43–60.73&nbsp;µg/kg, 81 (19.5%) of 415 samples examined in 2025 contained aflatoxin in the range of 0.87–40.08&nbsp;µg/kg. When 2024 and 2025 were evaluated together, detectable levels of aflatoxin were detected in a total of 253 dried fig samples, and 29 of them were found above the EU upper limits. The sample rate exceeding the EU upper limit on the basis of total fig samples is 2.2% and 2.7% for 2024 and 2025, respectively.
summary: Dried fig exports from Turkey show persistent aflatoxin contamination across consecutive years of monitoring. This paper analyzed 1,253 samples collected from export companies in Aydın province—838 samples in 2024 and 415 in 2025—using high-performance liquid chromatography with fluorescence detection. In 2024, 172 samples (50.2%) contained detectable aflatoxin ranging from 0.43 to 60.73 micrograms per kilogram; in 2025, 81 samples (19.5%) showed contamination between 0.87 and 40.08 micrograms per kilogram. Across both years, 253 samples contained measurable aflatoxin, with 29 exceeding European Union regulatory limits—representing 2.2% of 2024 samples and 2.7% of 2025 samples relative to the total tested.  The contamination pattern reveals a material entanglement between agricultural commodity production and regulatory compliance in a high-volume export system. Figs destined for European markets carry both the metabolic signature of mould activity in storage and the economic pressure to move product across borders; the toxin itself becomes a marker of where safety enforcement succeeds and where it fails, distributed unevenly across the supply chain. That nearly one-fifth to one-half of samples contain detectable aflatoxin—even as the fraction exceeding limits remains formally low—documents the routine presence of fungal toxins in a food commodity, normalized within acceptable thresholds yet persistent enough to require continuous monitoring and occasional rejection at the point of export.

title: Filamentous cyanobacteria exhibit a unique navigation strategy due to their chiral gliding
source: Cell Biology and Microbiology News - Biology news, Microbiology
excerpt: Cyanobacteria are among the most significant life forms in the history of our planet. As one of the first organisms to produce oxygen through photosynthesis, they shaped early Earth and created the atmosphere in which complex life could develop. A new study, published in Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, shows that filamentous cyanobacteria also developed a navigation mechanism to control their movement when gliding across surfaces.
summary: This paper describes how filamentous cyanobacteria navigate across surfaces using a gliding mechanism controlled by a chiral (asymmetrical) movement pattern. The research, published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, identifies this navigation strategy as a distinct biological capability. Beyond the title and abstract provided, the source text does not contain specific data on the organisms tested, the surfaces studied, the experimental methods used, or quantitative measurements of the gliding behaviour or chiral properties.  Filamentous cyanobacteria—organisms that fundamentally altered planetary chemistry by producing oxygen and enabling complex life—operate as both ancient architects of habitability and contemporary navigators of surfaces. Their capacity to direct movement through chiral gliding reveals how even microscopic organisms develop precise spatial strategies, yet the mechanisms linking this navigation ability to survival under variable conditions remain unspecified in this summary. The tension between cyanobacteria as foundational planetary engineers and as active agents responding to immediate material constraints suggests a deeper entanglement: their movement across surfaces may reflect not merely evolutionary refinement but also adaptation to specific chemical or physical conditions that shape where and how they persist in contemporary ecosystems.

title: Auranofin and iodoquinol as promising repurposing drugs against filamentous fungi: antifungal activity and cellular alterations
source: American Society for Microbiology: Microbiology Spectrum: Table of Contents
excerpt: Opportunistic fungal infections have become an increasing health threat, particularly in immunocompromised individuals, due to their high virulence, biofilm-forming capacity, and resistance to conventional antifungals. Given the limited therapeutic arsenal, drug repurposing represents a promising strategy for antifungal discovery. This study evaluated the antifungal activity and mechanisms of auranofin and iodoquinol against filamentous fungi listed as World Health Organization (WHO) priority pathogens, including Aspergillus fumigatus, Fusarium oxysporum, Scedosporium boydii, Lomentospora prolificans, Rhizopus oryzae, Mucor velutinosus, and Cunninghamella sp. Although Aspergillus flavus is not included in the WHO priority list, it was included in this study due to its clinical relevance as a major cause of aspergillosis and fungal rhinosinusitis. Auranofin displayed fungicidal activity against Aspergillus spp., L. prolificans, S. boydii, and R. oryzae, while iodoquinol exhibited potent but mainly fungistatic effects, with minimum inhibitory concentration values ranging from 0.625 to 20 µM. Both compounds inhibited early growth and reduced preformed biofilms by over 50% in biomass and metabolic activity. Stress susceptibility and fluorescence assays indicated that auranofin interferes with lipid homeostasis, cell wall integrity, and oxidative stress responses, whereas iodoquinol alters membrane lipids, mannose, and chitin distribution, suggesting multitarget surface effects. Scanning electron microscopy revealed consistent ultrastructural alterations, including hyphal deformation and increased extracellular matrix deposition in A. fumigatus and F. oxysporum treated with sub-inhibitory drug concentrations, corroborating the observed cellular stress responses. Drug interaction assays demonstrated additive or synergistic effects with voriconazole and amphotericin B, but not
summary: This paper evaluated two repurposed drugs—auranofin and iodoquinol—against eight species of filamentous fungi classified as World Health Organization priority pathogens, including *Aspergillus fumigatus*, *Fusarium oxysporum*, *Scedosporium boydii*, *Lomentospora prolificans*, *Rhizopus oryzae*, and *Mucor velutinosus*, plus *Aspergillus flavus* due to its clinical relevance. Auranofin demonstrated fungicidal activity (killing fungi outright) against *Aspergillus* species, *L. prolificans*, *S. boydii*, and *R. oryzae*, while iodoquinol showed potent but primarily fungistatic effects (inhibiting growth without killing), with minimum inhibitory concentration values between 0.625 and 20 micromolar. Both compounds suppressed early fungal growth and reduced established surface-bound microbial communities by more than 50% in biomass and metabolic activity. Scanning electron microscopy documented consistent physical damage to fungal cells, including deformation of hyphal filaments and increased extracellular matrix deposition in *A. fumigatus* and *F. oxysporum* at sub-lethal drug doses. The two drugs operated through distinct but overlapping mechanisms: auranofin disrupted lipid balance, cell wall integrity, and oxidative stress defenses, while iodoquinol altered the distribution of membrane lipids, mannose, and chitin across the fungal cell surface.  Within contexts of limited antifungal options and rising drug resistance, this paper documents how existing pharmaceutical compounds—originally developed for unrelated clinical purposes—can be redirected toward opportunistic fungal pathogens. The dual mechanisms of action and surface-bound microbial communities-disrupting capacity reveal how a single compound can simultaneously target multiple vulnerabilities in fungal physiology, yet the distinction between fungicidal and fungistatic effects suggests that the therapeutic utility of these repurposed drugs will depend on host immune status and infection site, embedding their efficacy within the variable biological and social conditions of immune-weakened populations where such infections concentrate.

title: Identification of Aspergillus at section and species levels by artificial intelligence-based microscopic morphology image recognition
source: American Society for Microbiology: Journal of Clinical Microbiology: Table of Contents
excerpt: Rapid and accurate identification of Aspergillus species in clinical microbiology laboratories is crucial for aspergillosis diagnosis and antifungal therapy. However, traditional methods still face challenges in distinguishing phylogenetically related species due to their morphological similarities. This study presents FungalNet, a deep learning model integrating ResNet-50 architecture with Focal Loss algorithm, specifically designed to enhance feature extraction for Aspergillus identification. A total of 12,000 high-resolution images were obtained from lactophenol cotton blue-stained slide preparations under a 100× oil immersion objective, among which 311 images were excluded through a novel quality control approach combining fivefold cross-validation and expert manual review. The performance of four deep learning models (FungalNet and three established models) for identifying Aspergillus species and sections was evaluated using the remaining 11,689 qualified images. FungalNet demonstrated superior classification performance, achieving overall accuracies of 98.45% and 97.85% at the section and species levels, respectively. These results indicate that FungalNet shows significant promise for rapid and accurate identification of Aspergillus species. With further optimization and multicenter validation, this tool could potentially be integrated into routine diagnostic workflows to enhance the efficiency and reliability of fungal identification in clinical settings.IMPORTANCEThis study integrates microscopic morphology identification with deep learning to address the challenge of accurate Aspergillus species identification. Twelve clinically isolated Aspergillus species belonging to eight different sections were included. From touch-tape slide preparations with lactophenol cotton blue staining under a 100× oil immersion objective, 11,689 qualified images were collected and analyzed using FungalNet (our proposed model) along with
summary: This paper presents FungalNet, a deep learning system designed to identify *Aspergillus* species from microscope images of stained fungal preparations. The study collected 12,000 high-resolution images from lactophenol cotton blue-stained slides prepared from twelve clinically isolated *Aspergillus* species representing eight different sections, examined under 100× oil immersion microscopy. After removing 311 images through quality control combining cross-validation and expert review, the researchers evaluated FungalNet—a model combining ResNet-50 architecture with Focal Loss algorithm—against three established deep learning models using the remaining 11,689 qualified images. FungalNet achieved classification accuracies of 98.45% at the section level and 97.85% at the species level, outperforming the comparison models. The authors propose that with further optimization and validation across multiple clinical sites, FungalNet could be integrated into routine diagnostic workflows to accelerate fungal identification.  Within clinical laboratory settings, this work reveals a material entanglement between diagnostic speed and diagnostic labour. The morphological similarities that make *Aspergillus* species difficult to distinguish by eye—the very feature that has historically demanded expert time and interpretive skill—become legible to the machine vision system through standardized staining and magnification. Yet the system's reliability depends on human gatekeeping: the quality control process that excluded over 300 images demonstrates that even high-resolution microscopy produces material variation that requires expert judgment to filter. The speed gain for laboratories thus rests on a prior labour of curation, suggesting that automation in clinical mycology does not eliminate human expertise but rather redistributes it, concentrating interpretive work upstream in image preparation and validation rather than downstream in species identification.

title: What is Aspergillus, the fungus behind recent hospital deaths?
source: English – The Conversation
summary: This publication is still hot off the press. That means the paper is not yet indexed by global databases like CrossRef, Semantic Scholar or OpenAlex. If you want to learn what the research might have to say about life on Planet Mould you'll just have to read it yourself!

title: Occurrence and health risk assessment of AFM1 in commercial yogurt in Thailand
source: Latest Results
excerpt: Milk and dairy products, including yogurt, provide essential nutrients and are widely consumed as valuable protein sources. However, considering then increasing prevalence of Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) in the diet of dairy cows, concerns arise regarding the possible contamination of milk and dairy products with AFM1, the major metabolite of AFB1 excreted in the milk of lactating animals. AFM1, although less potent when compared to AFB1, has been associated with an increase in the prevalence of human hepatic carcinoma. Hence, following a survey on AFM1 contamination of raw and processed drinking milk, this study aimed to evaluate the occurrence of aflatoxin M1 (AFM1) in yogurt available in Bangkok, Thailand, and to estimate the associated dietary exposure and health risk among different population groups. A total of 180 plain yogurt samples were randomly collected from supermarkets during March to June 2024. The samples were extracted using the QuEChERS methodology and analyzed using a validated liquid chromatography- tandem mass spectrometry method. With this method, residual amounts of AFM1 were detected in 12 out of 180 yoghurt samples, with concentrations ranging from 0.031 to 0.048 µg
summary: Raw milk consumed by dairy cattle becomes contaminated with Aflatoxin B1, a mould metabolite produced in feed. When lactating animals consume contaminated feed, they excrete AFM1—the primary metabolite of AFB1—into their milk. This paper surveyed yogurt samples collected from Bangkok supermarkets between March and June 2024, analysing 180 plain yogurt products using liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry to detect residual AFM1. The analysis found AFM1 in 12 of the 180 samples, with concentrations ranging from 0.031 to 0.048 micrograms per kilogram. Although AFM1 is less toxic than its parent compound AFB1, it has been linked to increased rates of liver cancer in humans, prompting the researchers to assess dietary exposure and health risk across different population groups in Thailand.  Within the food system documented here, yogurt emerges as a site where agricultural contamination—originating in animal feed—becomes embedded in a processed dairy product destined for human consumption. The detection of AFM1 in supermarket yogurt reveals how mould metabolites travel through commodity chains and accumulate in bodies via everyday consumption. Rather than a simple contamination event, this pattern reflects the material dependencies of industrial dairy production: the feed sourcing practices, storage conditions, and regulatory gaps that allow AFB1-producing moulds to persist upstream, only to reappear as AFM1 in the finished product on retail shelves. The health risk assessment becomes a measure not only of individual exposure but of structural vulnerabilities embedded in how milk is produced, processed, and distributed across Thailand.

title: Current status of aflatoxin contamination and mitigation efforts in mozambique: a systematic review
source: Latest Results
excerpt: Aflatoxin continues to be a major problem for public health and trade in Mozambique. The use of products and sub-products based on maize, cassava, and groundnuts, which are susceptible to aflatoxin contamination is one of the main causes. This systematic review provides an overview of the current state of aflatoxin contamination and mitigation efforts in Mozambique. The study reviewed scientific articles published between 1st January 2004 and 31st July 2024 in peer reviewed journals, after conducting the evaluation process, 667 articles were excluded, and 68 articles fulfilled the inclusion criteria and were included in this review. Based on these included articles, progressive research has taken place over the years looking for solutions to control aflatoxins throughout the crop value chain in Mozambique. However, aflatoxin contamination of products across the country remains high. The Aspergillus section Flavi species reported in the country are A. flavus (L morphotype), A. parasiticus, A. flavus (S morphotype) and A. tamarii. Although some S morphotypes (SB and SBG) such as A. texensis, A. toxicus, and A. minisclerotigenes have been also identified there are still no in-depth studies. Unless such species are studied, biological control efforts in the country will be impacted.
summary: This systematic review examined scientific literature published between January 2004 and July 2024 to assess aflatoxin contamination and control efforts in Mozambique. Of 735 articles screened, 68 met inclusion criteria for analysis. The review documents that aflatoxin contamination remains widespread across the country, driven primarily by the use of maize, cassava, and groundnuts—all crops susceptible to contamination by toxin-producing moulds. Four species from the Aspergillus section Flavi have been identified in Mozambique: Aspergillus flavus (both large and small morphotypes), Aspergillus parasiticus, and Aspergillus tamarii. Additionally, three further species—Aspergillus texensis, Aspergillus toxicus, and Aspergillus minisclerotigenes—have been detected but lack detailed study. This paper notes that research into control strategies has expanded over the two decades examined, yet contamination levels remain high, and the absence of in-depth investigation into lesser-studied species threatens the effectiveness of biological control programmes.  The research reveals a material entanglement between agricultural staples and public health vulnerability in Mozambique. The same crops that sustain food security and trade—maize, cassava, groundnuts—are also the sites where toxin-producing moulds establish themselves, creating a dependency on control measures that remain incomplete. The identification of multiple Aspergillus species, some still poorly characterized, exposes a gap between scientific documentation and practical intervention: moulds circulate through crop value chains faster than knowledge about their behaviour accumulates, leaving biological control efforts operating with incomplete information about the organisms they aim to suppress.

title: Longitudinal profiling of toxigenic fungi and mycotoxin risks in Coix lacryma-jobi L.
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Journal of Hazardous Materials
excerpt: Mycotoxin contamination in Coix lacryma-jobi L. threatens its safety as a food and medicinal crop, yet studies on toxigenic fungi in the field and associated dietary exposure risks remain limited. This longitudinal study (2021, 2023-2024) characterized mycotoxin contamination across the field-to-market chain, encompassing field occurrence, toxigenic fungal etiology, market samples contamination, and dietary exposure risks in real-world populations. ZEN was detected in 100 % of samples in 2021 (n = 121) and 2024 (n = 306). Among 273 fungal isolates, FGSC were responsible for ZEN contamination. Additionally, higher ZEN levels were found in husk-bran mixture than in polished seeds. Detection of Aspergillus spp. and aflatoxin in polished seed indicated contamination initiated during growth. 29 of 37 mycotoxins were detected, with widespread co-contamination patterns (ZEN+BEA, ZEN+STC, AFB1 +ZEN+STC) observed. Risk assessment revealed potential health hazards from ZENs and aflatoxins for consumers of edible coix. This study provides valuable insights for field management and market surveillance.
summary: Zizania (Job's tears) crops show consistent contamination with zearalenone across field and market stages, according to this longitudinal study spanning 2021 and 2023-2024. The fungal genus Fusarium Gibberella Species Complex was identified as the primary source of zearalenone in 273 isolated fungal strains. Zearalenone appeared in 100 percent of field samples in both 2021 (121 samples) and 2024 (306 samples), with higher concentrations detected in the husk-bran mixture than in polished seeds. Detection of Aspergillus species and aflatoxin B1 in polished seeds indicated contamination occurring during crop growth rather than after harvest. The study identified 29 distinct mycotoxins across samples, with widespread patterns of multiple toxins co-occurring in single samples—including zearalenone combined with beauvericin, zearalenone combined with stemphyltoxin, and aflatoxin B1 combined with zearalenone and stemphyltoxin. Risk assessment indicated potential health hazards from zearalenone and aflatoxins for people consuming edible coix products.  The contamination patterns documented here reveal how a single crop variety becomes a site where multiple fungal metabolites accumulate and persist through processing stages. The movement of toxins from field to market—and the differential concentration in different seed fractions—demonstrates how the material processing of food (the separation of husk from polished seed) does not eliminate exposure but rather redistributes risk across supply chains and consumer populations. The persistent detection of zearalenone across all samples and years, alongside the emergence of multiple co-contaminating toxins, suggests that dietary exposure to these fungal compounds is not incidental but structural to the production and consumption of this crop under current conditions.

title: Magnetic controlled label-free homogeneous electrochemical biosensing of aflatoxin B1 based on Nanochannel-gated signal amplification strategy
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Chemistry
excerpt: The rapid and highly sensitive determination of trace aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) is of great importance for food safety. In this research, a novel magnetic controlled label-free nanochannel spacer bioreactor with multiple signal amplification was developed based on nanochannels and ferrofluids for the first time. On the one hand, this design enables physical separation of AFB1 recognition and detection processes in one detection system, which can reduce the interference effectively from complex food samples while improving detection efficiency. On the other hand, the nanochannel spacer bioreactor combines exonuclease III-assisted target recycling and hybridization chain reaction signal amplifications, which improves the detection sensitivity effectively. The method achieved a wide linear range of 10-5 - 102 pg/mL, an ultralow detection limit of 4 × 10-6 pg/mL, excellent selectivity and anti-interference performance for AFB1 detection. This research presents a promising approach for highly sensitive and efficient detection of trace targets in complex food samples.
summary: This paper describes a magnetic-controlled detection system for identifying aflatoxin B1, a toxic compound produced by moulds, in food samples at extremely low concentrations. The device uses nanochannels—microscopic passages in a material—combined with ferrofluids (liquids containing suspended magnetic particles) to physically separate the recognition step (where aflatoxin B1 binds to a detector) from the measurement step. This separation reduces interference from other compounds naturally present in complex food matrices. The system combines two signal amplification methods: exonuclease III-assisted target recycling, which reuses the detected aflatoxin B1 molecule multiple times to generate stronger signals, and hybridization chain reaction amplification, which builds extended chains of genetic material to further boost detection output. The method achieved detection of aflatoxin B1 across a wide concentration range (10⁻⁵ to 10² picograms per millilitre) with a minimum detectable level of 4 × 10⁻⁶ picograms per millilitre, and demonstrated strong selectivity and resistance to interference from other food components.  Within the material entanglements of food safety monitoring, this paper positions aflatoxin B1 detection as a technical problem solvable through increasingly sophisticated biosensor engineering—yet the toxin itself remains a byproduct of moulds thriving in the very agricultural and storage conditions that capital and austerity have normalized. The nanochannel-gated amplification strategy treats the mould metabolite as a discrete target to be isolated and measured with precision, but this isolation obscures the broader ecological and economic conditions that allow aflatoxin-producing moulds to proliferate in stored grains and processed foods, particularly in regions where grain storage infrastructure reflects historical patterns of underinvestment and labour precarity.

title: Application of machine learning for predicting aflatoxin contamination risk in maize from Texas
source: tandf: Food Additives & Contaminants: Part A: Table of Contents
excerpt: In this study, we developed models for predicting aflatoxin contamination in Texas maize samples. These models were trained on individual or median historical aflatoxin contamination records using machine learning algorithms. The weather, engineering feature (AFRI), remote sensing (NDVI), and soil property data available in the public domain were used as the main input variables for model development. The study utilised 9,694 maize samples collected from 973 Texas counties spanning the period from 2010 to 2023. The performance of all models was evaluated using either a 70/30 training/validation split (30% for validation) or a one-year validation dataset (year 2017). The evaluation was conducted at two threshold levels (5 μg/kg and 20 μg/kg) for high contamination events. The gradient boosting decision tree (GBDT) models developed on median data achieved the highest correct classification rate and balanced accuracy of nearly 99%, while the hierarchical logistic regression (HLR) models for individual data points showed the lowest overall accuracy. The GBDT models were highly effective at predicting the presence or absence of aflatoxin contamination, displaying exceptional sensitivity and specificity.
