The overall accuracy of other models was also acceptable, particularly at the 20 μg/kg threshold, with values ranging from 0.73 to 0.87. The developed models revealed that aflatoxin contamination was significantly associated with higher precipitation/AFRI/NDVI during the pre-flowering period, but was highly linked to lower precipitation/AFRI/NDVI during the flowering and post-flowering periods. Of the 25 soil properties investigated, moisture content, clay percentage, pH, and calcium carbonate were also significantly associated with aflatoxin contamination in maize. The developed models, along with the key influential predictors identified in this study, should be applicable and useful for growers and other relevant parties to take preventive action against aflatoxin cont
summary: This paper developed machine learning models to forecast aflatoxin contamination risk in maize grown across Texas counties. The study trained these models on 9,694 maize samples collected from 973 Texas counties between 2010 and 2023, using publicly available data on weather patterns, the Aflatoxin Risk Index, normalized difference vegetation index measurements from satellite imagery, and soil properties as input variables. Models were tested at two contamination thresholds (5 micrograms per kilogram and 20 micrograms per kilogram) using either a 70/30 training-validation split or a held-out validation year (2017). Gradient boosting decision tree models trained on median historical contamination data achieved nearly 99% correct classification rates, while the hierarchical logistic regression models for individual data points performed less well. The models identified that higher rainfall, vegetation index values, and AFRI during the pre-flowering period were associated with contamination risk, whereas lower values during flowering and post-flowering periods increased risk; among soil properties, moisture content, clay percentage, soil pH, and calcium carbonate showed significant associations with contamination.  These findings reveal how aflatoxin risk in maize becomes legible and actionable through the layering of remote sensing, soil chemistry, and historical contamination records—a technological infrastructure that renders a fungal metabolite visible across agricultural space. Yet this predictive capacity depends entirely on the prior accumulation of contamination data from thousands of samples, meaning the models encode and reproduce the uneven patterns of exposure and testing that already exist in Texas farming landscapes. The ability to forecast contamination risk, and thus to intervene before harvest, emerges not from understanding the mould itself but from mapping the material conditions—moisture, soil composition, seasonal weather—that enable or constrain its growth, making prevention a question of managing the environment rather than the organism.

title: Rapid and reliable diagnosis of mucormycosis using colorimetric loop-mediated isothermal amplification
source: American Society for Microbiology: Journal of Clinical Microbiology: Table of Contents
excerpt: Current diagnostic approaches for mucormycosis are often limited by low sensitivity and prolonged turnaround times, which result in delayed treatment and poor clinical outcomes. We developed a novel diagnostic method utilizing a colorimetric loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) assay for the rapid and sensitive detection of mucormycosis. The assay incorporates specifically designed primers capable of detecting as low as 0.001 picograms (pg) of spiked genomic DNA from Mucorales fungi. This LAMP assay demonstrated a high sensitivity of 98% and a 100% specificity of detecting fungal ribosomal DNA (rDNA) in bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) samples collected from mice infected with Mucorales fungi (n = 48) or from an uninfected control group (n = 15). To align the assay with clinical antifungal therapy, a subset of infected mice was treated with either liposomal amphotericin B (LAMB) or a combination of LAMB and a humanized monoclonal antibody (VX-01) targeting the Mucorales-specific surface protein CotH3. Consistent with the treatment efficacy, the LAMP assay detected significantly lower fungal burdens in BAL samples from mice receiving the combination therapy compared to those treated with LAMB alone or placebo. Further validation was conducted using BAL samples from patients diagnosed with mucormycosis (n = 24) or aspergillosis (n = 17). The assay demonstrated a sensitivity of 79% and a specificity of 94%. These findings highlight the diagnostic potential of this LAMP-based assay as a point-of-care. Its high sensitivity, specificity, and rapid turnaround time position this assay as a promising tool for early and accurate detection of mucormycosis, with the potential to improve patient management and clinical outcomes.IMPORTANCEMucormycosis is a rapidly progressive and fatal fungal infection. Timely diagnosis is critical for effective treatment, yet current diagnostic tools are slow, insensitive, or require complex laboratory procedures
summary: This paper describes a colorimetric loop-mediated isothermal amplification assay designed to detect Mucorales fungi from respiratory samples. The assay uses specially designed genetic primers to identify fungal ribosomal DNA and achieved detection sensitivity down to 0.001 picograms of fungal genetic material. In mouse models, the assay demonstrated 98% sensitivity and 100% specificity when applied to respiratory lavage samples from infected animals (n = 48) and uninfected controls (n = 15). When tested on clinical samples from human patients, the assay achieved 79% sensitivity and 94% specificity in distinguishing mucormycosis (n = 24) from aspergillosis (n = 17). The assay also tracked treatment response, showing reduced fungal detection in respiratory samples from mice treated with combination therapy (liposomal amphotericin B plus a humanized antibody targeting the fungal surface protein CotH3) compared to single-agent treatment or placebo.  The diagnostic gap this paper addresses—the delay between symptom onset and confirmed diagnosis—sits at the intersection of clinical urgency and material constraint. Mucormycosis progresses rapidly, yet current diagnostic methods are slow enough that treatment initiation often precedes confirmation, creating a bind where empirical therapy must proceed without reliable evidence of infection. This assay compresses that diagnostic window by shifting from culture-dependent methods to genetic detection, but the gap between mouse model performance (98% sensitivity) and clinical performance (79% sensitivity) reveals how laboratory-optimized detection encounters the material messiness of human respiratory samples and the variable fungal burdens associated with active disease. The assay's capacity to track treatment response in animal models suggests potential utility not just for diagnosis but for monitoring whether antifungal interventions are reducing fungal load—a capacity that could reshape how clinicians assess whether a chosen therapy is working before clinical deterioration becomes visible.

title: Filamentous fungal communities associated with the eastern larch beetle (Dendroctonus simplex LeConte) in Minnesota, including new Ophiostomatales species
source: tandf: Mycologia: Table of Contents
excerpt: The eastern larch beetle (Dendroctonus simplex LeConte) is a native and historically nonaggressive inhabitant of Larix laricina trees in North America. Since 2001, however, D. simplex has experienced an uncharacteristic and prolonged outbreak that has impacted trees on approximately 90% of the L. laricina forests in Minnesota. To investigate the fungal communities within D. simplex galleries during this outbreak, we isolated fungi from 56 D. simplex-infested trees across seven sites in Minnesota. We recovered 45 fungal species, including four new species in the order Ophiostomatales, proposed as Graphilbum insulare, sp. nov., Ophiostoma itasca, sp. nov., Ophiostoma minnesotense, sp. nov., and Ophiostoma pseudoips, sp. nov. Additionally, based on phylogenetics and morphological traits, we recommend a new taxonomic classification for Leptographium simplex, comb. nov. previously described as Graphium simplex from D. simplex galleries in Vermont, USA. At the community level, most isolates were in the phylum Ascomycota (82% of the total relative abundance), followed by Basidiomycota (12%). The most frequently isolated orders were Ophiostomatales (59% of the total relative abundance), Hypocreales (9%), and Pleosporales (8%). One species, Grosmannia americana, accounted for more than half of all isolates recovered. These results improve our understanding of the fungi associated with a bark beetle outbreak and an understudied tree species.
summary: This paper documents fungal communities inhabiting galleries created by the eastern larch beetle within larch trees across Minnesota during an outbreak that began in 2001 and has since affected approximately 90% of larch forests in the state. The researchers isolated fungi from 56 beetle-infested trees at seven sites and identified 45 fungal species total, including four previously unknown species within the order Ophiostomatales: Graphilbum insulare, Ophiostoma itasca, Ophiostoma minnesotense, and Ophiostoma pseudoips. They also reclassified Leptographium simplex, a fungus previously recorded from beetle galleries in Vermont. The fungal community was dominated by the phylum Ascomycota (82% of total relative abundance), with Basidiomycota comprising 12%; at the order level, Ophiostomatales dominated at 59% of relative abundance, followed by Hypocreales at 9% and Pleosporales at 8%. A single species, Grosmannia americana, accounted for more than half of all fungal isolates recovered.  The outbreak itself—an uncharacteristic and prolonged departure from the beetle's historical role as a minor inhabitant—has revealed a previously undocumented fungal assemblage now integral to the beetle's ecological performance. This paper documents not simply a pathogenic fungal community but a material entanglement between beetle, tree, and fungi that has reorganized under conditions of outbreak, producing both novel species descriptions and novel ecological relationships. The dominance of Grosmannia americana and the concentration of Ophiostomatales species suggest that certain fungi have become structurally central to beetle survival and reproduction during this period of expanded beetle activity—a shift that reveals how the same fungal partners can operate simultaneously as markers of ecological disruption and as essential metabolic agents within a transformed forest assemblage.

title: Bacterial consortia enhance nutrient uptakes and molecular response in tomato seedlings under alkaline soil stress: a comparative study
source: Frontiers in Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: Nutrient deficiencies in alkaline soils (pH 7.9–8.5) frequently limit plant growth due to insufficient nutrient availability and uptake. This study investigated the effects of two bacterial strains, VITK-1 (Pseudomonas sp.) and VITK-3 (Burkholderia sp.), on nutrient absorption, growth, and gene expression in tomato (Solanum lycopersicum) seedlings grown in alkaline soil. Bacterial treatments were applied individually and as a consortium, and their ability to promote plant growth and nutrient solubility was evaluated. In vitro studies demonstrated the strains’ ability to solubilize essential nutrients, generate extracellular enzymes, and exhibit a variety of Plant Growth-Promoting Rhizobacteria (PGPR) characteristics, with strong antagonistic activity against Fusarium oxysporum f.sp. lycopersici and Ralstonia solanacearum (35.7%–76.5%). In vivo investigations revealed notable improvements in germination (73.3%), root and shoot development, and overall seedling vigor when compared to untreated controls. The bacterial consortium significantly improved protein (54.5%) and proline (69.5%) levels, antioxidant activity (50.7%), phenolic (60.9%), and flavonoid content (52.5%), and decrease
summary: Alkaline soils with pH between 7.9 and 8.5 restrict plant growth by locking nutrients into forms plants cannot absorb. This paper tested two bacterial strains—Pseudomonas sp. (VITK-1) and Burkholderia sp. (VITK-3)—applied singly and together to tomato seedlings grown in alkaline soil. Laboratory tests showed both strains could dissolve locked nutrients, produce enzymes that break down organic matter, and suppress two major tomato pathogens (Fusarium oxysporum f.sp. lycopersici and Ralstonia solanacearum) with counteracting activity ranging from 35.7 to 76.5 percent. When grown in alkaline soil, seedlings treated with the bacterial consortium showed germination rates of 73.3 percent and improved root and shoot development compared to untreated controls. The consortium also increased protein levels by 54.5 percent, proline (a stress-protective compound) by 69.5 percent, antioxidant activity by 50.7 percent, phenolic compounds by 60.9 percent, and flavonoid content by 52.5 percent.  Within the material entanglement of alkaline soil, these bacteria operate simultaneously as nutrient mobilizers and disease suppressors—organisms that solve the immediate problem of nutrient scarcity while also controlling pathogens that exploit stressed plants. The consortium's capacity to alter chemical conditions in the root zone and trigger protective compound accumulation in the seedling reveals how microbial activity becomes embedded in the plant's own stress response. What emerges is not simply a more vigorous plant, but a plant-bacterial assemblage whose productivity depends on sustained microbial presence—a dependency that reflects broader patterns of agricultural intensification where soil degradation (alkalinity, pathogen pressure) is managed through the controlled introduction of living organisms rather than remedied at its source.

title: The Aspergillus nidulans transcription factor SclB governs the transition from vegetative to asexual development
source: American Society for Microbiology: mBio: Table of Contents
excerpt: ABSTRACT Asexual reproduction in filamentous fungi is a common, efficient, and fast differentiation process for producing large numbers of asexual spores (conidia), which can be distributed through the air to colonize new environments. The whole process is tightly controlled by specific regulatory proteins. Among the major regulators is the zinc cluster protein of SclB (sclerotia-like B), known to influence various aspects of asexual growth and secondary metabolism in Aspergillus nidulans as well as other filamentous fungi. Two different growth conditions of A. nidulans were compared to obtain a mechanistic overview of the role of SclB, mainly during the transition of the fungus from vegetative to asexual growth. Chromatin immunoprecipitation (IP) was coupled with next-generation sequencing (ChIP-seq) and combined with transcriptomic analyses (RNA-seq). SclB coordinates this developmental shift mainly by controlling the expression of genes encoding a few, however, prominent regulators of conidiation. These include the transcription factors BrlA, VelB, and SclB and the pheromone oxygenase PpoC. Association of SclB to promoter regions requires the newly identified putative SclB response element with a nine-base-pair DNA motif. Scl2 is the corresponding protein in the fungal plant pathogen Verticillium dahliae and partially complements the A. nidulans Δ sclB asexual deficiency. This supports a partial functional complementation of this regulator among different fungal species. In summary, SclB coordinates the transition from vegetative growth to asexual reproduction in A. nidulans through in vivo transcriptional control over genes coding for established players of conidia formation. IMPORTANCE Fungi constantly adapt to environmental changes in their various habitats. Asexual spore formation allows for quickly leaving an unfriendly habitat through dispersal into the air. The asexual developmental program of fungi enables the production of a large number of spores in a s
summary: This paper documents how the regulatory protein SclB orchestrates the developmental shift in Aspergillus nidulans from vegetative growth to asexual spore formation. Using chromatin immunoprecipitation coupled with next-generation sequencing and gene-expression analysis analysis, the researchers identified that SclB controls this transition by regulating the expression of key developmental genes, including transcription factors BrlA and VelB, another copy of SclB itself, and the pheromone oxygenase PpoC. The protein binds to promoter regions through a newly identified nine-base-pair DNA motif. A related protein called Scl2 in the plant pathogen Verticillium dahliae partially restores asexual reproduction capacity in Aspergillus nidulans mutants lacking SclB, indicating functional conservation of this regulatory mechanism across different fungal species.  The capacity of SclB to govern rapid asexual spore production—enabling fungi to disperse through air and colonize new environments—reveals the material entanglement between fungal development and habitat instability. What this paper documents as a regulatory mechanism for adaptive reproduction is simultaneously a mechanism for rapid proliferation and dispersal under stress; the same developmental shift that allows escape from unfavourable conditions also enables the establishment of fungal populations in new locations, including those shaped by human activity, labour conditions, and infrastructure. The conservation of this regulatory logic across pathogenic and non-pathogenic species suggests that the capacity to transition quickly into spore-producing mode is a distributed fungal strategy for navigating turbulent ecological and economic landscapes.

title: Enhanced banana productivity, soil microbial community structure, and Fusarium wilt resistance by ultra-wide-narrow row planting pattern
source: Frontiers in Plant Science | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: The sustainability of banana production is severely constrained by labor intensiveness, Fusarium wilt epidemics, and mechanization incompatibility under conventional planting systems. A three-year (2019-2021) investigation systematically evaluated three distinct planting configurations for simultaneously addressing these challenges while maintaining consistent plant density at 2220 plants ha-¹. The three planting configurations included T3M (3.0 m/1.5 m wide-narrow row, 2.0 m spacing), T5M (5.0 m/1.5 m ultra-wide-narrow row, 1.4 m spacing), T6M (5.0 m/1.5 m ultra-wide-narrow row, 1.2 m spacing). T5M demonstrated superior performance across agronomic and biochemical parameters. T5M outperformed T6M by 23.27-40.12% greater productivity with 8.13% higher fruit number per bunch, while maintaining T3M’s commercial harvest stability and fruit quality, stable and higher commercial harvest rates. T5M enhanced fruit quality over T6M in 2021, increasing total soluble solids by 15.81%, ascorbic acid by 18.35%, and sugar-acid ratio by 16.93%. T5M prioritized reproductive growth, increasing aboveground biomass 12.99% and fruit dry matter 18.73% over T3M in 2021.Compared to T6M, T5M improved nut
summary: This paper reports findings from a three-year field trial (2019-2021) comparing three planting configurations for banana cultivation, all maintained at 2220 plants per hectare. The configurations tested were T3M (3.0 m by 1.5 m wide-narrow rows with 2.0 m spacing), T5M (5.0 m by 1.5 m ultra-wide-narrow rows with 1.4 m spacing), and T6M (5.0 m by 1.5 m ultra-wide-narrow rows with 1.2 m spacing). The T5M configuration showed the strongest overall performance, producing 23.27-40.12% greater yields than T6M while achieving 8.13% more fruit per bunch. In 2021, T5M also exceeded T3M in fruit quality metrics, with total soluble solids 15.81% higher, ascorbic acid 18.35% higher, and sugar-acid ratio 16.93% higher than T6M. The T5M pattern increased aboveground biomass by 12.99% and fruit dry matter by 18.73% compared to T3M, while maintaining the commercial harvest stability of the conventional T3M system.  The paper frames these agronomic gains as solutions to labor intensiveness and mechanization constraints, yet the findings reveal a more complex material entanglement. The ultra-wide-narrow row spacing that enables machinery access and reduces labor simultaneously restructures the soil microbial assemblage—the study's title signals altered soil community composition, though this paper does not elaborate on which microbial shifts correlate with improved Fusarium wilt resistance or whether such resistance reflects genuine pathogen suppression or merely altered plant-fungal encounter rates under the new spatial configuration. The planting pattern thus emerges not as a neutral technical fix but as a reorganization of the soil ecosystem that redistributes both productive capacity and disease vulnerability across the banana-fungus-soil system.

title: A minimal cross-kingdom SynCom promotes plant growth and suppresses wheat crown rot via coordinated rhizosphere microbiome remodeling
source: Frontiers in Plant Science | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: IntroductionWheat crown rot (WCR) caused by Fusarium pseudograminearum threatens wheat productivity, and sustainable control strategies are urgently needed.MethodsWe constructed a minimal cross-kingdom synthetic community (SynCom) consisting of Trichoderma harzianum T19 and Bacillus rugosus PM16, and evaluated its effects on wheat growth and WCR suppression. Rhizosphere microbiome assembly (full-length 16S rRNA/ITS sequencing) and metabolomic shifts were assessed to elucidate mechanisms.ResultsThe SynCom significantly suppressed WCR and promoted wheat growth under pathogen pressure, improving biomass, chlorophyll content, and yield-related traits. SynCom inoculation remodeled the rhizosphere microbiome by enriching beneficial taxa (e.g., Mortierella) and reducing pathogen-associated fungi, and it enhanced rhizosphere enzyme activities and nutrient availability. Metabolomics revealed accumulation of growth-promoting and defense-related metabolites, supporting coordinated microbiome–metabolome regulation.DiscussionA minimal cross-kingdom SynCom can establish a disease-suppressive and growth-promoting rhizosphere through coordinated restructuring of microbial communities and metabolit
summary: This paper documents the construction and testing of a minimal synthetic community—two microorganisms introduced together into wheat soil—designed to control a fungal disease and enhance plant performance. The synthetic community consisted of Trichoderma harzianum T19, a fungus, and Bacillus rugosus PM16, a bacterium. When applied to wheat plants infected with Fusarium pseudograminearum (the fungus causing wheat crown rot), the two-organism community suppressed disease symptoms and increased plant biomass, chlorophyll content, and yield-related traits. The researchers used full-length genetic sequencing of soil microbes to track how the synthetic community altered the existing microbial populations in the root zone—the soil zone around plant roots—observing enrichment of beneficial fungi like Mortierella and reduction of disease-associated fungi. Chemical analysis of root-zone soil revealed accumulation of metabolites linked to plant growth and disease defense, suggesting that the two introduced organisms triggered coordinated shifts in both microbial composition and chemical conditions.  The findings reveal a material entanglement characteristic of contemporary agriculture: the same fungal genus (Trichoderma) that can be weaponized as a biocontrol agent depends on co-introduction with bacterial partners to restructure soil communities in ways that benefit crop productivity. The disease suppression and growth promotion do not emerge from the two organisms alone but from their capacity to reorder the existing microbial landscape—enriching some taxa while excluding others—thereby creating conditions of simultaneous pathogen suppression and nutrient mobilization. This coordinated remodeling exposes how crop health under industrial pressure increasingly relies on deliberate microbial engineering rather than ecological resilience, binding agricultural productivity to the continued management and monitoring of introduced microbial assemblages.

title: Rapid diagnosis of a mixed pulmonary infection with Rhizopus microsporus, Aspergillus fumigatus, Pneumocystis jirovecii, and Cytomegalovirus in a Lymphoma patient using metagenomic next-generation sequencing: A case report
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Diagnostic Microbiology and Infectious Disease
excerpt: Immunocompromised patients are at high risk for life-threatening polymicrobial infections, often challenging to diagnose conventionally. We report a 60-year-old male with relapsed angioimmunoblastic T-cell lymphoma, fever, and pancytopenia post-chemotherapy. Chest CT showed scattered inflammation. Bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (BALF) culture grew only Klebsiella aerogenes, but fluorescent staining revealed aseptate hyphae. Metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS) of BALF identified concurrent infections with Rhizopus microsporus, Aspergillus fumigatus, Pneumocystis jirovecii, cytomegalovirus, and SARS-CoV-2 within 48 hours. Targeted therapy with isavuconazole, sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim, and ganciclovir was promptly initiated. Despite therapy, the patient deteriorated due to profound immunodeficiency and was discharged palliatively. This case highlights mNGS as a rapid diagnostic tool for mixed infections, though clinical correlation remains essential.
summary: Conventional culture and staining methods failed to detect the full scope of infection in this immune-weakened patient. Bronchoalveolar lavage fluid cultured only Klebsiella aerogenes, yet fluorescent staining hinted at fungal material through the presence of aseptate hyphae. Metagenomic next-generation sequencing completed within 48 hours identified four concurrent pathogens absent from culture: Rhizopus microsporus, Aspergillus fumigatus, Pneumocystis jirovecii, and cytomegalovirus, alongside incidental detection of SARS-CoV-2. The patient, a 60-year-old male with relapsed angioimmunoblastic T-cell lymphoma experiencing fever and pancytopenia following chemotherapy, received targeted antifungal and antiviral therapy with isavuconazole, sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim, and ganciclovir, yet clinical deterioration continued due to severe immunosuppression.  The polymicrobial assemblage documented here—where moulds capable of rapid tissue invasion coexist with viruses in the damaged lungs of a chemotherapy-treated patient—reflects the material entanglement of therapeutic intervention and pathogenic opportunity. The same chemotherapy regimen that targets malignant cells creates the immunological void in which multiple fungal and viral species establish simultaneous residence; the diagnostic technology that finally renders these organisms visible arrives too late to alter the trajectory already set by prior treatment decisions and the patient's exhausted immune system.

title: Cyanidin-3-O-glucoside alleviates aflatoxin B1-induced splenic immunotoxicity via gut microbiota remodeling
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Environmental Pollution
excerpt: While the hepatotoxicity of aflatoxin B<sub>1</sub> (AFB<sub>1</sub>) is well characterized, its immunotoxicity remains overlooked. This study investigates whether cyanidin-3-O-glucoside (C3G), a bioactive polyphenolic flavonoid, can alleviate AFB<sub>1</sub>-induced immunotoxicity. Our results demonstrated that C3G significantly ameliorated AFB<sub>1</sub>-induced splenic injury, which was associated with the suppression of the NLRP3/caspase-1/GSDMD pyroptosis pathway and reduced expression of IL-1β and IL-18. Furthermore, C3G modulated the gut microbiota by enriching specific beneficial bacteria (e.g., Alistipes and Candidatus Saccharimonas) and reversed AFB<sub>1</sub>-induced metabolic disorders. Transplantation of fecal microbiota from C3G-pretreated donor mice reproduced the protective effect of C3G in mice exposed to AFB<sub>1</sub>, whereas sterile fecal filtrate transplantation only offered partial relief, indicating that the core mechanism depends on viable microbiota. In summary, C3G alleviates AFB<sub>1</sub>-induced splenic injury by restructuring the dysbiotic gut microbiota into a more enriched community. This remodeling restores metabolic homeostasis and inhibits NLRP3-mediated pyroptosis via the gut-spleen axis. Our findings provide the first demonstration that C3G alleviates AFB<sub>1</sub>-induced splenic immunotoxicity by remodeling the gut microbiota via the gut-spleen axis, establishing a novel microbiota-dependent strategy mediated by natural polyphenols.
summary: Aflatoxin B1, a mycotoxin produced by moulds, damages the spleen through a mechanism involving programmed inflammatory cell death (pyroptosis) triggered by the NOD-like receptor protein 3 inflammasome complex. This paper demonstrates that cyanidin-3-O-glucoside, a plant-derived polyphenol, protects against this splenic injury by blocking the NOD-like receptor protein 3/caspase-1/GSDMD pyroptosis pathway and reducing production of inflammatory signalling molecules (interleukin-1-beta and interleukin-18). The protective effect correlates with restructuring of the gut microbial community, specifically enrichment of bacterial taxa including Alistipes and Candidatus Saccharimonas, alongside reversal of metabolic disruption caused by aflatoxin B1 exposure. Transplantation of intact microbial communities from treated mice reproduced protection, whereas sterilized microbial filtrates provided only partial relief, establishing that viable bacteria—not their metabolic byproducts alone—drive the protective mechanism.  The findings reveal a material entanglement between a fungal toxin, a plant compound, and microbial communities that operates across anatomical distance through the gut-spleen axis. Cyanidin-3-O-glucoside does not neutralize aflatoxin B1 directly but instead restructures the microbial ecology that mediates immune tolerance, transforming a dysbiotic state into one capable of metabolic homeostasis. This points to how exposure to mycotoxins—distributed unevenly across food systems shaped by agricultural practices and storage conditions—creates differential vulnerabilities that can be addressed not through toxin elimination alone but through microbial remodelling, a strategy that depends on access to specific plant-derived compounds and the capacity of individual microbiota to respond.

title: Controls on Development and Classification of Gypsum Mold-Vuggy Reservoirs: A Case Study from the Majiagou Formation, Jingbian Gas Field
source: acs: ACS Omega: Table of Contents
excerpt: This study focuses on the gypsum mold pore-type reservoir of the Ma541 sublayer in the Majiagou Formation, northern Jingbian Gas Field. Based on core experiments and comprehensive geological analysis, it systematically reveals the reservoir development mechanism synergistically controlled by sedimentary microfacies and multistage diagenesis, and conducts reservoir classification and evaluation. The research findings show that the fine-to-medium crystalline dolomite containing anhydrite nodules/columnar crystals developed in the gypsum-bearing dolomite flat microfacies of the restricted platform facies constitutes the main body of the reservoir. During the sedimentary period, the intercrystalline pores of dolomite developed to form a basic reservoir-permeability network; during the epigenetic stage, meteoric water selectively dissolved anhydrite nodules, and the water absorption and expansion during nodule dissolution were accompanied by a large number of fracture seams, forming a superimposed reservoir-permeability network of ″intercrystalline (dissolved) pores, gypsum mold pores, and fracture seams″; alternating hydrothermal dissolution–precipitation during the burial period complicated the early reservoir-permeability network; the temporal and spatial matching between Indosinian tectonic fractures and the Yanshanian peak gas generation period formed vertical migration channels. This paper proposes the ″sedimentary basement construction-epigenetic dissolution and expansion-effective fracture improvement″ three-element reservoir-controlling mechanism, which systematically couples the key roles of the three stages (sedimentary basement, epigenetic dissolution, and tectonic fractures) and combines quantitative physical properties and pore structure parameters, breaking through the limitations of previous single-factor genesis analysis and qualitative classification evaluation for such reservoirs. A reservoir classification and evaluation standard is established based
summary: This paper examines gypsum mold-vuggy reservoirs in the Ma541 sublayer of the Majiagou Formation in the Jingbian Gas Field, northern China. The study identifies fine-to-medium crystalline dolomite containing anhydrite nodules as the primary reservoir rock, developed within restricted platform facies. The research documents a three-stage pore network development: initial intercrystalline porosity formed during sedimentation; secondary pore creation during meteoric water exposure, when anhydrite nodules dissolved and the water absorption during dissolution caused fracturing; and further modification during burial through alternating dissolution and precipitation cycles driven by hydrothermal fluids. Vertical gas migration pathways were established where Indosinian tectonic fractures aligned temporally with peak gas generation during the Yanshanian period. The paper proposes a three-element mechanism linking sedimentary foundation, epigenetic dissolution and expansion, and tectonic fracture enhancement, moving beyond single-factor explanations to quantify how pore structure parameters control reservoir quality.  Within the material assemblages of hydrocarbon extraction, this study documents how mineral dissolution—the removal of anhydrite—paradoxically creates productive capacity through fracturing and pore enlargement. The gypsum mold pores and fracture networks that emerge are simultaneously products of chemical weathering and engineered resources for gas accumulation and recovery. The temporal alignment between tectonic stress and organic maturation reveals how extractive infrastructure depends on geological coincidences shaped by deep time; the reservoir's productivity is not inherent to the rock but emerges from the specific coupling of multiple destructive and constructive processes operating across different timescales and depths.

title: Genetic Dereplication of Multiple Penicillium expansum Biosynthetic Gene Clusters Reveals Cryptic Penta-Cyclopeptide Production
source: acs: Journal of Natural Products: Table of Contents
excerpt: Genetic dereplication by targeted deletion of major secondary metabolite biosynthetic pathways is an effective strategy to uncover novel compounds and cryptic biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs). In this study, we engineered a "flatline" strain of Penicillium expansum by knocking out key biosynthetic genes responsible for producing citrinin, patulin, roquefortine C, andrastin A, and communesins. Comparative metabolomic profiles between the flatline and wild-type strains revealed a marked increase in the production of five-residue cyclopeptides featuring the nonproteinogenic amino acid pipecolic acid in the flatline strain when cultivated on a rice medium. Further analysis led to the identification of known cyclopeptide MBJ-0110 and its new analogues assigned as 3-hydroxy-MBJ-0110 (1) and Val-MBJ-0110 (2). Their associated BGC was predicted using antiSMASH, whereby deletion of the nonribosomal peptide synthetase (NRPS) mbjA abolished the production of these cyclopeptides, confirming the requirement of mbjA as the core biosynthetic gene. A phylogenetic search showed a high level of conservation of MbjA across 19 species, predominately in the taxon Eurotiomycetes. By linking biosynthetic genes to these rare cyclopeptides, our study provides support for the genetic dereplication strategy as an effective means to uncover cryptic fungal secondary metabolism.
summary: Genetic dereplication—systematically silencing major metabolic pathways to reveal hidden chemical production—enabled researchers to engineer a "flatline" strain of *Penicillium expansum* with deletions of five key biosynthetic genes responsible for citrinin, patulin, roquefortine C, andrastin A, and communesins. When grown on rice medium, this engineered strain produced dramatically elevated levels of five-residue cyclopeptides containing the unusual amino acid pipecolic acid, including the known compound MBJ-0110 and two previously undescribed variants designated 3-hydroxy-MBJ-0110 and Val-MBJ-0110. Genetic confirmation showed that deletion of the nonribosomal peptide synthetase gene *mbjA* eliminated cyclopeptide production entirely, establishing this gene as essential for the pathway. evolutionary-relationship analysis revealed that the MbjA protein is highly conserved across 19 fungal species, predominantly within the Eurotiomycetes group.  The duality embedded in this finding reflects how *Penicillium expansum*—a pathogenic mould responsible for postharvest fruit decay and mycotoxin contamination—simultaneously harbours latent biosynthetic capacity for rare cyclopeptides. By suppressing the dominant toxin-producing pathways, the researchers exposed a secondary metabolic potential that remained obscured under standard laboratory conditions. This reveals how the same organism operates across multiple registers: as an agricultural threat defined by its visible toxins, and as a repository of chemical diversity that becomes accessible only through deliberate genetic intervention. The rice cultivation growth medium and the engineered genetic background together constitute a specific material assemblage within which novel fungal chemistry emerges—a localized site where metabolic potential is reorganized through human intervention.

title: Rhizosphere soil fertility and microbial community characteristics of Arundo donax cv. Lvzhou No.1 in coastal saline-alkali soils
source: Frontiers in Plant Science | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: Saline-alkali soils are widespread in ecologically fragile regions and are characterized by high salinity and alkalinity, leading to soil degradation and reduced productivity. To evaluate the potential of Arundo donax cv. Lvzhou No.1 for improving coastal saline-alkali soils, this study was conducted on Pingtan Island, Fujian Province, China. Three treatments were established: a blank control (CK), rhizosphere soils from one-year cultivation (R1), and five-year cultivation (R5). Soil physicochemical properties and microbial community structure were assessed using soil chemical analyses and high-throughput sequencing. Cultivation of A. donax cv. Lvzhou No.1 alleviated saline-alkali stress by reducing soil pH and salinity, with stronger effects under long-term cultivation. Soil fertility increased markedly, with organic matter (OM) and total nitrogen (TN) rising by 91.00% and 70.00%, respectively. Microbial diversity also increased, with fungal communities dominated by Ascomycota and bacterial communities by Proteobacteria. Functional predictions showed higher abundances of saprophytic genera (Acremonium, Fusarium) and enhanced bacterial metabolic pathways, including fatty acid synth
summary: Cultivation of giant reed (Arundo donax cv. Lvzhou No.1) on Pingtan Island reduced soil pH and salt concentration in coastal saline-alkali soils, with five years of growth producing stronger effects than one year. Soil organic matter increased by 91 percent and total nitrogen by 70 percent compared to uncultivated control plots. Microbial diversity expanded across both fungal and bacterial communities, with fungi dominated by Ascomycota and bacteria by Proteobacteria; functional analysis identified increased abundances of saprophytic fungi including Acremonium and Fusarium, alongside enhanced bacterial metabolic pathways for fatty acid synthesis.  Within degraded coastal soils marked by high salinity and alkalinity, this plant-microbial assemblage performs chemical remediation while simultaneously generating conditions for fungal proliferation. The saprophytic fungi now abundant in the root zone — organisms typically associated with organic decomposition and material breakdown — become agents of soil restoration, their metabolic activity tied directly to the accumulation of organic matter and the reclamation of otherwise marginal land. The same microbial shift that signals ecological recovery thus also marks a transformation in what materials and organisms now inhabit and profit from these formerly unproductive spaces.

title: Severe pneumonia caused by Chlamydia psittaci and Aspergillus terreus: a case report
source: Frontiers in Medicine | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: Chlamydia psittaci is a known cause of severe pneumonia and may be associated with invasive fungal co-infections; however, its complication by Aspergillus terreus is exceptionally rare. We report a case of a previously healthy 58-year-old woman with poultry exposure who presented with fever and dyspnea that rapidly progressed to respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation. After initial empirical antibiotics failed, targeted next-generation sequencing (tNGS) of bronchoalveolar lavage fluid concurrently identified Chlamydia psittaci and Aspergillus terreus, guiding successful targeted therapy with doxycycline and voriconazole, which led to full recovery. This case highlights the utility of tNGS in enabling rapid, simultaneous pathogen identification and guiding targeted therapy in severe pneumonia.
summary: A previously healthy 58-year-old woman with exposure to poultry presented with fever and difficulty breathing that rapidly deteriorated to respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation. Initial broad-spectrum antibiotics proved ineffective. Targeted next-generation sequencing of fluid collected from her lower airways identified two pathogens simultaneously: Chlamydia psittaci and Aspergillus terreus. Treatment with doxycycline and voriconazole, selected on the basis of these findings, resulted in full recovery. This paper documents an exceptionally rare co-infection and demonstrates the diagnostic utility of molecular sequencing in identifying multiple pathogens in severe lung infection.  The concurrent presence of a bacterial pathogen transmitted through poultry and a fungal pathogen in a single patient reveals how occupational or domestic exposure to animal reservoirs can create conditions for polymicrobial infection in the lungs. The rarity of this particular combination—and the initial failure of empirical treatment—suggests that standard clinical protocols do not account for such entanglements, leaving patients vulnerable until molecular methods expose the full microbial assemblage at work. The recovery achieved through targeted therapy underscores how diagnostic capacity itself becomes a material intervention, transforming invisible co-infections into treatable conditions and reshaping the relationship between exposure, disease development, and survival.

title: Meyerozyma caribbica NJC41 degrades aflatoxin B1 into the non-toxic aflatoxin P1 and inhibits Aspergillus flavus proliferation in kiwifruit
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Research International
excerpt: Publication date: 1 May 2026Source: Food Research International, Volume 231, Part 1Author(s): Xin-Chi Shi, Jin-Yan Ma, Fan Liu, Yan-Ping Ji, Ying Cao, Lei Chen, Yong-Hui Jiang, Su-Yan Wang, Su-Qin Zhu, Xin Chen, Xu-Chu Duan, Jie Gong, Dong Liu, Pedro Laborda
