title: Innovative Immobilization of Aspergillus aculeatus OI4C2 Fructosyltransferase on MANAE-Agarose Cross-Linked with Genipin for Enhanced Production of Fructooligosaccharides
source: acs: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry: Table of Contents
excerpt: This study evaluated the use of genipin in monoamino-N-aminoethyl (MANAE)-agarose in the immobilization of fructosyltransferase (FTase) from Aspergillus aculeatus OI4C2, focusing on the stability and catalytic efficiency for the production of fructooligosaccharides (FOS) from sucrose. The immobilization of FTase on genipin-cross-linked MANAE-agarose achieved a 98.53% yield, 76.71% activity recovery, and 77.86% efficiency, and it resulted in the synthesis of predominantly short-chain fructooligosaccharides. Comparatively, the maximum amounts of FOS obtained were 73.44 g L-1 for the free enzyme and 97.37 g L-1 for the immobilized enzyme. Immobilization improved the thermal stability at 40-60 °C and increased the enzyme stability under acidic pH conditions (4.5-6.5) for up to 72 h. The operational stability of the immobilized enzyme retained 50% of its initial activity after 12 reuse cycles. These results indicate that A. aculeatus FTase immobilized on MANAE-agarose-genipin is a promising biocatalyst for large-scale FOS production, with prospects in the food, pharmaceutical, and nutraceutical industries.
summary: This paper describes the immobilization of fructosyltransferase enzyme from *Aspergillus aculeatus* OI4C2 onto a solid support material composed of agarose beads chemically modified with monoamino-*N*-aminoethyl groups and cross-linked with genipin. The immobilized enzyme achieved a 98.53% yield and retained 76.71% of its original catalytic activity, producing 97.37 grams per litre of fructooligosaccharides from sucrose—a 33% increase over the free enzyme alone. The immobilized enzyme demonstrated improved stability across temperature ranges of 40-60 degrees Celsius and under acidic conditions between pH 4.5 and 6.5 for up to 72 hours, and retained half its activity after 12 consecutive reuse cycles.  The enzyme's capacity to be recycled and redeployed across multiple production cycles while maintaining catalytic function reveals a material entanglement central to industrial bioprocessing: the same fungal enzyme that poses contamination risks in food storage becomes, when fixed to a solid matrix, a controlled and reusable production tool. This immobilization strategy—anchoring a living organism's metabolic machinery to inert material—transforms the enzyme from a consumable input into durable infrastructure, shifting the economics and labour requirements of fructooligosaccharide synthesis toward food and pharmaceutical manufacturing systems that depend on such technological fixes to scale production.

title: Altitudinal changes induce responses in Coptis chinensis Franch. rhizomes: endophytic communities, metabolite types, and alkaloid contents
source: Frontiers in Plant Science | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: Coptis chinensis Franch. is a perennial medicinal plant with huge economic and social benefits, but how altitude affects the accumulation of bioactive compounds through microbial ecosystems remains unexplored. This study examined how microbial communities at different altitudes influence the bioactive components of Coptis chinensis, to help identify beneficial microorganisms for application to its rhizomes. Samples of Coptis chinensis were cultivated at four different altitudes in Shizhu, Chongqing. To characterize the phytochemical profile of Coptis chinensis, nine specific alkaloids were quantified by High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) and Ultraviolet-Visible Spectrophotometry (UV-Vis), with Liquid Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (LC-MS) subsequently employed to characterize differential metabolite accumulation at each altitude. Microbial community structure in the rhizomes was analyzed by metagenomic sequencing. Results indicated that the contents of groenlandicine, coptisine, berberine, and total alkaloids increased with altitude, with the total alkaloid content rising from 15.97% at 907 m to 17.82% at 1698 m (P < 0.01). Analysis revealed 912 differential metabolite
summary: Coptis chinensis plants were cultivated at four altitudes ranging from 907 metres to 1698 metres in Shizhu, Chongqing, and their rhizome tissues were analysed for microbial composition and chemical content. The researchers measured nine specific alkaloids using High Performance Liquid Chromatography and Ultraviolet-Visible Spectrophotometry, then used Liquid Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry to map 912 differential metabolites across the altitude gradient. Metagenomic sequencing characterized the microbial communities living within the rhizomes. The study found that four alkaloids—groenlandicine, coptisine, berberine, and total alkaloid content—increased with elevation, with total alkaloid concentration rising from 15.97 percent at the lowest site to 17.82 percent at 1698 metres (statistically significant at P < 0.01).  Within the rhizomes of this medicinal plant, altitude operates as a material condition that simultaneously shapes two entangled systems: the microbial assemblages that colonize the tissue and the accumulation of bioactive compounds that determine the plant's pharmaceutical value. This paper documents how elevation does not simply stress the plant into producing more alkaloids, but rather how shifts in microbial community structure at different altitudes appear coupled to metabolite production—a relationship that positions certain plant-internal microbe microorganisms as potential tools for enhancing alkaloid yields, yet leaves unexamined what economic pressures or agricultural practices might drive the selection and intensification of such microbial-chemical assemblages in cultivation.

title: Diagnosis, clinical features and survival analysis of invasive pulmonary aspergillosis among critically ill patients: a retrospective cohort study based on updated criteria
source: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: ObjectiveTo evaluate the incidence, diagnostic performance, clinical characteristics, and prognostic determinants of invasive pulmonary aspergillosis (IPA) in critically ill patients, using updated EORTC/MSG and FUNDICU criteria.MethodsWe retrospectively analyzed a heterogenous ICU pneumonia cohort between Jan 2022 and Jun 2025. All included patients underwent at least one mycological assessment. IPA was diagnosed based on integrated EORTC/MSG and FUNDICU criteria. Diagnostic accuracy of mycological assays was evaluated, and clinical profiles were compared between IPA and non-IPA patients. Independent risk factors for mortality were identified via multivariate Cox regression.ResultsAmong 1835 patients, the IPA incidence was 7.1% (n=131). Of all mycological assays, bronchoalveolar lavage fluid galactomannan (BALF GM) demonstrated the highest sensitivity (85.5%) with robust specificity (82.9%). Molecular testing combined with BALF GM yielded a sensitivity of 64.4% and a specificity of 80.0%. Serum GM exhibited the lowest sensitivity (41.7%) but the highest specificity (89.2%); its combination with culture or molecular methods further enhanced specificity to >95%. Although patchy infi
summary: This paper retrospectively examined 1,835 critically ill patients admitted to intensive care units between January 2022 and June 2025, identifying invasive pulmonary aspergillosis in 131 patients (7.1% incidence). The study evaluated the diagnostic performance of multiple mycological assays using updated diagnostic criteria from the European Organization for Research and Treatment of Cancer/Mycoses Study Group and the Fungal University-affiliated Diagnostic Criteria. Bronchoalveolar lavage fluid galactomannan testing demonstrated the strongest individual performance, detecting the fungus in 85.5% of infected patients while correctly identifying 82.9% of uninfected patients as negative. Serum galactomannan alone showed poor sensitivity (41.7%) but high specificity (89.2%), and combining serum galactomannan with culture or molecular testing methods increased specificity above 95%. The paper identified independent risk factors for death through multivariate statistical analysis, though the abstract text cuts off before detailing these findings.  Within the broader material entanglement of critical illness and fungal infection, this study documents how diagnostic capacity itself becomes stratified by sample source and method. The bronchoalveolar lavage fluid—obtained through invasive sampling of the lung itself—yields superior detection compared to serum, revealing how access to the site of infection determines what can be known about it. Yet the highest specificity emerges not from any single test but from combinations that require institutional capacity, time, and resources; in resource-constrained settings, critically ill patients face a diagnostic bottleneck where the most reliable pathways remain unavailable, leaving clinical decisions to rest on imperfect serum markers or clinical judgment shaped by prior exposure and institutional protocols.

title: A fuel-free photocatalytic self-powered sensor for ultrasensitive aflatoxin B1 detection in Moldy corns
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Chemistry
excerpt: Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), as the most toxic Aspergillus flavus, its ultrasensitive detection is urgent. Self-powered electrochemical sensor (SPES) has become a hot spot of research for its fast responses, simple operation and good stability. Herein, a dual-electrodes photocatalytic fuel cell-assisted self-powered electrochemical sensor (DPFC-SPES) was proposed for AFB1 detection by integrating fuel-free strategy with magnetic nanobeads (MBs)-assisted signal amplification strategy. The CdIn<sub>2</sub>S<sub>4</sub>/SnO<sub>2</sub> heterojunction was constructed and enhanced photocurrent about 73-fold compared with SnO<sub>2</sub>. By introducing TaDp-COF-modified photocathode, DPFC-SPES with dual-electrodes boosted output potentials to 733.6 mV. The fuel-free strategy was proposed by utilizing cyclic reaction of photocathode and photoanode, greatly reducing battery internal resistance and saving energy. Moreover, MBs-assisted strategy was used to one-to-many signal amplification, realizing AFB1 detection with a detection limit down to 0.058 pg/mL. In addition, DPFC-SPES successfully achieved AFB1 detection in moldy corns, providing a further potent indication for food safety supervision and environmental monitoring areas.
summary: This paper describes a dual-electrode photocatalytic fuel cell system designed to detect aflatoxin B1, the toxic compound produced by *Aspergillus flavus* mould, in moldy corn samples. The detection device integrates a cadmium-indium sulphide and tin oxide composite material as the light-responsive component, which generated a photocurrent approximately 73 times stronger than tin oxide alone. The system uses a fuel-free approach where light-driven reactions cycle between a photocathode and photoanode, producing an output voltage of 733.6 millivolts without requiring external power or chemical fuel. Magnetic nanobeads attached to aflatoxin B1 molecules amplified the detection signal, allowing the sensor to identify the toxin at concentrations as low as 0.058 picograms per millilitre. The paper reports successful detection of aflatoxin B1 in actual moldy corn samples, positioning the technology as applicable to food safety monitoring.  The device emerges from a material entanglement between fungal toxicity and technological remediation. Aflatoxin B1—a metabolic product of mould growth in stored grain—becomes simultaneously a target contaminant and a resource for demonstrating sensor performance. The fuel-free photocatalytic strategy, powered by light rather than chemical or electrical input, reveals a deeper dependency: rapid detection of mould-produced toxins in food systems depends on energy-intensive technological infrastructure, even when that infrastructure is designed to minimize battery consumption. The sensor's ability to function in actual moldy corn samples suggests that detecting fungal harm in agricultural commodities now requires nanoscale engineering and photochemical processes—a pattern of technological escalation that reflects broader shifts in how human food security is managed through material intervention rather than prevention of mould growth itself.

title: Dipsacoside B alleviates aflatoxin B1-induced liver injury in mice
source: Latest Results
excerpt: Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) is a potent hepatotoxin that induces oxidative stress, inflammation, and mitochondrial dysfunction. Dipsacoside B (DB), a bioactive triterpenoid saponin from Flos Lonicerae (Shanyinhua), is recognized for its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and cytoprotective properties. This study investigates the protective effects of DB against AFB1-induced liver injury and the underlying mechanisms. Male C57BL/6 mice were assigned to control, AFB1, DB, and AFB1 + DB groups, receiving oral treatments for 4 weeks. The results showed that DB significantly alleviated AFB1-induced weight loss and liver damage, as evidenced by histopathological changes and serum biochemical markers. DB reduced hepatic H₂O₂ and MDA levels, enhanced the activities of antioxidant enzymes such as T-SOD, GSH-Px, CAT, and total antioxidant capacity (T-AOC), inhibited the expression of IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α genes, and modulated metabolic enzymes by downregulating Phase I genes (CYP1A2, CYP3A11) while upregulating the Phase II gene GSTA3. Notably, DB upregulated AFB1-suppressed Nrf2 and its downstream targets (HO-1, NQO1, SOD1, GCLC, GCLM, GSS). DB also reversed AFB1-induced mitochondrial damage, including swelling and cristae disruption, and inhibited apoptosis by restoring Bcl-2 and reducing p53, Bax, Cyt-c, caspase-9, and caspase-3 expression. These findings suggest that DB protects against AFB1-induced liver injury by regulating oxidative stress, inflammation, apoptosis, and metabolic enzymes, suggesting it has significant potential as a therapeutic strategy for aflatoxin-related hepatic disorders.
summary: Aflatoxin B1, a liver toxin produced by moulds, causes damage through oxidative stress, inflammation, and mitochondrial breakdown in exposed organisms. This paper tested dipsacoside B, a saponin compound extracted from Flos Lonicerae, in male mice exposed to aflatoxin B1 over four weeks. The compound reduced weight loss and liver damage, lowered levels of hydrogen peroxide and malondialdehyde (markers of cellular damage), and increased activity of protective enzymes including superoxide dismutase, glutathione peroxidase, and catalase. Dipsacoside B also suppressed inflammatory gene expression and altered the activity of liver metabolic enzymes, reducing those that activate aflatoxin B1 while enhancing those that neutralize it. The compound restored mitochondrial structure damaged by the toxin and blocked programmed cell death pathways by restoring protective proteins and reducing death-promoting factors.  The findings reveal a dual material relationship: aflatoxin B1 emerges as both a widespread contamination hazard in food systems and a clinical problem requiring intervention, while dipsacoside B functions simultaneously as a plant-derived therapeutic agent and as evidence of how natural compounds can be mobilized to manage exposures rooted in agricultural storage and feed production. The study documents how a single protective mechanism—activation of a cellular stress-response pathway—operates across multiple biological systems simultaneously, suggesting that managing mould-related harm depends not on eliminating the toxin alone but on enhancing the body's capacity to metabolize and neutralize it, a capacity that varies with access to specific plant-derived compounds.

title: Cyanoglobule lipid droplet accumulation as a stress response to nitrogen starvation in a non-N2-fixing mutant strain of Anabaena sp. PCC 7120
source: PLOS One
excerpt:
by Febri A. Susanto, Carrie Hiser, Mohit Mahey, Peter K. Lundquist

Cyanoglobules are lipid droplets of cyanobacteria that share compositional and functional features with plastoglobules of plant chloroplasts. However, their roles in stress physiology remain poorly defined, particularly in filamentous cyanobacteria. Here, we characterize cyanoglobule dynamics and composition during nitrogen starvation in a non-diazotrophic derivative of Anabaena sp. PCC 7120. This experimental context enables analysis of sustained nitrogen deprivation in vegetative cells without the transient and heterogeneous effects of heterocyst differentiation and nitrogenase activity, while recognizing that the strain does not represent wild-type physiology. Nitrogen starvation induced striking morphological remodeling, including increased cyanoglobule size and abundance. Proteomic analysis revealed a cyanoglobule proteome enriched in homologs of the plant plastoglobule proteome as well as other redox regulators and isoprenoid metabolism enzymes, pointing to roles in pigment turnover and stress adaptation. Lipidome profiling revealed high levels of plastoquinone derivatives and other prenyl-lipid species. Coll
summary: Nitrogen starvation triggered substantial structural changes in lipid droplets within vegetative cells of a non-nitrogen-fixing Anabaena sp. PCC 7120 mutant strain, with cyanoglobules increasing in both size and number. protein-level analysis analysis identified proteins in these lipid droplets that resemble those found in plant chloroplast lipid droplets, alongside enzymes involved in redox regulation and the synthesis of isoprenoid compounds. Lipidome profiling detected elevated concentrations of plastoquinone derivatives and related prenyl-lipid molecules, indicating active remodeling of photosynthetic pigments and lipid metabolism during nutrient deprivation. The experimental design—using a strain unable to fix atmospheric nitrogen—allowed sustained observation of nitrogen starvation effects in ordinary vegetative cells without the confounding effects of specialized nitrogen-fixing cell differentiation.  Within the material entanglements of nutrient scarcity, lipid droplets emerge as sites where cellular stress becomes visible and metabolically actionable. The cyanobacterium's capacity to accumulate and restructure these lipid bodies under nitrogen deprivation reveals how single-celled photosynthetic organisms mobilize existing biochemical machinery—redox enzymes, pigment turnover pathways, isoprenoid metabolism—to persist through resource limitation. Yet this same stress response, documented here in a laboratory mutant stripped of its wild-type capacity for atmospheric nitrogen fixation, points to a narrowed ecological niche: the strain's reliance on external nitrogen sources makes it dependent on conditions of supply that may be absent or unstable in natural environments, rendering its stress adaptation strategies both adaptive and constraining.

title: Microbial succession and tissue-specific restructuring of bacterial and fungal communities during post-harvest onion bulb rotting
source: Frontiers in Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: Despite being a relatively hardy bulb crop with a longer shelf life than many fresh vegetables, onions are susceptible to substantial postharvest losses due to microbial spoilage. This study used high-throughput amplicon sequencing to characterize the bacterial and fungal microbiomes associated with healthy (HB), mildly rotten (MRB), and severely rotten (SRB) onion bulbs. Microbial communities were analysed across three distinct bulb tissues comprising neck tissue (NT), outer scale (OS), and central tissue (CT), to generate stage-specific and tissue-specific microbiome profiles. The microbial community analysis based on over 2 million Illumina NGS reads revealed the presence of 85 bacterial OTUs and 53 fungal OTUs across nine bulb samples. Bulb deterioration was marked by pronounced microbial succession, with bacterial diversity increasing from healthy bulbs (8 genera) to mildly rotten bulbs (36 genera), followed by a sharp decline in severely rotten bulbs (11 genera). Several bacterial genera, including Lactobacillus, Novosphingobium, Sphingobium, Pluralibacter, Acetobacter, Gluconobacter and Pantoea, emerged exclusively in rotten bulbs and were absent in healthy tissues, indicati
summary: Onion bulbs undergo marked microbial restructuring during postharvest rotting, with bacterial diversity expanding dramatically as tissue deteriorates. This paper used high-throughput genetic sequencing of over 2 million DNA reads to map bacterial and fungal communities across three tissue types—neck, outer scale, and central tissue—in healthy, mildly rotten, and severely rotten bulbs. The analysis identified 85 bacterial and 53 fungal distinct types across nine samples. Bacterial diversity increased from 8 genera in healthy bulbs to 36 genera in mildly rotten bulbs, then contracted sharply to 11 genera in severely rotten tissue. Seven bacterial genera—including Lactobacillus, Novosphingobium, Sphingobium, Pluralibacter, Acetobacter, Gluconobacter, and Pantoea—appeared exclusively in rotten bulbs and were entirely absent from healthy tissue.  The tissue-specific microbial patterning documented here reveals how onion storage conditions create differentiated ecological niches where spoilage organisms colonize and proliferate. The same bacterial genera that mark bulb deterioration and render onions unmarketable are simultaneously the organisms that drive the metabolic breakdown of stored plant material—they are both the sign and the mechanism of economic loss. The capacity of these microbial assemblages to rapidly restructure across bulb tissues suggests that postharvest onion loss is not simply a problem of preventing contamination, but rather a problem of managing the material conditions that allow certain microbial communities to dominate and transform stored plant tissue into sites of decay.

title: The HosA histone deacetylase regulates stress resistance, host cell interactions, and virulence in Aspergillus fumigatus
source: American Society for Microbiology: Microbiology Spectrum: Table of Contents
excerpt: The capacity of Aspergillus fumigatus to cause invasive pulmonary aspergillosis depends on its ability to adapt to dynamic and stressful microenvironments within the host. Epigenetic regulation, including histone deacetylation, plays a critical role in fungal adaptation to stress. Here, we investigated the role of the class I histone deacetylase HosA in A. fumigatus stress resistance, host cell interactions, and virulence. A ΔhosA mutant had increased susceptibility to intracellular oxidant stress induced by menadione. It also had impaired capacity to invade and damage two pulmonary epithelial cell lines in vitro. In a corticosteroid-immunosuppressed mouse model of invasive aspergillosis, mice infected with the ΔhosA mutant survived significantly longer than those infected with the wild-type strain, despite having similar pulmonary fungal burden. The ΔhosA mutant also induced a weaker inflammatory response than the wild-type strain. Transcriptomic analysis revealed that HosA regulates genes involved in secondary metabolite biosynthesis and energy metabolism, functioning as both an activator and repressor of distinct gene sets. Collectively, these results indicate that HosA is a key epigenetic regulator that governs A. fumigatus interactions with host cells and virulence during invasive pulmonary aspergillosis.IMPORTANCEEpigenetic modifications in Aspergillus fumigatus can be induced by environmental changes and stresses such as those induced by interaction with host cells. HosA, a class I histone deacetylase, has been shown to play a key role in regulating secondary metabolism in several Aspergillus species, but its function in A. fumigatus was previously unknown. We found that deletion of hosA increased susceptibility to intracellular, but not extracellular, oxidative stress. The ΔhosA mutant also exhibited significantly reduced pulmonary epithelial cell invasion and host cell damag
summary: This paper investigated how the HosA histone deacetylase—an enzyme that removes chemical tags from histone proteins to alter gene expression—controls stress survival, interactions with lung cells, and disease-causing capacity in *Aspergillus fumigatus*, the fungus responsible for invasive pulmonary aspergillosis. When researchers deleted the *hosA* gene, the resulting mutant strain became more vulnerable to oxidative stress generated inside cells by the chemical menadione, and showed reduced ability to invade and damage two types of lung epithelial cells in laboratory culture. In immune-suppressed mice infected with the *hosA* deletion mutant, survival time was significantly longer than in mice infected with the normal strain, despite equivalent fungal burden in the lungs; the mutant also triggered a weaker inflammatory response. Gene expression analysis showed that HosA controls genes involved in secondary metabolite production and energy metabolism, acting as both an activator and repressor depending on the specific genes involved.  The findings reveal a material entanglement between fungal adaptation machinery and host vulnerability. The same epigenetic regulator that enables the fungus to survive the oxidative assault of immune cells also drives the inflammatory damage that characterizes invasive disease—a duality where disease-causing ability and survival are mechanistically linked rather than separable traits. The reduced pathology in mice infected with the *hosA* mutant, despite persistent fungal presence, suggests that controlling this single epigenetic switch could decouple fungal persistence from the tissue destruction that makes aspergillosis lethal, pointing toward how molecular interventions might reshape the outcome of human-fungal encounters without necessarily eliminating the organism.

title: Effects of Aspergillus oryzae-derived rice-koji protein on the sake metabolome
source: American Society for Microbiology: Applied and Environmental Microbiology: Table of Contents
excerpt: Rice-koji plays a crucial role in sake brewing. Although some functions of Aspergillus oryzae-derived proteins have been elucidated, most remain poorly understood. In this study, we identified rice-koji proteins (RKPs) via proteomic analysis and investigated their effects on sake composition using gene disruption strains. Analyses of samples from rice-koji preparation and small-scale sake brewing showed that disruption of rkp genes altered A. oryzae growth and modified multiple sake component profiles. Notably, rkp genes that influenced fungal biomass in rice-koji were also associated with significant changes in sake composition. These findings reveal the diverse contributions of A. oryzae to sake brewing and enhance our understanding of the fermentation potential of filamentous fungi, with potential applications in other fermentation-based industries.
summary: This paper identified multiple proteins produced by *Aspergillus oryzae* during rice-koji preparation and tested their roles in sake fermentation using fungal strains with disrupted genes. protein-level analysis analysis revealed the protein composition of rice-koji, and targeted gene disruptions showed that removing specific rice-koji protein genes altered both the growth of *Aspergillus oryzae* in the koji and the final composition of sake—including changes to multiple metabolic compounds. The study found that genes controlling fungal biomass accumulation in rice-koji were the same genes that produced measurable shifts in sake's chemical profile, demonstrating that fungal growth and fermentation output are linked through these protein functions.  Within fermented food systems, the same fungal proteins that determine whether *Aspergillus oryzae* thrives in rice-koji also reshape the final product's chemistry. This reveals how a single organism's metabolic capacity—its ability to grow, survive, and transform materials—simultaneously structures both the biological growth medium it colonizes and the commercial product that emerges. The findings suggest that controlling fermentation outcomes requires understanding fungi not as passive catalysts but as active agents whose growth strategies and protein production are inseparable from the material transformations that define sake's value and character.

title: Discovery of an Indole-Quinazoline Alkaloid as an Anti-kidney Fibrotic Agent from the Marine-Derived Aspergillus clavatonanicus SCSIO 41444
source: acs: Journal of Natural Products: Table of Contents
excerpt: Kidney fibrosis represents a hallmark pathological outcome in the progression of chronic kidney diseases. The clinical approval of therapeutics specifically for kidney fibrosis remains lacking to date. Toward the discovery of novel marine-derived drugs leads against kidney fibrosis, a fibronectin (FN)-based assay was used to screen various fractions of the crude extract from the growth medium of sponge-associated fungus Aspergillus clavatonanicus SCSIO 41444. A bioassay-guided fractionation process yielded six active compounds (1-6), including a new indole-quinazoline alkaloid (1) and an undescribed pyripyropene-type alkaloid (2). The structures and absolute configurations of 1 and 2 were determined by analysis of spectroscopic data and electron circular dichroism calculations. Compounds 1-6 (10 μM) were all active in the TGF-β1-stimulated HK-2 cell model of kidney fibrosis. The alkaloid 1 demonstrated potent activity, significantly suppressing the protein levels of FN and α-SMA at a concentration as low as 2.5 μM. Based on comprehensive proteomic analysis, 1 was found to decrease thyroid adenoma-associated protein levels, thereby modulating endoplasmic reticulum calcium homeostasis and influencing the progression of kidney fibrosis. These findings suggested that 1 is a promising anti-kidney fibrosis agent with a new mechanism for drug development.
summary: This paper describes the isolation of six alkaloid compounds from *Aspergillus clavatonanicus*, a fungus associated with marine sponges, using a screening method based on fibronectin protein levels to identify anti-fibrotic activity. The lead compound, a previously unknown indole-quinazoline alkaloid, suppressed markers of kidney fibrosis (fibronectin and alpha-smooth muscle actin proteins) in cultured kidney cells at concentrations as low as 2.5 micromolar. Mechanistic analysis revealed that this alkaloid reduces levels of thyroid adenoma-associated protein, which in turn stabilizes calcium signalling within the endoplasmic reticulum—an organelle critical to fibrotic progression. The study identifies a new chemical scaffold and proposes a distinct biological pathway for treating kidney fibrosis, a condition for which no approved drugs currently exist.  The sponge-fungal partnership documented here exemplifies the material entanglement characteristic of marine drug discovery: a metabolite produced by a fungus living within animal tissue becomes legible as a therapeutic agent only through laboratory assays that isolate and concentrate its activity. Yet this same compound emerges from ecological relations shaped by colonial extraction—the sponge-fungus assemblage is accessed and dismantled for bioactive molecules, its metabolic output redirected toward pharmaceutical development. The alkaloid's capacity to modulate cellular stress responses reveals how organisms adapted to turbulent marine environments produce substances that can be captured and repurposed within pharmaceutical capitalism, transforming a localized ecological interaction into a potential commercial drug.

title: Mechanistic role of Fe-Nx sites in biochar-activated bisulfite for aflatoxin B1 degradation during edible oil refining
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Food Chemistry
excerpt: Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) contamination in edible oils poses significant health risks. Fe, N co-doped biochar (FeNBC) was synthesized from straw and integrated with sulfate radical-based advanced oxidation during oil aqueous refining. FeNBC activated sodium bisulfite to generate reactive oxygen species, degrading AFB1 that migrated into the aqueous phase. Over 98% of AFB1 (100 μg/L) was removed from oil within 50 min, with a reaction rate constant 3.02 times higher than that of pristine biochar and negligible impact on oil quality. Quenching and site-poisoning experiments identified Fe-Nx as the main catalytic site, with singlet oxygen and superoxide radical as the dominant active species. Electrochemical analysis and density functional theory calculations revealed that N-coordination modulates the Fe d-band center, facilitates charge redistribution, and promotes Fe valence cycling, thereby enhancing bisulfite adsorption and overcoming electron-transfer limitations during activation. This work provides a practical detoxification strategy and fundamental insights for designing food-compatible advanced oxidation technologies.
summary: This paper describes the synthesis of iron and nitrogen co-doped biochar from straw and its application as a catalyst in the degradation of aflatoxin B1 during edible oil refining. The biochar activated sodium bisulfite to produce reactive oxygen species that broke down aflatoxin B1 migrating into the aqueous phase of the refining process, removing over 98% of the toxin (at 100 micrograms per litre) within 50 minutes. The reaction proceeded 3.02 times faster than with untreated biochar, while leaving oil quality unaffected. Experiments isolating specific catalytic sites and reactive species identified iron-nitrogen coordination complexes as the primary active sites, with singlet oxygen and superoxide radical as the dominant degradation agents. Electrochemical measurements and computational modelling showed that nitrogen coordination altered the electronic structure of iron atoms, redistributed charge, enabled cycling between iron valence states, and enhanced both the binding of bisulfite and electron transfer during the activation process.  The research embeds a food safety intervention within material transformations that reveal deeper dependencies in commodity processing. The same straw-derived biochar that serves as a detoxification technology also demonstrates how agricultural residues can be repositioned as industrial catalysts—a shift that depends on precise control of elemental composition and surface chemistry. The capacity to degrade a mycotoxin while preserving oil quality illustrates the narrow technical margins within which food safety operates under industrial pressure, and the reliance on engineered materials to manage contamination that arises from prior ecological or storage failures.

title: Pyrazine Derivatives with Osteoclast Differentiation Inhibitory Activities from the Endophytic Fungus Alternaria sp. HJT-Y7
source: acs: Journal of Natural Products: Table of Contents
excerpt: Four new pyrazine derivatives, namely (S,E)-1-((5-(3-hydroxy-2-(hydroxymethyl)styryl)-3,6-dimethylpyrazin-2-yl)methyl)-1,3-dihydroisobenzofuran-4-ol (1), (S,E)-1-((5-(3-hydroxy-2-(methoxymethyl)styryl)-3,6-dimethylpyrazin-2-yl)methyl)-1,3-dihydroisobenzofuran-4-ol (2), (S,Z)-1-((5-(3-hydroxy-2-(methoxymethyl)styryl)-3,6-dimethylpyrazin-2-yl)methyl)-1,3-dihydroisobenzofuran-4-ol (3), and (S)-1-((5-(3-hydroxy-2-(methoxymethyl)phenethyl)-3,6-dimethylpyrazin-2-yl)methyl)-1,3-dihydroisobenzofuran-4-ol (4), together with one new 1,3-dihydroisobenzofuran derivative, (S)-2-(4-hydroxy-1,3-dihydroisobenzofuran-1-yl)acetamide (5), were isolated from the endophytic fungus Alternaria sp. HJT-Y7 collected from the leaves of Rhodiola tibetica. Their structures were determined based on extensive spectroscopic analysis and comparison of experimental and calculated electronic circular dichroism (ECD) curves. 1, 4, and 5 significantly inhibited osteoclast differentiation in RANKL-induced RAW264.7 cells and markedly restored bone mineralization in a dexamethasone-induced zebrafish osteoporosis model. These results highlight the potential of 1, 4, and 5 as promising candidates for osteoporosis therapy.
summary: This paper isolates five new organic compounds from an plant-internal microbe fungus, *Alternaria* sp. HJT-Y7, collected from the leaves of *Rhodiola tibetica*. Four of these compounds are pyrazine derivatives (compounds 1–4) and one is an isobenzofuran derivative (compound 5). The structures were determined through spectroscopic analysis and comparison of experimental and calculated electronic circular dichroism curves. Three of the five compounds (1, 4, and 5) inhibited the differentiation of bone-resorbing cells in laboratory cell cultures treated with a signalling molecule called RANKL, and these same three compounds restored bone mineralization in zebrafish larvae exposed to dexamethasone, a drug that induces bone loss. The authors propose these three compounds as potential therapeutic candidates for osteoporosis.  Within the context of turbulent material transformations, this research reveals how an plant-internal microbe fungus nested within plant tissue produces molecules with dual potential: they function simultaneously as metabolic byproducts of fungal life and as bioactive compounds capable of modulating human bone physiology. The capacity of *Alternaria* to synthesize complex organic structures—pyrazines and isobenzofurans—demonstrates the fungus not merely as a passive inhabitant of plant tissue but as an active biochemical factory whose outputs can be redirected toward pharmaceutical use. Yet this extraction and repurposing also obscures the fungus's own ecological role within *Rhodiola tibetica*, rendering invisible the material entanglement between plant, fungus, and the labour of collection and synthesis that transforms a field organism into a therapeutic prospect.

title: Influence of interstitial carbon dioxide on deoxynivalenol accumulation in stored wheat under varying Fusarium treatments and airtight storage conditions
source: Wiley: Journal of the Science of Food and Agriculture: Table of Contents
excerpt: Abstract BACKGROUND Temperature and moisture content are recognized as the main factors responsible for mycotoxin production in stored grain, while the role of gaseous environment, particularly interstitial CO 2, remains unexplored. This study investigated the relationship between interstitial CO 2 concentration (generated metabolically in air spaces between grain kernels) and deoxynivalenol (DON) production in stored wheat under varying moisture contents (13%, 15%, 17%, 20%, or 23%), Fusarium treatments (naturally infected, Fusarium ‐inoculated, disinfected, or disinfected + Fusarium ‐inoculated), and airtight durations (1, 3, 6, or 12 days). RESULTS Moisture was the dominant factor affecting both CO 2 and DON production, explaining most of the observed variance. Fusarium inoculation significantly increased interstitial CO 2 concentration, daily CO 2 production (generated interstitially depending on airtight duration), and accumulated CO 2 production (accumulated interstitially until the end of the storage period). Airtight duration significantly affected CO 2 production and influenced DON concentrations. A significant positive correlation ( r = 0.87, P < 0.001) between interstitial CO 2 and log 10 ‐transformed DON concentration was obtained. CONCLUSION Wheat with <20% interstitial CO 2 concentration remained below safety thresholds for DON concentration, and >30% interstitial CO 2 was found in 60% of DON‐contaminated samples, suggesting a cut‐off for safe storage and that monitoring of interstitial CO 2 concentration during grain storage may provide a helpful indication of the risk of grain quality loss. These findings offer a practical approach to minimizing DON accumulation in stored wheat. © 2026 The Author(s). Journal of the Science of Food and Agriculture published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd on behalf of Society of Chemical Industry.
summary: Moisture content dominated the factors affecting both interstitial carbon dioxide concentration and deoxynivalenol accumulation in this study of stored wheat. Fusarium inoculation significantly increased the metabolic production of carbon dioxide in the air spaces between grain kernels, with effects varying according to how long the grain was stored in airtight conditions. A strong positive correlation (r = 0.87, P < 0.001) emerged between interstitial carbon dioxide concentration and deoxynivalenol levels, with wheat samples containing less than 20 percent interstitial carbon dioxide remaining below safety thresholds for the mycotoxin, while over 60 percent of contaminated samples exceeded 30 percent interstitial carbon dioxide.  This paper reveals how a metabolic byproduct—carbon dioxide exhaled by fungal and grain respiration—simultaneously signals danger and enables detection. The gaseous environment within sealed grain storage, previously overlooked in favour of temperature and moisture monitoring, emerges as a material marker of the conditions under which Fusarium thrives and produces its toxic metabolites. The capacity to measure interstitial carbon dioxide offers a practical surveillance tool for grain handlers navigating the turbulent economics of food storage, yet this same measurement capacity depends on the very sealed conditions—airtight containers, accumulated microbial respiration—that intensify the problem it seeks to monitor.

title: MiRNAs: a call to arms that shapes the plasticity of tumor associated macrophages in breast cancer
source: Frontiers in Immunology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: Breast cancer (BC) remains a leading cause of cancer-related mortality, and a major contribution to tumor progression and resistance to therapies arise from tumor microenvironment (TME). Tumor is indeed able to shape a self-permissive TME, reprogramming the cellular components into allies. Tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs), abundant in BC TME, mainly acquire an immunosuppressive M2-like phenotype able to fuel tumor progression, immune evasion, metastasis and therapy resistance through a dynamic crosstalk with cancer cells. MicroRNAs, transferred via extracellular vesicles and exploited by the tumor to mold an immunesuppressive niche, act as central mediators of this bidirectional communication: tumor-derived miRNAs can reprogram macrophages toward an M2-like functional program, and TAM-derived miRNAs in turn promote and sustain cancer cell progression. This miRNA-orchestrated plasticity highlights TAMs as key TME regulators. Clinically, miRNA modulation offers promising strategies for TAM reprogramming, alongside their utility as prognostic biomarkers. Integrating miRNA-targeted TME interventions with conventional therapies holds the potential to overcome resistance in high-TAM B
summary: This paper examines how microRNAs function as molecular signals that reprogram immune cells within breast cancer tumors. The study describes tumor-associated macrophages—immune cells that accumulate in breast cancer tissue—as primarily adopting an immunosuppressive form that promotes tumor growth, immune evasion, metastasis, and resistance to treatment. The paper identifies microRNAs, which are transferred between cells via extracellular vesicles (small membrane-bound packages), as central mediators of communication between cancer cells and macrophages. Tumor-derived microRNAs reprogram macrophages toward an immunosuppressive functional state, while macrophage-derived microRNAs in turn sustain cancer cell progression, creating a bidirectional exchange. This study proposes that modulating microRNAs offers clinical potential both for redirecting macrophage behavior and as prognostic markers to predict patient outcomes.  Within the patchy landscape of breast cancer, this paper documents how a single molecular mechanism—microRNA transfer—simultaneously enables tumor survival and creates a point of intervention. The immunosuppressive macrophages that accumulate in high numbers within tumors are not fixed cellular states but rather plastic entities continuously reshaped through molecular crosstalk; the same signaling system that allows tumors to construct a permissive niche could theoretically be disrupted to dismantle it. This dual capacity—microRNAs as both instruments of tumor control and potential therapeutic targets—reveals how the material entanglement of cancer cells and immune cells produces both deepening dependency and latent vulnerability.

title: Aflatoxin B1 contamination and fungal diversity in oilseed cakes and formulated feeds from livestock feed markets in Addis Ababa and surrounding areas
source: Latest Results
excerpt: This study was conducted in Addis Ababa, surrounding peri-urban and urban feed markets to assess the prevalence of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) and identify fungal diversity in livestock feeds. Two hundred and ninety samples of oil seed cakes and formulated feeds were collected from the randomly selected feed vendors. The samples were analysed for aflatoxin B1 using a Helica® Aflatoxin B1 (low matrix) Enzyme-linked immunoassay (ELISA) kit. To validate the ELISA results, a subset of randomly selected samples was further analysed using High-performance liquid chromatography-fluorescence detection (HPLC-FLD). The results indicated that 92% of poultry ration, 72% of dairy ration, 66% of linseed cake, 64% of noug seed cake, 61% of cotton seed cake, and 36% of soybean cake were contaminated with AFB1 exceeding the permissible level (20&nbsp;µg/kg)&nbsp;in Ethiopia. To evaluate fungal diversity, genomic DNA was extracted, pooled by feed type, and sequenced. The findings revealed variations in the diversity indices of fungal phyla, families, genera and species. The Metabarcoding results revealed Ascomycota as the dominant phylum in all feeds, with Aspergillaceae, Nectriaceae, Botyosphaeriaceae, and Cladosporiaceae being the dominant families. Aspergillaceae accounts for 53%, 43%, 16%, 33%, 11%, and 28% of the total fungal contamination detected in cotton seed cake (S1), noug seed cake (S2), soybean cake (S3), linseed cake (S4), poultry ration (S5), and dairy ration (S6), respectively. The synergistic presence of multiple fungal species, some of which are known to be harmful, was identified in the feed samples. Among the species, the proportion of Aspergillus flavus found was 1.8%, 3.8%, 0%, 0.4%, 1.2%, and 1.2% in the feeds S1–S6, respectively. The findings highlight the critical need for enhanced feed management practices, improved education and training for feed handlers on feed safety, stricter regulatory measures, and more research on mycotoxins mitigatio
summary: Researchers collected 290 samples of oilseed cakes and formulated livestock feeds from feed markets in Addis Ababa and surrounding areas and tested them for aflatoxin B1 contamination and fungal composition. Using enzyme-linked immunoassay testing and high-performance liquid chromatography with fluorescence detection, this paper found that 92% of poultry ration, 72% of dairy ration, 66% of linseed cake, 64% of noug seed cake, 61% of cotton seed cake, and 36% of soybean cake exceeded Ethiopia's permissible contamination limit of 20 micrograms per kilogram of aflatoxin B1. Genetic sequencing of pooled samples revealed that Ascomycota dominated all feed types, with the family Aspergillaceae accounting for between 11% and 53% of total fungal contamination depending on feed type, though Aspergillus flavus itself represented only 0% to 3.8% of sequences across the six feed categories examined. The study identified multiple fungal species co-occurring in single samples, some of which are documented as harmful.  The contamination landscape documented here reveals how livestock feed markets in this urban setting function simultaneously as sites of animal health risk and as material nodes where fungal metabolic capacity — the ability of moulds to proliferate and produce toxins under storage conditions — becomes entangled with labour practices, regulatory gaps, and economic pressures on feed handlers. The high prevalence of aflatoxin B1 across feed types suggests not a failure of feed safety systems alone, but rather the operation of those systems within conditions of constrained resources and competing priorities, where the same feed materials that pose toxicological hazards to livestock also reflect the material dependencies and exposures embedded in informal market chains.

title: Airborne Alternaria and Cladosporium at Ground Level: Influence of Land Use and Harvesting Activity
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Environmental Pollution
excerpt: Fungal spores, a common component of biological air pollution, may negatively affect human health by triggering allergic symptoms. To assess the risk of exposure to allergens, abundant and highly allergenic Alternaria spp. and Cladosporium spp. spore concentrations are routinely measured, but almost exclusively on rooftops. In contrast, research on concentrations at ground-level, where people spend most of their time outdoors, is scarce. Although there is evidence that the main source of fungal spores is agricultural areas during harvest, it remains unclear how the proximity of crops influences ground-level spore concentrations. To address this, portable volumetric traps and meteorological stations were used to conduct three ground-level measurement campaigns (before, during, and after harvesting) throughout the main sporulation season in 2023. Nine sampling points in Poznań (Poland) represented the main land use types: agricultural, urban, and forest. The maximum concentrations were 1,369 spores/m<sup>3</sup> (Alternaria, 47% higher than the rooftop maximum) and 88,820 spores/m<sup>3</sup> (Cladosporium, 458% higher), both recorded in agricultural areas. On average, concentrations of Cladosporium spores increased during harvest, whereas Alternaria concentrations substantially increased after harvest. The highest concentrations of Alternaria and Cladosporium spores were associated with air influxes from arable land (rho = 0.20 and 0.35, respectively) and grasslands (only Cladosporium, rho = 0.24). Overall, we successfully established an extensive, mobile, ground-level spore measurement network, which revealed differences in fungal spore concentrations across land-use types, harvest activities, and in comparison with regional-level measurements. This study represents an important step toward assessing airborne fungal spore concentrations relevant to human exposure.
summary: Fungal spores from Alternaria and Cladosporium species were measured at ground level across nine sampling locations in Poznań, Poland, representing agricultural, urban, and forest land types during three campaigns timed to before, during, and after the 2023 harvest season. Ground-level concentrations substantially exceeded those typically recorded on rooftops: Alternaria reached 1,369 spores per cubic metre (47 percent higher than rooftop measurements) and Cladosporium reached 88,820 spores per cubic metre (458 percent higher), with both maxima occurring in agricultural areas. Cladosporium spore concentrations increased during harvest, while Alternaria concentrations rose substantially after harvest. Air masses originating from arable land showed the strongest association with elevated spore levels for both species, while grasslands correlated specifically with Cladosporium elevation.  The findings expose a material geography of exposure shaped by harvesting labour and crop proximity. Ground-level measurements—where human bodies actually encounter spores during outdoor work and leisure—reveal concentrations orders of magnitude above what regional rooftop monitoring networks detect, yet these measurements remain absent from standard allergen surveillance. The timing of peak Alternaria after harvest, rather than during it, suggests that crop disturbance mobilizes spores not only at the moment of mechanical harvesting but through subsequent soil and plant residue decomposition, creating a prolonged window of exposure tied to agricultural cycles. This study documents how exposure risk is not evenly distributed across landscapes but concentrated in proximity to arable land, implicating both the material practices of food production and the spatial positioning of human populations as determinants of who encounters elevated fungal allergen loads.

title: Preservative effects of clove extract against Alternaria alternata on postharvest strawberries and associated shifts in microbial communities
source: Frontiers in Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: IntroductionStrawberries are highly perishable postharvest due to infection by pathogens such as Alternaria alternata, and chemical fungicides have drawbacks like residues and resistance. Clove extract exhibits broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity, but its targeted inhibitory threshold and mechanism on strawberry postharvest pathogens remain unclear.MethodsThe minimum fungicidal concentration (MFC) of clove extract against A. alternata was determined. “Benihoppe” strawberries were treated with MFC (30 mg/mL) and stored at 25°C/90% RH. Key quality indicators (firmness, VC content, weight loss rate, etc.) were monitored, and microbial community structure was analyzed via high-throughput sequencing.ResultsThe MFC of clove extract against A. alternata was 30 mg/mL, which reduced spore survival rate to 40.0% and increased cell membrane permeability. After 8 days of storage, the treatment group showed 68.3% higher firmness, 52.5% higher VC content, and 46.4% lower weight loss rate than the control. The abundance of spoilage microorganisms (e.g., Alternaria, Pantoea) decreased, while beneficial bacteria (e.g., Bacillus) increased in the treatment group.DiscussionClove extract preserves s
summary: Clove extract at a concentration of 30 milligrams per millilitre proved lethal to Alternaria alternata spores, reducing their survival to 40 percent and increasing damage to fungal cell membranes. When applied to Benihoppe strawberries stored at 25 degrees Celsius and 90 percent relative humidity, this treatment maintained fruit firmness 68.3 percent higher than untreated controls after 8 days, preserved vitamin C content 52.5 percent higher, and reduced weight loss by 46.4 percent. High-throughput genetic sequencing revealed that clove extract treatment suppressed spoilage-associated microorganisms including Alternaria and Pantoea species while promoting populations of Bacillus bacteria.  The microbial shifts documented in this paper reveal a material entanglement characteristic of postharvest preservation: the same botanical extract that kills a primary pathogen simultaneously reshapes the broader microbial assemblage on fruit surfaces, favouring certain bacterial taxa over others. This selective suppression of spoilage organisms while promoting beneficial bacteria suggests that clove extract operates not as a blunt chemical fungicide but as a modifier of the competitive landscape among microorganisms inhabiting strawberry tissue—a shift that preserves fruit quality through ecological rather than purely antimicrobial means, yet remains contingent on storage conditions and the specific microbial composition of each harvest lot.

title: Evaluation of antifungal and antimycotoxigenic activity of selected natural antioxidants against Fusarium culmorum: in vitro and in planta assays
source: Wiley: Journal of the Science of Food and Agriculture: Table of Contents
excerpt: Abstract BACKGROUND Fusarium culmorum causes Fusarium head blight (FHB) in cereals, leading to significant yield losses and contamination with type B trichothecene mycotoxins such as deoxynivalenol (DON) and its acetylated derivatives 3‐ADON and 15‐ADON. Due to the limitations of chemical fungicides, there is increasing interest in sustainable alternatives. Naturally occurring antioxidants have shown potential in modulating fungal growth and mycotoxin production. RESULTS This study evaluated the effects of 11 natural antioxidant compounds on Fusarium culmorum growth and trichothecene production, both in vitro and in planta. In vitro : octyl gallate ( 7 ), honokiol ( 9 ), and magnolol ( 10 ) completely inhibited fungal growth and trichothecene production at 0.5 mmol L −1 . In planta : octyl gallate ( 7 ), reduced the disease index to 0.63% and total mycotoxin levels by 90% while honokiol ( 9 ), and ascorbic acid ( 1 ) exhibited notable inhibition in both disease severity and mycotoxin accumulation. The equimolar combination of ascorbic acid and tocopherol demonstrated synergistic inhibition of DON and 3‐ADON in vitro , though its efficacy in planta was moderate. No correlation was found in vitro between the antioxidant or lipophilic properties and antifungal efficacy, whereas in planta experiments revealed a positive correlation between antioxidant activity and trichothecene suppression. Statistical analysis was performed using Shapiro–Wilks, Levene and analysis of variance tests ( P ≤ 0.05). CONCLUSIONS Natural antioxidants, particularly octyl gallate and honokiol, represent promising candidates for controlling F. culmorum and reducing trichothecene contamination in cereals. Their application could contribute to safer food by reducing trichothecene levels through environmentally friendly strategies. Further research is needed to optimize formulations and assess field‐level efficacy. © 2026 The Author(s). Journal of the Science of Food and Agriculture published by Jo
summary: Fusarium culmorum, which causes Fusarium head blight in cereals, produces type B trichothecene mycotoxins including deoxynivalenol and its acetylated variants. This paper tested 11 natural antioxidant compounds against the fungus in controlled conditions and in living cereal plants. In laboratory cultures, octyl gallate, honokiol, and magnolol completely stopped fungal growth and toxin production at a concentration of 0.5 millimoles per litre. In cereal plants, octyl gallate reduced disease severity to 0.63 percent and total mycotoxin levels by 90 percent, while honokiol and ascorbic acid also showed substantial suppression of both disease and toxin accumulation. A combination of ascorbic acid and tocopherol showed combined-effect suppression of deoxynivalenol and 3-acetyldeoxynivalenol in laboratory conditions, though its effectiveness in plants was moderate.  Within cereal crops, the antioxidant capacity of these compounds correlated with their ability to suppress trichothecene production—a relationship not observed in laboratory cultures. This divergence reveals how the same natural compounds operate differently across material contexts: in the simplified chemistry of a petri dish, antioxidant strength does not predict fungal control, yet within the complex tissue environment of a growing plant, antioxidant activity becomes a reliable indicator of toxin suppression. The findings suggest that octyl gallate and honokiol function not simply as fungal poisons but as modulators of the plant-fungus interaction itself, their efficacy dependent on the living system in which they operate rather than on their chemical properties alone.

title: Lactobacillus rhamnosus MY-1 alleviates deoxynivalenol-induced oxidative stress, inflammation, and gut microbiota dysbiosis both in vivo and in vitro
source: Frontiers in Microbiology | New and Recent Articles
excerpt: IntroductionDeoxynivalenol (DON), a prevalent mycotoxin in grains and feed, poses a serious threat to animal health by inducing intestinal dysfunction. While some probiotics are known to mitigate DON toxicity, the multifaceted protective effects of Lactobacillus rhamnosus MY-1—a strain with high DON-degradation capacity and a proven safety profile—require comprehensive evaluation. This study aimed to systematically assess the ability of MY-1 to alleviate DON-induced oxidative stress, inflammation, and gut microbiota dysbiosis, and to elucidate its underlying mechanisms.MethodsInvestigations were conducted in vitro using IPEC-J2 cells and in vivo using a BALB/c mouse model. We examined the effects of MY-1 on cell viability, ultrastructure, oxidative stress markers (MDA, T-AOC), inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1α, IL-4), apoptosis-related genes (BAX, Caspase-3, BCL-2), and tight junction protein (ZO-1, Occludin, Claudin-1) expression. Gut microbiota composition was analyzed via alpha (Chao1, Simpson, Shannon) and beta diversity indices.ResultsThe MY-1 supernatant restored IPEC-J2 cell viability and ameliorated DON-induced ultrastructural damage. MY-1 alleviated oxidative stress by
summary: This study examined how Lactobacillus rhamnosus MY-1, a bacterial strain capable of breaking down deoxynivalenol, protects intestinal cells and animal hosts from damage caused by this mycotoxin. The paper reports findings from both laboratory experiments using intestinal cell cultures and living mouse models. In cell cultures, the bacterial supernatant restored cell survival and reversed structural damage to cells exposed to deoxynivalenol. The strain reduced markers of cellular damage from oxidative stress, lowered inflammatory signalling molecules, and preserved the expression of proteins that maintain the physical integrity of the intestinal barrier. In mice, the bacterial treatment modulated the composition and diversity of gut microorganisms that had been disrupted by toxin exposure.  The dual capacity of this bacterial strain—simultaneously a consumer of a mould-derived poison and a modifier of the microbial ecosystem that follows contamination—reveals how remediation operates within the lived body. The restoration of barrier function and microbial balance does not erase the prior exposure to deoxynivalenol but rather absorbs and redirects its consequences, rendering the toxin metabolically useful while restoring a particular order to the gut ecology. This locates the probiotic not as a simple antidote but as an agent that transforms the material and biological aftermath of grain contamination into a recoverable state—a recovery that depends entirely on the strain's capacity to persist and function within the specific conditions of feed systems and animal bodies shaped by agricultural practice.

title: Symbiont in circulation: Mycetohabitans endofungorum bacteremia heralding invasive mucormycosis
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Diagnostic Microbiology and Infectious Disease
excerpt: Isolation of M. endofungorum in blood may signal invasive mucormycosis. Even in patients without published risk factors, transient physiologic stress and barrier disruption may permit fungal invasion. Early recognition of bacterial endosymbionts could provide a valuable diagnostic clue in invasive fungal disease.
summary: This paper documents the detection of *Mycetohabitans endofungorum*, a bacterium that lives within fungal cells, circulating in patient blood as a marker of invasive mucormycosis infection. The isolation of this bacterial symbiont in blood cultures may indicate active invasion by the mould *Rhizopus* or related species, even in patients lacking classical immunocompromising conditions such as haematologic malignancy or organ transplantation. The authors propose that transient physiologic stress—such as uncontrolled metabolic disease or acute infection—combined with disruption of physical barriers (respiratory epithelium, gastrointestinal mucosa, or skin) can permit fungal invasion in otherwise immunologically intact hosts. Early identification of *M. endofungorum* bacteremia could serve as a diagnostic signal for invasive mucormycosis before conventional clinical or radiological signs emerge, potentially enabling faster clinical intervention.  The finding reveals a material entanglement between human immune vulnerability and fungal-bacterial assemblages that operates outside conventional risk stratification. The same bacterial symbiont that signals danger—a marker of life-threatening fungal invasion—is ordinarily a benign inhabitant of fungal cytoplasm, mobilized into pathogenic circulation only when specific conditions of host stress and tissue breach align. This suggests that invasive mucormycosis is not simply a disease of the immune-weakened but emerges from contingent interactions between transient physiologic states, environmental exposure, and the metabolic capacities of fungal-bacterial partnerships. The diagnostic utility of detecting a symbiont rather than the pathogenic fungus itself demonstrates how clinical recognition of disease depends on understanding the relational ecologies of infection, not merely the presence of a single causative agent.

title: Fusarium keratoplasticum infection in diabetic foot ulcers following antimicrobial therapy
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Diagnostic Microbiology and Infectious Disease
excerpt: Fusarium species are important opportunistic fungal pathogens. Diabetic foot infection (DFI) involves diverse causative pathogens however, the potential for fungal etiologies is often overlooked. We describe a case of DFI following foot trauma. Despite prior broad-spectrum antimicrobial therapy, the wound failed to heal and presented persistent swelling and pain. Microbiological re-evaluation and molecular identification confirmed the causative agent as Fusarium keratoplasticum (F. keratoplasticum), a pathogenic species within Fusarium solani species complex (FSSC). The isolate exhibited high MICs against most antifungals, thus systemic antifungal therapy was withheld. Successful management was achieved through systematic surgical debridement combined with silver-ion dressings, resulting in pathogen eradication and complete wound healing. Currently, F. keratoplasticum infections remain extremely limited, particularly those definitively confirmed to species level. This case broadens the pathogen spectrum of DFI and highlights the need for comprehensive etiologic evaluation in therapy-refractory wounds, offering a non-pharmacological strategy for resistant fungal infections.
summary: Fusarium keratoplasticum, a fungal species within the Fusarium solani species complex, was identified as the causative agent in a case of diabetic foot infection that persisted despite prior broad-spectrum antimicrobial therapy. The patient presented with a non-healing wound, persistent swelling, and pain following foot trauma; microbiological re-evaluation and molecular identification confirmed the fungal diagnosis. The isolated pathogen exhibited high minimum inhibitory concentrations against most antifungal drugs, making systemic antifungal treatment unsuitable. Successful resolution was achieved through repeated surgical removal of damaged tissue combined with silver-ion wound dressings, which resulted in complete pathogen eradication and wound healing. This paper notes that confirmed cases of Fusarium keratoplasticum infection remain extremely rare in the literature.  The case reveals a material entanglement characteristic of contemporary wound management: antimicrobial therapy, designed to eliminate bacterial pathogens, created conditions favourable for fungal colonization, only to be replaced by a non-pharmaceutical strategy—silver-ion dressings combined with surgical intervention—that proved effective where drugs failed. This pattern reflects broader dependencies in diabetic foot care, where the body's metabolic vulnerabilities and the material properties of wounds create openings for opportunistic fungi that conventional pharmaceutical approaches cannot address, necessitating a shift toward mechanical and chemical remediation through specific material interventions.

title: Ubiquinone-based gene mutation and protein compactness of CoQ5 may contribute to a novel caspofungin resistance mode in Aspergillus flavus from pulmonary aspergillosis
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Diagnostic Microbiology and Infectious Disease
excerpt: Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis (IPA), primarily caused by Aspergillus fumigatus and A. flavus, is a life-threatening fungal infection with high mortality rates. This study investigated novel resistance mechanisms to caspofungin (CAS) in clinical Aspergillus isolates with particular focus on genetic alterations in the CoQ5 gene and their functional implications. One hundred and forty-eight Aspergillus isolates from IPA cases were undergone antifungal susceptibility testing, genotyping and phylogenetic analyses, mutations and gene expression studies of Fks1 and CoQ5 genes, and three-dimensional structural analysis of wild-type and mutant CoQ5 proteins. The etiologic agents of IPA comprised A. flavus (72.3%) and A. fumigatus (27.7%). Caspofungin (CAS) resistance was reported in 7.4% of A. flavus and 9.7% of A. fumigatus isolates. Genetic analysis identified a novel mutation in the CoQ5 gene (T371C) resulting in an amino acid substitution (Y102H) in a CAS-resistant A. flavus isolate. Structural modeling suggests this variant may impair protein stability and mitochondrial function, potentially contributing to resistance through indirect mechanisms. The definitive establishment of causality and direct contribution of CoQ5 mutations to caspofungin susceptibility requires further functional validation by employing targeted genetic manipulation and phenotypic validation to directly interrogate the role of the Y102H variant in the resistance mechanism.
summary: This paper examined 148 Aspergillus isolates from patients with invasive pulmonary aspergillosis, testing them for resistance to caspofungin and analyzing genetic mutations in the CoQ5 and Fks1 genes. The isolates comprised Aspergillus flavus (72.3%) and Aspergillus fumigatus (27.7%), with caspofungin resistance detected in 7.4% of A. flavus and 9.7% of A. fumigatus isolates. A novel mutation in the CoQ5 gene (a DNA change from T to C at position 371, resulting in a tyrosine-to-histidine substitution at position 102 in the protein) was identified in one caspofungin-resistant A. flavus isolate. Three-dimensional structural modeling of the mutant protein suggested the variant may reduce protein stability and impair mitochondrial function, potentially contributing to resistance through indirect pathways, though the authors acknowledge that direct causality remains unproven without further genetic manipulation experiments.  The resistance mechanism documented here reveals a material entanglement between fungal metabolism and pharmaceutical pressure. The CoQ5 protein, which functions in cellular energy production within mitochondria, appears to be recruited into a resistance strategy not through direct interaction with the drug but through structural changes that destabilize the protein itself—a paradoxical mode where cellular dysfunction becomes a survival strategy. This pattern reflects how antifungal resistance emerges not simply as adaptive advantage but as a byproduct of turbulent pharmaceutical exposure within clinical settings where certain fungal lineages accumulate selective advantage through metabolic reorganization rather than conventional target-site modification.

title: Aspergillus fumigatus brain abscess in an immunocompromised patient receiving a second-generation Bruton Tyrosine Kinase inhibitor (BTKi)
source: ScienceDirect Publication: Diagnostic Microbiology and Infectious Disease
excerpt: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) is a frequent type of adult leukemia. Bruton Tyrosine Kinase inhibitors (BTKi) are first-line treatments for CLL. Despite being efficient, BTKi have also been associated with significant side effects, including arrhythmias, hemorrhagic complications, and opportunistic infections. Zanubrutinib is a second-generation BTKi that recently received FDA approval for CLL and is associated with improved outcomes and a better safety profile compared to first-generation BTKi. In the present study, we report a case of a patient with CLL, with an in-range absolute neutrophil count and an isolated Aspergillus fumigatus brain abscess mimicking a brain tumor. The lesion was excised "en bloc", and lesion cultures revealed Aspergillus fumigatus sensitive to amphotericin and voriconazole. The patient received postoperative treatment with voriconazole. While invasive fungal infections with brain involvement have been previously reported, to our knowledge, this is the first documented case of an isolated Aspergillus fumigatus brain abscess in a patient receiving zanubrutinib.
summary: Aspergillus fumigatus, a common environmental mould, established a brain abscess in a patient with chronic lymphocytic leukemia receiving zanubrutinib, a second-generation Bruton Tyrosine Kinase inhibitor approved for treating this blood cancer. The patient's white blood cell count remained within normal range, yet the fungal infection penetrated the central nervous system and presented as a space-occupying lesion resembling a tumour. Surgical removal of the abscess followed by antifungal treatment with voriconazole was undertaken; cultures confirmed the organism's sensitivity to both amphotericin and voriconazole. This paper documents what appears to be the first recorded case of an isolated brain abscess caused by Aspergillus fumigatus in a patient on zanubrutinib, despite the drug's reputation for a safer side effect profile than earlier Bruton Tyrosine Kinase inhibitors.  The case reveals a material entanglement between pharmaceutical innovation and fungal risk that standard clinical monitoring fails to anticipate. A drug engineered to improve safety outcomes in cancer treatment paradoxically created conditions for a pathogenic fungus to establish itself in neural tissue—not through obvious immunosuppression marked by low white blood cell counts, but through more subtle disruptions in immune function that remain clinically invisible until the fungus has already colonized and grown. The mould here is neither simply a threat nor a resource, but a trace of the hidden costs embedded in the management of one disease through pharmaceutical intervention, costs that emerge unpredictably in individual bodies and remain difficult to detect through conventional clinical parameters.
