The paper notes that genetic detection in blood was most sensitive for Mucorales (81.3 percent of cases with skin involvement), moderately sensitive for Aspergillus (40 percent), and least sensitive for Fusarium (15.4 percent).  Within burn units, molds simultaneously mark clinical deterioration and reveal the material vulnerabilities of severely injured bodies—the compromised skin barrier and suppressed immune response create conditions where multiple mold species can establish simultaneous infections. The escalating mortality tied to cumulative positive diagnostic criteria suggests that the presence of multiple molds is not incidental but constitutive of a particular state of bodily breakdown in which burn injury has created a permissive growth medium for fungal colonization. The diagnostic challenge documented here reflects a deeper entanglement: the same diagnostic tools designed to detect pathogenic molds in other contexts must be recalibrated for burn patients, where the ecology of infection is fundamentally altered by trauma and where clinical outcomes depend not on detecting a single pathogen but on recognizing the assemblage of multiple fungal species that exploit the same damaged tissues.

title: Dietary curcumin alleviates aflatoxin B1‐induced liver injury and improves growth performance in broilers via Nrf2 activation and NLRP3 inflammasome inhibition
source: Wiley: Journal of the Science of Food and Agriculture: Table of Contents
excerpt: Abstract BACKGROUND Aflatoxin B 1 (AFB 1 ), a potent mycotoxin frequently detected in improperly stored poultry feed, induces hepatic oxidative stress, inflammation, and growth retardation in broilers. Curcumin (CUR), a polyphenolic compound derived from Curcuma longa , exhibits strong antioxidant and anti‐inflammatory activities; however, its efficacy and mechanisms in mitigating AFB 1 ‐induced hepatotoxicity in broilers remain unclear. This study aimed to evaluate the protective effects of dietary CUR supplementation on growth performance and liver health of broilers exposed to AFB 1 . RESULTS A total of 720 broilers (eight replicates of 15 birds each per treatment) were fed a basal diet, an AFB₁‐contaminated diet (1 mg kg −1 ), or AFB 1 diets supplemented with CUR (100, 300, or 500 mg kg −1 ) for 42 days. AFB 1 exposure significantly reduced average daily gain and increased feed conversion ratio ( P < 0.05), whereas CUR supplementation – particularly 300–500 mg kg −1 – improved growth performance relative to the AFB 1 group ( P < 0.05). CUR also decreased serum alanine aminotransferase and aspartate aminotransferase activities, alleviated hepatic histopathological damage, enhanced hepatic antioxidant enzyme activities (superoxide dismutase, glutathione peroxidase), and suppressed malondialdehyde accumulation ( P < 0.01). At the molecular level, CUR upregulated Nrf2 and its downstream antioxidant genes while downregulating NOD‐like receptor protein 3 (NLRP3), caspase‐1, and interleukin‐1β, indicating attenuation of inflammasome activation and pyroptosis. CONCLUSION Dietary curcumin effectively mitigated AFB 1 ‐induced hepatotoxicity and performance loss in broilers through activation of the Nrf2 antioxidant pathway and inhibition of the NLRP3 inflammasome. These findings highlight CUR as a promising natural feed additive for protecting poultry against mycotoxin‐induced oxidative and inflammatory damage. © 2025 Society of Chemical Industry.
summary: Aflatoxin B1, a mycotoxin commonly found in poorly stored poultry feed, damages broiler livers by triggering oxidative stress and inflammation, reducing weight gain and worsening feed conversion efficiency. This paper tested whether curcumin, a plant compound from turmeric with known antioxidant properties, could protect broilers from this toxin. Over 42 days, 720 broilers received either standard feed, feed contaminated with aflatoxin B1 at 1 milligram per kilogram, or contaminated feed supplemented with curcumin at 100, 300, or 500 milligrams per kilogram. Aflatoxin B1 alone reduced daily weight gain and increased the amount of feed needed per unit of growth; curcumin at 300–500 milligrams per kilogram reversed these effects. At the tissue level, curcumin lowered liver enzyme markers of damage, reduced scarring and cell death in liver tissue, boosted protective enzymes like superoxide dismutase and glutathione peroxidase, and reduced harmful oxidative byproducts. Molecularly, curcumin activated the nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 antioxidant pathway and suppressed the NOD-like receptor protein 3 inflammasome—a cellular trigger for inflammatory cell death—by reducing caspase-1 and interleukin-1 beta.  Within contaminated feed systems, curcumin emerges as both a remedial intervention and a marker of deeper structural vulnerabilities. The same storage failures that generate aflatoxin B1 as a byproduct of mould growth in feed warehouses create the conditions requiring chemical mitigation; curcumin does not address the material conditions—moisture, temperature, ventilation—that allow toxin-producing moulds to proliferate in the first place. Rather, this paper documents how a natural additive can be layered into industrial poultry production to manage exposure without altering the infrastructural arrangements that make such exposure routine. The efficacy of curcumin thus depends on, and reinforces, a system where mycotoxin contamination is treated as an inevitable cost absorbed through supplementation rather than prevented through transformed storage practices.

title: Antifungal Activity and Resistance Risk of Cyclobutrifluram against Fusarium solani, the Causal Agent of Peanut Root Rot
source: acs: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry: Table of Contents
excerpt: Peanut root rot (PRR), caused by Fusarium solani, is a common and destructive disease. Cyclobutrifluram exhibits antifungal activity against Fusarium spp., yet its bioactivity and resistance risk against F. solani remain unclear. In this study, the EC50 values of cyclobutrifluram against 155 F. solani strains ranged from 0.0279 to 0.1102 μg/mL, with a mean of 0.0513 ± 0.0170 μg/mL. Treatment with cyclobutrifluram significantly inhibited conidial production and germination, reduced succinate dehydrogenase (SDH) activity, increased hyphal branching, and shortened the distance between septa or cell nuclei. Cyclobutrifluram provided excellent control over PRR. Seven laboratory-generated cyclobutrifluram-resistant mutants showed no or slight fitness penalties and exhibited positive cross-resistance only with pydiflumetofen, indicating a moderate-to-high resistance risk. Resistance to cyclobutrifluram in F. solani was conferred by four SDH substitutions (FsSdhBH248Y, FsSdhC1A83V, FsSdhC2A85V, and FsSdhDD184G), which was confirmed by molecular docking and gene replacement. This study provides crucial insights for developing effective control strategies to manage PRR.
summary: This paper documents the antifungal potency of cyclobutrifluram against *Fusarium solani*, the fungus responsible for peanut root rot, across 155 field-collected strains. The drug inhibited spore production and germination, disrupted the fungus's energy metabolism by reducing succinate dehydrogenase activity, and altered hyphal structure by increasing branching and compressing the spacing between cell compartments. Cyclobutrifluram delivered effective disease control in peanut crops. However, the study identified a significant liability: seven laboratory-generated resistant mutants emerged without substantial fitness costs and showed cross-resistance to pydiflumetofen, signalling moderate-to-high risk of resistance development in field populations. Genetic analysis pinpointed four specific mutations in the succinate dehydrogenase enzyme complex that confer resistance, confirmed through molecular modelling and experimental gene replacement.  The tension embedded in this finding reflects a familiar pattern in agricultural chemical control: a potent tool for managing crop disease simultaneously creates the conditions for its own obsolescence. The same metabolic target that makes cyclobutrifluram effective—the succinate dehydrogenase complex—proves vulnerable to rapid genetic adaptation in *Fusarium solani*, with resistant mutants bearing no apparent survival cost. This decoupling of resistance from fitness penalty suggests that once resistance emerges in peanut fields, it will persist and spread unchecked, transforming cyclobutrifluram from a solution into a driver of fungal adaptation. The moderate-to-high resistance risk documented here reflects not a flaw in the molecule but a structural feature of intensive monoculture agriculture: the reliance on single-target fungicides in environments where fungal populations are large, genetically diverse, and under constant chemical pressure.

title: Characteristics of individual cyp51A SNPs and combinations thereof impacting the azole phenotype in TR34-mediated resistance genotypes of Aspergillus fumigatus
source: American Society for Microbiology: Antimicrobial Agents and Chemotherapy: Table of Contents
excerpt: ABSTRACT The World Health Organization has flagged the rise of drug resistance in Aspergillus fumigatus as a critical concern. Elevated mutation rates in this pathogen contribute to the rapid development of resistance, complicating treatment efforts. We conducted a study on the prevalence of azole resistance among clinical A. fumigatus isolates in the Netherlands from 1994 to 2022 and identified 34 cyp51A variants. To investigate the impact of individual single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) and combinations thereof on the azole phenotype in TR 34 -mediated resistance genotypes, we focused on novel, recent mutations and explored the effects of SNPs L98H, T289A, I364V, and G448S and the combination of the mutations TR 34 /L98H, TR 34 /L98H/T289A/G448S, and TR 34 /L98H/T289A/I364V/G448S on azole affinity and susceptibility. We created the three-dimensional protein model of the Cyp51A protein with azoles, and the mutation was introduced to the wild-type cyp51A A. fumigatus strain by the CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing technique. Finally, in vitro susceptibility testing of A. fumigatus strains carrying the mutations was conducted to confirm the azole phenotypes observed in clinical isolates. The MICs of all four azoles against the mutated cyp51A strains, which harbored combination mutations, were higher than those of the wild type, with highly elevated MICs of itraconazole, voriconazole, and isavuconazole. Genotypes TR 34 /L98H/T289A/I364V/G448S mutant showed a consistent phenotype to the clinical strains, which are highly resistant to voriconazole but susceptible to itraconazole. In this study, we show that molecular dynamics simulations of amino acid substitutions in the cyp51A gene correlate to the structure–function relationship of in vitro phenotype.
summary: Aspergillus fumigatus isolates collected in the Netherlands between 1994 and 2022 showed a rising prevalence of azole resistance, with 34 distinct genetic variants identified in the cyp51A gene. This paper examined how individual genetic variations and combinations of variations in this gene affect resistance to four azole antifungal drugs. The researchers used computer modelling to predict how amino acid changes would alter the structure of the Cyp51A protein, then introduced these mutations into laboratory strains using genetic editing technology and measured how resistant the resulting fungi were to azoles. Strains carrying multiple mutations together showed substantially higher resistance levels than unmutated strains, particularly to voriconazole and isavuconazole, though the most heavily mutated strain remained susceptible to itraconazole despite carrying the TR34 resistance marker.  The pattern documented here reveals how a pathogenic fungus generates pharmaceutical resistance not as a binary outcome but as a graduated response shaped by the specific combination of genetic changes present. The same mutations that render this organism clinically intractable in one drug context can leave it vulnerable in another, creating a fragmented landscape of therapeutic options that depends entirely on which particular mutations accumulate in which individual isolate. This conditional resistance—where a single fungal strain's susceptibility profile shifts across the azole drug class—reflects how microbial adaptation to medical intervention produces not uniform danger but distributed and uneven vulnerability, a pattern that will shape clinical decision-making and drug availability differently across different patient populations and treatment contexts.

title: Molecular Dynamics and Quantum Chemical Insights into the Molecular Basis of Differential Toxicity of the Mycotoxin Deoxynivalenol (DON) and Its Microbial Derivatives DOM-1 and 3-epi-DON
source: acs: ACS Omega: Table of Contents
excerpt: Deoxynivalenol (DON) is a trichothecene mycotoxin from Fusarium species that contaminates cereal grains, posing health risks. DON binds to site A of the 60S ribosomal subunit in eukaryotes, triggering a ribotoxic stress response. Despite structural similarity, its metabolites, deepoxy-deoxynivalenol (DOM-1) and 3-epi-deoxynivalenol (3-epi-DON), are nontoxic, but the molecular basis of this difference is unclear. This study employed molecular docking, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, and quantum chemical calculations based on Symmetry-Adapted Perturbation Theory (SAPT) to analyze the interactions of DON and its metabolites with the ribosomal site. Our results reveal that DON adopts a unique binding conformation, enabling strong and stable interactions with an Mg2+ ion and nucleotide U2873, maintaining its position within the ribosomal pocket throughout 500 ns of MD simulations. In contrast, DOM-1 and 3-epi-DON fail to sustain these interactions due to the absence of the epoxide group or altered hydroxyl orientation, leading to their dissociation. Additional MD simulations of the complex with another potent toxic trichothecene, verrucarin A showed stable binding at the same site, emphasizing the importance of these molecular interactions for toxicity. Further, quantum chemical analyses highlighted the energetic contributions of electrostatic and induction forces in stabilizing DON within the binding pocket. These data are in line with experimental studies in HCT116 human colon cancer cells confirming the lack of cytotoxicity of DOM-1 and 3-epi-DON and demonstrating differential ribotoxicity of DON and its metabolites. These results highlight a clear structure-activity relationship, where modifications at key positions markedly affect trichothecene binding and toxicity. Together, these findings advance our understanding of trichothecene toxicity and support the development of detoxification strategies and novel therapeu
summary: Deoxynivalenol, a poisonous compound produced by *Fusarium* fungi that contaminates cereal grains, binds tightly to a specific site on the 60S ribosomal subunit and triggers a cellular stress response in eukaryotes. This paper used computational methods—molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulations lasting 500 nanoseconds, and quantum chemical calculations—to explain why DON causes harm while its chemically similar metabolites, deepoxy-deoxynivalenol and 3-*epi*-deoxynivalenol (3-*epi*-DON), do not. The analysis revealed that DON maintains stable binding to the ribosomal pocket through strong interactions with a magnesium ion and a specific nucleotide, whereas DOM-1 and 3-*epi*-DON cannot sustain these interactions because they lack DON's epoxide group or have altered hydroxyl group positioning, causing them to dissociate from the binding site. The quantum chemical analysis identified electrostatic and induction forces as the primary energetic drivers stabilizing DON at the ribosomal site. Testing in human colon cancer cells confirmed that DOM-1 and 3-*epi*-DON lack toxicity while DON retains its capacity to trigger the ribotoxic stress response, establishing a clear relationship between molecular structure and toxic effect.  The findings document how minor chemical modifications—the presence or absence of a single epoxide group, the spatial orientation of a hydroxyl group—determine whether a trichothecene molecule becomes a cellular poison or an inert metabolite. This structural specificity reveals the material precision required for toxicity, yet simultaneously exposes a vulnerability: the very chemical features that make DON dangerous also define the pathways through which it might be neutralized or transformed. The paper thus maps both the mechanism of harm and, implicitly, the chemical logic by which grain-contaminating fungi and their toxins might be rendered harmless through targeted molecular intervention.

title: Structural Diversification and Biosynthesis of Pesticidal Pyrroline-Derived Alkaloid from Penicillium citrinum SCSIO DF147
source: acs: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry: Table of Contents
excerpt: The pyrroline-derived alkaloid N-(2-methyl-3-oxodec-8-enoyl)-2-pyrroline (MOEP, 1) is a potential pesticide that remains to be structurally diversified for target screening and biosynthesis studies. Bioactivity-guided investigation of the marine-derived fungus Penicillium citrinum SCSIO DF147 yielded five classes of pyrroline-derived alkaloids (1-15), including five new compounds (2-4, 14, and 15). These compounds were first evaluated for chitinase (OfChtII and OfChi-h) inhibition, revealing that both the 3-pyrroline ring and the Δ8',9' double bond are key functional groups. Pesticidal and fungicidal evaluation highlighted compound 1, which outperformed the positive control diflubenzuron against the lepidopteran pest Plutella xylostella, causing 100% larval mortality after 5 days of feeding at 5 mM. The biosynthetic gene cluster (pasA-D) for 1 was identified and characterized. The cytochrome P450 PasB was shown to catalyze the final tailoring step, transforming intermediate 8 into the 3-pyrroline functional group in 1 via dehydroxymethylation and dehydrogenation. These findings provide insights for developing new chitinase-targeting pesticides.
summary: This paper describes the isolation and characterization of pyrroline-derived alkaloids from the marine fungus *Penicillium citrinum* SCSIO DF147. Bioactivity-guided screening yielded fifteen compounds across five structural classes, including five previously unknown compounds. Testing revealed that the 3-pyrroline ring and a specific double bond (Δ8',9') are essential structural features for activity against chitinase enzymes. Compound 1 (N-(2-methyl-3-oxodec-8-enoyl)-2-pyrroline) demonstrated pesticidal potency, killing 100 percent of larvae of the diamondback moth *Plutella xylostella* within five days at 5 millimolar concentration, outperforming the commercial pesticide diflubenzuron. The researchers identified and characterized the biosynthetic gene cluster responsible for producing compound 1, demonstrating that a cytochrome P450 enzyme (PasB) catalyzes the final chemical transformation—converting an intermediate compound into the active 3-pyrroline form through removal of a hydroxymethyl group and addition of a double bond.  Within the material entanglements of marine fungal chemistry and pest control, this paper reveals how a single fungal strain becomes simultaneously a source of bioactive molecules and a site for understanding insecticidal mechanisms. The structural features that make these alkaloids effective against insect chitinase—the very enzyme that allows insects to build their exoskeletons—also mark them as products of fungal metabolic pathways shaped by competition in marine sediments. The optimization of compound 1 for pesticidal use thus depends on understanding not just its target in the insect body, but the fungal enzymatic machinery that evolved to produce it, collapsing the distinction between natural product and synthetic pesticide into a shared biosynthetic logic.

title: Mycotoxin occurrence and risk assessment in plant-based meat, cheese, and fish alternatives based on an adapted UHPLC-MS/MS multi-method
source: Latest Results
excerpt: Plant-based alternatives to animal-derived products have gained significant prominence in recent years, with the market for these products experiencing continuous growth. In response to this trend, we developed an ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography method, coupled with tandem mass spectrometry (UHPLC-MS/MS) for the simultaneous quantification of 15 mycotoxins from Fusarium (deoxynivalenol (DON), DON-3-glucoside (D3G), 3-acetyl-DON, 15-acetyl-DON, HT-2 toxin, T-2 toxin), Alternaria (alternariol, alternariolmonomethylether (AME), tenuazonic acid (TeA)), and Aspergillus species (aflatoxin (AF) B1, B2, G1, G2, sterigmatocystin (STC), ochratoxin A) in plant-based meat, cheese, and fish substitutes. Sample extraction was performed using a QuEChERS (quick, easy, cheap, effective, rugged, and safe) approach, followed by dispersive solid phase extraction (dSPE) for clean-up. Quantification was achieved through stable isotope dilution analysis (SIDA). The method demonstrated limits of detection (LOD) ranging from 0.01 µg/kg (AFB2 and STC) to 14.1 µg/kg (D3G) and limits of quantification (LOQ) between 0.02 µg/kg (STC) and 41.9 µg/kg (D3G). This method was successfully applied to qua
summary: The source abstract describes development of an analytical method to detect 15 different mycotoxins produced by three fungal genera—Fusarium, Alternaria, and Aspergillus—in plant-based meat, cheese, and fish substitutes. This paper adapted ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with tandem mass spectrometry to simultaneously measure these toxins, which include deoxynivalenol and its derivatives, aflatoxins, ochratoxin A, and sterigmatocystin, among others. The method employed a quick, easy, cheap, effective, rugged, and safe extraction approach followed by dispersive solid phase extraction for sample cleanup and stable isotope dilution analysis for quantification. Detection limits ranged from 0.01 micrograms per kilogram for some aflatoxins and sterigmatocystin to 14.1 micrograms per kilogram for deoxynivalenol-3-glucoside, with quantification limits between 0.02 and 41.9 micrograms per kilogram across the 15 toxins measured.  The expansion of plant-based protein markets creates new sites where fungal metabolites accumulate and require monitoring—yet the choice of which toxins to measure, and at what detection thresholds, reflects prior regulatory and commercial decisions rather than comprehensive microbial ecology. This paper's focus on plant-based alternatives reveals a material dependency: as animal agriculture is displaced by crop-based substitutes, the fungal contaminants embedded in grain and legume supply chains become newly visible and newly regulated, shifting but not eliminating exposure pathways shaped by agricultural intensification and global commodity flows.

title: Seasonal cycles select for self-sustained circadian oscillators
source: Current Biology
excerpt: Circadian clocks comprise self-sustained oscillators that anticipate time-of-day changes in the environment. Dani et al. report that seasonal changes in daylength can alone provide a strong selective pressure to evolve this defining property. The salient properties of daily clocks are therefore molded by modulations of both daily and annual/seasonal periodicities.
summary: This paper presents findings on how circadian clocks—biological timekeeping systems that maintain internal rhythms independent of external cues—evolve self-sustaining properties under seasonal pressure. The research, conducted by Dani and colleagues, demonstrates that variations in daylength across seasons create selective pressure sufficient to drive the evolution of self-sustained oscillators, the defining characteristic of circadian systems. The study indicates that circadian clock properties are shaped not only by daily environmental cycles but also by longer seasonal cycles that modulate daylength throughout the year.  The research documents a material entanglement between biological timekeeping and climatic seasonality: organisms do not simply respond to immediate environmental signals but evolve internal mechanisms that anticipate predictable temporal patterns. Under this lens, the circadian clock emerges as both an adaptation to daily rhythms and a technology refined by seasonal constraint—a system whose apparent autonomy from the environment is itself a product of environmental selection. The capacity to maintain self-sustained oscillation becomes legible not as an intrinsic property but as a solution to the specific material problem of navigating landscapes where daylength itself is a scarce and seasonally distributed resource.

title: Thermoplastic Molding of Chitosan to Form Living Plastic Materials
source: Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents
excerpt: Abstract Numerous approaches for the solution‐based fabrication of chitosan‐based materials are reported, but most often result in materials with limitations in terms of stability in aqueous systems and mechanics unless chemical cross‐linking is utilized. In the present study, a thermomechanical compression method is presented for solid‐state processing of chitosan powders into dense bulk plastic‐like materials where the mechanical and physical properties can be tailored. To achieve this outcome, chitosan‐citrate complexes, formed through ionic cross‐linking and amidation, undergo thermal fusion at high temperature and pressure to generate robust materials with retention of the inherent properties of chitosan, including biodegradability and cytocompatibility. The chitosan‐based plastics can be doped with enzymes and antibiotics with retention of bioactivity and are also explored as living materials when microbial cells (e.g., Pseudomonas putida ) are included in the process and subsequently shown to maintain metabolic functions to degrade organic pollutants. This thermoplastic approach for solid‐state processing of chitosan enables the development of a variety of new materials and composites with embedded biomolecules for enhanced functions. This solid‐state fabrication of chitosan bulk materials approach eliminates the need for conventional solution‐based processing, enabling rapid material production via compression molding while reducing costs, minimizing waste, and improving overall manufacturing efficiency.
summary: Chitosan powders can be transformed into dense plastic-like materials through thermomechanical compression—heating and pressing chitosan-citrate complexes at high temperature and pressure to create robust solids that retain the original polymer's biodegradability and capacity to support cell growth. This paper demonstrates that the resulting plastics maintain mechanical and physical properties suitable for practical use without requiring chemical cross-linking agents. The materials can be loaded with enzymes and antibiotics while preserving their biological activity, and when inoculated with bacterial cells such as *Pseudomonas putida*, the embedded microorganisms remain metabolically active and capable of breaking down organic pollutants. The solid-state compression approach reduces manufacturing complexity and waste compared to traditional solution-based methods, enabling faster production at lower cost.  Within the material transformations of the Anthropocene, this work reveals a productive tension: chitosan itself—derived from shellfish waste streams—becomes a plastic substitute that can simultaneously host living degradative capacity. The bacterial cells embedded in the material matrix are neither passive fillers nor contaminants but functional agents whose metabolic labour transforms the composite into a remediation technology. Yet this embedding of microbial work within manufactured solids also represents a new form of biological capture and control, where the capacity of living systems to process pollutants is fixed into industrial form and extracted as a material property rather than emerging from autonomous ecological relations.

title: Prevalence of mycotoxin contamination in different types of tea: a systematic review and meta-analysis
source: Latest Results
excerpt: The consumption of tea as a beverage is considerably increasing worldwide. However, some questions are raised about its safety, particularly regarding the potential contamination by mycotoxins. The objectives of this systematic review and meta-analysis were to assess the overall prevalence of mycotoxin contamination in different types of tea (black, green, dark, white and pu-erh), to compare the contamination rate between two periods (2000–2017 and 2018–2022), and to investigate the impact of country’s income on this contamination. Included studies were selected from Scopus, Science Direct, Web of Science, PubMed and Google Scholar research databases, according to PRISMA guidelines. Statistical analysis was performed using MecdCalc software. Concerning the analytical methods used for mycotoxin detection, 84.6% of included studies used HPLC and or LC–MS/MS. In the 21 included studies, the mycotoxin contamination was predominantly by deoxynivalenol, with 43.8% (95% CI: 8.2%– 83.8%), followed by ochratoxin A (29.1% (95% CI: 11.9%– 50.2%)), enniatin (23.7% (95% CI: 1.7%– 60.1%)), citrinin (23.6% (95% CI: 6.2%– 47.8%)), zearalenone (17.8% (95% CI: 5.1%– 36.0%)), and aflatoxins (12.4% (9
summary: This paper presents a systematic review and meta-analysis of mycotoxin contamination across five types of tea — black, green, dark, white, and pu-erh — drawing on 21 included studies from multiple databases. The analysis found that deoxynivalenol was the most prevalent contaminant at 43.8 percent, followed by ochratoxin A at 29.1 percent, enniatin at 23.7 percent, citrinin at 23.6 percent, zearalenone at 17.8 percent, and aflatoxins at 12.4 percent. The majority of studies (84.6 percent) used high-performance liquid chromatography or liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry for detection. The review compared contamination rates between two periods (2000–2017 and 2018–2022) and examined whether a country's income level influenced contamination prevalence.  Tea consumption is rising globally, yet the material pathway from leaf to cup carries multiple fungal metabolites whose presence reflects uneven exposure to contamination risk. The prevalence of deoxynivalenol and ochratoxin A — compounds produced by field fungi during growth and storage — suggests that tea operates simultaneously as a commodity subject to post-harvest degradation and as a consumed substance whose safety depends on agricultural practices and infrastructure conditions that vary by region and economic capacity. This paper documents not merely a health concern but a material entanglement: the same drying, storage, and transport conditions that preserve tea's market value can also concentrate the metabolic byproducts of moulds, making contamination both a marker of where tea is grown and processed, and a consequence of how it moves through global supply chains.

title: Purpuric targetoid eruption revealing disseminated mucormycosis
source: Wiley: Journal of the European Academy of Dermatology and Venereology: Table of Contents
excerpt: A 76-year-old man with newly diagnosed diffuse large B-cell lymphoma was admitted for chemotherapy with curative intent. During treatment he developed febrile neutropenia and a respiratory infection that rapidly progressed to septic shock. Dermatology was consulted for a 3-day purpuric eruption on the left hemithorax and flank, composed of well-demarcated annular targetoid plaques with central induration and purpuric rims (Figure 1a). In the setting of refractory sepsis despite broad-spectrum antimicrobials, the morphology suggested septic emboli or vasculotropic infection. Punch biopsies from the centre and rim were sent for histopathology, culture and molecular testing and escalation to liposomal amphotericin B was recommended. Histology revealed extensive occlusive angioinvasive fungal thrombi (Figure 1b); PCR identified Mucorales, establishing disseminated mucormycosis, likely from a pulmonary source. This case highlights how recognition of a purpuric targetoid eruption in an immunocompromised, critically ill patient can provide a crucial clue to life-threatening invasive fungal infection. Open access publication funding provided by FCT (b-on). The authors have nothing to report. None declared. Not applicable. The patient in this manuscript has given informed consent to the publication of their clinical images. Data sharing is not applicable to this article as no datasets were generated or analysed during the current study.
summary: A 76-year-old man undergoing chemotherapy for newly diagnosed diffuse large B-cell lymphoma developed febrile neutropenia and respiratory infection that progressed to septic shock. A purpuric eruption appeared on the left side of the chest and flank, consisting of well-defined ring-shaped plaques with central hardening and purple borders. Skin biopsies from the centre and edge of the lesions revealed extensive fungal material blocking blood vessels, with molecular testing identifying Mucorales, confirming disseminated mucormycosis originating from the lungs. The patient's critical condition and failure to respond to broad-spectrum antibiotics prompted escalation to liposomal amphotericin B treatment. This paper demonstrates that recognition of purpuric ring-shaped skin eruptions in immune-weakened, critically ill patients can signal life-threatening invasive fungal infection.  The appearance of Mucorales in skin lesions during chemotherapy-induced immune suppression reveals a material entanglement between cancer treatment protocols and fungal invasion pathways. The same conditions that destroy cancer cells—neutropenia, barrier disruption, systemic inflammation—create ecological openings for opportunistic moulds to establish vascular invasion and disseminate from respiratory sites to distant tissues. The purpuric targetoid pattern itself becomes a readable trace of this entanglement: a visible record of where fungal thrombi have blocked blood flow and triggered tissue death, transforming the skin into a diagnostic surface that exposes the hidden metabolic vulnerabilities produced by contemporary oncology practice.

title: Aspergillus Serology Predicts Relapse in Chronic Pulmonary Aspergillosis: Implications for Personalised Follow‐Up Strategies
source: Wiley: Mycoses: Table of Contents
excerpt: ABSTRACT Introduction Relapses occur in up to 40% of patients after prolonged courses of antifungal treatment for chronic pulmonary aspergillosis (CPA). The factors predisposing to relapse remain poorly defined. Methods We conducted a retrospective study of adults treated for ≥ 6 months with oral azoles for CPA. Patients who completed antifungal therapy and were deemed not to require further treatment were included. Demographic, clinical, radiological and serological data at treatment completion were collected. CPA relapse was defined as symptomatic and radiological deterioration leading to re‐initiation of antifungal therapy. Cox regression and Kaplan–Meier analyses were used to identify predictors of relapse and mortality. Results Among 125 patients (56% male; mean age 61 years), median treatment duration was 36 months. Thirty‐two (26%) developed relapse; relapse rate at 1 year was 16%. Aspergillus sensitisation (specific IgE > 0.35 IU/mL) and elevated Aspergillus ‐specific IgG (> 40 mg/L) at treatment completion were independently associated with relapse ( p < 0.05). No patient with IgG < 40 mg/L relapsed. Underlying lung disease (COPD or prior tuberculosis), extent of radiological involvement, or treatment duration were not significantly associated with relapse or mortality. Conclusions Aspergillus sensitisation and persistently elevated Aspergillus ‐specific IgG at the end of antifungal therapy were independent predictors of CPA relapse. These parameters may reflect ongoing fungal airway burden and can help identify patients requiring extended or closer post‐treatment follow‐up. Underlying comorbidities were not associated with relapse risk.
summary: Aspergillus-specific antibody levels at the end of antifungal treatment predicted relapse in this retrospective study of 125 patients treated for chronic pulmonary aspergillosis. Patients with elevated Aspergillus-specific IgG (above 40 milligrams per litre) at treatment completion had significantly higher relapse rates;  no patient with IgG below this threshold relapsed during follow-up. Aspergillus sensitisation, measured as specific IgE above 0.35 international units per millilitre, was also independently associated with relapse. Among the 125 patients (56 percent male, mean age 61 years) who completed at least six months of oral azole therapy, 32 (26 percent) experienced relapse, with a one-year relapse rate of 16 percent. Median treatment duration was 36 months. Underlying lung disease such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease or prior tuberculosis, the extent of radiological involvement, or the length of antifungal treatment were not significantly associated with relapse risk.  The persistent elevation of Aspergillus-specific antibodies after prolonged antifungal treatment suggests a duality in what these serological markers represent: they simultaneously indicate successful immune recognition of the fungus and ongoing fungal presence in the airway. Rather than signalling cure, these antibodies may reflect a material persistence—fungal material or metabolic byproducts continuing to circulate or colonise lung tissue despite months of drug exposure. This finding reveals how the same immunological response that protects against acute infection can become a marker of incomplete clearance, positioning certain patients in a state of precarious stability where relapse becomes not a random event but a predictable consequence of incomplete fungal elimination.

title: Efficacy of Ipflufenoquin against Strawberry Gray Mold: Insights from AlphaFold- Based Structural Modeling and Genome-Wide Transcriptomic Analysis
source: acs: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry: Table of Contents
excerpt: Gray mold caused by Botrytis cinerea significantly threatens strawberry production. This study evaluated the efficacy of ipflufenoquin, a novel dihydroorotate dehydrogenase (DHODH) inhibitor, against fungal pathogens isolated from Korean strawberry fields in 2023. Ipflufenoquin demonstrated a high in vitro sensitivity to B. cinerea and broad activity against other pathogens. Fruit and greenhouse trials confirmed its robust control of gray mold, including strains resistant to multiple fungicide classes. However, treatment shifted the fungal community, promoting less sensitive genera, such as Cladosporium and Rhizopus. Structural modeling with AlphaFold2 and molecular docking confirmed that ipflufenoquin binds to the quinone binding tunnel of DHODH, correlating binding affinity with susceptibility. Additionally, RNA-seq analysis revealed that ipflufenoquin suppresses primary metabolic pathways while triggering a robust stress response, up-regulating detoxification and efflux transporter genes. This integrated study confirms the efficacy of ipflufenoquin against gray mold and elucidates its molecular impacts, offering essential data for sustainable management strategies.
summary: This paper documents the testing of ipflufenoquin, a dihydroorotate dehydrogenase inhibitor, against gray mold fungus (*Botrytis cinerea*) isolated from Korean strawberry fields in 2023. Laboratory tests showed the compound was highly effective against the target fungus and demonstrated activity against other fungal pathogens. Trials in fruit and greenhouse settings confirmed robust control of gray mold, including strains that had already developed resistance to multiple classes of existing fungicides. The researchers used AlphaFold2 protein modeling and molecular docking to show that ipflufenoquin binds to a specific site on the dihydroorotate dehydrogenase enzyme, with binding strength correlating to fungal susceptibility. Gene expression analysis revealed that the compound suppresses the fungus's basic metabolic processes while triggering stress responses, including increased activity of detoxification and efflux transporter genes that may contribute to resistance mechanisms.  Yet the treatment revealed an ecological cost embedded in the strawberry production system: controlling *Botrytis cinerea* created space for less sensitive fungal genera including *Cladosporium* and *Rhizopus* to proliferate. This pattern—where a targeted fungicide simultaneously eliminates a threat and reshapes the microbial community toward species that escape the same chemical pressure—illustrates how agricultural interventions operate within entangled systems of pathogen, crop, and capital. The compound's efficacy against resistant strains positions it as economically valuable for growers facing fungicide-resistant populations, yet its deployment appears to redistribute rather than resolve the underlying vulnerability of monoculture strawberry production to fungal colonization.

title: Identification and characterization of a novel broad-spectrum antifungal compound targeting the Aspergillus fumigatus cell wall and cell membrane and inducing oxidative stress
source: American Society for Microbiology: Microbiology Spectrum: Table of Contents
excerpt: ABSTRACT The substantial increase in fungal diseases, the limited availability of antifungal drugs, and rising resistance to existing treatments highlight the urgent need for developing novel antifungal drugs. Natural products, known for their diverse bioactivities and unique structures, remain critical sources for antifungal drug discovery. In this study, we aimed to identify novel antifungal compounds from soil microbes and isolated a novel 28-membered polyene macrolide, compound 1 , from Kitasatospora melanogena . This compound demonstrated potent antifungal activity, with MICs of 4–16 μg/mL against Aspergillus fumigatus , Aspergillus flavus , Candida albicans , and Cryptococcus neoformans . Treatment with compound 1 led to significant growth reduction and disruption of the cell wall and membrane in A. fumigatus compared to the wild-type. To understand the underlying mechanism, we performed a transcriptomic analysis of A. fumigatus exposed to compound 1 , revealing a substantial downregulation of genes involved in cell wall and membrane biogenesis, as well as those encoding integral membrane proteins and multidrug transporters. Additionally, compound 1 induced oxidative stress, leading to elevated reactive oxygen species levels. These multifaceted mechanisms highlight the potential of K. melanogena natural products in the development of novel antifungal agents. IMPORTANCE Fungal infections cause substantial morbidity and mortality worldwide, yet current treatments are limited and increasingly undermined by resistance. Natural products remain a proven source of antifungal agents, but few new scaffolds have been introduced in recent decades. We identified a novel polyene macrolide from Kitasatospora melanogena with potent activity against major fungal pathogens. This compound disrupts both the fungal cell wall and membrane while inducing oxidative stress, revealing a multifaceted mechanism of action. Our findings highlight soil microbes as valuable reservoirs for a
summary: This paper describes the isolation and characterization of a novel 28-membered polyene macrolide compound from the soil bacterium Kitasatospora melanogena, which demonstrated potent antifungal activity against multiple fungal pathogens including Aspergillus fumigatus, Aspergillus flavus, Candida albicans, and Cryptococcus neoformans, with minimum inhibitory concentrations ranging from 4 to 16 micrograms per millilitre. gene-expression analysis analysis of treated Aspergillus fumigatus revealed that the compound disrupts fungal cell wall and cell membrane integrity by decreasing the activity of genes involved in cell wall and membrane biogenesis, integral membrane proteins, and multidrug transporters, while simultaneously inducing oxidative stress through elevated reactive oxygen species levels. The compound's multifaceted mechanism of action—targeting structural integrity while triggering cellular damage pathways—positions soil-derived natural products as sources for addressing the growing problem of antifungal resistance.  The identification of this compound from a soil microorganism reflects a deeper material entanglement: the same soil ecosystems that harbour fungal pathogens also contain bacterial producers of antifungal metabolites, yet the extraction and development of such compounds depends on industrial cultivation and chemical isolation processes that abstract these organisms from their ecological context. The compound's capacity to simultaneously damage cell walls, disrupt membrane function, and generate oxidative stress reveals how fungal vulnerability can be engineered into a therapeutic asset, though the paper does not address whether widespread deployment of such a compound might select for resistant strains or alter the microbial assemblages from which it originates.

title: A combined genetic and chemical approach for identifying novel antifungal compounds against Fusarium graminearum
source: American Society for Microbiology: Microbiology Spectrum: Table of Contents
excerpt: ABSTRACT Chemical fungicides act by targeting specific proteins or pathways, known as modes of action (MoAs). In this study, we established a systematic screening platform using Fusarium graminearum gene knockout mutants to evaluate the efficacy of fungicides and identify novel antifungal compounds. Target genes corresponding to major fungicide classes were deleted by homologous recombination, and the resulting mutants exhibited altered sensitivity to the corresponding fungicides compared with the wild-type strain. Deletion of genes encoding known fungicide targets revealed distinct sensitivity profiles, supporting MoA prediction. Using the platform that detects sensitivity differences through OD ₆₀₀ changes, we examined a chemical library of 2,704 small molecules and identified 8 compounds with activity likely involved in the osmotic signal transduction pathway. Several of these compounds exhibited previously unreported antifungal activity, underscoring their potential as novel scaffolds for future development. Collectively, this platform provides a robust, mechanism-informed system for antifungal discovery, with broad applicability to synthetic agents, natural products, and biocontrol metabolites. IMPORTANCE This study provides a perspective on developing strategies to manage fungal pathogens with implications for agriculture and food security. The effectiveness of current fungicides is increasingly limited by resistance, narrow target ranges, and environmental concerns. By combining genetic approaches with chemical screening, this work offers a more systematic way to identify candidate antifungal agents and better understand their possible mechanisms. The framework may also be adapted for use in other areas, including medical and veterinary applications, suggesting potential broader applicability. In addition, the emphasis on exploring new molecular scaffolds supports efforts to develop more sustainable and long-term disease management strategies. Overall, this s
summary: This paper describes a genetic screening platform designed to identify antifungal compounds effective against Fusarium graminearum, a fungal pathogen of grain crops. The researchers deleted genes encoding known fungicide targets in the pathogen using homologous recombination, creating mutant strains with altered sensitivity profiles to existing fungicides. These mutants served as a diagnostic tool: screening a library of 2,704 small molecules against both wild-type and mutant strains revealed compounds that affected fungal growth through specific pathways. The study identified 8 compounds with activity linked to the osmotic signal transduction pathway—the cellular mechanism that allows fungi to sense and respond to water stress—several of which showed antifungal properties not previously documented. The platform detects active compounds by measuring changes in optical density at 600 nanometers, a standard measure of cell density and growth.  The dual logic of this approach—using genetic vulnerability to map chemical action—exposes a material tension in fungicide development. The same genetic deletions that make mutants sensitive to novel compounds also reveal why resistance emerges: fungi can survive by altering or abandoning specific pathways, a capacity that fungicides themselves select for under repeated application. The 8 newly identified compounds targeting osmotic signalling represent not a solution to resistance but a temporary advantage in an ongoing arms race shaped by agricultural intensification and monoculture. What this paper documents is not the discovery of a durable antifungal but rather the systematic production of knowledge about fungal vulnerability—knowledge that, once deployed in fields, will itself accelerate the evolution of tolerance in populations already stressed by the chemical and economic pressures of industrial grain production.

title: Occurrence of mycotoxins in fish feed and its consequences for aquaculture with special regard to the role of insect products
source: Latest Results
excerpt: Infection of plants with molds and inadequate storage conditions of plant materials contribute to contamination of crops like cereals, corn and soybeans with mycotoxins. Plant-derived ingredients serve as major protein sources in aquaculture feeds; however, the increasing use of insects as alternative nutrient sources introduces additional potential pathways for mycotoxin contamination. Strong evidence shows that exposure of fish to mycotoxins can negatively affect their health, leading to reduced growth, impaired immune response and reproductive disorders. However, knowledge of the effects of mycotoxins on farmed insects is still limited. Little is known about the insect metabolism of mycotoxins and their transfer to animal feed, which constitutes a weakness of safety assessments of insects as feed components. This review provides comprehensive information about the consequences of mycotoxin contamination of feed materials and feeds used in aquaculture. To facilitate understanding of the problem and its scale, the mycotoxin levels in plant materials and finished feeds used in aquaculture were compiled and analyzed together with the contamination levels of fish-derived food. Moreover, the available data on the effects of fish exposure to the selected mycotoxins are summarized, highlighting the complexity of the biological activity of these compounds. Finally, recent challenges to the safety of using insects as a protein source in fish nutrition are discussed. The information provided herein is important for assessing the health and economic risks of the growing use of modern feed ingredients in the sustainable aquaculture.
summary: Mold toxins produced during plant infection and poor storage contaminate cereals, corn, and soybeans destined for aquaculture feed. Plant-based ingredients remain the primary protein source in fish farming, but the shift toward farmed insects as alternative protein introduces new contamination pathways. This paper documents that fish exposed to mold toxins show reduced growth, weakened immune function, and reproductive failure. However, the metabolism of mold toxins in farmed insects remains poorly understood, and data on how insects process and transfer these toxins to feed are sparse—a critical gap in evaluating insect-based feed safety. This review compiles measured mold toxin levels across plant materials, finished aquaculture feeds, and fish-derived food sources, summarizes documented effects of specific mold toxins on fish health, and identifies unresolved safety questions around insects as a protein ingredient.  The expansion of insect farming as a sustainable feed source reveals a material entanglement: the same shift toward "modern" ingredients designed to reduce pressure on wild fish stocks and plant agriculture simultaneously introduces a biological pathway—insect metabolism—that remains largely opaque to safety assessment. Mold toxins move through feed chains in ways that depend on the specific organism processing them; what an insect does with a toxin differs from what a plant does, yet this difference is unmeasured and unaccounted for in current safety frameworks. The economic logic driving aquaculture intensification toward novel feed ingredients thus outpaces the knowledge required to identify where toxin accumulation occurs.

title: Basidiomycete-specific chitin synthase genes have clade-specific roles in cell wall formation and hyphal development in Pleurotus ostreatus
source: Microbiology: Most Recent Articles
excerpt: Chitin, an essential structural component of most fungal cell walls, is produced by transmembrane proteins called chitin synthases (CHSs). Our previous study identified novel basidiomycete-specific chss (chsb) clades (BI–BIII) and suggested functional differences between the chsb genes of clade BII and BIII. This study, together with our previous work, presents the first comprehensive functional analysis and identification of clade-specific roles of chsb genes in vegetative hyphae of the white-rot fungus Pleurotus ostreatus. Using homologous recombination, we disrupted chsb1 (clade BI) and simultaneously disrupted chsb2 and chsb3 (clade BII) to investigate their roles in vegetative growth, cell wall biosynthesis and stress response. Deletion of chsb1 led to reduced colony growth, impaired aerial hyphae formation, thinner cell walls and significant cell wall remodelling. In contrast, single disruptions of chsb2 or chsb3 caused mild phenotypes, while double disruption of these genes resulted in severe growth defects, complete loss of aerial hyphae, and abnormal septation. Relative amounts of chitin were increased in the Δchsb1 and Δchsb2Δchsb3 strains, whereas &#
summary: This paper presents a functional dissection of chitin-synthesizing enzymes across three distinct genetic clades in the oyster mushroom Pleurotus ostreatus. Using targeted gene deletion, the researchers disrupted chsb1 (clade BI) alone and chsb2 with chsb3 together (clade BII), then observed the consequences for hyphal growth, cell wall structure, and stress tolerance. Deletion of chsb1 produced measurable defects: reduced colony expansion, loss of upright filament formation, thinner protective cell walls, and widespread reorganization of wall composition. Disruption of chsb2 or chsb3 individually caused only minor effects, but deleting both genes simultaneously triggered severe impairment—complete absence of upright filaments, abnormal cell division patterns, and substantial growth failure. Both deletion strains accumulated higher absolute amounts of chitin despite their structural and developmental problems.  The findings reveal how a single structural polymer—chitin—can be simultaneously overproduced and functionally compromised depending on which enzyme variants are absent. In Pleurotus, the loss of specific chitin synthase genes triggers compensatory chitin accumulation that fails to restore normal cell wall architecture or growth capacity, suggesting that quantity of material and quality of assembly are decoupled processes. This material entanglement—where a fungus produces more of a critical polymer yet becomes more fragile and developmentally arrested—mirrors broader patterns in the Patchy Anthropocene where organisms and their products become simultaneously more abundant and less functional under stress, revealing how metabolic responses to disruption can paradoxically intensify vulnerability rather than resolve it.

title: Depletion of the Candida albicans TLO gene family reveals a requirement for alpha TLO genes for wild-type virulence
source: Microbiology: Most Recent Articles
excerpt: Candida albicans uniquely possesses an expanded family of genes (the TLO gene family) that encodes 10–15 paralogues of the Med2 component of the transcriptional regulator Mediator. Previous studies have shown that TLO null mutants are unable to form hyphae and are hypersensitive to environmental stress. However, the reason for the TLO gene expansion remains unclear, and the current study aimed to determine if reduction in the TLO family copy number affected virulence. In order to investigate this, we used CRISPR-Cas9 mutagenesis to generate two TLO-depleted mutants: one mutant retaining only TLOβ2 (CaTLO2) and the second mutant containing only TLOγ5 (CaTLO5). Both TLO-depleted mutants exhibited increased filamentous growth, increased susceptibility to specific stresses and reduced virulence in a murine model of oropharyngeal candidiasis (OPC). In vitro, the CaTLO5 mutant also exhibited impaired hyphal escape from macrophages and reduced hyphal invasion of oral keratinocytes. We then investigated if complementation with TLOα1, a gene previously shown to restore wild-type growth in a Δtlo null mutant, could restore virulence. In vitro infection models showed
summary: Candida albicans possesses an unusually large family of TLO genes—10 to 15 copies—that produce variants of the Med2 protein, a component of the transcriptional regulator Mediator. Previous work showed that complete loss of all TLO genes prevents the organism from forming hyphae (elongated filamentous structures) and increases sensitivity to environmental stress, but the evolutionary advantage of maintaining multiple copies remained unexplained. This study used genetic editing to create two mutants: one retaining only the TLOβ2 gene variant and another retaining only the TLOγ5 variant. Both depleted mutants showed increased filamentous growth in culture, heightened vulnerability to specific stresses, and reduced ability to cause infection in mice with oropharyngeal candidiasis (a fungal infection of the mouth and throat). In cell-based assays, the TLOγ5 mutant specifically showed impaired capacity to escape from immune cells called macrophages and reduced ability to invade oral skin cells. The researchers then tested whether adding back the TLOα1 gene variant—previously shown to restore normal growth in completely TLO-depleted strains—could restore disease-causing ability, though the abstract does not report the outcome of this complementation experiment.  The TLO gene expansion in Candida albicans appears to encode functional redundancy tied directly to pathogenic capacity: the same genetic duplication that allows the organism to maintain disease-causing ability under stress also constrains its ability to survive immune pressure and invade host tissues when copy number drops. The tension emerges not between pathogen and resource, but between genetic robustness and clinical vulnerability—a pattern shaped by the selective pressures of human mucosal colonization and the specific immunological barriers of oral epithelium.

title: Lack of a putative intrinsically disordered protein anchored to the sporangiospore cell wall causes fragile sporangium formation in Actinoplanes missouriensis
source: Microbiology: Most Recent Articles
excerpt: The filamentous actinomycete Actinoplanes missouriensis develops terminal sporangia on substrate mycelia via short sporangiophores. Each sporangium, surrounded by an outer envelope, contains a few hundred spores encapsulated by the sporangium matrix. In this study, we identified a spore surface-displayed protein, SspA, that is required for the structural strength of sporangia in A. missouriensis through suppressor screening using a spore release-deficient mutant. SspA has a sortase-dependent cell wall-localizing signal, and its mature part is predicted to be a putative intrinsically disordered protein. An sspA null mutant (ΔsspA) strain formed sporangia, but the mutant sporangia were highly fragile and collapsed immediately to release spores when suspended in aquatic solutions. Transmission electron microscopy revealed that the ΔsspA sporangia did not mature normally; the electron-dense sporangium matrix was not observed in the peripheral region of each spore, and the outer envelope of some sporangia was damaged. Peptide-tagged SspA proteins produced in the ΔsspA strain were detected on the surface of the zoospores using the HiBiT system. The heat tolerance of Δ
summary: The filamentous bacterium Actinoplanes missouriensis produces terminal sporangia—spherical structures containing hundreds of spores held together by a matrix and outer envelope. This paper identifies a protein called SspA, displayed on the spore surface and anchored to the cell wall through a sortase-dependent mechanism, as essential for sporangium structural integrity. The mature form of SspA is predicted to be an intrinsically disordered protein, a type lacking fixed three-dimensional shape. When the gene encoding SspA was deleted, sporangia still formed but became mechanically fragile, collapsing immediately when exposed to aqueous solutions and releasing spores prematurely. Transmission electron microscopy showed that sporangia lacking SspA failed to mature properly: the electron-dense matrix material that normally surrounds individual spores in the peripheral region was absent, and the outer envelope of some sporangia was visibly damaged. Peptide-tagged versions of SspA produced in the mutant strain were detected on zoospore surfaces using the HiBiT detection system.  The structural failure of sporangia without SspA reveals a material dependency embedded in the life cycle of this bacterium. A single protein—disordered in structure yet precisely positioned on the spore surface—appears to mediate the cohesion between individual spores and the matrix that contains them, a function that becomes visible only through its absence. This arrangement exemplifies how microbial survival strategies depend on specific material assemblages: the sporangium envelope, matrix, and surface proteins form an integrated system where weakness in one component cascades into collective fragility, suggesting that the robustness of these bacterial structures emerges not from rigid architectural design but from distributed material interactions that can fail catastrophically when disrupted.

title: Beyond Fumigatus: a molecular portrait of clinical Aspergillus diversity, pathogenicity, and antifungal resistance
source: American Society for Microbiology: Antimicrobial Agents and Chemotherapy: Table of Contents
excerpt: ABSTRACT Aspergillus infection poses a major clinical challenge, particularly in immunocompromised individuals, with invasive diseases associated with high mortality. While Aspergillus fumigatus remains the predominant species causing human infections, recent studies highlight the growing clinical significance of lesser-known and cryptic Aspergillus species, which often exhibit reduced susceptibility to standard antifungal therapies. In this study, we analyzed 196 clinical Aspergillus isolates from 107 patients treated at the NIH Clinical Center between 2019 and 2022. A total of 38 Aspergillus species across 11 taxonomic sections were identified, with non- fumigatus and cryptic species accounting for 77.1% of all isolates. The most frequently recovered species were A. fumigatus sensu stricto (22.9%), A. sydowii (8.7%), A. calidoustus (7.1%), A. nidulans (6.6%), A. tanneri (6.1%), and A. terreus (5.6%). Species-level identification was achieved in 43% of isolates using matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS). In contrast, PCR sequencing confirmed species identity in over 88% of isolates by targeting the internal transcribed spacer (ITS) region of rDNA, 81% using the β-tubulin gene, and 68% using the calmodulin gene. The most common underlying clinical conditions among patients were bronchiectasis (35%), chronic granulomatous disease (22%), and pulmonary non-tuberculous mycobacterial infection (17%). Out of 107 patients, eight died (8/107, 7.5%); six of these deaths occurred in patients with chronic granulomatous disease (CGD) and two in patients with RAG1 deficiency. Antifungal susceptibility testing showed that olorofim had the lowest minimal inhibitory concentrations across species. In contrast, the activity of triazoles and amphotericin B was variable, particularly against A. tanneri , A. calidoustus , and A. sydowii . This study presents one of the largest species-level data sets of Aspergillus isolates to date,
summary: This study analyzed 196 clinical Aspergillus isolates from 107 patients at the National Institutes of Health Clinical Center between 2019 and 2022, identifying 38 distinct Aspergillus species across 11 taxonomic sections. Non-fumigatus and cryptic species accounted for 77.1% of all isolates, with Aspergillus fumigatus sensu stricto representing only 22.9% of recovered organisms. Species identification success varied substantially by method: matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry achieved identification in 43% of isolates, while polymerase chain reaction sequencing of the internal transcribed spacer region succeeded in over 88% of cases, the β-tubulin gene in 81%, and the calmodulin gene in 68%. The most common underlying conditions among infected patients were bronchiectasis (35%), chronic granulomatous disease (22%), and pulmonary non-tuberculous mycobacterial infection (17%), with mortality of 7.5% overall but concentrated among patients with chronic granulomatous disease and those with RAG1 deficiency. Antifungal susceptibility testing revealed that olorofim demonstrated the lowest minimum inhibitory concentrations across species, while triazoles and amphotericin B showed variable activity, particularly reduced effectiveness against Aspergillus tanneri, Aspergillus calidoustus, and Aspergillus sydowii.  Within damaged or immunologically compromised lung tissue—bronchiectatic airways, granulomatous lesions, sites of mycobacterial coinfection—the Aspergillus species composition shifts dramatically from the canonical fumigatus toward a diverse assemblage of lesser-known organisms that resist standard pharmaceutical interventions. This paper documents not merely a clinical inconvenience but a material reorganization of fungal dominance within specific human ecologies: the same immune-weakened bodies that enable fumigatus infection simultaneously select for and concentrate species whose reduced drug susceptibility transforms them into both a therapeutic problem and, potentially, a marker of deeper structural vulnerabilities in how antifungal treatment regimens are designed and deployed.

title: Phosphorene based nano-sensors for mycotoxins in food products: a review
source: Latest Results
excerpt: Mycotoxins are toxic fungal metabolites that pose serious challenges to worldwide food safety. It necessitates the rapid and sensitive methods for real-time detection of these compounds in food products. Conventional detection approaches are accurate, but their complexity, high cost and time consumption often create obstacles. The use of nanosensors, particularly those based on 2D-nanomaterials like phosphorene, are novel means for quick, sensitive, and low-cost detection of mycotoxins. Its tunable bandgap, high surface-to-volume ratio, and high electron mobility enables fast, sensitive, and low-cost detection of mycotoxins in real-food samples. Advances in synthesis and functionalization of phosphorene have improved its stability and integration into electrochemical, optical and field-effect transistor based sensors. Such phosphorene-based nanosensors exhibited exceptional low detection limits with improved selectivity for real-time monitoring of complex food products. Overall, phosphorene derived nanosensors have been established to be an innovative technology for on-site monitoring of mycotoxin contamination, facilitating overcome the challenges in food quality control.
summary: This paper surveys phosphorene-based nanosensors as detection tools for mycotoxins in food products. The source identifies mycotoxins—toxic compounds produced by fungi—as a persistent food safety problem requiring rapid, sensitive detection methods. Conventional detection approaches, while accurate, are complex, expensive, and time-consuming. This study examines phosphorene, a two-dimensional nanomaterial with a tunable bandgap, high surface-to-volume ratio, and high electron mobility, as the basis for faster and cheaper detection systems. Recent advances in phosphorene synthesis and chemical modification have improved its stability and enabled integration into electrochemical sensors, optical sensors, and field-effect transistor sensors. This paper reports that phosphorene-based nanosensors achieve exceptionally low detection limits with improved selectivity for monitoring mycotoxins in real food samples, positioning the technology as viable for on-site contamination monitoring.  The dual character of this technological development reflects broader patterns in food safety infrastructure under resource constraint. Phosphorene nanosensors promise to democratize mycotoxin detection—moving it from expensive laboratory facilities to on-site, low-cost monitoring—yet this same shift relocates detection capacity away from centralized oversight toward distributed, decentralized points in food supply chains. The material properties that make phosphorene effective (its sensitivity, its capacity to integrate with multiple sensor types) are the same properties that render it dependent on continued synthesis, functionalization, and maintenance; the promise of accessible detection thus entangles food producers and retailers in new technical dependencies and supply relationships.

title: Occurrence of toxigenic fungi and mycotoxins in pig feeds: potential implications for workers’ exposure
source: Latest Results
excerpt: The objective of the study was to assess the potential exposure of pig farmers to toxigenic fungi and mycotoxins by investigating their presence in feeds to understand whether these matrices may represent a source of release into the environment and pose an occupational health risk. Nine feed samples were collected from several intensive farms located in Southern Italy and analyzed through a multi-methodological approach. A total of 35 molds were isolated and A. flavus turned out to be the most frequently species, representing nearly 46% (16/35), followed by A. niger and A. candidus, each at 11.43%. Among the 16 A. flavus strains, 8 possessed all 5 key genes of the aflatoxin biosynthetic pathway but only 4 showed the true aflatoxigenic capacity as confirmed by HPLC/MS-MS analysis (AFB1 range: 0.52–1030 µg/L). The most frequent mycotoxin was Ochratoxin A (OTA), occurring in 100% of the samples at mean concentration of 33.6 µg/kg while Fumonisin B1 (FB1) occurred in 97% of the feed samples at mean concentration of 247.1 µg/kg. Only one sample was contaminated by Aflatoxin B1 (5.84 µg/kg), classified as carcinogenic to humans. Our results confirm that feedstuffs can contribute to cont
summary: This study examined nine pig feed samples from intensive farms in Southern Italy to determine whether feeds serve as a source of occupational exposure to toxigenic fungi and their toxic metabolites. Researchers isolated 35 mold colonies total, with *Aspergillus flavus* comprising 46 percent of isolates, followed by *Aspergillus niger* and *Aspergillus candidus* at 11.43 percent each. Of the 16 *Aspergillus flavus* strains recovered, half carried all five genes required for aflatoxin production, yet only four strains demonstrated actual aflatoxin-producing capacity when tested by high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with tandem mass spectrometry, with aflatoxin B1 levels ranging from 0.52 to 1030 micrograms per liter. Ochratoxin A appeared in all nine feed samples at a mean concentration of 33.6 micrograms per kilogram, while Fumonisin B1 was detected in 97 percent of samples at 247.1 micrograms per kilogram; only one sample contained Aflatoxin B1 at 5.84 micrograms per kilogram, a substance classified as carcinogenic to humans.  The presence of these toxigenic fungi and their metabolites in feed materials reveals a material pathway through which occupational hazard enters the farm workplace. The genetic capacity for toxin production does not guarantee actual toxin release—a gap illustrated by the discrepancy between strains carrying biosynthetic genes and those demonstrating measurable aflatoxin output—yet the feeds themselves remain contaminated with multiple mycotoxins regardless of their origin, creating a tangible exposure risk for workers handling these materials daily. Feed contamination thus operates simultaneously as an indicator of agricultural vulnerability to fungal colonization and as a mechanism through which workers become exposed to carcinogenic substances embedded in the routine materials of intensive animal production.

title: Aflatoxin surveillance in Punjab’s dairy sector: feed and milk contamination
source: Latest Results
excerpt: This panel-type longitudinal study assesses the impact of socio-economic factors on aflatoxin contamination in milk and feed within Punjab, Pakistan. This study enabled monthly tracking of contamination and awareness levels, providing a dynamic assessment of changes over time. Utilizing a geographically representative cluster random sampling, 36 districts were stratified into five clusters (north, south, east, west and central) for comprehensive analysis. A total of 300 milk and 300 feed samples were collected monthly and analysed using competitive enzyme immunoassay kits. Data collected from face-to-face interviews and laboratory analyses were statistically analyzed using SPSS and R- software. Socio-economic variables, including education, income, landholding size, herd size, and milk yield, were examined in relation to contamination levels with adjustments for potential confounding factors. Results indicated significant correlations between education levels and aflatoxin awareness, with higher education correlating with reduced contamination. Aflatoxin levels were highest in the central region, linked to reliance on self-stored grains. Training on aflatoxin management significant
summary: This panel-type longitudinal study tracked aflatoxin contamination in milk and feed across Punjab, Pakistan over multiple months, collecting 300 milk samples and 300 feed samples monthly from 36 districts stratified into five geographic clusters (north, south, east, west, and central). Samples were analysed using competitive enzyme immunoassay kits, with data from laboratory analyses and face-to-face interviews examined using statistical software. The study found significant correlations between education levels and awareness of aflatoxin contamination, with higher education associated with reduced contamination levels. Aflatoxin levels were highest in the central region, a pattern linked to farmers' reliance on self-stored grains. Training on aflatoxin management showed significant impact, though the abstract does not specify the outcome measures.  Within Punjab's dairy sector, contamination emerges not as a uniform biological threat but as a stratified exposure shaped by material storage practices and educational access. The concentration of aflatoxins in the central region—tied to on-farm grain storage—reveals how economic constraints and agricultural infrastructure determine which populations encounter higher mycotoxin loads in their feed and milk. Education functions here as both a marker of awareness and a proxy for economic position: farmers with greater schooling possess resources to adopt safer storage methods and market engagement, while those dependent on self-stored grains face both the biological hazard and the structural conditions that produce it. The efficacy of training interventions suggests that contamination is not inevitable but rather contingent on knowledge and material capacity—a pattern that implicates broader patterns of rural inequality in how mycotoxin exposure is distributed across the region.

title: Production of ETP-Type (Epipolythiodioxopiperazine) Mycotoxins in Clinical Fungal Isolates
source: acs: Journal of Natural Products: Table of Contents
excerpt: Gliotoxin, an epipolythiodioxopiperazine (ETP) toxin, has been reported in several fungal species, but its distribution remains unclear. We analyzed 140 clinical isolates from two major hospitals using HPLC and LC-MS/MS to assess gliotoxin production. Gliotoxin was detected only in Aspergillus fumigatus isolates, with no production observed in any other species under multiple culture conditions. Molecular networking and comparative genomics revealed distinct ETP-related pathways in non-A. fumigatus species, including aspirochlorine derivatives in Aspergillus flavus and aranotin-related metabolites in Aspergillus terreus. These findings clarify species-specific ETP profiles and confirm A. fumigatus as the sole gliotoxin producer among the isolates examined.
summary: Gliotoxin, a toxic compound produced by certain fungi, was detected only in *Aspergillus fumigatus* isolates among 140 clinical samples from two hospitals analyzed using high-performance liquid chromatography and liquid chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry. No other fungal species tested produced gliotoxin under any of the culture conditions examined. Molecular networking and comparative genomics revealed that related fungal species produce distinct toxic metabolites instead: *Aspergillus flavus* produces aspirochlorine derivatives, while *Aspergillus terreus* produces aranotin-related compounds. This paper clarifies that gliotoxin production is restricted to *A. fumigatus* among the clinical isolates examined, while other *Aspergillus* species have evolved separate metabolic pathways for producing structurally related toxins.  The distribution of these species-specific toxins across clinical *Aspergillus* isolates reflects a material reality of hospital environments: the same genus of fungus circulating through medical settings produces different chemical weapons depending on which species colonizes a patient's lungs or wounds. Gliotoxin's restriction to *A. fumigatus* does not make the other species less clinically relevant—it redirects attention to their own toxic metabolites as equally important drivers of infection and tissue damage. What emerges is not a simple hierarchy of pathogenic threat but a fragmented landscape of fungal toxicity, where clinical risk depends on which particular species establishes itself in a given body, under given conditions of immune suppression, antibiotic exposure, or architectural vulnerability.

title: An integrated multiscale computational study of nephrotoxicity mechanism of citrinin and its metabolites (CIMs)：density functional theory, network toxicology, and molecular dynamics simulation
source: Latest Results
excerpt: Citrinin is a common mycotoxin found in food and poses risks to both human and animal health. While extensive research has been conducted on the nephrotoxicity of citrinin itself, the nephrotoxicity of its metabolites remains unclear.
